#1
DeckbuildingJun 202627K views

Using Math to Decide How Many Bad Cards to Run

It's okay to put *some* janky trash in your deck
We argue that Commander decks can include some bad or situational cards without harming consistency if they can reliably reach a post-engine state with high card draw.We recommend using hypergeometric calculations to check the odds of drawing too many early-game dead cards.By categorizing cards into premium enablers, situational blanks, and general support, deckbuilders can balance fun inclusions with a functional early game.
Use this in your deckSeparate your nonland cards into three buckets and use hypergeometric math to ensure your odds of drawing multiple blanks in the first 10 cards is low.
Pet CardsDeck ConsistencyCard EvaluationDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • Commander decks often have a pre-engine phase with limited card access and a post-engine phase where drawing extra cards makes individual card quality less important.
  • Including some situational or weak cards can be acceptable if the deck can reliably reach the post-engine state.
Evidence
  • Hypergeometric probabilities show that with about five situational blanks, the chance of drawing multiple such cards early is low.
  • Fast lands entering untapped early demonstrate how situational cards can be reliable when later card selection becomes available.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Categorize nonland cards into three buckets: high-quality enablers, situational blanks, and general support to assess early-game consistency.
  • Use a hypergeometric calculator to check that the probability of drawing an engine starter is high and the probability of drawing multiple blanks is low.
Limitation
  • If the number of bucket two cards grows into double digits, the risk of drawing too many dead early cards rises and can disrupt deck function.
Example
  • A Radha, Heir to Keld deck runs a single humorous seven-drop like Tyrant of Discord as a bucket two card that does not harm early consistency.
  • Protection spells like Heroic Intervention are dead early but valuable later, exemplifying how situational cards can be included sparingly.
More quotes
  1. It might actually be okay to have some bad cards in your deck.
  2. You can check how often you won't draw cards you don't want.
  3. I think of a lot of Commander decks as having two very distinct phases, pre-engine and post-engine.
  4. Getting to purge the trash for the first time in a game and sculpt a hand of seven beautiful, situationally applicable cards, delicious.
  5. You can almost think about all of these bucket two bad cards as blank for the purposes of your early game.
  6. Craft the foundation of your deck in a way that's maximally consistent and flexible, and the amount of room to sprinkle garbage into that bad boy might just surprise you.
  7. My Radha Heir to Keld deck has a single permanent funny seven drop deck slot. It was occupied by Panglacial Wurm in the past, and it's currently occupied by Tyrant of Discord, both not very good creatures.
  8. Treat this as your reward for being such a responsible deck builder in other ways.
  9. The first three turns of the game are the ones where you're going to be the hardest pressed to have a land that both enters untapped and gives the colors you need, and fast lands fill this exact profile.
  10. With any other commander, this deck's ratios would make no sense at all and it would flail around doing nothing most of the time.
Full transcript

I'm working on a little project right now, and I need your help.
All the details are on a YouTube post I'm linking, but basically I'm building a deck around an unknown event card created for me using feedback from both my in-person opponents and my online audience.
If you want to suggest cards that could go in the deck, you can do that under the YouTube post; and if you want to guarantee an on-color card going in the deck, you can send it to my PO box, address in the description.
With that said, let's get to the topic of your horrible pet cards.

Calculating Your Desired (and Undesired) Draws

Let's say you have a type of card that you want to see in a lot of games with a given deck: a mana rock, a one mana creature that gains life, or something else.
If that's the case, it can be helpful to run some calculations on how often you'll draw it, either by using a hypergeometric calculator like the one on my website, or by referencing the built-in calculators that a deck building website like Architect might have.
At this point, a lot of players are aware of these sorts of tools, but what they might not be aware of is that you can do the opposite; you can check how often you won't draw cards you don't want.
And just as you could use this to decide how many of a desirable card to include, you could do the same to decide how few of an undesirable card type to add, because it might actually be okay to have some "bad cards" in your deck.
But hold on, let's take a step back here.
What do I mean when I say "bad card," and why would I want to run any of these?

What Is a "Bad Card"?

Starting with that first question, the card we're talking about could truly be a "bad card." My Radha, Heir to Keld deck has a single permanent funny seven drop deck slot; it was occupied by Panglacial Wurm in the past, and it's currently occupied by Tyrant of Discord, with both of these being not very good creatures.
However, the meaning of "bad card" could also be a bit more complicated, being a card that is either situational or bad without certain other cards to support it.
As one example, in three different grindy decks I play, there's a card present that is there entirely for the purpose of life gain.
These cards are pretty worthless outside of this task, but they do an admirable job in a mid-to-late game situation where my life total is in a state of peril.
As another example, in my bracket two deck, I run a couple artifact creatures that untap things.
These cards on their own are terrible, but paired with other cards, they can be pretty powerful; and Voltaic Construct is technically part of a convoluted infinite combo the deck can assemble.
As a final example, protection spells fit both of these criteria as cards that aren't useful until you're doing something worth protecting, and whose usefulness isn't realized until an opponent tries to disrupt you.
So, this is what I mean when I say "bad cards": cards that frequently don't feel very good to draw for one reason or another, particularly in the early game.
But why does the category I'm constructing treat these different types of cards as the same?
And if they're bad in some sense, then why run them in the first place?

The Pre-Engine and Post-Engine Distinction

The answer is that I think of a lot of Commander decks as having two very distinct phases, "pre-engine" and "post-engine." In the early phases of a game, you only have access to a very small number of cards, and a lot of decks gain access to additional cards through the synergies between these early cards.
This could take a lot of different forms: maybe you have a Wall of Omens and a Teleportation Circle; maybe you follow up your Caustic Bronco with an Ishin; maybe you curve Thran Dynamo into a Pull from Tomorrow; maybe you just play a standalone draw engine like an Esper Sentinel or a Phyrexian Arena, or even a series of one-off spells like Night's Whisper and Harmonize.
Regardless, you're using stuff from your more limited early game card pool to help your deck reach a state where it's online and has access to a much wider range of the different tools within it.
And there are actually a lot of things that can help with this: card draw, tutors, filtering, ramp, synergy pieces that assist with any of those last four things.
All of these can help you build up to a point where you have access to a greater amount of your library.
Removal and defensive cards can also help with this, since they buy you the time to get that engine up and going.
Different cards will help different amounts with this, but quite a lot of cards will assist at least a little bit.
All of this is well and good, but you might still be wondering why exactly I'm drawing this "pre-engine" and "post-engine" distinction.
The answer is that once you're cycling through a more significant amount of your deck per turn, whether or not each individual card is useful in a wide variety of situations starts to become a lot less important.
If I draw five cards and one of them isn't that useful to me right now, that's okay; I still drew four other cards.
This is also the point of the game where you're going to start discarding to hand size if you don't have a Reliquary Tower effect, and this is one of my favorite points in the game to be in, getting to purge the trash for the first time in a game and sculpt a hand of seven beautiful, situationally applicable cards, delicious.
As a slight tangent, this sort of reasoning is also why I like a type of budget dual land that a lot of other people seem to dislike, the humble fast land.
A land that enters tapped if you control three or more other lands might seem kind of bad in theory.
In the average bracket three Commander game, you're hopefully making at least seven land drops; so, in less than half of land drops will this land enter untapped.
However, turn four and beyond is also a point in the game at which a lot of decks will be drawing or looting or otherwise filtering, enabling them to find their way to a variety of different lands with relative ease.
The first three turns of the game are the ones where you're going to be the hardest pressed to have a land that both enters untapped and gives the colors you need, and fast lands fill this exact profile.
So, if your deck is one that rounds a corner and starts to see a lot more cardboard at some point, having a somewhat subpar or situational card is a lot less of a problem from that point onward.
But what does this mean for the early game?
Well, as I suggested earlier, you can do a bit of math to see how likely it is for your game plan to get online.

The Three-Bucket System

One option here is to separate your non-land cards into three buckets.
"Bucket one" is high-quality drawn filtering, stuff that finds you what you need to succeed quickly and easily.
"Bucket two" is stuff that's basically useless for getting your deck online: hyper-situational cards, protection spells, later game tech pieces, finishers, and stuff that's just kind of bad that you include for shits and giggles, as is your god-given right.
"Bucket three" is all the other stuff outside of these two categories: ramp, removal, somewhat narrower draw, and sometimes applicable tech pieces.
Now, you perform two different checks here.
For one, how likely is it for you to draw one of your "bucket one" cards, your premium quality enablers?
The larger this number is, the better; but there are certainly reasons why this number could be lower for some decks.
Question two is how likely it is for you to draw one of your "bucket two" cards that helps you very little in the early game, and possibly more importantly, how likely is it for you to draw multiple of these?
If you're running five or so hyper-situational cards, the odds of drawing more than one of these is quite low.
By contrast, if that number starts creeping up into the double digits, you might have games where you draw an Overwhelming Stampede and a Heroic Intervention in your first 10 cards of the game, a Fervor and a Full Throttle, a Clever Concealment and a Storm Herd.
You can almost think about all of these "bucket two" bad cards as blank for the purposes of your early game.
Having a lot of these is generally going to slow your timeline for getting off the ground and lead to more stall outs; but you can counterbalance these tendencies with a greater amount of "bucket one" cards, high quality draw and filtering.
Hitting a string of situational cards you won't want until later in the game can be a problem, but it might not be if you have a lot of filtering and draw and tutors at the ready to cut you through this dead air.

So How Much Janky Nonsense Is Okay?

I mean, don't let me stop you from living your best life, especially if you're in more bracket two territory, but the realistic answer is that it depends on what the rest of your deck is doing.
If we're talking about some terrible pet cards that you would literally die for, take a look at how many other cards you're running that you'd be kind of bummed to draw before turn five.
If that number is very low, you're probably okay to add some garbage to your deck; treat this as your reward for being such a responsible deck builder in other ways.
If that number is stretching into double digits territory, you'll want to be a bit more careful.
How much general purpose draw and filtering are you running?
How broadly useful is your commander for generating resources and helping your deck get going?
My deck with the highest number of "bucket two" cards is my Meria of Ultron deck with a ton of protection as well as equipments to give extra damage and evasion.
With any other commander, this deck's ratios would make no sense at all and it would flail around doing nothing most of the time.
However, because Meria is a sweet draw engine and one of the best mana producers in the game, and because my deck is pretty decent at protecting her, it ends up working out most of the time.
Likewise, it's not a coincidence that my one bracket three deck where I allow myself to indulge in significantly more janky pet cards is also one where I have a mana dork commander and a massive pile of four cost ramp spells and six to seven cost bombs that I'm pretty much always happy to draw.
Craft the foundation of your deck in a way that's maximally consistent and flexible, and the amount of room to sprinkle garbage into that bad boy might just surprise you.

Thank You and Channel Support

Thanks for watching.
Make sure to check out the YouTube post in the description if you want to contribute to that.
Another thing you can check out is fantastic products from Dragon Shield.
I'm a long time enjoyer of their dual matte sleeves, with my Radha deck still going strong in the same set of peach sleeves thousands of shuffles in.
Sleeves that have been used that heavily have a really interesting shuffle feel to them, but that's not something you'll ever get to experience with most other brands.
Dragon Shield has also been expanding out into deck boxes and other storage methods, which I'm also a fan of; the deck boxes are elegant and effective.
Get 5% off your order at the link in the description, and you'll be helping us in the process.
If you want to support us more directly, you can sign up for my Patreon or a YouTube membership.
I'm in the process of moving right now, which is exciting in some ways but also expensive; so, any extra dough to throw at gas and Craigslist furniture is much appreciated.
A big thanks to my current patrons and YouTube members for their ongoing support.

#2
DeckbuildingJun 202663K views

How to Build a Hidden Commander Deck: Two Key Principles

The Two Rules for Making a Successful "Hidden Commander" Deck
To build a successful hidden commander deck, you must find functional analogs for your secret commander and minimize cards that only work with that commander.These rules ensure the deck works when the key card is absent or removed.We demonstrate by building a budget deck around Sibsig Ceremony with Athreos, Shroud-Veiled as a synergistic commander.
Use this in your deckFind at least one functional analog for your hidden commander and cut any support cards that do not also synergize with your visible commander.
Athreos, Shroud-VeiledWhiteBlackGreenAristocratsTokensReanimatorBudgetDeckbuilding RestrictionsSynergyResilienceDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • Two rules for hidden commander success: find functional analogs and minimize the footprint of synergy cards.
  • These rules follow from the simple fact that you often will not have your secret commander in play.
Evidence
  • Sunforger decks succeed because the instants they fetch are already independently useful.
  • Astral Slide decks succeed due to available direct analogs like functional reprints.
  • Sibsig Ceremony lacks inherent analogs and requires dedicated support, making it a trickier build.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Choose a commander that independently synergizes with the secret commander to reduce card slot demands.
  • Ensure support cards advance your visible commander's strategy to avoid dead draws.
Limitation
  • If a hidden commander demands heavy support without analogs, the deck becomes vulnerable to targeted removal.
Example
  • The Sibsig Ceremony deck used Athreos and other indestructible creatures to turn forced sacrifice into token generation.
More quotes
  1. Hidden commanders are an interesting subject, but they definitely aren't all made equal.
  2. Some of the classically touted examples of build-around cards, such as Sunforger, are deceptively simple to pull off because the cards they work with don't eat up that much deck space.
  3. How do I make weird niche hidden commanders work?
  4. Cards don't always get drawn and sometimes get removed.
  5. [The Sibsig Ceremony] This card keeps coming up.
  6. In the process [of building a deck], I ended up landing on two principles that should guide anybody building a deck centered around a card in their 99.
  7. One, find analogs for your central card, and two, minimize the footprint of cards that synergize with your central card.
  8. Both principles stem from the essential fact that you simply won't always have access to your hidden commander card.
  9. Astral Slide is a ridiculously powerful late game tool, so I'll almost always want a copy of the card at some point even if I survive the early to mid game.
  10. The Sibsig Ceremony provides us a lot of mana through its discount. So the first order of business in making sure the deck works without it is adding a lot of other ramp, finding analogs.
Full transcript
The Hidden Commander Question

At a panel I was hosting during MagicCon Vegas a few weeks ago, an audience member asked a question that really got me thinking: “I’ve been trying to build a hidden commander and I don’t know how to make it work.
It’s the Sibsig Ceremony, it’s green-black.
Creature spells cost two less to cast, and when a creature you control enters, if you cast it, destroy that creature, then you get a token.
I don’t want to just make it a token instead.”

Hidden commanders are an interesting subject, but they definitely aren’t all made equal.
I responded to this person’s question, as well as my co-host’s answer, by saying that some of the classically touted examples of build-around cards, such as Sunforger, are deceptively simple to pull off because the cards they work with don’t eat up that much deck space.
Red and white instants that cost four or less mana are good and you’ll already want some of them in your deck; equipments are also good, and you should be able to find some other options to grab if you want to run tutors for your Sunforger.
However, for a secret commander that typically requires more deck space to support it, as the Sibsig Ceremony often does, you’ll need to thread the needle a bit more carefully in deck building.

Looking for Analogs

The second question to consider is: are there analogs?
Are there cards that will do what you want to do with the Sibsig Ceremony that aren’t it?
Because if people get to know this deck, they will start removing this card very rapidly.
So that’s another thing you want to look at; if you are dedicating a lot of deck space to this card, what else can work with this card?
That’s kind of where you need to be looking.

And that felt like pretty good advice to give generally.
Cards don’t always get drawn and sometimes get removed.
I tangled with the Sibsig Ceremony myself a bit a year prior for a video building a deck based around cards chosen by my patrons, so I had some familiarity with that card in particular and its challenges, but I’m by no means an expert.
That deck was a little on the goofy side and the card isn’t really my first choice as a build-around in the first place.
So I figured it could be somebody else’s job to be a scholar on the Sibsig Ceremony in particular.
However, that plan was upended about a week later when I was spinning a wheel to decide what deck I’d build for the Magic Mirror podcast.

The Wheel Lands on the Sibsig Ceremony

“My card.
Pretty much the same thing.
Dragon swarm, dragon swarm, dragon swarm.
Oh, no. Oh, please no. Oh, yes.
Oh, god, no. The Sibsig Ceremony.
This card keeps coming up.
Let’s go.”

The set of wheels I created spun me into building a $60 budget deck with 50 or more subtypes in some way centered around the Sibsig Ceremony, this old nemesis of mine.
At this point, I just accepted that I needed to create a full-effort build-around for this card in order to shake whatever curse had caused it to follow me.
And to be clear, I do mean an honest-to-god bracket three deck, maybe a little janky by choice and by nature of the requirements going into this deck, but no jankier than that and with plenty of powerful plays up its sleeve.
In the process of doing precisely this, I ended up landing on two principles that should guide anybody building a deck centered around a card in their 99.
One of these being something I directly identified at the panel, and the other one being something I kind of alluded to more indirectly and have since developed further.
These principles are: one, find analogs for your central card, and two, minimize the footprint of cards that synergize with your central card.
Both principles stem from the essential fact that you simply won’t always have access to your hidden commander card.
So, here, I’m going to walk you through my process of building a Sibsig Ceremony deck and in doing so, demonstrate how these two rules produced a successful hidden commander deck.

Why the Sibsig Ceremony Is Tricky

To start with, let’s discuss the nature of hidden commanders a bit and why the Sibsig Ceremony is one of the trickier ones you can choose.
The phrase “hidden commander” simply refers to a deck where one of the cards in the 99 is built around more heavily than the commander itself.
My Sharuum Astral Slide deck is an example of this, where Astral Slide is a key piece of how the deck works and Sharuum plays more of a secondary role in adding flexibility to the deck.
This type of deck usually involves some number of tutors to find the core card, although whether or not a deck tutors for its hidden commander every game might depend on the nature of the card a bit.
Astral Slide is a ridiculously powerful late-game tool, so I’ll almost always want a copy of the card at some point even if I survive the early to mid game without it.
For purposes of adhering to principle one, having analogs, Astral Slide makes my job easy because it has a functionally identical card as well as a similar enchantment in a different color, effectively giving me three copies of my key card.
I don’t need to get creative finding cards to do something similar; they’re just handed to me.

Other hidden commanders make principle number two the easy one, allowing a player to minimize the footprint of cards that work with their central card quite easily and not feel like their deck space is being gobbled up.
As I mentioned earlier, Sunforger is a great build-around because the cards it works with span a wide range of different roles, and since they don’t even need to be in hand for Sunforger to synergize with them, the challenge of figuring out the right number of instants to guarantee setup and payoff lining up is basically nonexistent.
So, these are two secret commanders where one of the two principles I mentioned earlier is basically guaranteed, easing a bit of the challenge of deck building.
However, some hidden commanders don’t make either of these two principles easy, and this brings us to the Sibsig Ceremony.

Building Around the Sibsig Ceremony

Let’s start, as I did when building this deck, by tackling what this card does.
The first line of text is 100% upside: it makes our creature spells cost two less.
That’s a pretty great effect for three mana.
Urza’s Incubator and Semblance Anvil come the closest to this rate among artifact-based producers, but each of these has a substantial downside or limitation.
So does the Sibsig Ceremony, though.
The second line of text destroys creatures when they enter if they were cast and creates a 2/2 Zombie.
This does place a serious limitation on what’s going to make sense to go with this card, though it’s definitely not an insurmountable limitation.

Choosing the Commander and Early Synergies

When I was searching for a commander for this card, I knew I wanted it to be something on the more generic side to work out with the other restrictions I’d been given.
And I also knew I wanted it to do something that combines relatively easily with the Sibsig Ceremony to produce positive outcomes.
In other words, I wanted something low footprint, a commander that kind of just works with the Sibsig Ceremony without me needing to put in a lot of extra effort or bring in additional cards.
This is how I landed on Athreos, Shroud-Veiled.
The card is six mana, it’s indestructible even when I have it fully online via devotion, and it has an ability that works well alongside ETBs and on-death effects among creatures I play.
Even without anything else on my side of the board, the card is just pretty solid if I get it into play on turn four off a turn three ceremony and start throwing coins on opposing creatures, effectively making Sibsig Ceremony a worn powerstone for this precise function.

To go along with this, I grabbed a handful of other indestructible and pseudo-indestructible creatures: Erebos, Bleak-Hearted as another god that works well with my zombie tokens, Yahenni as a sac outlet, Breathkeeper Seraph as a unique soulbond creature that can save itself and others, and all four in-color Myojins.
These are eight-mana spirits that enter with an indestructible counter and can remove this counter to do a variety of powerful effects.
So in these instances, we’re taking advantage of the Sibsig Ceremony’s powerful ramp potential with creatures that don’t care about the rest of its text box.
And already amongst these indestructible creatures, we can see a lot of points of cohesion.
Erebos plus Myojin of Blooming Dawn can draw a billion cards, and any big Erebos plays are assisted by a free sac outlet like Yahenni.
Athreos, Shroud-Veiled and Myojin of Grim Betrayal both work fabulously alongside conventional board wipes, as does Breathkeeper Seraph to a lesser extent, and Myojin of Cleansing Fire is a board wipe.
The picture that’s being painted so far is of a big-mana controlling aristocrats-oriented deck.
And to minimize friction, we’re going to want to expand on that vision and make sure it’s a consistent feature of the deck, starting with mana.

Enabling Mana Without the Ceremony

The Sibsig Ceremony provides us a lot of mana through its discount, so the first order of business in making sure the deck works without it is adding a lot of other ramp, finding analogs.
Heartless Summoning is the easiest choice here as a similar two-mana creature discount plus downside sort of effect.
Another solid choice is ramp on ETB creatures like Solemn Simulacrum, Scampering Surveyor, and Kor Cartographer.
These work on their own as an alternate ladder from four to six mana to get the commander and give an immediate coin destination, and they also function quite enjoyably with the Sibsig Ceremony in play, costing two less mana and getting their 2/2 bodies replaced with 2/2 zombies.
Past these, I chose to add Crowded Crypt as a synergistic rock, a Sol Ring, Mer Convert for subtype purposes (the wheeled decree that the deck needs to have 50 or more subtypes, remember), and seven other two-mana rocks to round things out.
This deck ended up pretty high-curved, so playing a mana rock is really what we want to be doing with our two-mana turn.

Death as an Advantage

So, okay.
Step one of this was making sure that this deck can make use of the Sibsig Ceremony’s mana generation and that it has plenty of other ways to generate mana in the card’s absence.
Great.
But let’s think a little harder about this second line of text.
It’s intended to be a downside, but does it have to be?
The biggest area where it isn’t a downside is when it comes to creatures that we actively want to die.
Take Junji, the Midnight Sky.
Both the discard and the reanimate modes of this death trigger are solid three-mana effects, and with the Sibsig Ceremony, you get that plus a zombie and a death trigger and a thing in your graveyard you can use for other purposes, more on those things later.
Same with Ao, the Dawn Sky; one mode is a sort of Collected Company effect, and the other is a big amplification to our board if we’re already loaded up with zombies.
Both are great to get for three mana, and even better with some extra goodies tacked on top.
We’ve also got Erebos, Bleak-Hearted and Dark Prophecy as powerful draw engines when creatures die, which pair nicely with both zombie tokens and our non-token creatures meeting an untimely demise.

All of this on-death stuff goes great with the Sibsig Ceremony, though it does also pose a bit of a problem: these cards are a lot less good if we don’t have a way for creatures to die.
If we assume the Sibsig Ceremony will always be in play, we’re going to run into some problems when it’s not.
So, this deck also integrates a number of ways to sacrifice our creatures as well, analogs for the Sibsig Ceremony’s destroy effect.

Analog Sacrifice Effects and Recursion

Season of Loss is a flexible removal tool that can also draw, Fanatical Devotion is a sac outlet that can also save creatures from destruction, and there are five other cards that do some sort of sacrificing task.
These are perfect here because they replace the creature death portion of what the Sibsig Ceremony does, but most of them are also great when paired with the Sibsig Ceremony.
And this is the perfect relationship to create with a build-around card: you want the best possible spread of situational usefulness on a card, not just the one that’s best when paired with a hidden commander.

The same logic applies to this deck’s 19 ETB creatures.
Each of these will have a bit of a spread when it comes to how effective they are, but the floor is decently high, making their footprint relatively low.
As a baseline, they’re fine.
A deck that’s casting a standalone Simulacrum, a standalone Chupacabra, a standalone Grotesque Abomination, at worst, you’re talking about bracket two midrange deck territory.
Bring in the Sibsig Ceremony and suddenly the power of these jumps a couple notches up from that, particularly for the three-to-four-mana ETB creatures.
Those creatures are around 2/2 already, so the Sibsig Ceremony basically just looks like a two-mana discount that shoves their real body into the graveyard.
Speaking of which, this is where the aristocrats stuff we mentioned earlier comes into play again.

This deck has five cards that recur creatures, and it also has the seven sources of creature sacrifice I mentioned earlier for when the Sibsig Ceremony isn’t in play to assist creatures with their journey to the graveyard.
And when you’re using one of those and have a bit more flexibility on your timing for creatures dying, Athreos also works as a recursion engine, offering a ton of value generation alongside one of the deck’s four sources of continual sacrifice.
If you’re getting a Sepulchral Primordial trigger every turn, a Krob Trigger every turn, or a couple Ashnod’s Altar triggers every turn, you’re going to be feeling pretty fabulous about things.

Flicker and the Mirror

There are also a few flicker cards baked into the deck to enhance the ETB plan further, as well as an extra spicy inclusion: Mirror of Life Trapping, an artifact that exiles creatures when they enter if they were cast and then returns them.

#3
Meta CommentaryMay 2026135K views

The Problem With Magic's One-Off Set Structure

The Subtle Reason Why Sets Feel Worse Now
We argue that Magic's shift from multi-set blocks to yearly one-off sets has made the game feel rushed and disconnected.It acknowledges that three-set blocks often had filler sets that didn't convey the story well.We propose that an alternative set structure could better balance immersion and mechanical iteration.
Use this in your deckWhen building around a new mechanic from a one-off set, ask if it is likely to receive future support, as most mechanics now remain isolated.
Format HealthDesign IntentMeta Trends
Principle
  • Multi-set blocks enable players to become immersed in a plane's setting and storyline over time.
  • Mechanical themes develop more fully when given multiple sets to iterate and expand.
Evidence
  • No new plane has had a multi-set block since Ixalan, which concluded over 8 years ago.
  • Later sets within a block historically sold worse than the first set.
  • Currently, Magic releases five or more one-off sets per year, each on a different plane.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Deckbuilders should evaluate new mechanics for their standalone power, as one-off sets rarely provide follow-up support.
  • Without mechanical iteration across sets, synergy-based archetypes become riskier to build around.
Limitation
  • Three-set blocks sometimes forced players to experience a poorly received plane for an entire year.
  • Crafting satisfying limited environments across three connected sets was difficult for developers.
Example
  • Dragon's Maze, the third set of Return to Ravnica block, attempted to feature all 10 guilds but felt cramped and failed to convey its maze race story.
More quotes
  1. Having every set be a new plane, particularly with five or more sets per year, makes things feel rushed, leaving no time to breathe and become immersed in a particular world.
  2. The gradual pivot from three-set blocks to purely one-off sets has been an unambiguous tragedy.
  3. I could tell you that we as magic players are fallen and need to return to the glory days of 2013, where magic sets came in threes and where bold duels could take place on a middle school lunch table.
  4. Dragon's Maze simply didn't feel like a proper third set, rather just a little bit of extra Ravnica with some attached plot that not enough effort was put into guiding us through with its design.
  5. Born of the Gods felt more like Theros B-sides than anything else, just some kind of odd designs to go along with a group of five gods in order to allow the 15 gods to be evenly spread between the three sets.
  6. Fate Reforged feels more like the middle of an Animorphs cover than it does its own cohesive world.
  7. It's okay to have a hat set. However, for that sort of set to feel justified, it has to feel differentiated in some way from what surrounds.
  8. The biggest reason I like two-set blocks is that they're able to tell the story of a place much better than a one-set block could.
  9. Seeing that clear change communicated through the design of a set would be great for getting players to engage more with the lore and ask more questions, as opposed to one-off sets that present a snapshot of things and then dash away.
  10. One-off sets can be great as a way to do something funky and unusual, to explore some bold new aesthetic territory without committing too hard.
Full transcript
Introduction

And yet, to lay my cards on the table here, I do not like the new status quo we find ourselves in.
Having every set be a new plane, particularly with five or more sets per year, makes things feel rushed; it makes it feel like there’s no time to breathe and to become immersed in a particular world.
And if I really wanted to let nostalgia take the wheel on this one, I could tell you that the gradual pivot from three-set blocks to purely one-off sets has been an unambiguous tragedy, that we as Magic players are fallen and need to return to the glory days of 2013, where Magic sets came in threes and where bold duels could take place on a middle school lunch table.

I’d love to be able to contribute yet another uncomplicated piece of ammunition to the siege on the decisions currently being made by Hasbro, but in truth, the issue of blocks is a bit more complicated than that.
I do think there is something wrong with what we’re currently doing, where Standard-legal sets take us to six or seven different planes every single year, but three-set blocks don’t really represent a better vision of the game, just one with a different set of problems.
So here, I want to lay out my case for what has gone wrong, both past and present, and talk about an alternative approach to the way we conceptualize sets and their structuring.
And I’ll be getting into a very specific missed opportunity that occurred in 2024 that provides a fascinating case study.

A Brief History of Blocks
The Dissatisfaction with One-Off Sets

So, as you can tell, I really don’t think what we’re doing in the present is ideal.
However, that doesn’t mean the way sets were structured in the past was necessarily better.
And to explore that, we really need to talk about three-set blocks.

The Flaws of Three-Set Blocks

The following two blocks also each contained a set that didn’t really fit in, but in the case of those, it was the middle set.
Theros, the set, established the plane as a setting based around Greek myths and also introduced characters such as Elspeth as a monster-slaying champion and Xenagos as a satyr planeswalker who aspired to godhood.
Born of the Gods was the second set of the block and featured the story of Xenagos ascending to godhood amidst growing chaos within the pantheon.
Journey into Nyx was the third set and focused on the consequences of Xenagos’s godhood and the ensuing showdown that resulted from it.
That all sounds fine and good, but in terms of gameplay, the second set, Born of the Gods, was very strange, and a big reason why it stands out as odd is that the other two sets actually did a really great job conveying the progression of the block through their mechanics.
Theros was more focused on the mortal plane through its heavy usage of heroic and monstrosity as mechanics, whereas Journey into Nyx was much more focused around the pantheon of the gods and the conflict within it, which could be seen in its constellation mechanic and extra high density of enchantments.
Born of the Gods, on the other hand, featured a couple of kind of weird mechanics and didn’t really convey a strong sense of place or time.
It felt more like Theros B-sides than anything else, just some kind of odd designs to go along with a group of five gods in order to allow the fifteen gods to be evenly spread between the three sets.
It played an undeniable role in tracing Xenagos’s storyline, but in most other ways than that, the set was a bit of a disappointment.

The same goes, to a lesser extent, for the Tarkir block.
Khans of Tarkir focused on the five warring factions in a world where dragons have gone extinct, and Dragons of Tarkir features an alternate timeline where dragons are still around and where Tarkir’s clans and characters have altered fates.
The middle set, Fate Reforged, was a little odd, though.
Lore-wise, it involved Sarkhan traveling back in time to when dragons were still around alongside the five clans and intervening in the battle between Ugin and Nicol Bolas.
This is cool as a way to transition between Khans and Dragons, but in terms of gameplay, it just sort of felt like an awkward mashup of the two sets.
Fate Reforged used three clan keywords from Khans and two of the changed ones from DTK; it had some dragons rather than none or a whole bunch; and the clans were less three-color focused than Khans, but not quite the two-color pairings from Dragons.
For a middle set separated from the other two by 1,300 years, this feels more like the middle of an Animorphs cover than it does its own cohesive world.

To be clear, it is still easily the most defensible third set of the three blocks I’ve mentioned, and to be honest, I can imagine this feeling like a genuinely compelling three-set block with a handful of changes to this middle set.
Nonetheless, in the format it ended up in, Fate Reforged feels like a continuation of a trend: one of the sets in a block being a little awkward and feeling more like a stepping stone for story purposes than a fully thought-out landscape of its own.

The Two Extremes and a Middle Ground
Why Two-Set Blocks Work
Missed Opportunities: Murders at Karlov Manor
#4
DeckbuildingMay 202648K views

Using Fulcrum Cards to Add Subthemes in EDH

Snail's Guide to Giving Your Deck More Sauce
We explain how to expand a Commander deck's synergy by adding subthemes through fulcrum cards that bridge archetypes.We stress evaluating a card's floor and considering the deck space required for support.Players learn to combine themes intentionally, balancing novelty with consistency.
Use this in your deckWhen considering a subtheme card, test its floor by imagining a game where your engine stalls, and cut it if it provides no value.
BlinkTokensDiscardSynergyCard EvaluationDeck ConsistencyDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • Fulcrum cards connect two archetypes by allowing one's setup to pay off the other.
  • A card's floor performance in a subpar state determines whether it earns a slot.
Evidence
  • Professional Facebreaker creates treasure with minimal evasion, while Dross Scorpion often just grants a single untap.
  • Ret to the Mystic is worse than a merfolk looter in a deck where 40% of creatures already cycle.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Assess the support cards a subtheme requires; if it demands many slots, it may dilute your main plan.
  • Count the fulcrum cards linking two themes; abundant bridges make a hybrid deck more cohesive.
Limitation
  • Theme pairs with sparse natural synergy, like cast-from-exile and landfall, risk being inconsistent.
  • Conditional enchantments like Astral Slide become dead draws without cycling enablers.
Example
  • In an Arcades defender deck, Doorkeeper's milling subtheme diverts from combat damage and needs extra support.
  • An initiative-focused deck with legendary and background themes has few direct connections but can be built with intentionality.
More quotes
  1. Subthemes can make your deck better or they can make your deck worse. Either might be a desirable outcome depending on what you look for in a game of Magic: The Gathering.
  2. Having your deck do more stuff, have more textured play paths, and produce more unexpected results is desirable for players who have already played a lot with more linear styles of deck.
  3. A good place to start in expanding your deck's synergy universe is what I'll call fulcrum cards: things that naturally tie together multiple subthemes.
  4. An important consideration when thinking about [fulcrum] cards is the floor of the effect. I'm stat brained, so I think about the 25th percentile game: how will the card feel when it's not living up to our wildest dreams?
  5. It's important to figure out what works as a minor part of a deck and what has no business being outside a deck that doesn't heavily support it due to its low floor.
  6. [Ret to the Mystic], a little bird, gets mugged by a merfolk looter in this particular deck.
  7. Sometimes you're just going to have a bit of a hard time combining two themes. Does this mean that you shouldn't smash together two very different synergy families? No.
  8. Artifacts plus ETB and tokens plus one/one counters have been done tens of thousands of times before for good reason, but you always have the opportunity to do something different, unique, and more specifically suited to who you are as a player.
  9. Card selection tools like filtering spells and conditional tutors will reduce the play pattern variance of a deck, but if you add them to a deck that would otherwise have extreme variance due to many moving parts, you'll likely end up with something still very fresh and dynamic to play.
  10. Is Spread the Sickness the best removal this game has to offer? Definitely not, but it feels immensely at home in this deck and I wouldn't dare cut.
Full transcript
Why Add Subthemes?

Ah, subthemes.
They can make your deck better or they can make your deck worse, and either of those two things might be a desirable outcome depending on what you look for in a game of Magic: The Gathering.
Hi there, I’m Alex, and today I’m here to tell you about a passion of mine: making decks do more things.
Most EDH decks will have a theme, merfolk, tokens on attack triggers, and so forth, and this is a nice place to start in getting a deck to do something.
However, eventually that one theme might start to get a little boring, and your eyes might begin to wander.
That on-attack trigger makes tokens; can I play harder into that?
I really like this zombie creature that makes me discard cards; can I make my deck focus more on discard?
This ETB creature searches up legendary cards; I wonder how that might fit into my blink deck.
And this makes sense.
Having your deck do more stuff, have more textured play paths, produce more unexpected results, all of this will be desirable to a lot of players, particularly players who have already played a lot with more linear styles of deck.
So here, I’m going to be making a little guide on how to answer the sorts of questions I mentioned earlier about specific cards you’re finding yourself transfixed by, and at the end, you might have an exciting arsenal of brand new questions to probe your deck with.
With that said, let’s begin.

Fulcrum Cards

Assuming you’re not trying to obliterate your deck’s consistency, a good place to start in expanding your deck’s synergy universe is going to be what I’ll call “fulcrum cards.” This is a thing that naturally ties together multiple subthemes, and it can take a variety of different forms but typically comes in the form of a payoff from one theme that rewards another.
Throne of Geth ties together artifact sacrifice and counters; Displacer Kitten ties together the flicker and spellslinger archetypes; Strength from the Fallen ties together enchantments and creature-stuffed graveyards with a resulting payoff that can work well for a power matters deck.
These are pretty clear examples of fulcrum cards, but there are a lot of other examples that are less obvious but absolutely there if you go looking.
Professional Facebreaker rewards a board of evasive creatures with some artifact tokens that can crack for mana; Quicksmith Genius rewards artifacts entering with rummages that can feed graveyard strategies; and Squee, Dubious Monarch rewards a stocked graveyard with a reanimated attacker that generates tokens.
Why were all of those examples read three times, you might ask?
No particular reason, but you should definitely put them all in the same deck.
Regardless, all of these cards represent points of fusion between different archetypes, connective glue that involves setup for one archetype turning into payoff for another.

An important consideration when thinking about this sort of card is the floor of the effect.
I’m stat brained, so I’d be thinking about maybe the 25th-percentile game for a card, but basically what we’re wondering is how will the card feel in a subpar situation when it’s not living up to our wildest dreams.
For some of the cards I mentioned earlier, the floor is pretty high.
Professional Facebreaker will usually manage to generate at least a couple treasures, and Displacer Kitten can at least serve as protection even if no juicy ETB creatures are present.
For others, the floor might be a lot lower though.
Dross Scorpion is a card I run in one of my decks that works as a fulcrum point between artifact creatures hitting graveyard and tap effects, but this card could easily end up being a poorly stated creature with a single untap on death, and that’s important to keep in mind.
It gets even worse for non-creature permanents.
In my most recent deck review, one of my supporters was trying to integrate a smallish Astral Slide flicker package into a deck, and unfortunately, without any cycling cards in hand, that card is just a three mana textless enchantment.
This is where a bit of discernment is necessary.
It’s important to figure out what works as a minor part of a deck and what has no business being outside a deck that doesn’t heavily support it due to its low floor.

As a related example of this, I’ve had a handful of people suggest Ret to my mystic for my Astral Slide deck.
At first glance, this might make sense.
I’m playing a deck trying to cycle cards; this card gives all my creatures cycling, and it’s a wizard to top it all off, grabable with either of my wizard cycling cards.
However, the issue with adding a card like this is that at its floor, it will always consume a card slot that could have gone to an ETB creature or some card draw or a card that itself has cycling and can replace itself.
And then, how high do you think the ceiling will be in a deck where over 40% of the creatures already have cycling?
It looks good at first, and I’m sure it’s great in a more general graveyard deck, but once we do the floor-ceiling calculation, it becomes clear that this little bird gets mugged by a merfolk looter in this particular deck.

As you might have guessed, a major factor in the floor-ceiling discussion is the amount of deck space required to support a particular card.
A lot of possible fulcrum cards might already be within your synergy family of choice and might create resources that are already useful to what you’re doing even if they theoretically might tie naturally into a different subtheme.
We can see this with the treasure example earlier, where treasures are just a naturally useful tool for basically all decks.
This stands in contrast to something like a Doorkeeper in an Arcades, the Strategist deck.
How do you make target player milling feel useful for a theme that usually wins through combat damage and doesn’t traditionally utilize the graveyard very much?
I’m sure plenty of people have done it, but it requires more divergence from the traditional Arcades game plan than a lot of other defenders payoffs and will likely demand more deck space to be any good as a result.

Another angle for thinking about deck space could be to consider how closely interlinked two different themes are to one another.
Sometimes there will be a whole bunch of fulcrum cards for a particular set of two themes, with one I’ve utilized in the past being artifacts and ETB effects.
Thought Monitor, Conjurer’s Closet, Myr Battlesphere, Mimic Vat, the Mages, there’s a whole lot of options there.
And if you’re playing a bunch of these, your dual theme deck experience might end up feeling decently compact despite its somewhat mechanically broad nature.
However, many other sets of two themes have dramatically less innate shared DNA.
If I’m trying to make a deck that combines cast from exile and landfall, well, there are two landfall cards with “exile you may play” text, and there are also cards that reward playing lands from exile in addition to casting spells, which work well for us.
And then if we dive into “from anywhere other than your hand” effects as well, we might find some roundabout synergies in other landfall cards such as Courser of Kruphix and Hedron Crab, among others.
Hopefully you can see what I’m getting at here.
Sometimes you’re just going to have a bit of a hard time combining two themes.

Building with Intention and Goals

Now, does this mean that you shouldn’t smash together two very different synergy families?
No. Artifacts plus ETB, tokens plus one counters, these have been done tens of thousands of times before for good reason, but you always have the opportunity to do something different, something unique, something more specifically suited to who you are as a player.
What you should do though is know that’s what you’re doing, figure out why you’re doing that, and then figure out a plan of action based on what your goals are.
Let’s say you’re building an initiative deck with a heavy emphasis on legendary permanents in general and the background enchantments in particular.
Cool.
A bit tricky, but there are some possible points of connection here.
Next, it’s important to figure out the reason for building the deck because that will affect the decisions you make.
If you’re trying to build a decent bracket three deck with this set of restrictions as a challenge, you’ll want to dial in hard on the points of cohesion and maybe consider ways you can get the different elements to come together more often.
Maybe we run partner commanders to get full use out of Dungeon Delver and pick colors that offer the most utility in putting together our game plan.
If we decide that Dungeon Delver is a key card for us, maybe we add some cards like Enlightened Tutor, Muddle the Mixture, and a couple other helpful tutors in whatever colors we end up in so that we can get that card more often, and then maybe we pair that with other useful cards that fit the same tutoring restrictions.
If we have the goal of getting the most mechanically fluid version of initiative legendaries, there are some cool decision paths we can run down, and the end result will likely be a very unique bracket three deck.
Some people might worry that tutors will homogenize the play experience of a deck, but this won’t necessarily be true.
Card selection tools like filtering spells and conditional tutors will reduce the play pattern variance of a deck, but if you add them to a deck that would otherwise have extreme levels of play pattern variance due to having a lot of moving parts, you’ll likely end up in a place that is still very fresh and dynamic to play with.

Now let’s say we’re building with a different goal.
We’re just trying to have a good time and are likely aiming for a more laid-back bracket two style deck.
This is also a very valid goal, and honestly, it’s a pretty fantastic reason to make a deck that might be a little bit mechanically dubious.
If your starting point is a naturally slower-paced political mechanic like initiative and if you’re introducing extra layers of synergy rather than filling up your deck with high quality staple creatures, you’re going to end up with something that plays quite well at lower power tables.
You’ll want to make sure you’re running enough filtering so that your deck functions and so that you’re having fun throughout the whole process, but the play pattern of a deck that gradually filters for different elements of its cool funky game plan is one that is perfect for a bracket two game in both pace and nature.

A Final Note on Lower Power and Removal

As a final note here, playing at a lower power table is also a great place for a somewhat mechanically disjointed deck because incentives align perfectly on what sorts of vegetables you should be running, particularly removal.
Lower power opponents won’t require all the best removal the game has to offer, and this means that you can do something that’s already nice to do: run some of the higher mana options with added utility for your synergy families.
My bracket two Incubators deck has 16 cards I’ve categorized as interaction, which is a decent number, but if I click through these on the architect page, literally all of them do something else for the deck.
This is nice for keeping a mechanically messy deck from feeling too bad, and it also lets me play with cards that are a bit more unique within the Commander format.
Is Spread the Sickness the best removal this game has to offer?
Definitely not, but it feels immensely at home in this deck and I wouldn’t dare cut it.

Thanks for watching.
I’m going to be at Magic Con Vegas in just a week or so, and I’ll be hosting a panel along with the Trinket Mage and a couple friends of the podcast.
Come watch that or get a sticker or just say hi, whatever floats your boat.
If you want to support what I do here, you can grab some high quality sleeves from Dragon Shield, affiliate link in the description, or you can support me more directly through Patreon or a YouTube membership.
These lovely people, some of whom are on screen now, help let me keep doing what I do here, and they also receive bonus videos from me once a month or so plus a chance at a deck review every three months.
If you want to join this illustrious crowd, the links and/or buttons to do so are probably on your screen somewhere unless something has gone horribly wrong.

#5
DeckbuildingApr 2026105K views

The Long Road to a Satisfying Aggro Deck

How I Failed to Build an Aggro Deck for 3 Years
We spent three years iterating on a commander aggro deck, finding early versions unfocused and lacking card draw.Attempts to add the initiative proved distracting, but discovering Bruna, the Demagogue as commander provided both damage and value, finally aligning with the goal of pressuring opponents' life totals.
Use this in your deckDefine the precise emotional experience you want your deck to create, then evaluate every card against that goal.
LiesaWhiteBlackAggroStaxDesign IntentSynergyTable PoliticsDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • Deckbuilding should begin by defining the specific play experience you want to create, such as making opponents constantly worry about their life total.
Evidence
  • The initial Liesa deck, with 16 hate bears and low draw, felt more like a stax deck than an aggro deck and bored the pilot due to lack of cards.
  • Adding the initiative generated value but made the deck cumbersome and less focused on life pressure.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Ensure your aggro deck includes enough draw to keep your hand full and the game interesting; otherwise, both you and opponents are locked out.
  • When adding a new mechanic, test if it directly advances your primary goal; if it distracts or complicates, cut it.
  • A commander like Bruna can unify damage and value, making the deck more consistent and enjoyable.
Limitation
  • The initiative's need for physical props and its occasional absence made games awkward and diluted the deck's aggressive identity.
  • Cutting damaging cards to make room for value pieces pulled the deck further from its life pressuring ideal.
Example
  • Bruna, the Demagogue transformed the deck by providing card draw and +1/+1 counters, satisfying the need for both value and damage.
  • Switching commanders from Liesa to Bruna maintained the hate bear shell while better suiting higher power tables.
More quotes
  1. I wanted to make my opponents think about their life total a lot.
  2. An aggressive game plan can mean the ability to knock opponents out of the game early, but even before that happens, the first 20 to 30 damage dealt to an opponent already influences the way they play the game.
  3. In retrospect, the biggest [issue] is that [the original deck] felt more stax than it did Liesa.
  4. The initiative is a mechanic [that] is like monarch, but needlessly complicated for entirely aesthetic purposes.
  5. A commander deck can be a compact, self contained thing, and here I had introduced an extra mechanic that required me to use a full sheet of paper as a prop.
  6. Liesa is an inevitability commander, an unstoppable life total tax that always represents a way to grind the game toward a single digit hit point slugfest.
  7. By contrast, Breena is very much a tempo commander.
  8. This deck came out of the gate swinging and routinely knocked 50 points of life off of its opponents within the first six turns of the game, even in games it eventually lost control.
  9. I was not just looking for an aggro deck. I was looking for a sort of inevitability aggro experience, where opponents would spend time dealing with the consequences of a painfully low life total.
  10. A small part of me wishes she drained opponents for three per spell, but alas, my girl Liesa just does not quite have that kind of sauce.
Full transcript
The Deck Tech Tradition and the Elephant in the Room

Any deck I find myself playing for a long enough period of time generally earns itself its own dedicated video, a deck tech.
That happened 21 months after I built Shroom Slide, seven months after I built Glissa Incubators, four months after I built my Malcolm Time Sieve deck, one month after building my Radha deck, and also one month after putting together an updated version of my Maga deck.
In part, this is for entirely ordinary YouTuber reasons: I need to figure out two things a month to talk about, and a deck I like to play makes for an easy way to make one of those two.
However, this is also because my favorite decks tend to be evocative, meticulously crafted to scratch a very specific itch, and for this reason they also often serve as good lenses through which to analyze a specific play style or a specific motivation when building a deck.
Relevant to this discussion is the fact that my decks tend to revolve around a specific vibe I find appealing, whether that be forcing the table into a cutthroat low-resource game, unstoppably accumulating resources for myself, or navigating a strategic minefield to a narrow control victory.
Because my decks feature specific play patterns I find personally satisfying, I like to think they can be helpful documents of how I crafted an enjoyable experience for myself, and possible inspiration for how others can do the same.
It’s with all this in mind that we need to talk about the elephant in the room, my Liesa deck.
I first built this deck in April 2023, 35 months ago, and it has stayed among my five to six core decks ever since.
I’ve even talked about it in a handful of different videos because it has accomplished some interesting things and offered some intriguing lessons.
What it hasn’t done, though, is ever be quite satisfying enough to make a whole video about; it’s never scratched the itch I wanted to scratch well enough to be worth talking about.

The Aggro Itch: Life Total Pressure

This is kind of funny to me because I’ve known precisely what itch I wanted to scratch basically the whole time.
I wanted to make my opponents think about their life total a lot.
I said precisely this in a Discord message I sent to a friend in June of 2023, and I also laid out this thought process in a video about aggro in October of that year.
An aggressive game plan can mean the ability to knock opponents out of the game early, but even before that happens, the first 20 to 30 damage dealt to an opponent already influences the way they play the game.
It makes them play more conservatively, attack less confidently, and feel a lot less confident about the life-paid tools that are such a common feature in Commander.
And, relevantly here, a low life total especially does that if I can keep around a creature that drains opponents whenever they cast spells.
So, I was already in a great starting position with my command zone being a commander who dodges the command tax and drains anybody who casts spells.
So, how exactly did I manage to fail at adequately scratching this itch for multiple years?

The First Liesa: Hate Bears and Stax

Well, it’s been a complex road of confused intentions and muddy goals with multiple rounds of heavy reworks; this is where the command zone comes into play for this particular deck.
My hate bears can whittle down life totals a bit and generally gum up the works of the game, and then Liesa is my follow-up that can really start to cut down life totals in this slowed environment.
This theory largely worked out in reality.
I had a suite of 16 hate bears alongside modest counts of protection, ramp, draw, and removal, as well as some extra tools to help Liesa in cutting down opposing life totals.
Still, I found myself having some issues with that original deck.
In retrospect, the biggest one is that it felt more stax than it did Liesa.
However, at the time I was only distantly aware of this, and more focused on another undeniably present problem: the deck didn’t have all that much draw, which just kind of made it boring to play.
My opponents couldn’t do much, but neither could I.

The Initiative Experiment

So, in early 2024, I did a round of tweaks on the deck to get more value generation in there and generally to make it more interesting to play.
I added a lot of value generation: three more general purpose options and five cards that contain a particular mechanic I wanted to play around with, the initiative.
The initiative is a mechanic that causes a player to travel through the Undercity, which triggers an effect in a room whenever a player takes the initiative, whenever they start their turn with the initiative, or whenever they take back the initiative from an opponent who stole it by attacking them.
It’s like monarch, but needlessly complicated for entirely aesthetic purposes.
Anyway, this had some undeniable appeals.
In a slow-paced game, any once-per-turn triggers naturally gain a bit of extra relevance.
Also, my deck already benefited from running a small handful of defensive cards due to its grindy, life-total-race-winning commander, and these defensive cards also help with keeping the initiative.
Also, I didn’t have an Undercity token, so I drew one on a full-sized piece of paper and brought miniatures to Commander games so the people could represent their positions on it.
Who wouldn’t love that?
Well, me, and that was part of my issue with this version of the deck.
A commander deck can easily be this pretty compact, self-contained thing, and here I’d introduced an extra mechanic that required me to use this full sheet of paper prop or else buy a replacement that’s irritatingly small.
It just felt like a kind of pointless extra hassle to need to deal with, particularly given that my deck only ran five initiative cards and didn’t draw any of them in some games.
The other part of my issue was that I’d cut a bunch of damaging cards in order to make the round of changes I’d made, and while those damaging cards weren’t great, their absence pulled the deck even further away from the life total pressuring ideal I’d been striving for.
I had a functional deck that I didn’t mind playing, but the itch was still not being scratched.
So, it was time for another change.

Breena Takes the Helm

One of my favorite cards in the deck was Bruna, the Demagogue.
She is a three mana creature with the text, “Whenever a player attacks one of your opponents, if that opponent has more life than another of your opponents, that attacking player draws a card and you put two +1/+1 counters on a creature you control.” It might not immediately be clear what that means, but basically drawing her instantly granted the deck a whole bunch of both value generation and damage in the form of +1/+1 counters, and also created a sort of political game for my opponents.
In theory, she offered opponents card advantage, but trading a card for two +1/+1 counters on one of my creatures didn’t usually feel too bad, and her benefits were much more consistent on my side of the table.
After four or five games of drawing her and greatly enjoying what she did to the deck, I decided to rework the deck with her in the command zone.
In theory, this swap made sense to me.
Liesa is a damage-focused commander who operates through the use of a life tax, and Bruna is a damage-focused commander who operates through the use of +1/+1 counters; my theory about having a hate bear oriented 99 along with a commander who pushed a lot of damage was still absolutely intact.
The other appeal here was that hate bears are a type of card better suited to somewhat higher power games, and Bruna was a more powerful commander than Liesa, so that was an added bit of congruence.
With this new bird in the zone, I was better equipped to handle the sorts of higher power Commander games that my hate bear suite was itching for.

The Tempo Problem

So, how was that?
Well, in a lot of ways I was correct, and the deck I ended up producing was undoubtedly a workable one that I could have some fun playing.
It got going fast; Bruna’s +1/+1 counters could turn my hate bears into terrifying beaters, and the card advantage options meant it rarely totally ran out of steam.
Even better, with such a potent command zone, I could afford to run more interaction than ever before without sacrificing other things.
So, what was the problem?
Put simply, the nature of a Breena command zone is dramatically different from the nature of a Liesa command zone, and it’s different in a way that totally destroyed the feel of the deck that I was going for.
On some level, Liesa is an “inevitability” commander, an unstoppable life total tax that always represents a way to grind the game toward a single-digit hit point slugfest.
With her in the command zone, the 99 could focus on keeping opponents in check and putting in chip damage, knowing that every chunk of health my opponents lost was a spell they couldn’t safely cast later in the game.
By contrast, Breena is very much a “tempo” commander, as I laid out in my glass cannon commanders video last year.
Like Liesa, she helps me deal a ton of damage to opponents, but the profile of that damage is front-loaded and reliant on creatures sticking around on board.
If I had a smooth first several turns, I might be swinging in with a few 7/7s or 8/8s or 9/9s on turn five, and if those creatures stuck around, there was a solid chance I’d be winning that game.
The problem was, if some of my creatures got removed or if, God forbid, there was a board wipe played that I couldn’t dodge, all of that damage momentum would slow to a halt.
In that case, I’d still have some card advantage from Breena drawing me an extra one to two cards per turn, but it would take some time for me to redevelop a board that could once again threaten opponents, and whether I could pull that off successfully might come down to a variety of different factors.
To be clear, what I’m describing is a very normal type of commander deck.
If your deck works like this, that’s fine, and I did enjoy some things about playing it.
It even makes sense as an aggro deck.
This deck came out of the gate swinging and routinely knocked 50 points of life off of its opponents within the first six turns of the game, even in games it eventually lost control of.
The problem was, I wasn’t just looking for an aggro deck; I was looking for a sort of inevitability aggro experience, where opponents would spend time dealing with the consequences of a painfully low life total.
And this deck was decidedly not that.

Returning to Liesa

So, how did I solve my aggro problem?
How did I create a deck that scratches this itch of wanting opponents to spend a lot of time looking at their life total?
Well, step one was going back to Liesa.
Part of me was a little hesitant to do this for purely power reasons, because the Breena deck had been sort of bracket four curious, and Liesa is a pretty firmly mid power sort of commander.
A small part of me wishes she drained opponents for three per spell, but alas, my girl Liesa just doesn’t quite have that kind of sauce.
However, this fact also gives some opportunities, the biggest one of which I’ll get to in a bit.
To start with though, let’s talk about how I fixed some of the issues from the previous Liesa decks.
A big piece of this was the beatdown suite.
An issue with the previous iterations of the deck was that the 99 didn’t actually do a good job of getting life totals low enough for Liesa to matter.
In the case of the original deck, that was a result of running a bunch of mediocre damage tools such as Blood Artist effects, and in the initiative version, that was just not really running very many damage tools in general, unless you count making target opponent lose five life once every five turns.
So, here, assisted by a more lax budget than I was using in the past, I picked out a bunch of new damage options: Ankh of Mishra, Wound Reflection, Suture Priest, and the iconic political aggro tool Hunted Horror.
This card is so much fun; if you have an opponent who’s receptive to the idea of smacking Frederick over there who’s playing a gross value pile, it’s effectively a 13/13 in stats for two mana.
In terms of value, I dialed into the small creature beats realm and grabbed Enduring Innocence, Assemble the Players, and Elegy Acolyte.
This deck is running more draw-oriented cards than the original, and while it’s running significantly less value generation in total than the initiative version of the deck, much of the draw here is quite dense and impactful.
Barrow Ghoulf was a final inclusion here; it’s just an all-around solid beatstick with a bunch of value attached.

Cutting the Hate Bears

The biggest area I tweaked this deck, though, was dead simple: I cut a lot of hate bears.
This was kind of painful because I really like having hate bears in my deck.
It feels good to have a way to slow opposing game plans, to build around the restrictions of those tools so I’m breaking parity, and to be able to keep a high power table in check with something like a Rule of Law effect.
And to be clear, I’m still running some: taxes, enter-the-battlefield-tapped effects, and even Mindcensor; however, the real game plan smashers like Hushbringer and Archon of Emeria got left behind.
One reason for this I alluded to earlier: these cards feel most appropriate and most needed in higher bracket games, and Liesa isn’t a commander that really belongs at those tables.
The other reason for this choice was that stax in general, and Rule of Law effects in particular, have this weird clash with Liesa because they cause opponents to cast fewer spells, but Liesa wants opponents to be casting more spells, or at least wants the fact that spells cost two life to feel relevant.
It almost felt like doing her a little dirty, as well as undermining the goal of the deck.
Like, think about it: if I have an opponent at a lowish life total, Liesa in play, and also a Phyrexian Censor in play, is Liesa reducing the number of spells my opponent wants to play?
The answer is, who knows?
They’re not running a whole ass Phyrexian deck; they can only play one spell per turn anyway.

#6
DeckbuildingFeb 202672K views

Blight Curse Precon Review: Johnny-Style -1/-1 Counter Deck

Blight Curse is a Johnny Playground
We review the Blight Curse precon, highlighting its commander's explosive draw potential and the diverse Johnny-style synergies from minus one counters.We note that the limited and varied card pool requires careful balancing to avoid dead draws.The alternate commander enables a creature theft strategy.
Use this in your deckAdd more cards that place minus one counters on all creatures to create asymmetric draw engines with the commander.
AmzuBlackGreenCountersCard DrawReanimatorUpgradesSynergyDesign IntentDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • The commander's ability has no per-turn limit, allowing it to draw multiple cards from a single spell that places counters on many creatures.
  • The deck appeals to Johnny players by offering varied synergy pieces that combine in creative and powerful ways.
Evidence
  • In 20 test games, the deck consistently built towards strong midgame plays rather than early explosive starts.
  • The deck contains four cards that put minus one counters on all creatures, which become one-sided draw engines with the commander.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Add more cards that distribute minus one counters to all creatures to maximize card advantage from the commander.
  • Include proliferate effects to multiply existing counters and accelerate your value engine.
Limitation
  • The fragmented pool of minus one counter cards means some will be dead draws if they do not fit the current board state.
  • Cards designed to weaken opponents may be inefficient when used on your own creatures for card draw.
Example
  • The alternate commander, the Reaper King No More, steals opposing creatures that die with minus one counters, supported by the deck's removal suite.
  • Hapatra and Flourishing Defenses turn minus one counter placement into token generation, rapidly building your board.
More quotes
  1. Blight Curse is pretty phenomenal as far as pre-constructed Commander decks go.
  2. This strikes me as quite a Johnny sort of precon.
  3. Liliana Death Wielder is an intro deck planeswalker from Amonkhet and it is truly god awful; it'd be barely playable at four mana and in reality it's seven mana because it's an intro deck planeswalker.
  4. I guess this was added to the deck to be an educational first cut for new players, à la Temple of the False God.
  5. This deck has a better relationship with its alt commander than most precons do.
  6. Anti-U has a lack of limiting phrases in the text box: no 'one or more creatures' and no 'once per turn'.
  7. Some cards put minus one counters on all creatures, rewarding wide boards with massive card advantage, whereas others gain minus one counters over time, pushing a slower, grindier play style.
  8. She is a powerful draw engine that allows somebody piloting the deck to see more of it, which is super useful for a deck whose cards are highly varied in how they work.
  9. There are so many different ways the various synergy elements can be pieced together, whether it be making a value engine, a removal engine, having a single explosive turn, putting together a wide board,.
  10. The minus one counter synergies take a variety of different shapes, and the setups and payoffs within this deck piece together in some really cool and sometimes really powerful ways.
Full transcript

Quick announcement up top.
I'm now partnered with Dragon Shield.
Follow my affiliate link in the description to get a discount on your online sleeve purchases, and keep an eye out in the upcoming weeks for an awesome custom sleeve design that me and my artist friend are cooking up at the moment.
Now, on to it.

What the Deck Is Trying to Do

Lauren Ecliped is here, and my patrons have decreed that I will be reviewing the minus one/-1 counter precon, which is great because Blight Curse has been a lot of fun to dig into and is pretty phenomenal as far as pre-constructed commander decks go, in my opinion.
Not because it's a perfect deck, and we can work on that part a bit later, but for a lot of other reasons we'll be getting into.
As usual, I'm going to start by talking about what the deck is trying to do; then I'll be discussing what I like about it, and I'll finish up with some ways it can be improved.
To start with, let's talk about this deck's main commander, Anti-U.
She is a four mana 4/4 with ward blight 2, and whenever one or more minus one counters are put on a creature, you draw a card if you control that creature.
If you don't control it, its controller loses one life.
The first thing I notice about this is a lack of limiting phrases in the text box: no “one or more creatures” and no “once per turn.” What this means is that this has the potential to be a pretty explosive value generator, easily drawing five or more cards per turn with the right setup, present on a four mana 4/4 commander with a protective ability on it , that's pretty good.
The downside here is the specificity.
In total, there are an okay number of cards that put minus one counters on creatures, but it's not a particularly generous pool to work with when you consider that these cards function in a wide variety of different ways.
Some put minus one counters on all creatures, rewarding wide boards with massive card advantage, whereas others gain minus one counters over time, pushing a slower, grindier play style.
