Plenty of others are one-time use.
And a lot of cards that use minus one counters are primarily intended to put counters on opposing creatures and are balanced with that in mind.
Do you put these counters on your own creatures instead for a bit of card advantage?
All this to say, Anti-U is a powerful commander within her own synergy sphere, and the challenge in building her is going to be navigating a limited card pool to create a deck that plays to her strengths without ending up too disjointed.

Looking at this deck's cards that place minus one counters, there are nine cards that specifically place minus one counters on your creatures, another nine that place minus one counters on any creature, two that place counters on each opposing creature, and four cards that place counters on all creatures.
This array of different minus one counter cards is accompanied by a number of different payoff cards, which likewise operate in a variety of different ways.
Eight of these trigger when creatures with minus one counters on them die, with most of these providing either card advantage or reanimating the creatures under your control.
Another four cards plus the commander reward the active placement of counters itself.
And the synergies get much more granular from here, including amplifier cards like Evolution Sage, cards that modify the effect of minus one counters like Kolrath Knight, and weird roundabout synergies like Tree of Predition.
Some of the setup and payoff combinations here are quite good, especially the ones involving cards that place minus one counters onto all creatures, and I expect that piecing together one of these zingy pairings is a big way this deck wants to get ahead.
Naturally, this deck has a lot of tools to slow opposing creature-based game plans given its focus on minus one counters, and these creature shrinking options are joined by five cards that deal with artifacts, enchantments, or both.
Rounding out the 99 of this deck are moderate counts of both draw and ramp, much of which involves minus one counters in some way.
Through some powerful synergies, I expect this deck will try to get ahead in explosive fashion, slow opposing game plans with interaction, and then gradually wear down opponents with a mixture of creature attacks and incremental damage.

What I Like About This Deck

To start with, what I like about this deck: this strikes me as quite a “Johnny” sort of precon.
Because the deck was drawing from a limited card pool, the minus one counter synergies take a variety of different shapes, and the setups and payoffs within this deck piece together in some really cool and sometimes really powerful ways.
Just thinking about Anti-U, the cards that place minus one counters on all creatures end up being a Shamanic Revelation for you and a Ratchet Charm for your opponents , both effects that can be very impactful depending on the state of the game.
Any proliferate effects can amplify the effect of previous counters, creating further value and damage at a brisk pace.
These plays can also be doubled down on with an additional payoff card like Hapatra or Flourishing Defenses, which can really put you in the driving seat of a game fast.
This deck also has two different cards that reanimate creatures that die with minus one counters on them, both at three mana, and this means that it's possible to perform some powerful creature stealing maneuvers pretty early in the game.
All this to say, this deck can do some powerful things, and the way it operates seems like it would make for an exciting play experience for any players who are especially interested in the way cards synergize.
Just a minute ago, I talked about the two cards that reanimate opposing creatures that die with minus one counters on them, and one of these two is actually the alternate commander for this deck, the Reaper, King No More.
That's another thing I like about this deck: it actually feels like it has an alternate commander.
The power level of the card is nice, basically being a sidegrade of Necroskitter that trades some raw power for a bit more utility, and the deck does have a fair amount of cards that place minus one counters on opposing creatures in it.
So, this deck has a better relationship with its alt commander than most precons do, in the sense that if you really like this card, you can run it as the commander and probably have an okay time with it.
If you were doing that long-term, you'd want to tweak the deck a bit since Anti-U is pretty pivotal to the deck's card economy, but the Reaper is already much closer to feeling good as a commander in this deck than most alternate commanders.
And I'll talk a bit later about some possible ways to adapt this deck to him.

As usual, I play tested this deck 20 times and kept track of how the first five turns went in an Excel doc.
Overall, it felt decent.
There weren't many games where the deck was really popping off super early like I had when I was testing the World Shaper precon last year, but most games the deck was doing some decent stuff and setting up for some cool plays later in the game.
One neat thing is how varied the deck felt.
There are so many different ways the various synergy elements can be pieced together, whether it be making a value engine, making a removal engine, having a single explosive turn, putting together a wide board, and so on.
The commander also really helps with making the deck feel good for two reasons.
For one, she offers an easy default minus one counter payoff, reducing the strain on the deck to find other payoffs.
Secondly, the fact that she's a powerful draw engine allows somebody piloting the deck to see more of it, which is super useful for a deck whose cards are highly varied in how they work and what they accomplish.
As a final note about what I like here, the manabase is fine enough for what this deck is doing.
Upgrade it if you like, but I'll be leaving it alone in my tweaks.

What I Dislike About This Deck

Getting into what I dislike about playing this deck, the big thing is “dead cards.” I had several play tests where I kept accumulating cards in hand that were entirely useless in my current game state.
A lot of these cards are just a bit more specific in their play pattern or in the way they synergize with other cards, being a little niche or hard to fully utilize with current tools.
There is one frequently dead card that we should get out of the way first, though: Liliana Death Wielder is an intro deck planeswalker from Amonkhet and it is truly god awful.
It'd be barely playable at four mana, and in reality it's seven mana because it's an intro deck planeswalker.
I guess this was added to the deck to be an educational first cut for new players, à la Temple of the False God, but I don't really like that an iconic planeswalker like Liliana is reduced to serving that role here.
Anyway, that's our first cut, and it should be a cut basically regardless of what you want to do with this deck.

Another thing I dislike about this deck's current form is its creature count.
This feels like a deck that wants to be running 35 to 40 creatures based on the number of cards that care about creatures, but instead it's running 27.
This adds to the awkwardness when the deck keeps drawing into enchantments and sorceries and struggles to get much use out of something like Soul Snuffers or Grave Venerations that really want more creatures floating around in the ecosystem.
So, these are the main things I want to be focused on in my upgrade: reducing dead draws and upping the creature count.

Alternative Build Paths

Before I get into my other nine cuts though, I really want to talk about alternative build paths because that's another reason I really like this precon as a product.
There are a lot of little elements that could easily spark some inspiration and be expanded outward.
A few proliferate cards help the deck amplify its minus one counters, and one way to modify this deck could be to lean harder into that mechanic and add other cards that can utilize it, such as Astral Chronicopia or Twitching Doll.
There may not be a massive pool of minus one counter cards in the game, but there sure are a lot of other counter cards that can utilize some of the same payoffs and support.
Another synergy area this deck gives a taste of is graveyard synergies.
There are several cards that use graveyards as well as Archfiend that cares about discarding.
These aren't very well supported now, but you could introduce some more looting effects into the deck to help with these, and this extra filtering would also likely help the deck in general in pursuit of assembling engines.
Finally, you could also adapt the deck to work better with the Reaper in the command zone.
There are several great cards that put minus one counters on opposing creatures that you could add if you don't mind paying a bit, such as Persistent Constrictor and Spitting Dilophosaurus, and these paired with a bit more draw would likely make for a very different but still functional play style.
This would also likely work well paired with a more graveyard heavy or proliferate heavy approach as well; you can do a bit of mixing and matching among these.

My Upgrade: Cuts and Additions

For my upgrade, I'm choosing to go for a more streamlined approach that eliminates some potential dead weight and shores up some weak spots while increasing the creature count and improving tools for incremental minus one counters.
Let's start with cuts.
Archfiend of Ifnir is getting cut because this deck has five other cards to discard and three of those are cycling lands.
This was one of my first observations after plugging this deck into Archidekt.
I saw an Archfiend of Ifnir, I dropped “discard” into the search box, and boom, that's an easy cut unless you're feeling highly motivated to get more self-discard into this bad boy.
I'm also cutting Puka Covenant and Persist due to this deck's lack of graveyard enabling.
The former requires your creatures dying and stuff in your graveyard to be gotten back, which is too niche a set of requirements for the current form of this deck; Persist will be useful a bit more often, but its ceiling is also much lower, so it doesn't excite me either.
I am keeping the rest of the graveyard synergies around, though, as they have a lot more utility than these two.

Speaking of persist, Puppeteer Clique , this card looks like it synergizes with this deck, but in practice you'll probably be struggling not to turn off its keyword more often than you'll be enjoying that extra Anti-U trigger.
I'm also cutting Grim Poet.
This one is a bit painful as it's a funky card with some neat possibilities here, but seven is such a large amount of mana to pay for something with no guarantee of it doing much.
From here, the final four cuts are non-creature filler, which will be getting replaced with creatures and other options better suited to this deck.
Harmonize and Hoarders' Greed are getting cut for alternate draw, Commander's Sphere for an alternate mana rock, and Cathartic Reunion for a better removal spell.
I will say the fact that I'm spending four of my ten swaps targeting perfectly passable filler speaks well to this deck's card quality; there's just not many truly objectionable cards here.

Now for the additions.
I'm starting here with four low-cost creatures with blight abilities from the Lurein Eclipsed main set: Donhand Dissident, Gristle Glutton, Scuzzback Scoundrel, and Shadow Urchin.
Adding these lowers our curve, ups our creature count, makes it easier to trigger the commander on demand, and as an added bonus, three of these four have fun alliterative names.
You could also add the haste one if you wanted to, but I don't see this deck wanting haste on a single creature all that often.
As replacement creature-based draw, I'm adding Generous Patron, which can potentially net quite a solid amount of cards.
To replace the mana rock and the creature removal we cut, I'm adding Staff of Completion and Grim Affliction, both of which can also proliferate.
From here, I'm also a bit concerned about this deck.

#7
DeckbuildingJan 2026259K views

How to Avoid Common Commander Deckbuilding Mistakes

The 5 Most Common Deckbuilding Mistakes, and How to Avoid Them
We analyze recurring deckbuilding mistakes observed in two years of deck reviews.It identifies unfocused game plans, vulnerability to disruption, and lack of proactivity as common issues.We advocate for tighter synergy focus, versatile protection over narrow spells, and ramp or haste to increase consistency.These lessons aim to help players refine their Commander decks into more cohesive and resilient strategies.
Use this in your deckEvaluate each card by how directly it supports your deck's primary goal; if it only provides marginal synergy, remove it in favor of cards that more clearly advance your win condition.
Deck ConsistencyResilienceWin ConditionsSynergyDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • A deck should have a clear primary game plan; mixing multiple payoff directions weakens the deck's focus.
  • Restraint in card inclusion is crucial; even cards with thematic synergy can dilute a strategy if they do not support the core win condition.
Evidence
  • Unfocused macro goals were found in seven reviewed decks, making it a common issue especially among newer players.
Deckbuilding Application
  • When selecting payoffs for a synergy like landfall, choose ones that all contribute to a unified strategy (such as tokens and buffs) and avoid adding cards that generate incremental life gain or damage if they do not align.
  • To protect a fragile game plan, use versatile tools like counterspells, haste enablers, or ramp instead of narrow protection spells that are dead when behind.
Limitation
  • Protection spells can be ineffective when you are behind on board, making them a risky inclusion in decks that need to catch up.
  • Relying solely on expensive commanders or resource-intensive setups increases vulnerability to disruption.
Example
  • A landfall deck aiming to win via token beatdown should avoid including cards like Grazing Gladeheart (life gain) or Tunneling Geopede (incremental damage) because they dilute the primary aggressive plan.
  • Using haste sources like Anger or end-step enablers like Leyline of Anticipation can effectively protect key creatures without dedicated protection spells.
More quotes
  1. Protection feels great to have when I'm ahead, but when I'm behind, they're the most useless pieces of garbage clogging up my hand that I can possibly imagine.
  2. The trick is learning to crank up the specificity, turning a soup of generic payoffs into a deck that knows what it is and where it is going.
  3. A necessary lesson here is in restraint, knowing when to exclude a card, even when it theoretically has a bit of synergy with what you're building.
  4. The worst feeling is having a creature eat dirt before it could do anything at all.
  5. If you're adding a goad card to your deck, its effectiveness will be inversely proportional to the number of good cards already in your deck.
  6. [If you overload on goad], pretend cards are fogs instead and suddenly your deck with 12 fogs in it is looking a little suspect.
  7. Some decks are a bit of a glass cannon. They have the potential to be very powerful, but can easily get kneecapped and do nothing.
  8. If you're cruising through Scryfall with a certain search term related to your synergy family, it's easy to just add endlessly until your deck space has filled up.
  9. I don't like protection spells very much. I've had multiple protection heavy decks that just felt kind of bad to play.
  10. With goad in particular, players treat goad as a plan for winning in and of itself when it's really not a mechanic that can be relied upon to do.
Full transcript

I have been doing reviews of my supporters' decks for almost two years now, and over that time, I've noticed some patterns, some common stumbling blocks that a lot of players tend to hit.
This isn't just the new players, either; I'm sampling here from players who watch my videos and like them enough to give me money about it, and that's to say nothing of the deck building mistakes I occasionally catch myself making and need to correct.
Having just finished up a deck review, which you can check out on my second channel if you'd like, I did the sort of normal thing you might expect of me.
I went back through every single deck I've ever reviewed, looked at what feedback I gave, and tallied up the number of each general family of critique I gave.
We cover the most common deck building mistakes I see and a brief discussion of how to avoid each of them.

Number Five: Unfocused on the Macro Level
Number Four: Vulnerable to Opposing Hate

If you like we so far, you should smash that like button, eviscerate that subscribe button, and obliterate your current state of not giving me a few dollars by signing up for my Patreon or a YouTube membership.
I made a supporter-only companion piece to this that covers some deck building mistakes I've made this year, and a preview for that will be at the end.
With that said, let's get to number three.

Number Three: Not Proactive Enough
Number Two: Not Reactive Enough
#8
DeckbuildingJan 2026100K views

How Much Ramp Your Deck Really Needs

You Might Have Too Much Ramp (but you also might not)
Ramp serves two distinct functions: smoothing out the early curve and increasing total mana over the game.We provide a framework to decide if a deck needs more ramp based on its curve, commander cost, and game plan.Hypergeometric calculators can quantify the right amount, with roughly 18 ramp spells giving a high chance of curving out.
Use this in your deckInclude about 18 ramp spells to achieve a 77% chance of having one playable by turn three.
RampDeck ConsistencyMana BaseCurveSynergyDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • Ramp spells perform two separate functions: curving out and increasing total mana quantity.
  • Deciding on ramp requires considering the mana values of other cards and the desired pacing of plays.
Evidence
  • In deck reviews, more decks needed additional ramp than additional card draw.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Use a hypergeometric calculator to match the number of ramp spells to your desired chance of curving out.
  • Aim for around 18 ramp spells for a 77% chance of having one by turn three.
  • Choose ramp cost that aligns with your curve gaps, such as two-cost ramp if you lack two-drops but have many four-drops.
Limitation
  • Adding ramp without purpose can dilute deck synergy and make it less powerful.
  • Mana dorks are efficient for curve but fragile against board wipes; prioritize land ramp for long-game mana growth.
Example
  • A dinosaur deck wanting to cast its four-cost commander on turn four can ramp on turn two to have four mana on turn three and then cast it on schedule.
More quotes
  1. Adding ramp feels like a somewhat dated suggestion, a tip offered to newer players in 2018.
  2. In 2025, card draw and synergy feel like bigger focal points for EDH critique, with a trendy hot take being that people run too much ramp.
  3. Ramp spells don't fulfill one single task. Rather, they perform two separate functions: curving out and increasing total mana quantity.
  4. Adding ramp to a deck without thinking about why you're adding it is both a commonplace action and a recipe for decks that aren't as lean and powerful as they could be.
  5. Ramping on turn one is theoretically the most mana efficient, but basically all one mana ramp will be in the form of mana dorks, which could amplify the weakness to board wipes.
  6. An Elvish Mystic will be great for a buttery smooth curve out, but an EDH game is a hostile climate for a squishy guy like that in the long term.
  7. A deck with a high 30s land count is naturally going to start missing land drops around turn six absent any draw or filtering.
  8. If you're just getting started with a new deck, a good starting point will be to aim high with both draw and ramp.
  9. [My] Malfagore deck runs very little ramp. The goal is to push all players, myself included, into a low resources state.
  10. Consistent land drops are really all that's needed to keep up with [my commander's] command tax.
Full transcript

In my last video, I discussed some patterns I’d noticed during my time reviewing decks, and I gave five common deck building mistakes that I’ve seen people make.
However, that video only covered some of my observations; the other thing I calculated was how often I suggested adding more of different types of cards to a deck.
After interaction, the second most common element was a tie between lands and ramp.
To be honest, this surprised me a little, particularly the ramp part.
Adding ramp feels like a somewhat dated suggestion, a tip offered to newer players in 2018 for how to keep up with the competition in the hottest new format on the block.
In 2025, card draw and synergy feel like bigger focal points for EDH critique and where players should be focusing, with a trendy hot take these days being that people run too much ramp, that the focus on ramp of the past decade has gone too far in some cases.
Undoubtedly there is probably some truth to that.
Ramp has become an accepted part of basically every deck, and adding ramp without thinking about why you’re adding it is both a commonplace action and a recipe for decks that aren’t as lean and powerful as they could be.
Yet, there have been nine decks that I’ve seen that I thought needed more ramp, as opposed to only six that needed more draw.
So, here, I want to get to the bottom of that discrepancy and hopefully build a framework that can help you tell if your deck is in need of ramp or in need of something that’s not ramp.
The first point that needs to be made is that ramp spells don’t fulfill one single task; rather, they perform two separate functions: “curving out” and increasing total mana quantity.

Curving Out
Increasing Total Mana

So, these are the reasons why you might want to play a lot of ramp in your deck, and I do think it is helpful to start by laying them out like this.
However, this still leaves a couple other questions on the table.
For one, how much ramp should a deck be running, assuming it wants it in the first place?

How Much Ramp Should You Run?
When a Deck Doesn’t Want Ramp
Lands as a Replacement for Ramp
#9
Meta CommentaryDec 20253,076 words in transcript

Why the Hybrid Mana Proposal Sparked Strong Reactions

The Hybrid Mana Debacle, and how WotC caused it
We argue that the intense community backlash to the proposed hybrid mana color identity change is excessive given its low stakes.We suggest the reaction stems from recent player frustration with Wizards of the Coast decisions and the timing of the announcement.We note that a similar suggestion was well-received in a past podcast, indicating the current context amplified the response.
Use this in your deckEvaluate if your deck's color identity feels restrictive for hybrid cards, and discuss potential house rules with your playgroup.
Format HealthDeckbuilding RestrictionsDesign IntentMeta TrendsMeta Analysis
Principle
  • The hybrid mana change is a minor clarification that fixes an unintuitive aspect of color identity without altering the format's core restrictions.
  • Community reactions to rules changes are often amplified by pre-existing dissatisfaction with the company rather than the change's merits.
Evidence
  • our earlier podcast proposing the same hybrid mana fix received agreement from viewers, showing the idea is not inherently controversial.
  • The surge of heated YouTube videos and comments against the proposal indicates the response was disproportionate to the change's actual impact.
  • Wizards of the Coast's diminished community trust from decisions like the Spider-Man set and product overload intensified the negative reaction.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Consider whether your decks would benefit from hybrid mana flexibility, and discuss house rules with your playgroup to enhance personal expression.
Limitation
  • We do not advocate for abolishing color identity entirely, only for addressing specific edge cases like hybrid cards and indicator-defined identities.
Example
  • The community's anger over the Spider-Man set and upcoming Universes Beyond overload created an atmosphere where the hybrid mana suggestion became a target for pent-up frustration.
More quotes
  1. The hybrid mana change seems like such a low-stakes issue no matter what side you fall on. But the community response to the suggested change has felt like what it would be if Wizards of the Coast were suggesting literally abolishing color identity.
  2. The online Magic community has been hotboxing anger and resentment in all of its various spaces and subspaces and niches for the past several months. With the Spider-Man set now behind us, that anger has been looking for a new target to explode out at, and this month, that something ended up being the suggested hybrid mana change.
  3. A lot of the arguments against hybrid mana are reheated versions of the arguments defending color identity I've heard.
  4. It's like rhetorical homeopathy. The angle you're arguing is very minor, almost inconsequential, and when you argue it with the passion of somebody whose mother just got insulted, it makes you look like a goofball.
  5. Does hybrid mana weaken color identity? My answer would be only a tiny bit at most, particularly if Wizards of the Coast is more careful with the hybrid color pie in the future.
  6. I think a lot of players forget that [this is a minor change] and just pretend they're arguing against the abolishing of color identity, and the result is that conversations end up feeling deeply strange.
  7. Wizards of the Coast already has colorless cards they can use to give a power card to any commander deck. So, the best tool for this is already available.
  8. Will Wizards of the Coast start rubbing their hands together evilly and sending hybrid stockup or hybrid beast whisperer to the printers the second the hybrid mana change happens? Maybe, but probably not. Once again, this is rhetorical homeopathy.
  9. The fact that it was Wizards of the Coast making an announcement suggesting this change rather than a handsome and big-brained YouTuber talking about it in a podcast definitely had an effect, because Wizards of the Coast is in a rough spot right now with community trust.
  10. The commander format has been identified as a cash cow, and a whole lot of decisions about what gets printed, when it gets printed, and how it gets printed seem to be motivated by milking that cash cow.

A Noteworthy Reaction

Eminence Mechanic Defense and Its Design Problems

In Defense of Eminence
We argue that eminence is largely a fine mechanic, but its bad reputation stems from specific design decisions on Edgar Markov and The Ur-Dragon.These commanders are too powerful and crowd out other options in their tribes due to low-cost, high-impact eminence abilities and five-color identities.Wizards should limit future eminence commanders to fewer colors to avoid overshadowing niche options.
Use this in your deckWhen building a vampire or dragon deck, use a two-color commander like Bladewing or a mono-black vampire to keep your deck distinct, instead of defaulting to Edgar Markov or The Ur-Dragon.
WhiteBlueBlackRedGreenTokensRampCard DrawDesign IntentFormat HealthPower LevelMeta Trends
Principle
  • Eminence itself is a reasonable ability structure. Its negative perception stems from overpowered designs on specific cards.
Evidence
  • Edgar Markov and The Ur-Dragon are currently the two most popular commanders, demonstrating the raw power of their eminence abilities.
  • Edgar Markov's eminence creates a vampire token each time you cast a vampire spell, with no restrictions on number or mana cost.
  • The Ur-Dragon's eminence reduces the cost of all other dragon spells by one, and its five-color identity allows it to be slotted into any dragon deck, marginalizing other commanders.
Deckbuilding Application
  • When selecting a commander for a tribal deck, prefer one with two or fewer colors to preserve the distinct identity of your archetype, rather than defaulting to a five-color eminence option.
Limitation
  • This argument primarily addresses the original cycle of eminence commanders. Newer eminence designs like Sidar Jabari of Zhalfir may demonstrate improved balancing.
Example
  • Edgar Markov dominated vampire decks because his Mardu colors let him lead black-red decks without needing white, providing a strict upgrade with no downside.
  • The Ur-Dragon's cost reduction and five-color identity made it the optimal dragon commander, crowding out alternatives like Bladewing, Atarka, and Scion of the Ur-Dragon.
More quotes
  1. Mostly, eminence is a largely fine keyword that I would like to see again in some form. Its bad reputation is primarily due to some questionable design decisions on Wizards of the Coast's part when they designed some of the original Eminence cards.
  2. [Atraxa, Praetors' Voice] lost her position as the most popular commander. Then last month, she lost her position as the second most popular commander as well. What replaced her were [the Ur-Dragon] and Edgar Markov, both commanders with the keyword eminence.
  3. Edgar Markov, the playable creature, is a six-mana 4/4 with first strike and haste that gives all your vampires a +1/+1 counter when he attacks. Not a bad effect, but certainly not something most Edgar players will be rushing to spend six mana to get.
  4. These two crowd other commanders out of lanes. Dragons and vampires are two of the most iconic Magic: The Gathering creature types, and they're naturally going to be popular choices for players new to the commander format. The issue is that both of these commanders ended up being by far the strongest option for their creature type.
  5. If [the Ur-Dragon] had the same eminence text but was mono red or even red blue, suddenly that's an actual niche.
  6. If you instead make a five-color option that's dramatically better than the others, and if that power doesn't even require casting the card, you destroy a lot of the reason to consider other options.
  7. All other Eminence abilities are easier to get value with, with Sidar Jabari of Zhalfir only requiring attacking with knights, Inalla only requiring wizards to enter, and the other three being almost entirely impossible to stop the effects.
  8. This uninteractability is another point of critique that you'll most commonly find from the removal lovers of the world.
  9. I'll lay my cards on the table here and say that in principle, this feature of Eminence doesn't strictly bother me. There are lots of things that can't be interacted.
  10. Setting aside these more specific examples, it's also important to remember that deleting opposing stuff doesn't need to be the end-all be-all of EDH. And I find that this intense focus on spot removal tends to indicate a lack of creativity from players.
Full transcript

One of the most controversial and often maligned mechanics anywhere in the Commander format is eminence.
Eminence is a keyword officially on five legendary creatures and unofficially on a sixth, and its effect is simple: it lets one line of text on a commander also work while the card is in the command zone.
So Sedara Jabari of Zafir’s first line of text allows you to loot, to draw and discard, whenever you attack with one or more knights, and this happens regardless of whether he himself is on the battlefield or in the command zone.
Then he has another line of text that only applies while he’s on the field and plays off the eminence line, reanimating a night creature whenever he deals combat damage to an opponent.
This is how all six of the eminence creatures work: there will be the eminence ability that helps the deck a bit earlier in the game, and then the commander will be a sort of follow-up to that once played, amplifying the advantage gained by the eminence ability.
If you’re somebody who’s totally new to the keyword, this could seem like a pretty reasonable sort of ability structure, a relatively unobjectionable way for a commander to be designed.
And here, I’m going to argue just that.
Mostly, eminence is a largely fine keyword that I would like to see again in some form, and its bad reputation is primarily due to some questionable design decisions on Wizards of the Coast’s part when they designed some of the original Eminence cards.

The Rise of Eminence Commanders

There are a lot of places I could start my daring defense, but I’m going to start with two interesting things that happened this year.
In May, Attra Prader’s voice lost her position as the most popular commander.
Then last month, she lost her position as the second most popular commander as well.
What replaced her was the dragon and Edgar Marov, both commanders with the keyword eminence.
If you’re unfamiliar with these, you’re probably asking, “What could these commanders do to make them this popular?” And let’s start there.
Edgar Marov’s eminence ability reads, “Whenever you cast another vampire spell, create a 1/1 black vampire creature token.” More mid-powered Edgar decks will typically run a wide range of different mana values and enjoy the extra vampire every turn or two.
Whereas higher-powered Edgar decks will blast the table with an endless wave of low-cost vampires to take advantage of the complete lack of any restriction on Eddie’s eminence ability, as well as the plentiful gooffs Mardu vampires has access to.
Edgar the playable creature is a six-mana 4/4 with first strike and haste that gives all your vampires a +1/+1 counter when he attacks.
Not a bad effect, but certainly not something most Edgar players will be rushing to spend six mana to get.

What about the dragon, the current most popular commander?
His eminence ability simply reads, “Your other dragon spells cost one less to cast.” Unlike Edgar Marov, the dragon’s creature form is quite good if you have the mana for it.
With him being a nine-mana 10/10 flyer with the ability, whenever one or more dragons you control attack, draw that many cards, then you may put a permanent card from your hand onto the battlefield.
This collective package doesn’t require much explanation.
The eminence ability is a powerful mana discount for dragons, and the field ability is a powerful value engine with dragons, collectively producing the two primordial resources for generically playing the game as a dragon deck.

So, these are our two most popular commanders at present day.
They’re also controversial and disliked by a lot of players, particularly Edgar Marov.
I am among those players, and I don’t think either of these cards should have been printed in the form they were.
However, the reasons why these cards are seen as problematic by me and some other players are complicated and have layers to them.
The simplest answer is because they’re powerful, and I actually would consider this a major feature in what makes these cards problematic.
However, power alone doesn’t make a commander an issue.
Cece Weatherlight Captain is one of the most powerful commanders in the game, but I don’t think most people would consider her any sort of problem.
She operates in a unique way and within a niche that has other good options for players who want a different play style, and her CEDH variants don’t tend to bleed play patterns into casual in the same way some other commanders do.
So, what makes these weaker commanders more of an issue by comparison?
One of these is simple.
Being associated with an iconic creature type imbues them with a natural casual commander aura.
A tutor engine like sis obviously feels weird at a table of precons, but that big old dragon deck might pass the “vibe check” just fine.
This is the classic feature of a “pub stomper” commander: not strictly being powerful, but being out of place, easily wriggling its way into games it might not really belong in.

Another power-associated issue I’d identify with these is something I alluded to when I was talking about Cece earlier.
These two crowd other commanders out of lanes.
Dragons and vampires are two of the most iconic Magic the Gathering creature types and they’re naturally going to be popular choices for players new to the commander format.
The issue then is that both of these commanders ended up being by far the strongest option for their creature type, and that fact then pushed any new dragon or vampire player looking for a shortcut to a strong deck to get there by paying a lot of money for the singular strongest commander.
Edgar Marov spent four years over the $50 mark and the dragon spent two years at a similar price, with both of their peak periods corresponding with the surge in the popularity of the commander format that started in late 2020.

Initially the connection with eminence might seem pretty surface level.
A powerful eminence card takes over a creature type and a weaker eminence card might not do this.
But this misses a key feature, which is how eminence interacts with colors.
See, the dragon’s color identity is part of its issue.
If dragon had the same eminence text but was mono red or even red blue, suddenly that’s an actual niche.
Bladewing could have stayed the black red choice.
Atark could have stayed the red green choice.
And the ever funky scion of the dragon could have stayed the choice for players who wanted all five colors.
But if you instead make a five-color option that’s dramatically better than the others, and if that power doesn’t even require casting the card, you destroy a lot of the reason to consider other options.
Even if you don’t want to play with a five-color mana base, the discount alone makes dragon already better than most of the other options, even if it’s never cast.
The same goes for Edgar.
Vampires are traditionally mono black, followed by black red, followed by black white.
If Edgar had been put into the relatively unexplored in 2017 color identity of black white, suddenly vampire players would have a choice between the more established vampire identity of black red and the newer Ixelon centered identity of black white with this spicy new commander.
Instead, both of these are slurped up by Edgar.
And the fact that most of his power is in his eminence ability means that players don’t even need to try to be in three colors.
Just stick him at the head of your black red vampire deck and cast him when you draw your command tower and bing bang boom, your deck just received a strict upgrade.
To be clear, I don’t think it’s wrong for Wizards of the Coast to have wanted big powerful uniting options with lots of colors to hand out to some iconic creature types.
However, Eminence has some unique problems in fitting this role.
And for this reason, I would lean toward two colors or less on Eminence commanders as a rule, especially if they’re in a niche where they risk overshadowing other options.
I might have even done this with the more recently printed Sedar Jabari of Zafir, which works with knights, though at least that one has a better balance between the power of the eminence ability and the power of the castable creature.

The Importance of Specificity

Now, it might sound like this niche element being tied to iconic creature types is a big part of my problem with Edgar and the air dragon.
And in a sense, it is, but tying Eminence to cards with a creature type actually accomplishes an important goal: increasing the specificity of Eminence abilities.
See, having an effect baked into your gameplay for free is pretty potent.
Some players might find it inherently problematic, and my personal opinion is that those players are underestimating the sheer power that a traditional command zone can offer a deck.
But still, getting an effect without paying for it is certainly notable, and it’s important that this effect is specific so as to contain how out of hand it can get.
This is true for both the trigger of the eminence ability and for the payoff of it.
Ideally, it should be a specific trigger that provides a specific output.
But if you’re going to make one a bit more general, you really need to crank up the specificity of the other.

Let’s take the example of an eminence ability that gets this right in my book, that of Arabo, Roar of the World.
On paper, this ability is by no means weak.
Giving +3/+3 to a creature every combat step from the very first turn of the game is quite powerful, allowing for some hefty beatdown early and amplifying later damage done with various effects that play off of damage dealt.
However, this effect, in addition to typically falling under the generally less problematic aggro archetype, is specific.
It’s an effect that makes a cat more powerful at attacking.
There’s a couple double strike cats and a singular infect cat that will offer the occasional early kill on a blockerless opponent, but with the cat limitation, you don’t have access to a whole lot of cards that would be powerful if they were a lot bigger on an early turn.
This specificity counterintuitively has a diversifying effect on the play patterns of Arabo decks.
Rather than being all-in on taking advantage of the +3/+3 buff on turns two to four when it’s the most powerful, Arabo players need to devise ways to make use of the effect more holistically throughout the game, and this can take a variety of different forms.
Arabo’s eminence is also uniquely interactable among eminence commanders.
A sorcery speed +3/+3 pump doesn’t typically do anything until creatures are making contact during combat, which leaves ample opportunity to interact with it.

Uninteractability and Eminence

I like this design for Arabo, but you may have noticed that I did say uniquely interactable.
All other Eminence abilities are easier to get value with, with Sedar Jabari only requiring attacking with knights, Inala only requiring wizards to enter, and the other three being almost entirely impossible to stop the effects of.
This uninteractability is another point of critique that you’ll most commonly find from the removal lovers of the world.
These commanders all have effects that can’t even be gotten rid of by dusting them with a sword supply shares.
Now, I’ll lay my cards on the table here and say that in principle, this feature of Eminence doesn’t strictly bother me.
There are lots of things that can’t be interacted with.
Barring hard stacks pieces, drawing a card at start of turn, playing lands, untapping permanence, and so forth.
This game is also full of hexroof creatures, uncounterable spells, and abilities that can’t even be responded to.
Setting aside these more specific examples, it’s also important to remember that deleting opposing stuff doesn’t need to be the end-all be-all of EDH.
And I find that this intense focus on spot removal tends to indicate a lack of creativity from players.
A couple months ago, I discussed on a second channel video that I actually enjoy not having many tools to deal with very large creatures in one of my decks, as it forces me to think harder with my attacks and utilize the deck’s more aggressive tools in some situations.
This is a game with many axes on which to attack opposing resources, whether that be board, hand, library, life total, or the stack, and it’s good to be willing to move outside your comfort zone sometimes among those different options.

Now, does all of this mean the uninteractability of eminence creatures is never a problem?
No. And in practice, this feature of eminence can be a real pain point for players, with Edgar Marov being one of the more egregious examples of an eminence ability feeling bad due to its uninteractability.
His Eminence ability triggers on cast for a card type with lots of great low-cost options any number of times per turn.
If I’m at a low life total and I counter two vampire spells that the Edgar player plays, they still get the two tokens.
If they have a source of haste, Edgar’s in red, remember these vampires can attack immediately.
And these easy-to-obtain and hard-to-prevent 1/1s aren’t just creatures to attack and block with.
Vampires as a type have a bunch of really powerful cards that take advantage of the raw number of vampires in play, most of which can only be prevented with a counterspell.
And that’s to say nothing of the blood artists and other aristocrats tools that powerful Eddie decks tend to be crammed with.
An eminence ability that grants creature tokens for casting spells was always going to be a little tricky to pull off regardless of creature type, and that ability being for vampires in a deck with black puts it fully over the line into dangerous territory.
The tokens are too easy to get and there’s too many things that can be done with them.

In this extended discussion of how Edgar plays, the point I’m trying to make is that the frustrating play patterns of him and some other eminence commanders are the result of specific design choices, not a problem with the eminence mechanic as a whole.
Gavin Verh who worked on the desig.

#11
Format TheoryOct 2025181K views

Glass Cannon Commander Design and Its Pitfalls

The Problem with Glass Cannon Commanders
We argue that glass cannon Commanders like Kaalia and Animar are powerful enablers but have fragile game plans.Their decks often rely heavily on the commander, making them vulnerable to removal and leading to unsatisfying play experiences.We advise evaluating deck construction to mitigate this built-in fragility.
Use this in your deckAudit your deck’s mana base: if your average mana value exceeds 4 and your commander is an enabler, you need more than 36 lands and 11 ramp spells to function independently.
WhiteBlackRedMidrangeDesign IntentResilienceDeckbuilding RestrictionsDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • Glass cannon Commanders are efficient enablers that can create high-variance games, where the deck either dominates or stalls entirely based on the commander's survival.
  • Decks built around enabler Commanders risk becoming non-functional if the commander is repeatedly removed, because the rest of the deck depends on the commander to operate.
Evidence
  • Kaalia of the Vast decks average only 36 lands and 11 ramp spells despite an average mana value above 4, creating a mismatch that makes hardcasting threats difficult without the commander.
Deckbuilding Application
  • When constructing an enabler commander deck, ensure your mana base and backup options can sustain the game plan even if the commander is neutralized.
Limitation
  • Even when including protection for the commander, decks like Kaalia remain vulnerable to board wipes because they rely on a fragile attacker and a slow creature-based strategy.
Example
  • Kaalia of the Vast is a classic example of a glass cannon: a 4-mana 2/2 that must attack to cheat creatures into play, making her easy to disrupt and stranding high-cost cards in hand.
More quotes
  1. Powerful designs like Yuriko and Edgar Markov stretch the mechanics of the Commander format to its outer limit, and all sorts of other cards are controversial in their own right, even if they're less obvious mechanical outliers.
  2. I want to talk about glass cannon Commanders, Commanders that are powerful and explosive, but also fragile.
  3. [These cards], while certainly having some benefits, can risk misleading players and causing them to stumble into play experiences they don't necessarily want.
  4. Fundamentally, all Commanders will be an enabler, a payoff, or something.
  5. Whereas a payoff Commander is the amplifying adornment atop a solid 99, an enabler Commander will often be step one to doing anything in the game, with the rest of the deck living atop.
  6. Why play some neutered halfway consistent Kaalia deck when you can live out your most daring Mardu fantasies, to stray ever closer to the sun with your cool flyers?
  7. Due to the fact that both of these Commanders are enablers that discount large amounts of mana on creatures, in the most ideal games with the best draws and the least opposing interaction, they're exploding out of control.
  8. Quite a lot of Commanders feature the phrase 'draw a card' or some equivalent in their text box. And while you could argue that generic value is a little overplayed as a payoff for a Commander to grant, this design choice has had the undeniable boon of improving the resilience of a lot of play styles.
  9. Your command zone secures you exactly one element of your deck, and though you can try to sculpt your 99 to lead to a well-balanced experience, there will still only be one card in your deck that you're absolutely guaranteed to see.
  10. This is why the most explosive decks out there typically feature an explosive commander focused on either damage or raw board size.
Full transcript
Glass Cannon Commanders

Something else that's fragile, though, is your Magic: The Gathering decks, which is why you should consider some extremely cool-looking accessories from our sponsor, Void Monster.
Void Monster is run by Magic: The Gathering artist Forest Imel and features some pretty fantastic original designs.
The color playmats are a great evergreen option, and there's also a new drop every month available for only a limited time.
This month is the Devoid drop, which includes a playmat, a deck box, and a bonus mana tracker card if you buy the bundle.
I love the sort of chalky austere exterior paired with the writhing blue tentacles on the interior; this perfectly encapsulates Eldrazi to me.
If this drop interests you, you can follow the link in my description and pre-order before October 19th.
Thanks to Void Monster for sponsoring this piece.

The First Commander Set and Its Glass Cannons

Take Kaalia.
She's a four-mana 2/2 flyer in Mardu colors who lets her controller put into play an Angel, Demon, or Dragon tapped and attacking whenever she attacks.
The merging of these three big flying creature types associated with three different colors is quite iconic and specific to Kaalia, and though her creature-cheating functionality shares some similarities with more recent designs like Akroma, the Unrepentant and Mister Foxglove, no other card accomplishes this cheating quite the way Kaalia does.
The same goes for Animar.
Though there are other cards that can provide deep discounts for creature spells, such as Rakdos and Hamza, these generally require a bit more specificity in their play patterns to pay off big.
Animar, on the other hand, is a fully preassembled creature storm engine.
The only step to making creatures cheaper is casting creatures, and it's not hard to see how this could lead to some dramatic turns without much setup required.
In short, both of these Commanders have a specific and iconic niche that they embody well.

This is one part of their popularity, and the other is simply that they're efficient at their role.
Each of these cards has a high ceiling, and achieving a powerful game state doesn't require as many steps as a lot of other Commanders, simply requiring having cards in hand ready to deploy and avoiding opposing removal.
But what about the floor?
The biggest weakness of these two Commanders is that their game plans are a bit brittle and not particularly hard to set back to basically square one.
To fully get into why I'd apply a label like "glass cannon" to these, we should really discuss the nature of what Commanders provide for various decks.

Fundamentally, all Commanders will be an "enabler," a "payoff," or something in between.
Payoffs are the most common.
Ishin doubles on attack triggers, amplifying the volume of gameplay produced with these, and Atraxa gives proliferates, which amplify the power of cards that use counters.
These feel nice to play because the play pattern is simple and effective: you start putting together a game plan with cards the Commander synergizes with, and then you drop the Commander after a bit to turbocharge that game plan.
Enabler Commanders are less common.
Zur the Enchanter is the only card in the top 100 Commanders I'd call a pure enabler, as he isn't really a payoff for anything at all aside from maybe having a pair of lightning greaves and some cool enchantments to grab in the library.
You play him so you can start tutoring out enchantments, and then your game plan gets built up from there.
This end of things is closer to where Animar and Kaalia live, with Animar being a slight enabler and Kaalia being a moderate enabler.
On a basic level, being an enabler isn't a bad thing.
My favorite decks feature Commanders that fall closer to the enabling end of the spectrum.
However, enabler Commanders are inherently trickier to make play well because of the nature of the enabler-payoff relationship.
Whereas a payoff Commander is the amplifying adornment atop a solid 99, an enabler Commander will a lot of times be step one to doing anything in the game, with the rest of the deck living atop it.
To be clear, this isn't always a problem.
If there are other ways to get the enabling component, it can be fine.
If the enabling component is hard to prevent or set back, it can be fine.
And if the enabling component isn't essential to the deck's functionality, it can be fine.
The issue should hopefully be becoming clearer.
These caveats go frequently unfulfilled with each of these Commanders.

Kaalia of the Vast's role is to cheat permanents into play, and she's generally an important enabler for the big flying creatures game plans she's played with.
Can the deck do its game plan without her?
Not really.
The average Kaalia deck has 36 lands and 11 pieces of ramp in it, numbers far too small for a deck with an average mana value over four.
Next you might ask, can it at least protect her?
And the answer is kind of.
Hasty boots are golden here, and you can certainly add other cards that can protect Kaalia early, such as Mother of Runes or Deflecting Swat.
However, this is complicated by the fact that the deck is also vulnerable to board wipes, since it's a low-velocity big creature midrange deck.
So ideally your protection suite can help that out as well.
This might allude to the fundamental challenge with building Kaalia: deck space.
Ideally the deck will want some single-target protection to deal with early pinpoint removal, as well as some board protection and a healthy draw suite to let it manage wipes, plus a healthy count of lands and ramp to help the deck bounce back from all challenges.
Going higher on the counts of all of these things could let the deck feel more flexible and resilient, but also risks removing some of the bite of the deck.
As the count of Angels, Demons, and Dragons dips lower to offer other things to the deck, the aggressive options to choose from for Kaalia triggers will also typically dip, and you'll run out of creatures to cheat out earlier and more often.
It's no surprise then that a lot of Kaalia players take the opposite approach: lower land counts, lower protection counts, focusing on powerful beaters rather than card draw, even including amplifier permanents like Ishin and Windcrag Siege that can further pump up the explosiveness of the deck.
Why play some neutered halfway consistent Kaalia deck when you can live out your most daring Mardu fantasies, to stray ever closer to the sun with your cool flyers?

On the surface, Animar might seem quite a bit different from this.
It's typically more reliant on early draws to start presenting a threat at the table, but its relationship with opposing interaction is arguably less problematic.
At worst you'll empty your hand into a board wipe and lose a bunch of resources, but unlike with Kaalia, the 99 typically features a decent amount of draw and ramp, allowing for smoother recoveries.
At best, Animar can turn a deck into a high-velocity machine.
Even without an Ancestral Statue infinite combo, with the right draw engines, ramp engines, and maybe a haste enabler, you can empty a hefty portion of your hand onto the board in a single turn, possibly deleting an opponent in the process if you can swing or get some Terror of the Peaks triggers.
The Commander also has a bit of built-in protection as well, unlike Kaalia, dodging about half of all popular pinpoint removal.
So there certainly are differences between these.
Where these Commanders are similar, though, is in their power profile and in the difference between the floor and the ceiling of their game plan.
Due to the fact that both of these Commanders are enablers that discount large amounts of mana on creatures, in the most ideal games with the best draws and the least opposing interaction, they're exploding out of control.
Meanwhile, if draws are worse, particularly for Animar, or if there's more interaction, particularly for Kaalia, the function of discounting creature spells isn't as helpful.

Quite a lot of Commanders feature the phrase "draw a card" or some equivalent in their text box.
And while you could argue that generic value is a little overplayed as a payoff for a Commander to grant, this design choice has had the undeniable boon of improving the resilience of a lot of play styles.
The Commander of a deck is a card that's available at all times, and assuming the draw condition isn't too hard, having that permanent eighth card in hand be a draw spell reduces the risk of stall-outs and improves recovery.
If you need to hit land drops, drawing cards can find you lands.
If you're looking for specific synergy pieces, drawing cards can get you closer to those as well.
If you just got board wiped, as long as you drew some cards before that happened, you'll hopefully have some options for rebuilding.
A value engine commander typically won't be the most threatening or aggressive choice, but it will usually be a safer and more consistent one, all things equal.
Your command zone secures you exactly one element of your deck, and though you can try to sculpt your 99 to lead to a well-balanced experience, there will still only be one card in your deck that you're absolutely guaranteed to see.

Jodah, the Unifier: A Modern Glass Cannon
The Appeal and the Pitfall
#12
Meta CommentarySep 202566K views

Why Some EDH Players Resist Pre-Game Deck Pitches

r/EDH and Communication
We examine a Reddit post advocating for brief pre-game deck pitches and the backlash from players who prefer to deduce strategies through gameplay.We argue that in casual EDH, the lack of a dominant winning goal leads players to substitute competitive skills with encyclopedic format knowledge, treating games as deductive puzzles.We acknowledge the appeal of this mystery-driven playstyle but question its opposition to transparent communication.
Use this in your deckGive a brief pitch of your deck's strategy before the game to improve multiplayer alignment.
WhiteBlueRedRule ZeroPower LevelThreat AssessmentSocial and Politics
Principle
  • In casual EDH, the absence of a dominant winning goal leads players to seek substitute skills to find meaning in gameplay.
  • Players often replace competitive skills with encyclopedic knowledge of the format, treating games as deductive puzzles.
Evidence
  • Negative reactions to pre-game deck pitches on Reddit show a faction that prefers to deduce strategies through play rather than be told.
  • Comments like 'revealing what your deck does by playing it sounds way more fun' illustrate the desire for in-game discovery.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Before a game, provide a short description of your deck's strategy to help opponents calibrate their threat assessment.
Limitation
  • The deduction-based playstyle can be genuinely enjoyable, testing players' knowledge and pattern recognition.
  • Not all players will embrace transparency, and the mystery-driven approach remains a valid preference.
Example
  • Upon seeing a commander like Tasa, experienced players immediately anticipate typical combos such as Ashnod's Altar.
  • Observing a Blood Artist prompts players to consider sacrifice outlets and loop potential within the opponent's colors.
More quotes
  1. Brackets are a useful tool, but they don't address specific play pattern preferences.
  2. A good solution to this problem is to have players come to the table with a basic 10-second pitch for what their deck is doing.
  3. Revealing what your deck does by playing it sounds way more fun to me than everyone giving a synopsis on how the game is going to go before it even starts.
  4. Leaving some mystery to the game is fun. It means we can play to figure out each other's weakness instead of basically being told.
  5. There seems to be a large online faction of Commander players who think this way, who treat Commander as some sort of detective game where a large part of the gameplay is figuring out what's going on in opposing decks, or alternatively as some sort of show that you wouldn't want to spoil the ending.
  6. EDH players are aimless goblins wandering through the world looking for meaning.
  7. I don't even need to have a conversation with my opponent. All I need to do is see that TASA pull up and I'm already squaring up for some Ashnod's Altar BS.
  8. EDH is already an unbelievably information dense format. Dingus.
  9. The information density of EDH is untouched by any other widely played format.
  10. The game of figuring out what my opponent's deck does is just not one I think is beneficial to have at the heart of the EDH format.
Full transcript
The Reddit Post and the Ten-Second Pitch

First thoughts: this is fine.
A very normal opinion to have and a reasonable suggestion.
I already do some version of this, as do most people I play with in public.
At least half the time when I’m reading the commander of a stranger I’m playing against or when I make a comment about it, they’ll drop me a basic pitch for how they’re using the commander or what their deck is doing.
The only critique I might have of this idea is that it’s maybe a little odd to have a pitch like this rehearsed word for word and delivered like some sort of theatrical monologue, but the broader point about communication is good.
Or so I thought.

Critical Reactions and the Detective Game Mentality

In the broader scheme of things, this doesn’t matter.
A modestly visible Reddit post attracted some people with opinions I disagree with, who then got a handful of upvotes; big whoop.
However, I can’t get this line of thinking out of my brain, and these opinions are similar to comments I’ve received on my channel in the past.
There seems to be a large online faction of Commander players who think this way, who treat Commander as some sort of detective game where a large part of the gameplay is figuring out what’s going on in opposing decks, or alternatively as some sort of show that you wouldn’t want to spoil the ending of.
So, I’m going to take this Reddit post and its comments as a jumping-off point to talk about that broad faction, this group of players that I find fascinating, and I’m going to see if I can figure out what motivates their thinking, why they’re like this, and if there’s anything to the vision of Commander they strive after.

As a disclaimer, we i more conceptual and social-theory-oriented than most of what I make, so I’d recommend treating it as some fun ideas to chew on rather than cold hard facts to destroy the libs with or whatever.
Though this is sort of a soft response to the Trinket Mages video about me in April, so I guess this can be cold hard facts to destroy him with.

Part One: EDH Players Are Aimless Goblins Wandering Through the World Looking for Meaning

The same goes for EDH.
People will create goals in the deck building process, and one of those goals will frequently be making a deck that is good under some set of criteria, at least if you’re not one of the mythical bracket one gamers.
So that will be a part of deck building.
But what about in games?
There are all sorts of skills tested in a competitive 1v1 Magic environment: turn sequencing, mastery of a given deck list, assessment of opposing threats, probabilistic thinking, playing to outs, and so on.
A 1v1 Magic tournament will stress test a player’s skills in all of these things to the limit, and in many cases, gains will be won and lost by the collective weight of a lot of tiny decisions.
As in deck building, EDH’s more casual nature makes things trickier in the sense that winning isn’t as dominant a goal, and plays that are sub-optimal in the strict sense might not necessarily be considered misplays in the same way they are in 60-card formats.

As a result of this, a lot of EDH players subconsciously go looking for something to replace some of these competitive skills, and in a format as diverse as EDH, a very common substitute is knowledge.
A certain and fairly large subset of EDH players seem almost obsessed with knowing as many possible plays their opponents can do as possible.
This is especially true for players playing at a level with sudden ways to win, where not being able to recognize a combo piece or a central enabler for an opponent’s deck can rapidly lead to a game loss.
Whether it’s as an outlet for an underutilized competitive instinct or as a trauma response to getting OTK’d one too many times, being able to shrewdly navigate the ins and outs of opposing plays and figure out how threatening a particular card is within the entire landscape of the EDH format is a skill that a lot of players have built up.

This is especially true for commanders.
A lot of commanders have a handful of cards they pair pretty powerfully with, and having it locked away in your dome what those cards might be for a given commander can give you a leg up in determining the possibilities for what an opponent might be doing.
And if we assume that most decks within a given commander will be somewhat similar, having an encyclopedic knowledge of commanders and what their typical decks look like can actually serve as somewhat of a replacement for “rule zero.” I don’t even need to have a conversation with my opponent; all I need to do is see that TASA pull up and I’m already squaring up for some Ashnod’s Altar BS.
So, this is a skill that a lot of players have put effort into honing, whether consciously or unconsciously, and it shouldn’t be surprising that a lot of them want to use this skill.

For a player who has spent a lot of time around various Magic cards and is fully engaged in a game, it’s not hard to imagine this being a lot of fun.
I see my opponent play a Blood Artist effect and I start looking for possible loops, think about what sac outlets and supporting pieces are in color for my opponent.
I see a Karn’s Bastion hit the board and start trying to deduce what counters are soon to pop into existence.
I see an opponent with two lands untapped on my turn and I try to figure out what sorts of disruption they might be able to put together with red, blue, and white mana.
I would be lying if I claimed that the description of the Commander format I just laid out doesn’t sound like fun; it’s appealing to play a game like that.
So, what’s the problem?
Surely there’s nothing wrong with people enjoying the format this way.

Part Two: EDH Is Already an Unbelievably Information-Dense Format. Dingus.

This is an undoubtedly nice feature, but on the flip side, this lack of a metagame ironically poses an entirely different set of challenges onto players that aren’t found in a competitive 1v1 format.
You can’t learn the four most common decks you’ll be playing because there are thousands of possible decks you might find yourself facing.
This might be manageable in a play group with unchanging decks, but playing out in the wild with strangers is a whole different story.
Any set of four EDH decks will likely have more than 200 unique non-land cards, which is a fair amount to learn if the decks are unchanging and an ongoing gauntlet; for newer players in a public setting where each game might present dozens of new cards, it’s even tougher.
I think most of us have been in a game among strangers where it becomes clear that one or more players are checked out from what opponents are doing, fatigued by the process of keeping up with all the new cardboard that’s hitting the table.

So, that’s the case I’m trying to present here.
EDH has three times the opponents and often around ten times the number of unique cards in opposing decks as compared to 1v1 formats.
There are 30,000 cards legal in the format now and thousands of brand new cards entering the format every year.
Each deck tends to play far more permanents than are seen in 1v1 formats due to the high life totals and the multiplayer aspect, and the result is that boards rapidly clog up with assorted permanents for players to keep track of.
The information density of EDH is untouched by any other widely played format, and that is a feature about it that we need to keep in mind when we’re playing it.

All of this is why I find the knowledge competition version of casual EDH a little frustrating and seek to reduce it as much as possible.
And that’s as somebody who is probably better equipped to threat-analyze than at least 80% of EDH players.
It’s not that I can’t figure out how potentially game-ending a sequencing of opposing plays is on the fly; it’s that I don’t think that should necessarily be a constant task expected of all EDH players.
The game of figuring out what my opponent’s deck does is just not one I think is beneficial to have at the heart of the EDH format.
There are even plenty of days that I personally wish to avoid this part of the format, to kick my feet back and focus on resolving my cascade triggers instead of Sherlock Holmesing my way to what the white-knuckled power gamer across the table is packing.
But still, with all that said, I could still see somebody thinking that I’m throwing out the baby with the bathwater here.
Sure, knowing a rough outline of what opposing decks are doing might reduce the mental load of the format and make it more approachable for all people, but what about the theatricality of it?
What about surprises and zany moments and unexpected twists?

Part Three: The Grand Reveal, or How to Be Terrible at Creating Compelling Social Experiences

At this point, you’re probably asking, “What does this have to do with EDH, Alex?” Well, a very similar dynamic takes place there.
I dedicated a whole video to this topic about a year ago, but the basic idea there was that there are some inherent and unavoidable problems with decks that like to win in some grandiose way.
I have a deck that wins by getting three Fiend Hunters into play at the same time to Fiend Hunter each other indefinitely and cobble together an ETB-based win from there.
I also brewed a list with the goal of casting Rogu Rock an arbitrarily large number of times before Jessaing all three

#13
DeckbuildingAug 202577K views

Using EDHREC Effectively in Commander Deck Building

How I use Commander's Most Misunderstood Tool
We teach a balanced method for using EDHREC in Commander deck building.We recommend consulting EDHREC early to understand popular builds, then exploring independently to preserve creativity, and finally using filters with indicator cards to discover cards for your specific archetype.This approach avoids both over-reliance and dismissal of the tool.
Use this in your deckChoose a card that uniquely represents your deck's strategy and use it as an indicator card in EDHREC's filter to find niche cards for your build.
EDHREC DataCard EvaluationDesign IntentDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • Use the default EDHREC page only for broad awareness of common themes, not as a blueprint for your deck.
  • Treating commanders as monolithic entities ignores the diversity of possible builds.
Evidence
  • The Animar page shows that more than half of decks are combo-focused, which will shape how opponents react to your deck regardless of your build.
  • Scryfall searches often miss niche synergistic cards like Dour Port Mage or Whitemane Lion, which EDHREC can surface when filtered.
Deckbuilding Application
  • After establishing your deck's core concept, return to EDHREC and apply the filter option with indicator cards to see only lists that match your specific archetype.
  • During the initial creative phase, avoid looking at card popularity percentages (such as 47% of decks using Deadly Dispute) to prevent subconscious bias.
Limitation
  • The default EDHREC page is less useful for commanders with highly divergent builds because it pools all versions together, burying niche options specific to your intended archetype.
  • Relying on EDHREC without early independent exploration results in decks that are flat, formulaic, and missing creative tools.
Example
  • On the Zada page, Fists of Flame is a top card, showing that the base page often highlights explosive plays that may define a commander's reputation.
  • Looking at the Prosper page can reveal the category of 'cast from anywhere other than your hand,' leading to cards like Memory Worm that might not appear in typical Scryfall searches.
More quotes
  1. I have spoken before about how using EDHREC for all of your deck building needs can lead to decks that are flat, formulaic, and missing lots of great tools.
  2. In reality, EDHREC is an intensely useful website that has the capability to make your decks better, more interesting, even more unique in some ways if you know how to use it correctly.
  3. The longer you spend on this unaltered EDHREC page without looking elsewhere, the more you'll get sucked into the stuff on this page and subconsciously let it decide what your deck wants to be for you.
  4. I find that the progression of gradually building up in cards and plugging in different Scryfall searches as I figure out different ways the deck could play is really valuable as a creative step.
  5. EDHREC can help you fill in the gaps with popular cards that Scryfall searches might have missed.
  6. We as EDH players have a tendency to treat commanders as whole entities, like characters selected from a grid in a fighting game.
  7. Decks with a given commander can vary dramatically from one another. And the more diverse a given commander's decks are, the less helpful the base EDHREC page will be.
  8. The filters option allows you to filter out decks that do or don't include a given card.
  9. The presence of more expensive ramp will generally be a good sign that a deck has a big mana game plan.
  10. If I filter for decks that include Thalia and exclude Soul Warden, life gain is basically obliterated from the page.
Full transcript
An Intensely Useful but Misunderstood Tool
When to Use EDHREC: Early for the Lay of the Land, Late for Filling Gaps

Early in the process of deck building, I just want to get a lay of the land.
EDHREC is useful as a shortcut for seeing the most popular and often the most powerful stuff you can do with a commander.
In some cases, these will be the flashiest plays you can do with a given commander; we can see Fists of Flame at the top of the Zada page, Curiosity at the top of the Niv-Mizzet, Parun page, and Parhelion II at the top of the Greasefang page.
Even if you don’t intend to use the stuff at the top of an EDHREC page, it’s good to know what’s there.
If you’re looking to build a deck around Animar, a popular commander, it’s good to know that more than half of Animar players are playing him as some form of combo deck, because that will impact how your deck is perceived and what other players will be on the lookout for.
In other cases, the top of an EDHREC page might be more like the points of agreement between different builds of a commander.
For something like Atraxa, Grand Unifier, we see some popular removal spells and some flicker cards to repeat the commander’s powerful ETB, but after that, the play rates of different cards fall off pretty fast with players choosing a variety of different approaches for the specifics of their deck.
In this case, I could go, “Okay, I guess I’ll start with flicker and then decide the specifics of how I want to do that.” And if I want to be among the roughly 25% of cEDH players playing Atraxa, judging by this page, I’ll typically look at an EDHREC page at first to see any super obvious build paths that other people have been doing, but then once I’ve decided to build a particular commander, I’ll click away for a while.
It’s useful to know the basics of what other people are doing, but the longer you spend on this unaltered EDHREC page without looking elsewhere, the more you’ll get sucked into the stuff on this page and subconsciously let it decide what your deck wants to be for you.
In cases when I’m using a top-down approach to build a deck, basically finding a commander I like with a card or two in mind, I like to get away from EDHREC pretty early so that the foundations of the deck aren’t too influenced by what’s popular.
In the early stages of building a deck, I don’t really want to know that 47% of other players are using Deadly Dispute because that colors my opinion of the card at a stage in deck building when I want to be exploring possibilities and keeping an open mind to different options with the commander.
Other players might be different, but I find that the progression of gradually building up in cards and plugging in different Scryfall searches as I figure out different ways the deck could play is really valuable as a creative step; it’s a stage in deck building that I want to preserve as much as possible.
Regardless of your exact preferences, if you’re starting your deck building journey on EDHREC, I strongly recommend you click away to fuss around with your own thing for a while at some point.

For me, EDHREC comes back into the picture a bit later, once I’ve discovered a fair number of cards and have a decently strong concept of my deck but haven’t fully ironed out an initial draft yet.
Whereas the initial usage of EDHREC was to get a lay of the land and see a handful of basic build paths, in this later stage of EDHREC usage, I am looking for specific cards.
Scryfall is a lovely tool, but there are some things that it just won’t hit in any reasonable number of searches.
Dour Port-Mage won’t show up in any common searches you might make for a flicker deck, and if you’re building a Karametra deck, self-bouncers like Whitemane Lion might not even enter your radar if you’re not already in the know.
This is where EDHREC is helpful; it can help you fill in the gaps with popular cards that Scryfall searches might have missed.
Even if a particular card isn’t showing up on the EDHREC page due to not being popular enough, looking at EDHREC can still help you find that card.
If you’re building a cast-from-exile deck, you’ll probably find a bunch of payoffs for casting things from exile from Scryfall searches on your own, but a brief trip to the Prosper EDHREC page might clue you in on the words “from anywhere other than your hand.” Suddenly, that’s a whole new avenue to take a stroll down, and you might find yourself a lovely Memory Worm.
Get in here, you funky little guy.

How to Use EDHREC: Filtering to Find Your Specific Deck

So, what’s the solution?
The answer is filters, which I make very heavy use of once I’m further along and in that card-gleaning stage of EDHREC usage.
By default, the website will draw data from all decks in its database for a given commander, but the filters option allows you to filter out decks that do or don’t include a given card.
Now, you could put all sorts of stuff in this, but the most useful type of card will be what I like to call “indicator cards,” which are basically just cards that fit very cleanly in or out of a given archetype.
