For example, if we go to the page of Kellan, Inquisitive Prodigy, we see a mélange of different elements, but the most prominent ones are things involving clues, which kind of makes sense given that blue-green clues is an established Innistrad archetype.
This is going to be helpful to some people, but what if I’m looking for a higher-curved deck, maybe even considering the inclusion of Keruga as a companion?
In that case, this page won’t do much for me before a bit of filtering.
The one card near the top of the page that really stands out as a big mana option is Map the Frontier, a four-mana sorcery that searches up two basic lands and/or deserts to the field.
Actually, this works great as an indicator card because it’s not the sort of card that most decks will want; mana ramp past two, maybe three mana stuff will typically only be included in a deck if it fits with the deck’s synergies.
So, the presence of more expensive ramp will generally be a good sign that a deck has a big mana game plan.
Sure enough, that’s reflected after filtering: we see lots of higher-cost cards that would require mana investment to succeed, as well as lots of great tools for a Keruga deck.

So, that’s one example, but what about an example of an anti-indicator card?
A while back, I was brewing a Kardur control deck, sort of like a red flicker deck.
A major goal of the deck is to have a basically unlimited supply of Disrupt Decorum available since Kardur has that effect on ETB, and ETBs can be repeated with a card like Jaxis that can produce a token copy of a creature each turn.
I thought this was a sensible enough plan for the commander, but when I went to the EDHREC page, a number of the more commonly played cards themselves did goad-related things.
Initially, I was pretty shocked that around half of all Kardur players felt the need to run cards that do the same thing as their commander, but that’s their business, not mine.
The important thing is what happened next: I filtered the results.
One option could be to filter by cards I know I want in the deck, such as a couple red flicker staples like Jaxis and Conjurer’s Closet.
However, the result is pretty muddy, with Disrupt Decorum now showing up in an even greater proportion of decks.
The issue is that these red flicker cards don’t do anything to indicate a player who is averse to redundancy as I am to some extent; there are plenty of Kardur maxers out there who will take the flicker stuff and the additional copies of goad cards because they just like Disrupt Decorum that much.
No, the way to start sculpting a helpful EDHREC page in this case is to exclude the cards that do the same thing as the commander, such as Disrupt Decorum and Geode Rager.
After that, the page starts looking a lot more helpful.
There’s still some of this that’s probably outside what my deck would want to do, but at least the good stuff is mostly out of the way.
Vengeful Ancestor is only in 12% of the remaining decks after this filtering, down from 43% on the base page.

As a final example of filtering, let’s talk about how to combine includes and excludes.
Back when I had a Liesa hate bears deck, I’d periodically check EDHREC to see if anything was catching my eye, but for this to work, I needed to filter the page first.
The most popular build of Liesa is life gain, and though I don’t mind a little life gain to offset Liesa’s drain, it’s definitely not what the deck is built around.
A first step could be excluding some core life gain cards such as Soul Warden, but this isn’t all that effective because life gain is just such a dominant archetype and there are plenty of life gain decks not including a certain life gain card for one reason or another.
A different approach is to filter for inclusion of Thalia, Guardian of Thraben, which is basically the first card I’d add to any hate bears deck.
This is a bit more effective, but life gain cards still start creeping in at around 25% inclusion.
What works best is the combined approach: if I filter for decks that include Thalia and exclude Soul Warden, life gain is basically obliterated from the page, with Well of Lost Dreams and two Sanguine Bond effects being the only life gain payoffs remaining on the page.
This also still leaves a healthy 550 decks since I didn’t need to throw a whole bunch of cards into my filters in order to get the desired results.
You can do something similar with price.
If you’re looking for a budget deck and a base EDHREC page is helpful aside from the cards being too pricey, you could exclude an expensive card and include something cheap like a solid budget land; suddenly, the cards on the page will be much more in your budget.

It becomes more clear just how important filtering is when you think about an EDHREC page as a cross-section of a handful of different overlapping decks.
Some commanders will be pretty straightforward and less diverse.
If we go to the Prosper EDHREC page, there are almost 30 non-land cards that show up in more than 50% of Prosper decks.
Meanwhile, if we look at a similarly popular but more open-ended commander, Alora, Artful Provocateur, that number is less than half of that, and some commanders like Glissa Sunslayer have almost no cards above the 50% played threshold.
For commanders with a singular, more streamlined play path, the base EDHREC page can be moderately helpful; however, for a more diverse commander, the page might be pure soup, an unfiltered mishmash of different archetypes interspersed across the page.
I actually made a video last year about exactly these sorts of commanders, with Archalos, Francisco/Malcolm, and Skeleton Ship as examples.
The EDHREC pages of these commanders are equal parts interesting to dig into and instructive for seeing the limits of an unfiltered EDHREC page.
I’m also very interested in what causes a commander’s EDHREC page to be more diverse or less diverse, so I actually ended up working with my brother to analyze a whole bunch of EDHREC pages, and we made a whole video about it over on my second channel, complete with a tool to analyze your own commanders.
I’ll include a preview of that at the end.
Regardless of what exact commanders you’re using, filters are an important thing to have in your repertoire if you’re using EDHREC.

#14
DeckbuildingJul 2025355K views

Building EDH Decks with Counterweight Commanders

The Strange Method for Building EDH Decks that I Can't Stop Using
We teach the concept of counterweight commanders, which offset a deck's weaknesses rather than directly synergizing with its theme.By interrogating what the command zone truly provides, builders can design decks where commander and 99 have a mutualistic relationship.Examples like Rada, Glissa, and Liesa show how this approach mitigates downsides such as lack of early plays or vulnerability to aggression.
Use this in your deckIdentify your deck's primary weakness and choose a commander that offsets it, rather than one that simply synergizes with its theme.
DiscardComboRampDeck ConsistencyCurveSynergyDesign IntentDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • A counterweight commander serves to cover the weaknesses of a given deck instead of directly synergizing with its cards.
Evidence
  • Many players only view commanders as enablers or payoffs for themes, missing the broader strategic potential of the command zone.
  • The concept emerges from rigorously interrogating what the command zone offers in exact detail.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Cut low-cost ramp from the 99 if your commander can consistently provide it, as with Rada enabling a cascade deck with only four-drop or higher spells.
  • Use a low-cost deathtouch commander like Glissa to offer early blocking and draw, allowing the rest of the deck to focus on high-mana-value battlecruiser cards.
  • Pair a commander that efficiently depletes life totals with a 99 full of hatebears to close games in a slowed-down environment.
Example
  • Rada: guarantees turn-two ramp, permitting a deck with no cards below four mana and strengthening cascade effects.
  • Glissa: provides first strike, deathtouch, and card draw, making her an ideal blocker and early play for a greedy late-game deck.
  • Liesa: serves as a resilient, damage-dealing commander alongside hatebears that hinder opponents and slow the game.
More quotes
  1. A counterweight commander is a commander that serves to cover the weaknesses of a given deck.
  2. Almost all popular commanders fall into one of two categories: they're an enabler for a particular thing, or more commonly, they're a payoff for a particular thing.
  3. A lot of players internalize this as the way to build Commander decks, but this actually misses a lot of the possibility space of the format.
  4. The idea of a counterweight commander emerges naturally from the process of rigorously interrogating what in exact detail your command zone is offering you.
  5. If we think about a commander deck as a stool, I basically removed one leg from that stool and then shoved my commander under there as a replacement.
  6. By fully covering my low-cost ramp, R allows me to have nothing below four mana in the 99, thereby strengthening the cascade cards.
  7. When [my commander] dies early I shrug, garner a bit of table sympathy, and then proceed normally starting on turn four.
  8. My Glissa deck is a pile of generic dudes, my R deck is a cascade flavored pile of generic dudes, my Liesa deck is a lower curved and somewhat staxy pile of generic dudes, and if you squint around the interesting bits my Malfegor deck is also kind of a pile of generic dudes.
  9. The counterweight commander method is all about rejecting direct synergy with the commander in favor of a game plan that meshes with the commander indirectly.
  10. Without that direct synergy relationship, it is easy to end up grabbing best-in-class cards rather than ones that perform a specific dynamic or synergistic game plan.
Full transcript

In my video about my Malagor deck, I introduced the concept of "counterweight commanders"; these are commanders that, rather than directly synergizing with the cards in a deck, instead offset a weakness of the deck.
That concept, as applied to that deck, went roughly as follows.
I knew I wanted a deck that focused on grindy, low-cost creatures and discard spells.
Grindy, low-cost creatures have the downside that they're small and often easy to block, and discard spells have the downside that opponents can choose to play their most valuable creatures as fast as possible to avoid losing them to discard.
By selecting Malagor as the commander, the deck always has access to a tool that it can use to clear out opposing boards, mitigating the downsides of the cards in the 99.
The cards in the 99 likewise nullify Malagor's weakness of making me hellbent by making opponents lose cards as well and by providing ways of generating value that stick around after my hand is gone.
So here we have a deck where the commander isn't directly related to what the deck does, but instead has a mutualistic relationship with the deck, meshing together with it to form a cohesive whole that benefits both commander and 99.

Now, this is undoubtedly the most elegant version of a "counterweight commander" among my decks, but in retrospect I think that fact almost serves to obscure the concept a bit.
A "counterweight commander" can look like that, but it can also look like a lot of other things.
So, seeing as how this is a subject that is fundamentally related to my deck building, and how I haven't dedicated a video to it outside of a little five-minute thing in July of last year, I'm going to give the concept a full discussion.
I'm going to discuss the conceptual roots of it, give some additional examples, talk about how it fits into the broader EDH ecosystem, and discuss some of the limitations of this style of deck building.

The Conceptual Roots of Counterweight Commanders

To start with, let's return to the definition I gave earlier: a "counterweight commander" is a commander that serves to cover the weaknesses of a given deck.
It's easy to imagine that this is a revolutionary innovation that I came up with and have been employing under the radar for years before finally now spilling my secrets; however, the truth is much more mundane.
Fundamentally, the idea of a "counterweight commander" emerges naturally from the process of rigorously interrogating what, in exact detail, your command zone is offering you in a given deck.
As Commander players, it's easy to slip into a mode of existence where we forget the rules of the format, or rather, where we calcify a specific understanding of the commander format in our brains.
Almost all popular commanders fall into one of two categories: they're an enabler for a particular thing, or, more commonly, they're a payoff for a particular thing.
Decks are supposed to have one or more themes and then a commander that helps out with one of those themes; a lot of players internalize this as the way to build Commander decks, but this actually misses a lot of the possibility space of the format.

Let's think about the Commander format as somebody who has never heard of it before and is reading the rules of the format.
You have a 99-card deck with only one of any given card, on the surface, not great for purposes of consistency compared to a 1v1 format.
However, this is bolstered by an additional card that you always have access to, an eighth card in hand effectively.
This makes the command zone a critical resource for any player looking to maintain a level of consistency in their deck.
One way to utilize this is the ever-popular payoff card: you can run Isshin as your commander to enhance the effectiveness of your on-attack triggers, or Wilhelt to increase the efficiency of your zombies.
These are reasonable, as they allow a deck builder to focus most of their effort on these sorts of cards, knowing that even if they're a little subpar alone, these cards are guaranteed to have a little boost as long as the commander sticks around.
However, there are lots of other things that a player might want to guarantee, and other ways that guaranteed things can mesh into a game plan.

A Meticulous Command Zone: Francisco and Malcolm

My high-power Francisco and Malcolm deck is one that tries to land a one-drop pirate turn one, Francisco turn two, and Malcolm turn three, and then uses Francisco's filtering and Malcolm's treasures to curve into a turn-five or -six infinite-turns combo with Time Sieve.
Now, this is not an example of a "counterweight commander" and could even be considered a conventional engine commander: my command zone generates resources given the input of a key card type performing a specific action (pirates dealing damage to players).
However, this is undoubtedly a more intentional command zone than your typical build of something like Admiral Beckett Brass, even as it's similar on paper.
Francisco guarantees that I have a two-drop available every single game, and Malcolm is a key piece of the deck's combo and also guarantees a source of available mana for my thirteen counterspells even when my lands are tapped out.
As I said, this isn't an example of a "counterweight commander," but it involves a similarly meticulous focus on what the command zone offers a player.

Radha, the Pure Counterweight

For a similarly curve-out oriented example, my Radha deck has the goal of playing Radha, Heir to Keld on turn two as a mana dork and explosive-vegetation analog, on turn three a stream of cascade spells, and six- to seven-cost bombs from turn four onward.
By fully covering my low-cost ramp, Radha allows me to have nothing below four mana in the 99, thereby strengthening the cascade cards and allowing for extremely high top-deck quality even with a land count of 41.
Now, this actually is an example of a "counterweight commander," and a wonderfully simple one at that.
I identified that a turn-two Radha in the command zone could allow me to eliminate the normally space-hogging element of low-cost ramp in a big-mana deck without losing too much speed, and then the rest of the deck was constructed to further take advantage of that choice.
If we think about a commander deck as a stool, I basically removed one leg from that stool and then shoved my commander under there as a replacement, making additions from there with the fantastic fit and wood savings I'd achieved.
Curve-out is almost always a factor in my commander deck building choices, but it's not always quite as dominant a factor as it is in my Radha deck.

Glissa Sunslayer: Mitigating Battle Cruiser Weaknesses

For a now somewhat deprecated example of a "counterweight commander," let's talk about my Glissa Sunslayer deck.
Glissa Sunslayer is a three-mana 3/3 deathtouch first strike that allows its controller to choose between drawing a card, destroying an enchantment, and removing three counters from something whenever it hits a player.
When I first saw this card, I noticed two things about it: it can offer a stream of draw, and it's a really good blocker.
What sort of deck makes use of a low-cost creature with that sort of profile?
A greedy battle cruiser deck, of course.
A big-mana deck can benefit from having an early utility play guaranteed, as this makes it possible for a deck to focus more of its space on higher-costed cards while still having early plays.
In addition, the first strike/deathtouch pairing makes Glissa a fantastic blocker, allowing the deck to ward off early aggression, a thing that might otherwise cause problems for a greedy late-game deck.
I eventually decided that my Radha deck scratches the same itches as this one more effectively, but it's nonetheless useful as a case study for weakness mitigation.

Liesa, Shroud of Dusk: A Clock in a Hate Bear Shell

As another example, my Liesa deck pairs a commander who is good at deleting opposing life totals with a 99 made up of lower-cost hate bears and utility creatures.
The deck's hate bears hinder opposing game plans and slow down the pace of games, and Liesa is here to be a reliable way of cutting down life totals in that slowed environment since she's a great beater that is effectively immune from commander attacks.
In both of these cases, the commander's mana value is important, and they're often played when that much mana is available, but they can be pushed back a bit if the deck has a better play.

Overall, this counterweight style of commander choice is a really fun one; it provides mechanically textured gameplay, and it also scratches my hipster itches since it means that my decks are almost always different from opposing decks.
However, this isn't a perfect method and indeed can lead to some problems.

The Pitfalls of the Counterweight Method

One problem that can arise is that when the commander is one of very few cards to play a particular role in that deck, that deck's functionality can be greatly hindered if the commander becomes unavailable.
One example of this is a Torsten deck I built several months back.
It's a deck that's entirely creatures and lands, and which uses playing and blinking Torsten as its only draw, effectively drawing seven cards per Torsten entry.
Upsides: it packs a lot of draw into just a handful of deck slots, allowing space for a ton of ramp to guarantee fast rollouts.
Downsides: oh boy, if this deck draws poorly or gets its board wiped too early, it can sit around twiddling its thumbs for multiple turns on end with all of its card draw behind an imposing seven- or nine-mana barrier.
When this deck works, it really works, but the play pattern can just as often be jarring and awkward.
So this is an issue that can come up with this sort of deck building, but for it to cause problems as it did in this case, two things really need to be true.
For one, the deck needs to have no meaningful backup quantity of the thing the commander is providing.
My Malagor deck would really struggle if it didn't have a way to clear out opposing boards, but it runs a decent removal suite in addition to the commander for that exact reason, including a handful of small-creature wipes intended to hinder token players who might otherwise get out of hand.
By contrast, the Torsten deck has literally no draw outside of the command zone due to how tight space in the deck is, unless you count Savala as draw.
Secondly, the thing the commander is providing needs to be a key requirement for the long-term functioning of the deck in order for there to be a problem.
My Radha deck has absolutely no low-cost ramp aside from Radha herself, and as some people have pointed out, the commander getting removed immediately does cause me to basically skip my turn three; but this rarely happens because it's not actually a particularly good usage of removal.
This is an inevitability deck, not a tempo deck, and a one-turn setback for a slower deck just isn't that big a deal in mid-power EDH.
When Radha dies early, I shrug, garner a bit of table sympathy, and then proceed normally starting on turn four.
By contrast, my Torsten deck requires constant access to him, and any barrier to utilizing him stonewalls the deck's game plan.

The other potential issue with "counterweight commanders" is that it can lead to decks that some might find a bit bland in terms of card choice.
As you might have noticed earlier, I actually titled my original video on this topic "A Weird Trick to Making Cool Midrange Decks," which certainly indicates something.
It is true that a lot of decks do end up moving somewhat in this direction: my Glissa deck is a pile of generic dudes, my Radha deck is a cascade-flavored pile of generic dudes, my Liesa deck is a lower-curved and somewhat staxy pile of generic dudes, and if you squint around the interesting bits, my Malagor deck is also kind of a pile of generic dudes.
This fundamentally comes down to the nature of the strategy at its core.
The "counterweight commander" method is all about rejecting direct synergy with the commander in favor of a game plan that meshes with the commander indirectly.
In EDH broadly, the commander of a deck will often be a central node to begin building the deck's synergies off of, so without that direct synergy relationship it's easy to end up grabbing best-in-class cards rather than ones that perform a specific dynamic or synergistic game plan.
This doesn't mean that all decks with this method need to lack synergy; my Malagor deck has a bit of direct synergy with self-discard and some other synergy packages that relate to the sorts of game states that my demon dragon friend promotes, and my Radha deck likewise features a decent amount of cascade as a natural offshoot from having a commander that allows me to run very few low-cost cards.
It's fully possible to make interesting, synergistic decks within the bounds of this method, but it is undoubtedly more difficult, and I consider finding interesting dynamics and card choices within the "counterweight commander" framework to be one of the fun challenges of the method.

A Final Example: Kambal, Consul of Allocation

To cap things off, I'm going to give one more example of a "counterweight commander" deck that is something of a deviation from the midrange framework, and, actually, it was the first one I ever built.
In 2019, I built a $25 budget combo-control Orzhov deck with Kambal, Consul of Allocation as the commander.
Unlike a lot of common control commanders, Kambal doesn't help generate value or control the board; rather, he's simply a three-mana 2/3 that drains an opponent for two points of life whenever they cast a noncreature spell.
So what's he here for?
Well, primarily life gain, which Kambal guarantees as a near-constant source.
Now, life gain isn't a particularly useful thing on its own.
Life totals are a key factor for winning the game, yes, but tempo and value tend to be more important contributors to the winning and losing of games, particularly in a format like Commander where life totals are high by default.
Nor is this a deck with much in the way of life-gain synergies or effects that trigger when life is gained, so where does life gain fit in here?
Well, for one, control decks tend to be weak to life-total pressure, so this helps with that, but life gain also helps control decks in another way.
See, in a deck with a lot of board wipes, opponents will deploy creatures in tactical numbers.
If a control player is at 28 life, a midrange player might choose to drop maybe 10 or 15 power worth of creatures on board, knowing that number could threaten a control player's life total without requiring the control player to spend a card to answer it.
In this context, life gain makes it safe to ignore those threats, accumulating advantage through other means.
That's exactly the role Kambal plays, making him a quintessential "counterweight commander" that shores up a fundamental vulnerability of the control strategy.

#15
Meta CommentaryJul 2025316K views

What Commander Players Miss from Other Formats

The Lessons that EDH-only Players Don't Learn
We argue that the decline of 60-card casual play leaves Commander players unprepared for grindy, low-life-total games.It claims that experience in other formats improves life total management, blocking discipline, and handling resource scarcity.We also caution against rigid deckbuilding templates, suggesting flexible adoption based on a deck's plan.Venturing outside EDH can thus sharpen a player's skills.
Use this in your deckDetermine your ramp count by testing how often your deck needs an early ramp spell, not by a fixed number like 8-10.
MidrangeRampDeck ConsistencyThreat AssessmentMeta TrendsFormat HealthStrategy
Principle
  • Playing 20-life 1v1 formats teaches players to treat life total as a resource, a skill that Commander's high life totals and explosive win conditions often fail to develop.
Evidence
  • Commander players frequently face win conditions that reduce life totals from full to zero in one attack, causing them to undervalue incremental life loss.
  • In grindy games where resources are scarce, primarily Commander players tend to view the situation as a lost cause, whereas players with 60-card experience remain vigilant and seek marginal advantages.
  • The habit of blocking with useful creatures is rarely punished in Commander but becomes critical in low-life formats, leading Commander-only players to instinctively avoid necessary blocks.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Deckbuilding templates for ramp and draw are often treated as universal in Commander, but 60-card formats demonstrate that ramp should be included only when a deck's plan demands it, encouraging more flexible construction.
Limitation
  • These skills can still be developed within Commander, but because resource-scarce games occur less frequently, it takes significantly more playtime to internalize them.
Example
  • A Commander player might keep a valuable creature back instead of blocking an attack that leaves them at 3 life, not realizing that the next unblockable hit will kill them.
More quotes
  1. EDH has effectively become the casual way to play paper Magic; the days of 60-card kitchen table gaming are in the past, replaced by a diverging path for newer players.
  2. Commander as a format clearly isn't going anywhere in the casual scene, so it's pointless to try and fight against.
  3. Commander players are often less prepared for grindier games where every point of life total matters.
  4. Life total is a resource, and players of 20-life formats learn when to spend and when to save.
  5. In formats outside Commander, a player must ask themselves why this ramp spell is here, and without a clear answer it gets cut.
  6. Many Commander deck builders add ramp staples without considering what the cards actually accomplish.
  7. I play ramp because I want more mana, come on now.
  8. The step of asking how much mana a deck actually needs and when it will want to play ramp spells is one that a lot of players forget to complete.
  9. One other deck building skill that EDH isn't great at teaching players is figuring out optimal card quantities.
  10. Singleton formats outsource a lot of decisionmaking about card quantities to the nature of the format.
Full transcript
The Shift to Casual Play and the Questions It Raises

I made a comment in July that EDH has effectively become the casual way to play paper Magic, and it really has; the days of 60-card kitchen table gaming are in the past, replaced by a diverging path for newer players: either play Arena and get your one‑on‑one fix from the comfort of your couch, or meet up with three other people and play Commander.
The nostalgic part of me certainly wants to push back on this, and I’ve spoken before about some of the reasons why Commander perhaps isn’t the best on‑ramp for newer players, but there are also some clear upsides to Commander.
The fact that it’s a casual format by designation, rather than being casual gameplay on the fringes of competitive formats, means a much greater degree of stability than something like 60‑card kitchen table Magic, a format whose current form basically vanishes off the face of the Earth whenever the friends who want to play it with you stop wanting to play it.
More importantly, Commander as a format clearly isn’t going anywhere in the casual scene, so it’s pointless to try and fight against it.
That said, the collapse of 60‑card casual Magic, along with the slow decline in popularity of draft that I’ve noticed over the last ten years, have had me thinking: what are we losing exactly?
What impact is it having that there is a growing population of Commander players who have played little to none of other formats?
To be clear, this isn’t intended as old man grumbling, I can certainly get old man grumbly sometimes as somebody who has been playing Magic for more than half of my life, and maybe I’d be more old man grumbly if I were a Modern player or something, but no, this is a genuine question.
To get some insight into it, I’m going to answer a slightly different set of questions here, : What is the experience of playing formats that aren’t Commander, and how does that experience upon a player?
What impact might that sort of experience have on the way they play Commander?
And do these impacts make it worth venturing outside EDH from time to time for us primarily EDH players?

The Grindy, Low‑Slung Nature of 20‑Life Formats

To start with, let’s talk about the gameplay experience of a 20‑life 1v1 format.
A defining feature of these formats, as compared to Commander, is that they’re low‑slung and grindy, with smaller threats being more relevant.
With this fact in mind, what I’m about to say may initially seem a bit confusing: playing these formats can directly help with building the skills required for playing Commander.
Commander and 20‑life formats certainly are different, but counterintuitively this is exactly why it can be helpful to have played the latter; it can give a player breadth of experience.
See, playing Commander gives players lots of experience in fighting against big, bombastic wincons.
For starters, you’ve got the obvious examples like combo decks and combo‑adjacent plays, like a massive X‑spell or a Craterhoof on a board full of tokens, plays that reduce one or more life totals to zero in one fell swoop and often do so with large excesses of damage.
Even outside of these, life total can frequently feel irrelevant in EDH regardless of whether it is or not.
Even with a conventional big‑creatures mid‑range deck without a splashy singular finisher, a lot of times most damage will be dealt in one or two attack swings; this comes down to the snowbally nature of a lot of EDH decks.
All sorts of cards are intended to push a deck to greater and greater volumes of gameplay, whether that be effect doublers or mana doublers or other forms of action‑compounding engines.
This often means that decks buckle down until they have a massive board of creatures to attack with before swinging wide, sometimes for entire life totals in one go, somebody might have to count out 35 damage to this player, 29 damage to that player, and so on, and under that context opponents don’t even really need to think about their life total, regardless of if a handful of points of difference would have been relevant or not.
Like, come on, you lost because the opponent popped off, simple as.

However, what this means is that Commander players are often less prepared for grindier games where every point of life total matters.
The results of this can be seen when it comes to blocking and life total management.
Players who are more experienced in formats where life totals are relevant will be much more careful of certain life total thresholds and more willing to block to stay above them.
Players of 20‑life formats learn to internalize the idea that “life total is a resource” and to know when to spend and when to save that resource, but Commander players who have spent a lot of time dealing with bigger one‑ or two‑shot wincons won’t internalize this lesson nearly as quickly.
EDH’s higher life totals also train players to mostly disregard the idea of blocking with useful creatures because it feels better to keep them around, and most of the time that’s the correct decision in terms of strategy as well; unlike in a 20‑life format, where blocking with a key creature to stay alive for an extra turn or two is a very common necessity that players get used to biting the bullet on, this isn’t necessary nearly as often in EDH due to higher life totals and heftier wincons.
This means that when it becomes a necessity, a lot of players instinctually refuse to do it, even when it means sacrificing an amount of life that will almost certainly lose them the game.

The Value of Scraping Through Low‑Resource Environments

Another aspect of non‑Commander gameplay, and particularly limited formats, is low‑resource environments, where players are topdecking or working under tight constraints in some other way.
These games aren’t wildly common in Commander due to how common ramp and draw are, with variation by playgroup obviously, and this means that you need to play a lot more EDH to get comfortable under these circumstances than you would to get comfortable under more common EDH scenarios.
The results of this can be quite problematic.
When a game has involved some form of stacks or heavy attrition and resources are dwindling, you can usually tell who has played other formats and who hasn’t.
A lot of primarily EDH players will be looking at the situation as a lost cause, a game gone wildly off the rails, whereas players who have done their time in the trenches of limited or 60‑card formats will be more engaged and vigilant, looking for outs and bits of incremental advantage to grab on to.

Rethinking Ramp and Other "Necessities" in Deck Building

Outside of gameplay, the experience of building 60‑card decks can help with EDH deck building as well.
One way this can manifest is when building decks that are exceptions to traditional norms of EDH deck building.
See, people tend to have rough templates they follow when they build Commander decks, myself included.
A low double‑digit number of draw spells is good for maintaining a consistent stream of cards turn over turn, maybe more if those draw spells are smaller or less if the deck has other ways to compound value or tutors to find its value engines.
Likewise, a low double‑digits amount of ramp will be good for a deck that wants to have a single piece of ramp most games but is fine occasionally not having one, and adjustments will be made up or down depending on how often a deck wants ramp, what size of ramp a deck wants, and how aggressively a player is willing to mulligan.
These are references I follow, and I like them as a starting point because they’re flexible; they prescribe numbers of cards based on what a deck wants to do and what other tools a deck has available to it.
However, the trouble comes when references like these are less flexible and when they’re treated as gospel regardless of what deck they’re being applied to.

Ramp is a great example for demonstrating this because it’s one of the biggest differences between EDH and other formats.
Basically all EDH decks have some amount of ramp in them, and most have a decent amount; eight to ten slots spent on ramp is common.
By contrast, most 60‑card decks do not.
You will get specific decks focused on high‑cost plays, such as Tron, that focus a lot of deck space on ramping, and you’ll get mid‑range decks that run a spattering of efficient dorks to hit their impactful three‑, four‑, and five‑drops a turn early in some games in order to gain a tempo advantage.
Broadly, though, “ramp” isn’t widely played in 60‑card formats because it often isn’t worthwhile to expend a card just for a bit of acceleration.
This is likewise true in limited; I’ll run an efficient dork like a Lir Elves, and I’ll run a card like Dance of the Tumble Weeds or Poison Dart Frog that has some extra utility attached at a good rate, and occasionally I’ll add some lesser ramp if I have critical high‑cost plays that my game plan ended up needing to revolve around.
Aside from these instances, though, it’s almost never correct to play ramp in draft or sealed; draw is usually sparse, and mana and cards are better spent contesting the board and threatening life totals.
The result of all of this is that in formats outside Commander, ramp spells are viewed very critically.
A player must ask themselves, “Why is this here?” and if that question doesn’t have a clear and persuasive answer, the card will generally get cut.
By contrast, many people building a Commander deck will throw together their template‑built EDH deck with the requisite count of ramp cards picked from among the available staples.
These will often be added without consideration for what the cards are accomplishing.
You’ll get Cultivate in two‑color decks with a four‑mana commander, Gilded Lotus in decks with an average mana value of 2.5, Rampant Growth in an artifact‑centered deck with a three‑mana commander, and so on.
Fundamentally, the issue is that a lot of players don’t stop to consider what “ramp” is offering them because they’re playing in a format where it is an unquestionable fact of life: “I play ramp because I want more mana, come on now.” In most decks this won’t be the end of the world; decks do want ramp spells, and even if players add ramp that is a bit sub‑optimal for their deck, their deck is still spending slots on a thing that it needs, and the specifics can be corrected later.
However, just as there’s variety in how much ramp 60‑card decks want, there will be some Commander decks that don’t really want ramp in any sort of conventional quantities.
I have two different decks where this is the case.
In both my Malagor deck and my Francisco Malcolm deck, I don’t want to spend the entirety of my turn two or three playing ramp spells because I have more useful cards I want to be developing.
Both decks include a handful of rocks for purposes of weaving into a later turn, because this is still EDH we’re talking about, and getting a future mana boost off of a one‑mana investment is still useful for keeping up in later turns, but these aren’t really a key card type, and each deck only runs a low single‑digits quantity of two‑mana rocks so as not to draw more than one in the early stages of a game, plus a Sol Ring to cash in on my Wizards of the Coast‑mandated dose of variance.
The step of asking how much mana a deck actually needs and what times it will want to play ramp spells is one that a lot of players forget to complete, and playing other formats where the inclusion of ramp isn’t such a foregone conclusion is great for getting perspective on what these cards are actually accomplishing in a deck.

The same can go for other card types.
When is it correct to exclude or go light on card draw?
When is it correct to exclude board wipes?
These are things that EDH players will consider necessities, but depending on the deck they might not be nearly as important as a player might assume.

Singleton Constraints and Learning Optimal Card Quantities

One other deck building skill that EDH isn’t great at teaching players is figuring out optimal card quantities.
A feature of Singleton formats is that they restrict how many of a particular sort of card a player can run; some cards will have many similar options, but other types of card will be more unique, and a player will have no choice but to add the number of it that exist to the deck and no more, regardless of how much they like it.
This outsources a lot of decision‑making to the nature of the format.
Players don’t get to choose how many cards to add to their deck that create additional dragons when a dragon attacks; that decision is already made for them.
Whereas in 60‑card formats up to four of a given card can be run, giving players room to flex counts up and down on various cards and see how they feel, in EDH there often isn’t this same level of choice.
The result of this is that when players have access to a greater number of a particularly appealing effect, if they’ve played EDH but not other formats, the instinct might be to load up on all available copies of that effect regardless of how many would actually be optimal for the deck.
This is likely something that will be figured out in due time so long as players are willing to challenge their assumptions about how their decks work from time to time, but having less of an intuitive sense of how many copies of a card is appropriate is certainly something that can lead to wasted time and games played with an off‑kilter deck.

The Cumulative Benefit of Broader Magic Experience

Long story short, yes, playing formats outside EDH can imbue players with important experiences, experiences that will be especially useful on the fringes of the Commander experience.
Commander players will have no trouble getting used to playing against snowbally value piles, dealing with board wipes, building conventional synergy‑based mid‑range, developing a growing knowledge of common staples, and so on.
Playing lots of Commander will make you better at a lot of things in Commander, and playing enough Commander in diverse environments will even grant a degree of proficiency in the things I mentioned earlier: life total management, low‑resource environments, unconventional deck types, and so on.
These certainly aren’t forbidden knowledge or anything, but playing formats outside EDH will help to instill certain lessons faster than others and develop an intuitive sense for why they’re true.
It’s also good to play other formats because cards are designed for other formats and the further back in time

#16
DeckbuildingJul 2025239K views

Why Some Good Cards Are Wrong for Casual Decks

Stop Shortcutting the Fun Part of your Deck
In casual Commander, not every powerful card belongs in your deck.Adding cards that generate easy resources or simplify decision-making can bypass the engaging game states you intentionally built around.Consider whether a card enhances or short-circuits the experiences you enjoy, and be willing to cut even strong cards that undermine your deck's core identity.
Use this in your deckCut any card that simplifies your deck's engaging decision-making or resource management into a single, automatic action.
DiscardControlMidrangeCard EvaluationDesign IntentDeckbuilding RestrictionsDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • Casual decks are intentionally not perfectly optimal, forgoing powerful cards to preserve a desired play experience.
  • Some cards can diminish fun by shortcutting the engaging decision-making or resource tension your deck is built around, even if they don't directly win games.
Evidence
  • In a Malakir discard deck, Waste Not was excluded because it generated effortless resources, removing the gritty low-resource struggle that made the deck enjoyable.
  • In a cycling deck, Monument to Endurance was excluded because it reduced a complex choice-intensive engine to a simple cycle to draw loop, trivializing tutoring and resource decisions.
  • Draw doublers like Alhammarret's Archive and Teferi's Insight were also cut from the cycling deck for similarly automating value generation.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Evaluate cards based on whether they enhance or undermine the interactive decisions and resource scarcity your deck intentionally creates.
  • Be willing to cut even highly synergistic cards if they eliminate the need for the tricky, engaging choices central to your deck's identity.
Limitation
  • Not all powerful cards are problematic; cards like Cacophodon can align with and bolster the intended play style without shortcutting the fun.
More quotes
  1. What you find fun about playing your deck may be a lot more fragile than you think, much more sensitive to the blunt instrument of an out-of-place card or two than you'd expect.
  2. A deck is casual when it is not perfectly optimal in its ability to perform tasks and win games, and when this state of being is intentional.
  3. Plenty of casual EDH players understand that they'd prefer to win with Purphoros and a bunch of goblins rather than Dualcaster plus Twinflame.
  4. The fun part of the deck for me is when I'm clawing for advantage in a scrappy game, and if a two mana enchantment just hands me a bunch of advantage, it shortcuts.
  5. [With Waste Not,] it basically just turns my deck into a generic oppressive Stax deck in the process.
  6. The issue with Monument to Endurance is that it shortcuts around this whole thing. Its ability doesn't compete with anything else in the deck and the correct first choice per turn will almost always be card draw.
  7. Suddenly the task of gaining advantage has one simple route of administration: find the card that makes my cycling cards do more cycling, play it, and then the advantage makes itself.
  8. These generic do thing get card synergies would suck the identity and a lot of the interesting decision making out of the deck, thereby removing a lot of what makes it fun to me.
  9. If you find the process of building advantage a particularly fun one, you'll want to be careful with the cards you add.
  10. I like playing the role of a final boss with this deck, pumping my volume of gameplay up to seismic proportions before lumbering in the direction of victory with my large creatures.
Full transcript
When Is a Good Card the Wrong Choice?

I have a question for you: let’s say that there’s a card that by most considerations is a great fit for a deck; it’s pretty powerful, it meshes well with the way the deck functions mechanically, and it improves a specific aspect of the deck that was maybe a bit of a weak spot before.
When is putting that card into that deck the wrong choice?

Casual Means Imperfect by Choice

Let’s take a step back.
Commander, as it is played by most of its player base, is a casual format.
This means a lot of things to a lot of people, but the definition I’ll use here is that a deck is casual when it is not perfectly optimal in terms of its ability to perform tasks and win games and when this state of being is intentional.
If you are a casual EDH player, by definition there are cards in your deck that could be switched to different cards if you wanted to win more games and compete with more powerful decks.
This is intuitively true to people when it comes to the cards that most visibly contribute to that winning: the win conditions.
Not every deck includes the most powerful two-card infinite combos in them, and even among the ones that do, not all of them run the sort of tutor density that would help their deck to execute said combos consistently.
Plenty of casual EDH players understand that they’d prefer to win with Purphoros and a bunch of goblins rather than Dualcaster Mage plus Twinflame, and another segment of casual EDH players understand that they’d prefer to win by attacking with the aforementioned goblins rather than dealing damage just by having them pop into existence.
So this is an area where players tend to understand their preferences.

However, as you pull back away from what shape a deck’s win will look like and dive into the functional meat of decks, things get a little hazier.
You may know approximately how you enjoy winning, but do you know what forms of resource building are most enjoyable to you?
Do you know the sort of interaction suite and style of table engagement that will give you the most satisfying game?
Do you know how powerful you want each of these components to be?
This is likely an overwhelming sequence of questions to a lot of people, or even a baffling sequence of questions to some.
This is a card game where you’re at least in some accents trying to win games, and the natural place a lot of people end up is finding the most powerful options out there or at least playing with the best options they have access to.
And by all means, don’t let me tell you not to play good cards; I play lots of good cards.
However, I am also here to tell you that there are some good cards that, like a Dualcaster Twinflame combo, may influence the amount of fun you have with the game, cards that may not even necessarily have anything to do directly with winning.
What you find fun about playing your deck may be a lot more fragile than you think, much more sensitive to the blunt instrument of an out-of-place card or two than you’d expect.
If this all sounds like it’s gearing up for a bunch of aimless good-card hate, well, why don’t you just hear me out for a bit.
This is all pretty abstract, so a couple recent examples of this I’ve experienced are probably in order.

Quick side note: I also released a Patron video recently that included some discussion of the new bracket system, and I’ll be including a portion of that at the end.
Stick around for that if you feel like, but with that said, I want to start off with what may be a familiar example.

First Example: The Malfegor Grind

In October of last year I discussed my Malfegor deck, and that one is a Vibes deck through and through.
The basic gist is that it is a deck that pairs discard spells and small creatures to generate card advantage to try and force the game into a scrappy, low-resource state reminiscent of a lot of grindy 60- and 40-card gameplay, a state where my deck has an edge but typically only a slight one.
A gritty game plan, no doubt, and you’re allowed to hate it, but I love the sorts of environments this deck can create, and I likewise enjoy the task of grinding advantage with a handful of low-cost creatures within those environments.
With that in mind, a useful exercise for understanding what this deck wants is to consider two powerful cards: one that ended up in the final deck and one that did not.
Kroxa is a monster of a card; it’s an effective discard engine that can give opponents a whiff of the pain to come as early as turn two, and which also makes for a killer beater that is difficult to get rid of.
It is very good at doing everything this deck wants to be good at: making opponents discard, being resilient, and applying life total pressure, and that congruence is why it works in the final deck despite being a crazy good card in a low-budget deck.
Waste Not, on the other hand, is a card that did not end up in the final deck, and that’s because it is good at things the deck is not supposed to be good at.
This is a grindy midrange deck looking to plunge the game into a low-resource slugfest best, and Waste Not handing me easy resources boosts me out of that state, changing the whole dynamic of the deck.
The fun part of the deck for me is when I’m clawing for advantage in a scrappy game, and if a two-mana enchantment just hands me a bunch of advantage, it shortcuts that, basically just turning my deck into a generic impressive Stacks deck in the process.
This was the example of a good card being a bad idea that got me thinking harder about the concept, but there’s a similar and even more striking recent example that ended up inspiring this piece.

Second Example: The Shorikai Astral Slide Deck

In mid January I was looking at spoilers for Aetherdrift, seeing if there was anything new for my Shorikai Astral Slide deck, and I saw the card Monument to Endurance.
On the surface this thing is a slam dunk in a cycling deck; cycled cards get effortlessly transfigured into resources at the rate of one per turn, allowing for an easy card advantage engine that can pivot into a win condition and which can even provide Treasures where needed.
Can you believe all of that could come from a single Magic card?
It’s almost too good to be true; actually, not almost, it is too good to be true in this case.
I discussed in my video about this deck that at its heart this is a control deck that cares about navigating a sequence of tricky choices about where to divert the deck’s resources: do I tutor for a high-cost artifact that gives me card advantage or one that slows down my opponents?
Do I flicker an ETB creature that shuts down a problematic spell or ability or one that makes my board larger?
Do I use Shorikai’s recur ability to bring back a key blocker or a card that pushes my own advantage?
These are the sorts of choices that make this deck a highly fun and engaging experience for me when I’m looking to sit down and think hard during a game.
The issue with Monument to Endurance then is that it shortcuts around this whole thing; its ability doesn’t compete with anything else in the deck, and the correct first choice per turn will almost always be card draw.
Suddenly easily the most effective draw engine in my deck isn’t a multi-card flicker-based engine that I need to piece together and then operate with lots of decisions along the way; no, this is a “do thing, get card” engine where taking a game action, cycling in this case, is immediately and directly rewarded with drawing cards.
This simplification of value generation decisions has a cascading effect of simplifying my tutoring decisions; it will almost always be correct to hunt up a one-piece engine like this, so my tutoring decisions will reflect that.
I’ll wizard cycle for a Trophy Mage to grab my Monument to Endurance because anything else would be an irresponsible choice.
Suddenly the task of gaining advantage has one simple route of administration: find the card that makes my cycling cards do more cycling, play it, and then the advantage makes itself.
There are two other cards I exclude for similar reasons: the draw doublers Alhammarret’s Archive and Teferi’s Ageless Insight are undoubtedly strong in a cycling deck, but they’re strong by shortcutting the value-generating part of the deck, allowing me to get ahead by simply cycling rather than making use of Astral Slide and ETB creatures.
These generic “do thing, get card” synergies would suck the identity and a lot of the interesting decision-making out of the deck, thereby removing a lot of what makes it fun to me.

Not Every Deck Hates “Do Thing, Get Card”

Now you might assume based on what I just said that I hate all draw that’s too generic or simple or powerful, or even that I dislike the sort of “do thing, get card” draw I mentioned earlier.
That’s not the case, though; I dislike it in the case of my Shorikai deck specifically because it shortcuts past what I find fun about the deck, simplifying the rich tapestry of decisions that the deck presents me with and rendering the more interesting forms of value pointlessly weak by comparison.
If you find the process of building advantage a particularly fun one, you’ll want to be careful with the cards you add, but this won’t be the case for all decks.
I run a bunch of these sorts of simple, strong value engines; I mentioned earlier in my R deck Beast Whisperer and Garruk’s Packleader and such like, and those feel great there.
If anything, I wish my draw was even more straightforward sometimes.
Many people who’ve seen me playing this deck have witnessed me exasperatedly resolving my fifth Raggadragga trigger in a turn, playing as quickly as I possibly can in order to keep my turn length under 5 minutes despite casting dozens of mana worth of spells.
This is because exactly how I build advantage isn’t something I care about; there are two things I find fun about playing this deck: having a ton of mana, which enables me to have these intense and complex turns with intricate ordering, and figuring out how to beat down my opponents with a big old pile of budget bombs.
I do run draw in the deck, but I treat it as a means to an end, with that end being allowing me to spend my ample mana and not end up hellbent too much of the time.
In the case of this deck, what would shortcut the fun would be running more powerful bombs and finishers that allow me to convert mana advantage into defeated opponents more efficiently.
I like playing the role of a final boss with this deck, pumping my volume of gameplay up to seismic proportions before lumbering in the direction of victory with my large creatures.
I do have ways to close out games, but they’re not super fast or sudden unless I’m at 20 or more mana, and this gives opponents a fighting chance to claw their way back into the game and best me.
In some games, these big, dynamic back-and-forths are some of my favorite games with the deck, and if I spent a bunch of effort tuning the deck to win faster, I wouldn’t get these sorts of games nearly as often.

Third Example: Prophetic Bolts and Amplifier Permanents

For a similar example, let’s talk about my friend Hans’s Laya undefeated slick shot deck named “Is it Bizit?” This is a control-oriented deck that plays as many Prophetic Bolts as possible, with “Prophetic Bolt” as a category referring to an Izzet card that can both deal damage and draw one or more cards.
When I asked Hans what he finds fun about the deck, he responded simply, “I like to two-for-one people twice.” Prophetic Bolt in a 1v1 format is the archetypal mana-expensive two-for-one card: you pay five mana to hopefully remove a creature with four or less toughness as well as finding a card in the top four cards of your library, thereby generating two cards’ worth of value with your one Magic: The Gathering card.
This type of card feels less impactful in EDH, what with its ample value engines and two additional opponents and such like, but allowing each of these spells to be used an additional time suddenly makes for a much more tempting proposition.
The resulting deck looks to build value while keeping opponents in check, gradually adding additional mana in the form of mana rocks as well as occasional amplifier permanents before trying to overwhelm opponents with value and gradually burn them down.
It was one of these amplifier permanents that ended up being a problem for shortcutting the fun: Thousand-Year Storm.
See, this deck typically keeps around a handful of plotted Izzet instants that were weaved onto an end step, holding them in exile until they’re ready to be cast for free on a later main phase.
This means that with a minimal amount of planning, a Thousand-Year Storm turn could easily reach five or more spells, which could mean that the final couple spells of the turn are being copied several times.
This won’t always be an immediate game win, but it effectively nullifies value as a concept in the deck; the deck is likely adding 10 or more cards to its hand each turn that Thousand-Year Storm is in play and possibly digging dozens of cards deep into its library.
Worse, Thousand-Year Storm negates the concept of mana as well; when a five- or six-cost card can be copied half a dozen times, the progression of adding on mana rocks and amplifiers becomes entirely pointless.
Anything before the Thousand-Year Storm barely matters, and whether or not it results in an imminent game win more or less comes down to a removal check for opponents unless the deck was far behind when the storm came into play.
So Thousand-Year Storm does shortcut the fun in the case of this deck by pushing the deck beyond what it’s supposed to care about: getting two-for-ones.
However, this isn’t to say that all powerful amplifier permanents are off the table; the new Niv-Mizzet printed in Foundations feels shockingly okay here.
It does end up somewhat negating the resource source of cards, but mana is still a big limiting factor for the deck, and not needing to worry nearly as much about value does change the nature of the deck, but the deck still feels like a version of itself and the things that are fun about it still remain fun even if the play pattern becomes a bit different.
The same goes for the card that ended up replacing Thousand-Year Storm: Swarm Intelligence certainly amplifies the next gameplay substantially, but it’s still in the realm of being reasonable; the difference between a two times amplification and a three to four times amplification end

#17
Format TheoryJul 2025238K views

The Homogenization of Commander Decks

The Sluggish Era of Tall Commander
Commander's 40 starting life and casual nature encourage decks to focus on ramping and drawing cards to build large threats.Over time, this has led to homogeneous midrange decks that all rely on similar ramp and draw packages.Social pressures against aggressive or disruptive strategies further entrench this pattern, making the format increasingly sluggish and uniform.
Use this in your deckAsk whether your deck is too focused on setting up its own game plan and missing opportunities to disrupt opponents early.
RampCard DrawMidrangeFormat HealthMeta TrendsDeckbuilding RestrictionsMeta Analysis
Principle
  • The format's 40 starting life makes it necessary for decks to accumulate significant mana and cards to overwhelm multiple opponents.
Evidence
  • Originally, preconstructed decks featured high-cost spells, reflecting the intended slower pace of the format.
  • As powerful draw engines have been printed and deckbuilding has become more streamlined, decks have increasingly focused on ramp and draw as self-amplifying engines.
  • Players often consider aggressive, stax, or combo strategies rude, leading to a social environment that discourages countering tall decks.
  • Even when commanders could handle ramp or draw, decks still include redundant amounts of these effects, showing the depth of the homogenization.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Consider including early aggressive plays or stax pieces to counter opponents who spend turns ramping.
Limitation
  • Social rules against certain strategies limit the format's ability to self-correct, as there is no formal metagame or banlist to keep greedy decks in check.
  • The pressure to let everyone do their thing can lead to games where players are essentially goldfishing, reducing meaningful interaction.
Example
  • Specific cards like Armageddon, Winter Orb, Gaddock Teeg, and poison counters are often socially banned, allowing ramp-heavy decks to go unchecked.
More quotes
  1. A common unspoken rule of Commander is that people should get to have a game plan, to do a thing, to do their thing.
  2. Commander's 40 starting health itself serves as a built-in 'do the thing' enabler.
  3. The funny thing about ramp and draw is that they have an amplifying effect: ramp gives mana to cast more ramp spells, and card draw finds more card draw.
  4. As more and more powerful draw engines have been printed over the last 10 years, along with the EDH developing a more streamlined deck building culture, decks that focus heavily on draw and ramp have become more and more prevalent.
  5. The polite counterplay [to greedy decks] is to go bigger, to get more mana and more cards, and escalate harder.
  6. Commander as a casual format has the awkward dynamic that there isn't a metagame per se, and everything that happens is basically moderated by a giant glob of expectations.
  7. As counter measures become less and less widespread, players will feel more and more emboldened to chase big greedy decks to their logical end point.
  8. The dominance of tall Commander means that players are able to do big expressive plays with a wide degree of freedom.
  9. One issue with the gradual upward race of tall Commander is information overload: EDH boards are wildly complex these days.
  10. Long turns beget long turns: if the game's turns are a sequence of back to back 5 to 10 minute fiddle fests, other people are going to start chatting or checking their phone or gazing off into space.
Full transcript

A common unspoken rule of Commander is that people should get to have a game plan to “do a thing,” to “do their thing.” On the surface, this makes a lot of sense; it’s a casual format and people are here to have fun.
In other casual multiplayer games, universal participation is understood to be a core tenet, and steps are taken to ensure that all players get to play a part.
In a role‑playing game like D&D or Pathfinder, you don’t want to let a player be a wizard only to then have them spend the entire campaign in places with some mysterious anti‑magic field , that’s not ideal gameplay.
So, broadly, it’s understandable that Commander echoes this understanding and even allows players 100 cards’ worth of expression in how they play the game.
This is all fine and good, but what’s easy to forget is that Commander is a multiplayer offshoot of a generally two‑player game, an offshoot that didn’t always exist.

The fact that the format has a higher life total than 60‑card 1v1 formats is intentional; it works to make space for decks that would otherwise struggle.
There are a lot of potential creative strategies that simply can’t hold up in a 20‑health format where attacking people with creatures early is just a bit too good.
In this way, Commander’s 40 starting life itself serves as a built‑in “do the thing” enabler , no extra effort needed from players.
This higher life total was intended to accomplish a task, and it did so admirably; however, over time it has accomplished a lot more than just this.
I’m getting ahead of myself, though.
Commander has basically grown up with this identity of being a slower format, a more laid‑back form of Magic can be played.
This is reflected in the mana curves of a lot of the original precons; zany high‑cost stuff is a staple feature of the format, and these sorts of cards are often core pillars of their respective decks.

Frameworks: Buildup and Climax

There are a couple frameworks we could use to think about how EDH decks compare to decks in a 60‑card, 20‑health format.
One is the turning point of a given game: basically, if we separate each game into a buildup phase where players are preparing and a climax phase where players are starting to perform whatever action will win them the game, roughly when is there a transition?
In a lot of classic 60‑card decks, this turning point is quite early.
Delver decks look to land a turn‑one threat and gradually chip down opponents, and other decks like Jund similarly look to land early threats.
In contrast to this, most Commander decks spend the first several turns of the game ramping, setting up engines, tutoring, and starting to piece together synergy packages.
A big piece of this likely comes down to life totals: 40 life means that a threat needs to be larger to pose a risk to opponents.
Whereas in Legacy a single mana can be spent on a deck’s win plan, followed by a long sequence of turns spent purely defending that plan and suppressing an opponent, a much larger mana investment is necessary to break through 40 life without the help of an infinite combo.
In addition, even if a deck defeats a single opponent, there are still two opponents at the table, so it’s often wise to invest an even greater amount of mana up front to formulate a threat that can subdue two or more players in rapid succession.

The fact that larger investments are needed to win games means that draw and ramp are almost necessities; plans will involve lots of cards that don’t directly contribute to winning a game.
This is another point of contrast between Commander and 60‑card 1v1 formats.
In a burn deck, an ever‑popular archetype in 60‑card formats, a majority of a deck will often be spent on the task of reducing opposing life totals.
Though you could point to control decks as spending less deck space on direct wincons, these decks still use cards like removal and draw to build up to the dominant game state they need to be in to win; a Lava Spike performs a similar role for an aggro deck as an Up the Beanstalk does for a control deck, and this comes down to the fact that different decks are different.
If we focus on ramp, removal, and draw as categories of card, a large portion of decks in 60‑card formats exclude one, two, or even all three of these things, which is notable when you consider that basically all Commander decks are expected to have all of them.
This is also in spite of the fact that Commander decks have access to, well, a commander , an additional card that is available at all times, a permanent eighth card in hand.
A lot of people fail to acknowledge how incredible that is; a deck could easily pick out a commander that mostly fulfills a particular role to allow their deck to focus less on that, but this isn’t generally what happens.
Taking a look at some popular recent commanders, they are mostly payoff or escalation of some kind: when a thing happens, either more of that thing or something different happens.
A lot of commanders will draw cards in some form or other, but these decks will then still include a lot of card draw.

The Homogenization of Commander

These features paint a specific picture of Commander as a format: decks are expected to run decent quantities of ramp and draw because they’re expected to be bigger, taller than decks in 60‑card formats, and this is facilitated by having more cards and more mana.
This much is unsurprising; on some level these attributes are encoded into the very DNA of the format.
However, over time this DNA has twisted and metastasized into an increasingly homogeneous version of the format, one where decks are expected to look a certain way.
Decks have grown bigger and taller over time; they gain more mana and draw more cards than decks in the past did.
The funny thing about ramp and draw is that they have an amplifying effect: while a win condition or a removal spell won’t generate more of those things, ramp gives mana to cast more ramp spells and card draw finds more card draw.
As more and more powerful draw engines have been printed over the last 10 years, along with the EDH community developing a more streamlined deck‑building culture, decks that focus heavily on draw and ramp have become more and more prevalent.
In tandem with this, deviation from this version of Commander has become increasingly challenging from a social standpoint.
When players see opponents doing some sort of tall game plan, one deck‑building reaction could be to go under it; this is common in competitive 1v1 formats.
You play aggro to rip away your opponent’s life total while they’re still in the setup phase of their greedy nonsense; you play combo to take advantage of them ignoring you; you play stax pieces to clamp down on specific play patterns.
All of this is possible in some form or other in Commander: you can play Voltron or group slug, you can play combo, and you can play stax pieces.

Social Pressures and the Chilling Effect on Counterplay

The issue is, these things are often considered rude at a lot of mid‑power EDH tables.
It’s uncool to see your opponent doing some greedy nonsense and to then try to clamp down on it; the polite counterplay is to go bigger, to get more mana and more cards, and escalate harder.
Commander, as a casual format, has the awkward dynamic that there isn’t a metagame per se, and everything that happens is basically moderated by a giant glob of expectations that players and their opponents decide on verbally or otherwise.
While certain plays might not be explicitly disallowed, there will be varying levels of social acceptance of various different options a player could decide on.
These social pressures then reinforce themselves as different players enter and exit the format under these new conditions.
If a player base decides that Armageddon is unacceptable, well, Winter Orb is almost as bad, and Gaddock Teeg is a bit too staxy as well, and Archon of Valor’s Reach also constricts player agency, doesn’t it?
And I don’t know about poison counters and commander damage either , those seem pretty sus as well.
As countermeasures become less and less widespread, players will feel more and more emboldened to chase big greedy decks to their logical end point; as snowballing mid‑range decks are allowed to become a more and more dominant part of the EDH landscape, they become all but synonymous with the format , they become the way to play casual Commander.

Now, I don’t want to necessarily say that this is inherently a purely bad thing.
I have multiple decks that are absurdly tall or absurdly greedy, and I’ve also made decks with the explicit intent of being taller than other decks.
The dominance of tall Commander means that players are able to do big expressive plays with a wide degree of freedom; it means that new players are allowed to explore the format without getting immediately pressured down by decks built with the intent to kneecap slower and greedier decks.
Any given deck’s “thing” might take more or less time than any other deck’s “thing,” and allowing the most of these things to happen involves pushing back the point at which decks begin to focus on closing out games, letting decks focus more on the buildup stage.
If the goal is to accommodate the most of these and allow the most people to do the most of their thing, tall EDH is really the only way to go.
However, it’s worth keeping in mind that this form of Commander isn’t without issues all of its own.

The Costs: Information Overload, Long Turns, and Board Wipes

One issue with the gradual upward race of tall Commander is something I spent an entire 20‑minute video on a couple months ago: information overload.
EDH boards are wildly complex these days.
While 60‑card 1v1 formats feature the relatively simple board state of two players, EDH features the board states of four players , board states that are often far more complex than ones that would be found in 60‑card formats.
As a result, players in a game of Commander need to process a lot more different cards than players in a 60‑card format, and doing so will use mental energy that could be spent on planning turns, strategizing, politicking, or performing alternative forms of threat analysis.
This will naturally fall hardest on newer players, who are familiar with fewer cards and thus need to take more time to process the cards being played in a game.
Some of this added information is inherent to Commander as a format; having four players is kind of baked into how we do things here in EDH, and having a massive 30‑year‑long card pool is likewise a fact of life.
Commander is unavoidably going to be more complicated than 60‑card formats, and that’s just kind of the price of entry; it was originally popularized by judges, after all.
What’s not a fact of life is everybody playing these big escalatory mid‑range piles, with each deck developing more and more utility or value permanents over the course of a game, each with probably way too much text.
Commander is inherently complex, but it doesn’t need to be this complex.

Big complex board states paired with decks that want to perform exponentially more actions as a game progresses lead to an additional problem: turn lengths.
Casual EDH games have a reputation for being mercilessly long at times, and I’d argue a lion’s share of this is due to long turns.
Long games aren’t always a bad thing; I’ve had multi‑hour back‑and‑forth grinds that were genuinely exciting.
However, when turn length is the primary cause of a game’s length, things are rarely this enjoyable.
One of the issues is that long turns beget long turns: if the game’s turns are a sequence of back‑to‑back 5‑ to 10‑minute fiddle‑fests, other people are going to start chatting or checking their phone or gazing off into space.
Then, when it does get back around to somebody’s turn, instead of planning ahead for it, their mind has been wandering and they need to recalibrate on what they’ll be doing, increasing how long their turn takes.
This cycle reminds me of some of the less pleasant D&D encounters I’ve partaken in, and it’s a cycle that can really take a toll on the goal of maintaining a dynamic game of EDH.
The issue of long turns and thereby long games then ends up increasing the social acceptability of more aggressive strategies; this pattern is most clear with Voltron, an archetype that involves putting a bunch of auras or equipments on a particular creature and then defeating opponents with it one by one, but it really applies to focusing down opponents in general.
Despite the fact that strategically it might be correct to fully focus down a particularly scary player, this is somewhat unpopular in part because of how long EDH games go.
If knocking out a player meant them sitting around for 10 or 20 minutes for the game to finish, I doubt most people would have too big of an issue with that; however, when it means sitting around for 45 minutes or even an hour while the remaining players fiddle around with their fussy boards of snowball mid‑range, it’s a bit less ideal.

In addition to the other issues I’ve mentioned, the current Commander landscape is one that favors board wipes quite heavily.
Players will put together these wild snowbally packages that explode out of control in a matter of turns, and when there are multiple of these sorts of decks with taboos against stax and aggro, the only option is to board wipe, and to board wipe frequently.
The collateral damage of this will be the players trying to advance their own game plan at a normal pace, trying to gradually build up something of their own.
This is the paradox of “doing the thing”: the form of Commander that gives players the most time to develop a slower game plan is the exact same form of Commander that cultivates board wipes as this constant and unavoidable factor.
You get to do the thing, and I sure hope you liked doing that thing, because you’re going to get to do it about five more times after getting board wiped repeatedly.

Can These Problems Be Solved?

So those are some of my problems with the state of Commander as it currently exists, this wildly tall version of the format, and a natural follow‑up question might be: can those problems be solved in a way that improves the format?
I mentioned the taboos against shorter strategies such as aggro and stax, and I know some of you probably thought to yourselves, “But Alex, even without taboos, aggro simply isn’t a viable strategy in casual Commander, is that?”

#18
Meta CommentaryJul 2025232K views

Deckbuilding Creativity vs. Actual Gameplay Experience

The Impossible Search for a "Fun" Combo Deck
We reflect on the disconnect between creative deckbuilding and actual gameplay in Commander.We built a Maze's End combo deck that could win consistently by turn 7, but opponent treated it like any other combo deck.A friend's Yidris cascade deck faced similar indifference, as players easily disrupted it.The core insight is that deckbuilding and playing are separate activities, and creative building doesn't inherently make games more fun.
Use this in your deckRun at least 20 dedicated ramp spells to enable a consistent turn 7 Maze's End win, and include Scapeshift or Reshape the Earth to cheat all Gates into play at once.
