Archelos, Lagoon MysticBlueBlackGreenComboRampControlBudgetDeckbuilding RestrictionsWin ConditionsSynergyDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • Deckbuilding creativity is not rewarded in Commander games on its own. The gameplay experience often differs from the builder's narrative.
Evidence
  • our Maze's End deck, despite weeks of tuning, was met with indifference. Opponents simply acknowledged the combo and moved on.
  • A friend's Yidris cascade deck was religiously hated out because players recognized the combo and focused on removing the commander.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Use 20 or more ramp spells to consistently assemble a Maze's End win by turn 7 or 8.
  • Cheat all Gates into play with Scapeshift or Reshape the Earth to bypass the slow Gate accumulation.
  • Include Amulet of Vigor to untap the Gates immediately and win on the same turn, avoiding a vulnerability window.
Limitation
  • Maze's End is slow and visible. Opponents can track your progress and apply pressure before you reach 10 Gates.
  • Even a creatively built combo deck ultimately behaves like a standard combo deck, vulnerable to countermagic and commander removal.
Example
  • The Aros Maze's End deck is an example of an optimized alternate win condition deck that still produced dull game moments.
  • The Yidris cascade deck illustrates how creative execution is irrelevant if opponents can stop the commander's combat trigger.
More quotes
  1. The issue with Mesa's End is that it's both visible and slow. Opponents can track the progress of the deck to its wincon with pinpoint accuracy.
  2. What if I simply played all the gates at once?
  3. I spent weeks and dozens of hours tweaking until I'd achieved a deck that could pull off a Mazes End win consistently by turn seven or eight, for a bit over a hundred bucks.
  4. In an actual game of Commander, this plays just like any other one card plus Commander combo deck.
  5. Despite having a creative execution, [the Yidris] combo was still a combo and opponents treated it as such.
  6. Deck building creativity is not rewarded in and of itself in Commander games.
  7. An inherent weirdness of a collectible card game like Magic: the Gathering is that deck building and playing are two separate phases of the game.
  8. The platonic ideal of an EDH win is brief; there is no ceremony where the winning player gets to revel in their glory and be celebrated for their deck building or gameplay accomplishments.
  9. I had constructed narratives of grandeur as I was reaching out striving for a powerful Mazes End deck, but once it was in my hand it was clearly something far more mundane than I had expected.
  10. Despite understanding all of that, I have a pattern of continually coming back to build new creative combo decks; it's an itch that I can't seem to stop scratching.
Full transcript
The Maze’s End Dream

Maze’s End is a card that sat in the back of my trade binder as well as in the back of my mind for 10 years: simply get 10 Gates and then you win the game.
It was always an intriguing card, in the way most alternate wincons are, but I could never find a good reason to build a Maze’s End deck.
Every so often I’d come back to it, most notably to ponder a Child of Alara commander deck early last year, but it wasn’t until September that I finally found a good vehicle for it.

The issue with Maze’s End is that it’s both visible and slow.
Opponents can track the progress of the deck to its wincon with pinpoint accuracy, and the time frame to arrive at that win condition leaves a long period where opponents can apply pressure.
In addition, the 10 Gate requirement means that deck builders are encouraged to play a sequence of pretty mediocre tap lands or else need to activate Maze’s End a large number of times.
However, there is a way to get around all these problems: what if I simply played all the Gates at once?
Cards like Scapeshift and Reshape the Earth allow deck builders to do just this, getting all the necessary Gates for Maze’s End out all at once.
This can leave a turn cycle before victory due to the lands entering tapped, unless a player has an Amulet of Vigor in play or, rather, an untapped Turtle friend causing all other permanents to enter either tapped or untapped depending on his own status.
Archelos is a fantastic vehicle for this sort of deck because not only is he a combo enabler, he can also help out with ramping faster as well as slowing down opponents.

In addition to running 12 Gates, the deck ended up running several bounce lands to give a mana boost with the commander, some basics to be grabbed with land-grabbing spells, and a handful of other utility lands, for a grand total of 43 lands, not including Maze’s End.
Of the two cards that allow the deck to win, one costs nine mana and the other requires 10 lands, so naturally ramp ends up being a very big part of the deck.
There are 20 dedicated ramp spells, as well as an additional four ramp spells that serve double duty by also offering a way to tap down the commander; being able to force everyone’s stuff to enter tapped until my next turn is a pretty sweet side benefit.
From there, the deck mostly consists of draw, tutors, and interaction.
The most potent of the deck’s tutors is Emergent Ultimatum, a card that basically serves as an additional combo piece since any combination of Reshape the Earth, Scapeshift, and Boundless Realms will result in getting enough lands to win.
As far as interaction goes, I always like to have a lot of counter spells in my blue combo decks, so this deck runs 11 of those and six other cards intended to control the state of the game in some capacity.

The deck took a lot of tweaking to get it to the point where it functioned appropriately: how much ramp?
how much draw?
what sort of lands?
how should the deck go about surviving and preventing opponents from winning?
how important is the commander, and how much should I be leveraging him for non-combo purposes?
I spent several weeks and dozens of hours tweaking and tuning and pruning and answering tough questions until I’d finally achieved the seemingly impossible: a deck that could pull off a Maze’s End win consistently by turn seven or eight, with plenty of interaction held up, all for a bit over a hundred bucks.
It was an accomplishment, a realization of my past ambitions, an optimized utilization of a silly alternate wincon card I’d been thinking about for 10 whole years.
And if the story ended here, it would be an unambiguously happy one, an uncomplicated tale of self-actualization and cardboard triumph.

The Reality of Playing Combo in Commander

However, it was in fact somewhat complicated by a simple fact: Magic: The Gathering is a card game, and decks are meant to be played in games against people.
In my first few games with the deck, I was kind of surprised with the way people responded to me pulling off my massive land swing.
I’d get excited and start grabbing Gates, and other people would basically go, “Oh, you got the combo?
Okay, cool.” Another game: I had constructed a grand narrative for this deck because it was genuinely challenging to build, but in an actual game of Commander, this plays just like any other “one card plus Commander combo deck.” Satisfy conditions, get pieces, stop opponents from winning long enough to put things together, protect the combo, and do the combo.
It’s not necessarily that the deck was entirely uninteresting or entirely devoid of fun gameplay; it’s an acceptable combo deck.
The real surprise was the expectation versus the reality of the situation.
I had constructed narratives of grandeur as I was reaching out, striving for a powerful Maze’s End deck, but once it was in my hand, it was clearly something far more mundane than I had expected.

On some level, it’s a little strange that this happened to me because I had watched my friend Hans have a very similar experience a few months prior building a Yidris deck.
This was a deck that used Yidris’s unique combat damage ability to cascade into an “each opponent discards their hand, you draw 28 cards” combo off of two cantrips.
It was otherwise built in a midrange shell containing ramp, removal, value engines, and some very specific protection spells that were chosen in order to still get value when they were cascaded into.
So what’s the problem there?
Well, once again, despite having a creative execution, the Nexus combo was still a combo, and opponents treated it as such.
In fact, this deck was even more problematic than the Archelos deck due to the fact that all of its shenanigans could be stopped by simply killing or fully blocking the Yidris, and since not doing that meant possibly ceding a massive value swing to the Yidris player, players would religiously hate out the Yidris.
On paper, everything lined up for the deck, but real life is not made out of paper.

Creativity Is Not Its Own Reward

A question I’ve asked myself is, what are these decks saying exactly?
They say something about combos, about humans, and about how we build decks, but that’s not the most interesting thing they say.
The most interesting thing these goofy combo decks tell me is that deck-building creativity is not rewarded in and of itself in Commander games.
That might sound like the whining of some hipster, but I’m not intending that statement as any sort of value judgment about the state of the game; rather, it provides an interesting jumping-off point for thinking more critically about the game and how it’s experienced by the people who play it.

An inherent weirdness of a collectible card game like Magic: The Gathering is that deck building and playing are two separate phases of the game.
You’re not playing Dominion or some other card game where deck building and playing happen in tandem; no, these things are clearly demarcated.
Limited formats provide the most conventionally game-like structure for these phases, in the sense that a four- or five-hour limited event literally contains separate timed phases for assembling decks and playing the game.
A Magic: The Gathering draft, where players pick cards from packs in a circle and build decks with their resulting card pool before entering a series of timed rounds, actually resembles a lot of board and card games in structure.
As a simple example, For Sale is a game that contains one phase where players bid against each other on property cards, and then a second phase where players use their lineup of properties to secure point cards.
Like a Magic: The Gathering draft, the first phase involves a mixture of strategy and luck to determine how good a player’s group of cards is, and then the second phase uses the quality of that group as well as some further strategy and luck to determine the ultimate outcome of the game.

This is a clear enough line to draw with limited Magic: The Gathering events, but the relationship between these different phases gets aggressively more convoluted from here.
Let’s think about a low-level Standard event.
In the deck-building phase, players on their own time and in their own spaces complete the task of assembling the best Standard deck they can and the best sideboard to face off against the decks they expect to face.
Then the playing phase sees the players, now congregating in a game store at a specific time, play a sequence of games to determine a winner.
This is very different from most games in that the different phases occur in different settings on different timelines, but the structure is still roughly the same: the first phase sees players complete one goal, which then contributes to the outcome of a second goal.

However, taking things a step further into the realm of Commander, things get even murkier because you lose a lot of the element of competitiveness.
The first phase does still involve building a deck, and the second phase does still involve playing that deck, but the goals are less clear.
Unlike a Standard tournament, there isn’t usually a prize for winning an EDH game aside from bragging rights, and even in store-run casual Commander events, the subject of prize support is one with no shortage of disagreement and controversy.
Simply winning isn’t codified as a goal in the same way as it is in 60-card constructed events.
This difference in how winning is perceived then trickles back down to the deck-building phase.
If everybody was sincerely trying their best to make the most powerful deck in that deck-building phase, Commander games would be full of net-decked cEDH lists, but most players choose to show up to EDH games with decks that look quite different than that.
Winning will be a contributing factor to the sorts of decks that EDH players will build, but it will be accompanied by a mélange of other factors rendered subtle in competitive 1v1 formats: wanting a deck to do a certain cool play, wanting to have a deck feel a certain way, wanting a deck to look a certain way, wanting a deck to produce the funniest moments, and so on.

A lot of these goals will mesh well with the task of having fun in that second phase, the playing phase.
Obviously, players playing decks that feel expressive or cool to them in some way will usually lead to them having more fun, and the goal of winning will likewise produce a lot of fun moments for people.
However, there are also goals that don’t mesh as well with having fun in a game of Commander, and the best example of these is decks that try to win in some especially creative or expressive way.
As it turns out, winning the game is generally a pretty brief process; a long period of back and forth can be fun, but when a game reaches a point where one person is all but guaranteed to win, it’s generally for the best that things stop very soon after.
This is also why non-deterministic combo or lockout decks are often unpopular: they stretch out the length of a win, requiring opponents to make an unfortunate choice, either concede to save time or waste an extra 10 minutes on the 5% chance that the comboing player fizzles.
But okay, I’m getting off topic.
The platonic ideal of an EDH win is brief; there is no ceremony where the winning player gets to revel in their glory and be celebrated for their deck-building or gameplay accomplishments.
The game just ends and people move on to either another game or any of the other things that might occupy their life.
Winning in that creative way might be cool for that first time or two, but maybe not even that.
The really memorable games are usually the ones where something unexpected happens, not the one where a player trots out their creatively devised wincon they spent hours creating.
But spending hours to creatively devise and perfect a wincon still has an allure to it, doesn’t it?
Despite understanding all of what I just said, I have a pattern of continually coming back to build new creative combo decks.
It’s an itch that I can’t seem to stop scratching.

The Tribal Fiend Hunter Deck

Another one I built in paper a few months back was one I like to call “tribal Fiend Hunter.” The basic idea behind it was this: you know Fiend Hunters, those creatures that exile something else until they leave play?
Well, what if you built a deck with the goal of getting three of them and then having them exile each other repeatedly in an indefinitely long loop, just a few fellas having a really phenomenal day of fiend hunting?
This is a somewhat unique spin on a combo deck; instead of finding a handful of different pieces in separate categories, you just need to find three of the eight Fiend Hunters that exist within the green-white color identity.
The deck then wins through some means or other, a couple of the Fiend Hunters also create tokens or counters, and three of them exile multiple cards, which allows for infinite ETB triggers.

Speaking of ETB triggers, I went with the commander choice of Torsten, Founder of Benalia.
Torsten has a lot of benefits, with the biggest one being that he draws a bunch of cards on ETB, a fact which can be paired with cards that flicker in order to draw the number of cards that the deck needs to draw in order to find three Fiend Hunters: 37 on average.
So the deck begins with a mixture of Fiend Hunters and sources of blink.
From there, most of the deck ended up being occupied by lands and ramp creatures, 42 and 29, respectively.
This might seem like a lot, because it is; I have a seven-mana Commander I want to cast, and since he’s the only source of draw in a deck that wants to draw a ton of cards, the deck doesn’t really do anything before he gets cast.
The mana dorks of the deck are carefully selected to offer the most utility during the fiend hunting combo step: there are six different ones that can give infinite mana, infinite +1/+1 counters, or some other resource that can hopefully lead to me winning, and the next removal likewise fits into this mold.
Overall, the goal here was to have a combo deck that is as fluid and flexible as possible, using the power of Fiend Hunters to creatively piece together wins in a variety of different ways.
Initially I considered using some pricier cards in the deck like Preston, the Vanisher, or even switching the commander to Atraxa for a four-color version, but eventually I came to the conclusion.

#19
Meta CommentaryJul 2025211K views

The Double Standard of Resource Denial in EDH

You're Probably Wrong about Stax in EDH
The EDH community accepts board wipes but shuns mass land destruction and stax due to social norms, not card balance.Players prepare for common strategies but not for discouraged ones, making those strategies more oppressive.We critique the inconsistent definition of player agency, as snowballing decks can deny agency as much as stax locks.
Use this in your deckDiversify your ramp package with artifact and creature sources, and include lower-cost spells to withstand land destruction and stax effects.
StaxFormat HealthMeta TrendsRule ZeroTable PoliticsSocial and Politics
Principle
  • Social norms, not objective power levels, determine which resource-denial strategies are accepted in EDH.
Evidence
  • Players could mitigate mass land destruction with indestructible lands, artifact ramp, and land recursion, but they rarely do because the strategy is socially discouraged.
  • Classic stax pieces universally target mana and card draw, fundamentally denying the same resources that board wipes deny by removing creatures.
Deckbuilding Application
  • To reduce vulnerability, include artifact and creature based ramp, land recursion, and a lower mana curve.
Limitation
  • While some stax cards are justifiably banned for locking players out, the broader stigma ignores that snowballing midrange decks can equally prevent opponents from making meaningful game actions.
Example
  • In a world where board wipes are absent, a single Wrath of God would feel as taboo as Armageddon does now, illustrating how preparation norms dictate perceptions.
  • A stax soft lock with a 90% win chance feels more objectionable than being behind an aggressive dragon player with the same odds, despite identical comeback potential.
More quotes
  1. Mass land destruction is acutely painful largely because people don't prepare for it, and people don't prepare for it because it's not commonly accepted as a major part of the format.
  2. Frequent board wipes being accepted as a fact of life at most EDH tables isn't some natural fact that got plucked out of the ground wholly formed, and the fact that mass land destruction is mostly absent from EDH tables isn't a natural fact either. The reason why people are frequently prepared for one and frequently unprepared for the other comes down to social factors.
  3. The stax archetype is traditionally all about denying your opponents the tools they need to play the game.
  4. The gameplay goal of [Lantern control] was less to win than it was to prevent the opponent from playing Magic: The Gathering, to assemble a Rube Goldberg machine that could prevent the unlucky opponent from taking any meaningful game action.
  5. Player agency is important in EDH. On this much I agree. It's good when all players get to play a part in an EDH game and it's never a great feeling when somebody feels truly left out of a game. However, over time we as players have developed a skewed vision of what player agency actually looks like.
  6. These situations are identical in terms of ability to perform meaningful game actions. It's just as easy to break out of the stax player's grip as it is to catch back up to the Ur-Dragon player, yet the first one is generally thought of as the much more objectionable one.
  7. A lot of players seem to think of their deck as more of a fidget toy than an actual game piece. You have some sort of synergy thing that your deck is meant to do, and being able to complete a few entirely insignificant game actions on your turn to scratch that brain itch registers as a more positive thing than being able to do nothing at all.
  8. Large portions of Commander decks are snowball midrange in some form or other, decks with abundant tools to keep getting ahead once they're already ahead. Why don't these violate conventions of player agency?
  9. For three mana, [Teferi's Protection] shuts down all board interaction and forms of damaging a player until that player's next turn, with a counterspell being the only possible option for counterplay. It's the sort of effect whose all-encompassing nature would be valid at an inflated mana cost of something like five mana, but instead it's costed at three, the same amount as most board wipe dodges. You get invulnerability as just a little bitty tacked-on side benefit.
  10. Looking at the stax primer from ten years ago is fascinating, as a number of cards listed as stax or stax-adjacent sound weird to a modern Commander player's brain. Edicts are one instance of this, and wraths are another.
Full transcript
A Thought Experiment: Board Wipes as Social Norm

Imagine for a moment that you’ve never heard of a board wipe before, no Wrath of God, no Blasphemous Act, no Toxic Deluge, and certainly no Farewell.
Alternatively, we can imagine that you have heard of these things like you’re vaguely familiar with them, but nobody really plays them because why would you?
That’s not how Commander is played.
We can imagine the resulting environment of decks that commit hard to the board with no fear of punishment, decks that prioritize proactive plans and pushing damage rather than developing slower engines and sculpting a hand.
Players run less ramp and likely less card draw in some cases as well, because there’s less of a need to weave that stuff into the rest of your game plan, and there’s a heavier focus on building an unbeatable board of synergistic nonsense.
So let’s say that’s the universe we live in now.

Now let’s say somebody casts a Wrath of God.
Everybody’s collections of mid-range are wiped out in one fell swoop; backup plans are scarce, hands dwindling in size, and looks of dejection, resentment, and anger even cross the faces of everybody else at the table.
The unspoken rules have been broken, and the format as it was known by these hypothetical people thrown on its head with a single card.
Nobody prepared for this because why would they?
This isn’t how the format is played.

Mass Land Destruction and the Social Contract

Where am I going with this?
Well, one could argue this is very similar to the relationship the current EDH community has with mass land destruction.
Just as there are ways people mitigate board wipes now, there are all sorts of ways you could mitigate the impact of a mass land destruction spell like Armageddon: you could play more indestructible lands, you could diversify your ramp package with creature- and artifact-based ramp, you could place a heavier emphasis on land recursion or run Second Sunrise effects, you could run more extra land play effects and be careful to keep some lands in hand at all times, and you could run lower-curved decks that require less mana to do effective plays.
Mass land destruction is acutely painful largely because people don’t prepare for it, and people don’t prepare for it because it’s not commonly accepted as a major part of the format.

It’s worth keeping in mind that frequent board wipes being accepted as a fact of life at most EDH tables isn’t some natural fact that got plucked out of the ground wholly formed, and the fact that mass land destruction is mostly absent from EDH tables isn’t a natural fact either.
The reason why people are frequently prepared for one and frequently unprepared for the other comes down to social factors.

Are Wrath of God and Armageddon Equivalent?

Now, does that mean that Armageddon and Wrath of God are equivalent cards in some objective sense, sharing a common DNA even if they’re interpreted differently by the people who play EDH?
The answer to that is more complicated than it seems, and I’ll touch back on the question a little later, but I’ll start by exploring the answer that most players would give you: no, they’re obviously not.
Why aren’t they?
Well, lands produce mana, a fundamental resource required for playing the game, whereas creatures are just the guys you hit your opponents with.
If creatures are wiped, players can usually keep casting spells, whereas if lands are wiped they often cannot.

Defining Stax: Resource Denial

To bring a key piece of vocabulary into this, mass land destruction is a form of “stax.” Stax is a broad category of card with conflicting etymological claims that I will not be getting into, but the most basic definition of stax is that it’s resource denial.
A 2013 post on MTG Salvation’s EDH forum lists 14 cards as the universal colorless stax package; of these, four remove lands, four keep lands or other permanents tapped down, three increase the cost of spells, and the other three perform other effects that are broadly in line with the rest of these things.
The common theme is that all but one of these target mana in some way, and it’s also common to see cards that target card draw among classic stax pieces.
Broadly, a stax deck is one that looks to restrict the table’s resources and then break parity on those restrictions, basically, to do something to everybody and then play in a way that isn’t as affected by that thing.
Examples include playing a Winter Orb with a bunch of mana rocks on board, playing a Smokestack with a Bitterblossom on board, and playing mostly creature spells with cards that tax noncreature spells on board.

In common EDH parlance, there are a number of other cards that also fall into the stax descriptor: cards that prevent players from casting certain spells, cards that limit how many spells a player can play, cards that prevent abilities in some way, and even cards that prevent players from using graveyards.
These are also sometimes called “rule cards.” The stax archetype is traditionally all about denying your opponents the tools they need to play the game.

The Lantern Control Extreme and Player Agency

A Modern deck known as Lantern control is the most distilled version of this idea: control opposing draws to prevent them from finding anything useful, prevent the opponent from attacking with a card known as Ensnaring Bridge, and then eventually win with incremental mill or another slow but compact win condition.
As Sam of Rhystic Studies laid out in his video on the deck, the gameplay goal of the deck was less to win than it was to prevent the opponent from playing Magic: The Gathering, to assemble a Rube Goldberg machine that could prevent the unlucky opponent from taking any meaningful game action.
This amplified version of things makes it clear why many stax strategies are taboo in EDH: they are all about restricting the agency of players.

This stands in contrast to the most popular visions for the EDH format, that it’s a palette to be drawn upon, a format where more expressive strategies have room to breathe.
This vision of the format was codified by the RC (rest in peace) in the cards they choose to ban and why.
Sway of the Stars is banned because it nullifies all the gameplay that came before it, making those actions irrelevant; Iona is banned because it has the potential to lock a whole player out of casting spells if their deck is monocolored; a number of other cards are banned for making agency-denying strategies just a bit too easy.
Whether these bans were deserved is outside the scope here, but they do indicate a pattern: player agency is important in EDH.
On this much I agree, it’s good when all players get to play a part in an EDH game, and it’s never a great feeling when somebody feels truly left out of a game.

The Skewed Vision of Agency in Commander

However, over time we as players have developed a skewed vision of what player agency actually looks like.
Being able to perform meaningful game actions is the bar for player suppression when it comes to Lantern control, but when it comes to EDH the definitions are much murkier.
We can think about two different situations in an EDH game as an example.
In situation A, the table is in a soft lock from a stax player with a 90% chance of losing to that player in the next few turns.
In situation B, the table is wildly behind in resources compared to an Ur-Dragon player and has a 90% chance of losing in the next few turns.
These situations are identical in terms of ability to perform meaningful game actions; it’s just as easy to break out of the stax player’s grip as it is to catch back up to the Ur-Dragon player, yet the first one is generally thought of as the much more objectionable one.

One possible reason for this is that a lot of players seem to think of their deck as more of a “fidget toy” than an actual game piece.
You have some sort of synergy thing that your deck is meant to do, and being able to complete a few entirely insignificant game actions on your turn to scratch that brain itch registers as a more positive thing than being able to do nothing at all.
This isn’t some radical observation, but things get a bit uncomfortable when we consider that large portions of Commander decks are snowball mid-range in some form or other, decks with abundant tools to keep getting ahead once they’re already ahead.
Why don’t these violate conventions of player agency?
Shouldn’t decks give opponents more opportunities to catch back up?
I’ve mentioned excessive counts of “win more” cards as a strategic folly in past videos, but isn’t this deckbuilding choice a bit rude as well?
You can say “just play removal,” but that logic works equally well for a stax piece like Ward of Bones as it does for a turbocharged engine like Kamahl, Fist of Krosa, even as the former is going to catch you a lot more side-eye than the latter.

Teferi's Protection, The One Ring, and Hidden Agency Denial

As another example, let’s talk about one of the most agency-denying cards that’s widely played in EDH: Teferi’s Protection.
Being completely disallowed from interacting with a player in any form for a whole turn cycle is wildly disempowering.
A core feature of Magic: The Gathering is that there are different axes on which to attack your opponent, life total can be hit, noncreature permanents can be knocked out with removal, spells can be countered, and creatures can be hit with either removal or by forcing blocks.
Even if somebody plays a fog or counters a removal spell, there is still usually a counterplay that a prepared player can execute; not so with Teferi’s Protection.
For three mana, all board interaction and forms of damaging a player are shut down until that player’s next turn, with a counterspell being the only possible option for counterplay.
It’s the sort of effect whose all-encompassing nature would be valid at an inflated mana cost of something like five mana, but instead it’s costed at three, the same amount as most board wipe dodges, you get invulnerability as just a little bitty tacked-on side benefit.

I have a similar problem with The One Ring.
It’s a powerful draw spell for four mana; it’s indestructible; and it makes a player invulnerable to most forms of life loss until their next turn.
In both cases, these are cards with one primary mode but also a stapled-on effect that dramatically reduces the number of meaningful game actions opponents can take.
People know these cards are strong, but I haven’t seen much complaining about these particular clauses being on these cards, and it’s not hard to guess the reason why: these cards don’t restrict the “fidget toy.” Opponents can still putter around inconsequentially with value permanents even if they’re about to die to a massive turn from the Teferi’s Protected or One Ringed player.

What I’m illustrating with these examples is my biggest issue with the way people talk about stax, and really the term “stax” itself, in the context of EDH: it does a poor job of describing the things people have a problem with, and often excludes a lot of other cards with very similar play patterns.

Board Wipes and Edicts: Stax in a Different Era

Looking at the stax primer from 10 years ago is fascinating, as a number of cards listed as stax or stax-adjacent sound weird to a modern Commander player’s brain.
Edicts are one instance of this, and wraths are another.
These may seem like strange inclusions for a stax-specific guide given the current dominance of these cards and the current unacceptability of stax as an archetype, but in the context of older eras of Commander and 60-card formats it makes a lot of sense.
It’s accepted as a fact of life now that board wipes will be lurking around every corner of a Commander game and that every deck will have one or more of these sorts of tools, but most 60-card games feature decks that have none of these things.
Developing a powerful board in a 60-card format is a genuinely game-winning proposition, and creatures are useful as tools to deal damage to opponents, not just as another permanent type that draws cards or has a cool ability.
Under this previous conceptualization of the game, wiping a board full of creatures is ripping away resources from opponents in the same way that ripping away artifacts is, or maybe even lands.
These aren’t identical, but the parallels are absolutely there.

Another card type that echoes back to my frustration with Teferi’s Protection is defensive cards.
Attacking a player is a game action, and when a Lantern control deck runs Ensnaring Bridge, that is one of the game actions they are looking to curtail.
We can even think about goad, Mound was correct to call it a cousin to stax, as though it does have the escape valve of getting less effective once the table is down to a 1v1, it’s still a form of limiting player agency.

Stax Versus Control: Proactive and Reactive Denial

All of what I just said is fun theorizing, but it doesn’t paint a full picture of stax and what it means for the EDH format, both as a style of card and as an archetype.
The subject of player agency naturally dovetails into a strategy that is very much adjacent to stax: control.
Some of you probably raised your eyebrows at me using Lantern control as an example of stax, it’s right there in the name.
Well, the two strategies are deeply intertwined; they both care chiefly about the range of actions opponents are able to take.
However, there are a handful of commonly cited distinctions between the two.
The most basic distinction is that stax is predominantly permanent-focused, whereas control is typically instant- and sorcery-focused.
This is largely true in that the cards people associate with stax are often permanents and the cards people associate with control often aren’t, but this isn’t a distinction that tells us very much about the game.

A more interesting distinction I’ve heard is that stax is proactive whereas control is reactive.
This isn’t exactly true within the context of EDH, but it’s not true in a very nuanced way that is interesting to explore, so explore we shall.
To start with, when I say stax is proactive and control is reactive, what I mean is that stax plays its answers before problematic spells are cast, whereas control plays its answers after problematic spells are cast, often to counter or remove them.
Neither of these styles are considered proactive in the broader landscape of Magic, but positioning them as proactive versus reactive in comparison to each other isn’t an unreasonable distinction.
On the surface level, there is an undeniable pattern here; a decent portion of stax pieces

#20
DeckbuildingJul 2025200K views

Choosing the Optimal Land Count for Your EDH Deck

All of the Reasons You Should Put More Lands in your EDH Deck
We explain why many Commander decks benefit from higher land counts, focusing on factors like mana curve, draw efficiency, and mana sinks.Using a Ruric Thar deck as a case study, it shows how missing land drops forces decks to waste mana on ramp instead of threats.The deck's 41-land count is a deliberate choice to ensure consistent mana development.We provide a framework for evaluating your own deck's land needs.
Use this in your deckIncrease your land count if you frequently find yourself spending mana on ramp spells just to make your land drops.
RedGreenRampMidrangeMana BaseCurveDeck ConsistencyDesign IntentDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • A deck's mana curve determines how many lands it needs: higher curves require more lands to cast spells on time.
  • Missing land drops in high-curve decks forces you to spend mana on ramp just to hit land drops, which should have been spent on threats.
Evidence
  • We found that with 40 lands, we frequently had to use ramp to make land drops, so we increased to 41.
  • Many players slavishly stick to a preferred land count like 36 or 38, ignoring a deck's specific needs.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Track how often you use ramp to hit land drops; if it is frequent, increase your land count.
  • Ensure your land count is high enough to cast expensive draw spells on curve without relying on ramp.
Limitation
  • Decks that aim to win by turn six can run fewer lands because they do not need as many land drops.
  • Decks with a lot of cheap cantrips can run fewer lands because they can easily find them.
Example
  • The Ruric Thar deck averages a nonland cost of 5.33 and has no spells below four mana, so it runs 41 lands to avoid mana screw.
  • Its cheapest draw effects cost four mana and require further hoops, making early land drops critical.
More quotes
  1. I suppose maybe people consider setting the land count of a deck they're adapting to their preferred number as a part of putting their own spin.
  2. I've also seen people react with shock or skepticism when I mentioned that one of my decks has a land count in the 40s.
  3. You don't need to play a land on turn seven if there is no turn seven.
  4. What happens if you draw 10 cards and then end the turn with a hand containing six lands and eight non-land cards? You discard four or five lands. That's what.
  5. When a deck can readily access powerful draw that is a bit mana expensive, running a higher land count is often an all-around win.
  6. Playing a six drop is twice as card efficient as playing two three drops.
  7. I bumped the land count from 40 up to 41 specifically because I had too many games where I was effectively paying mana for land drops that I could be making for free normally.
  8. Running a land count closer to your ideal land proportion in your opening hand gives you more wiggle room to use your mulligans to hunt for non-land cards.
  9. A 32 land deck will often need to play a Bojuka Bog on curve in order to not miss a land drop, and this may end up greatly reducing the effectiveness of the card's ETB.
  10. Some decks care about what's on top of their deck more than others, an attribute that I like to call the krip coefficient.
Full transcript
Introduction

Howdy, folks.
My throat is feeling better, enough to record a YouTube video, so here I am.
I plan to upload weekly for the next month to clear out the backlog of videos I’ve been working on, so hopefully that can fill the deep hole in your heart that I’m all but certain my absence has wrought.
I also have a big video about manabases coming up later this month, and with that in mind I figure I may as well run back through a subject that is near and dear to my heart: land counts.
I did talk about this a bit in my lands video almost a year ago, but there was a bunch of other stuff in that video and my thinking has developed a bit since then, so I figure this piece can serve as a more targeted guide.

The Question and an Example Deck

The question I’ll be looking to help you answer in we i, how many lands should my deck have in it?
And, perhaps more specifically, should my deck have more lands in it?
To help me out with answering this question, I’ll be using a deck I built in March of last year: my R battle cruiser deck.
You can watch the original video about it if you want, but the relevant information for we i that it is a deck with a mana-dork commander, a pile of four-mana ramp spells, and a bunch of six- and seven-drops filling out the rest of the deck, resulting in this deranged mana curve.
It also runs 41 lands.
A bunch of people have made decks riffing off of this deck, which is super cool; I always love to see my decks being an inspiration for people.
What I’ve also noticed, though, is that a common first change for people is cutting three to four lands from the deck.
I guess people must assume that I pulled the number 41 out of a hat, or maybe that’s just my personal preference.
I know I’ve frequently seen land counts referenced online as an across-the-board preference of sorts for people; you’ll see people say that they prefer 35 lands or 36 lands, or that they’re a 38-land sort of person, and other such statements.
I suppose maybe people consider setting the land count of a deck they’re adapting to their preferred number as a part of putting their own spin on it, maybe.
I’ve also seen people react with shock or skepticism when I mention that one of my decks has a land count in the 40s, so maybe people just aren’t sold on a count that high.
Regardless, I want to discuss the different factors that should influence land count, discussing how each of them applied to this deck as an example, and at the end of that process maybe it’ll be a bit more clear why I went with the number I went with, and you might have a bit more insight on how many lands might be right for your deck based on its particular attributes.

Mana Curve: The Primary Influence

For starters, let’s talk about mana curve.
Mana curve refers to the mana costs of the different cards in a deck, and on the most basic level the average mana cost of a deck is an indicator of how much mana that deck needs to do what it wants to do.
As a general pattern, lower-curved decks will want less mana than higher-curved decks and will thus also want a lower land count than higher-curved decks; however, in the context of Commander this is more of a correlation than it is a rule, and there are two main factors that contribute to this correlation.
One of these factors is how quickly a deck is looking to win the game, with lower-curved decks typically winning earlier than higher-curved decks.
Power level and how proactive a deck is will both affect how quickly a deck looks to be closing out games, but regardless, this land count versus win-speed pattern tends to be a reasonably consistent one to see; simply put, you don’t need to play a land on turn seven if there is no turn seven.
Another way that mana curve can influence the number of lands a deck wants is in terms of how accessible the deck’s ways to dig for more lands are.
A deck with lots of low-cost draw and filtering can afford to run a lower land count as long as it can find the colors to cast those digging cards, whereas decks that feature draw and filtering that cost four or more mana will want to run a higher count in order to consistently get enough mana to pay for that draw or filtering, or at least supplement with ramp.
Likewise, decks whose source of dig is more complex, such as a deck that needs both a Garruk’s Uprising and some big creatures to trigger it, will want to be careful to run enough lands to avoid getting stalled out on mana before getting the chance to fulfill the condition that will draw them into more.

Most of my reasoning so far has been in terms of how low a land count a deck can afford to run, but it’s also useful to think about the question of mana curve in terms of how high a count a deck could afford to run.
More complex or mana-expensive forms of draw also typically draw more cards, and if a deck has a lot of heavy-duty draw, such as a green deck running a number of cards that draw cards equal to a creature’s power, it can be possible to run a quite high land count without really feeling negative impacts from it.
What happens if you draw 10 cards and then end the turn with a hand containing six lands and eight non-land cards?
You discard four or five lands, that’s what.
When a deck can readily access powerful draw that is a bit mana expensive, running a higher land count is often an all-around win; either you haven’t gotten enough mana to play the draw yet, in which case a high land count helps you have a higher chance of getting there, or you have played the draw, in which case you’re turning through enough cards to render that slightly increased land density negligible.

Spending Mana and Avoiding Flooding

What this relates to really is how good a deck is at spending its mana.
Let’s think about what we’re worried about when adding more lands to a deck: it’s flooding, right?
We’re worried that we’ll draw a bunch of lands and, instead of having cool, impactful plays to make, will be stuck sitting around with a handful of lands.
This is a valid concern, and one way to mitigate it is to run fewer lands, but another way is to make sure that your deck is consistently able to turn through excesses of mana.
Having a higher curve alone will provide some insulation against getting flooded, since playing a six-drop is twice as card-efficient as playing two three-drops; this doesn’t necessarily mean that you should pump up your curve to avoid getting flooded, but it does mean that drawing a lot of lands will be more problematic for some decks than others.
As far as tools to reduce flooding go, the sort of big draw I mentioned earlier is a fantastic way to improve a deck’s ability to spend mana, and there are a few different options here.
Decks can run a lot of it, decks can run less of it and run filtering or tutors to find their way to it, and decks can also just put it in the command zone.
There are also other ways to improve ability to spend mana; MDFCs can be nice non-land plays if a land isn’t needed, and there are also impactful mana sinks like X-cost and multi-use activated abilities.
Utility lands can be another great way to avoid running out of gas, as long as their abilities are relevant to a deck’s goals.

Putting It All Together: The R Battle Cruiser Deck

So, to pivot over to our example here, how does my R deck do on these criteria that I’ve mentioned?
In terms of mana curve, it has a horrendously high average nonland card cost of 5.33 and nothing below four mana in the 99; its cheapest ways to dig for additional mana are some draw effects at four mana that require additional game actions to actually begin drawing, and I guess some cascade cards on five.
You could say that the deck’s ramp helps it out in terms of getting lands, but this deck would much rather ramp and hit its land drops; each land is effectively worth two mana, meaning that every land drop that this deck needs to ramp out rather than playing normally bears an expensive opportunity cost.
I bumped the land count from 40 up to 41 specifically because I had too many games where I was having precisely this experience, effectively paying mana for land drops that I could be making for free normally.
Okay, so high curve and a high threshold to dig for more lands, these features tell me that a low land count would be tricky to pull off with this deck.
But what about its ability to spend mana in effective ways?
For one, the high curve alone gives the deck a nice boost in maintaining cards in hand; it just can’t play cards very fast early in the game, so its hand size stays big for a while into the game even as it’s spending similar amounts of mana per turn as the rest of the table, and sometimes more.
In addition, though the number of draw spells in the deck isn’t wildly high, like 9 or 10, if I’m being generous about what I consider draw, the deck sees a ton of cards due to the amount of cascade in it, and the fact that several of the draw spells cost four mana means that they pop up reasonably often due to the nature of the mechanic.
Finally, I play a handful of mana sinks such as Captivating Crew for the situations when the deck has an absurd amount of mana but is low on places to put it, and I also have a Panglacial Wurm for when I top-deck a ramp spell and would quite like a wurm to go along with it.
With all of these tools for spending mana, I rarely feel flooded with this deck even though the deck runs 41 lands.

Mulligans, Extra Lands, and Utility Lands

That covers the biggest factors that influence the deck’s land count, but let’s talk about a handful of others that can come into play in certain decks.
For one, the power of mulligans.
You can watch my mulligan video if you want a full rundown of this topic, but the basic gist goes something like this: depending on how they’re built, decks will use their mulligans to find some things more than others.
Some decks will be prioritizing having a piece of ramp in their starting hand, some will prioritize a synergy piece to go with their commander, some will prioritize a low-cost creature, and some will prioritize having three or more lands.
In a probabilistic sense, this is kind of a zero-sum activity; you can only use your mulligans to hunt for so many things, and if you stretch yourself thin on this front you might find yourself dropping down to five-card hands more often than you’d like.
What’s good to keep in mind then is that running a land count closer to your ideal land proportion in your opening hand gives you more wiggle room to use your mulligans to hunt for non-land cards, and 42 is the number of lands in a deck that offers the highest chance for a three-land hand.
My R deck wants a piece of ramp every game without exception, a fairly stringent goal for non-land cards, so running a land count close to this three-land proportion means more three- to four-land hands and more ability to aggressively mulligan for ramp.
Your deck may be more flexible in what non-land cards it wants in its starting hand, in which case a lower land density and a focus on mulliganing for lands may be a more worthwhile plan, but it’s good to consider the trade-offs regardless.

Another reason somebody might want to run a higher land count is that it can allow a deck to have extra lands in hand more of the time.
An obvious application of this is extra-land-play cards; these are often some of the most efficient ramp spells out there, but being able to take full advantage of them requires running a land count high enough to have extra lands in hand.
A less obvious application is that a higher land count gives a player greater control on the timing of when certain lands are played.
A 32-land deck will often need to play a Bojuka Bog on curve in order to not miss a land drop, and this may end up greatly reducing the effectiveness of the card’s ETB; the same goes for Mystic Sanctuary, Mortuary Mire, and Talon Gates of Madara.
Getting substantial utility out of these lands without missing a land drop gets easier the more lands a deck runs.

The “Krip Coefficient”

Finally, let’s talk about one reason a deck might want fewer lands.
Some decks care about what’s on top of their deck more than others, an attribute that I like to call the “krip coefficient.” An example of a deck with a high krip coefficient would be a conventional build of a commander such as Jhoira, Weatherlight Captain; most Jhoira decks run lots of low-cost artifacts, often alongside cost reducers, and then seek to cast a ton of artifact spells in a single turn.
This is a play style that draws lots of cards one at a time and wants drawn cards to be either an artifact or an artifact payoff card, and as a result these decks will generally want to run as few lands as they can get away with.
Storm decks, as many Dargo decks might be considered, care more about the top of their library than just about any other type of deck and will thus usually try to run a very low land count.
A lot of enchantress and spellslinger decks will also rank highly on the krip coefficient, wanting to run as many of their respective card types as possible.
Even my Shorikai slide deck could fit into this mold to some extent with its relationship with cycling cards, though the existence of cycling lands and land-cycling cards makes its relationship with lands quite different than these other decks.
The biggest factor here is that these are high-velocity decks, decks that churn through cards at a fast rate, and also decks that run lots of low-cost cards.
On the other end of things, you’ve got decks with larger-scale draw, particularly ones that have lots of high-cost cards.
My R deck is more on this end of things; it runs a couple cards that draw cards one at a time, like Beast Whisperer and Soul of the Harvest, but outside of top-deck situations with these in play it doesn’t care too much about what percentage of lands it draws.

Conclusion: A Checklist for Land Count

So that’s how I came to the land count in this particular deck: very high average mana cost, a high threshold to access draw, strong ability to spend mana and maintain cards in hand, and a need to mulligan aggressively for ramp rather than for lands.
You can follow a process for this like I did here, and I’m showing a checklist on screen right now that you can screenshot if you like, though this is a rather crude summary that shouldn’t be treated as gospel for every deck.
I should also note that, though I position 35 to 37 as a moderate count with the number of MDFCs.

#21
Meta CommentaryJul 2025186K views

The Ethical Case for Playing Simple EDH Decks

The Strange Ethical Case for Playing Boring Decks
We question whether the hipster ethos of building unique Commander decks is good for the format.We argue that high card diversity causes information overload, especially in pickup games with strangers, making it harder for new and experienced players.Playing simpler or more common decks might be more ethical and lead to greater overall fun.
Use this in your deckLimit the number of obscure cards in your deck to reduce opponents' information overload during pickup games.
Format HealthMeta Trends
Principle
  • Playing highly unique or complex decks in casual Commander can create an overwhelming experience for others, particularly new players.
  • Choosing simpler, more familiar strategies may be a more considerate way to maximize fun in pickup environments.
Evidence
  • A four-player Commander game can feature over 150 unique nonland cards, approximately ten times more than a typical 60-card format duel.
  • The format's rapid release of thousands of new cards each year makes it difficult for even experienced players to recognize every card.
  • Processing unfamiliar cards consumes mental energy that players would otherwise use for threat analysis, planning, and politicking.
  • New players face a steep learning curve as they must digest a huge volume of unfamiliar card text and board state information.
Deckbuilding Application
  • When building for unknown pick-up groups, consciously limit the number of niche or rarely seen cards to help lower the cognitive barrier.
Limitation
  • The creative freedom enabled by Commander's vast card pool is a legitimate and cherished part of the format for many dedicated brewers.
  • The argument is not that players should abandon unique decks entirely, but that they should weigh the social cost of high card diversity in certain contexts.
Example
  • our friend Hans recently built a deck so filled with obscure cards that even we, a Commander expert, could not recognize many of them, illustrating information overload even among veterans.
More quotes
  1. I am an honest-to-god, dyed-in-the-wool EDH hipster.
  2. Is having a weird-off with the table where everybody tries to do the most performatively unusual things actually a good way to play Magic?
  3. Might it even be more ethical, better to one's fellow Magic players, to play a deck that's simpler or does something more common?
  4. Commander offers an elegant one-size-fits-all solution with a broad card pool and a slim ban list.
  5. Pick up EDH can be an absolute information overload, and this broader problem can be broken down into two separate subproblems, card diversity and gameplay diversity.
  6. With Wizards of the Coast increasing the number of brand new cards they print into the Commander format from the high hundreds per year to multiple thousand per year, keeping up even as an experienced EDH player can be difficult.
  7. Getting a foothold in that environment will be a difficult and time-consuming process even in a setting with somewhat consistent decks and players.
  8. [In cEDH,] fewer cards are viable, and that's good, actually.
  9. Whereas in cEDH, there is a smaller group of viable strategies with off-meta tech being a spicy exception rather than the rule, high-power casual is packed to the rafters with off-meta because there isn't a meaningful meta.
  10. I'd also wager that this is where a big dose of frustration comes from in pick-up magic, particularly in games that straddle the line between mid and high power, and where players enter with differing expectations.
Full transcript
The Hipster's Nagging Doubt

I am a hipster.
I am an honest-to-god, dyed-in-the-wool EDH hipster.
I like to play weird, unusual decks that do weird, unusual things with weird, unusual cards.
The idea that I might be playing a deck that does something nobody has done before is extremely cool to me, and that specific task is something that is far more possible to do in EDH than in just about any other format.
The format has a card selection that's almost as wide as Vintage is; it has thousands of different options for commanders.
The fact that it doesn't have a single set power level means that no deck is fully squeezed out as long as you're willing to shop around for an appropriate table to play at.
The level of freedom that the format offers to deck builders can be genuinely overwhelming for the people who aren't used to that, but for the most dedicated brewers of the world, it's basically one big playground of possibilities.
Do you have a funky vision you want to realize?
EDH can absolutely accommodate that.

I do firmly believe all that about the format, and I have long had the goal of pushing the envelope on how weird and niche casual EDH can get.
But for the past couple months, I've also had a nagging thought.
Is the version of the EDH format I'm describing actually a good one?
One that leads the most people to have the most fun?
Is having a weird-off with the table where everybody tries to do the most performatively unusual things actually a good way to play Magic?
Might it even be more ethical, better to one's fellow Magic players, to play a deck that's simpler or does something more common?
So, here, I'm going to explore that nagging doubt.
But first, I need to lay out a bit of context.

The Pickup EDH Context

The period between 2002 and 2011 saw the EDH format grow from a scarcely known novelty to a format Wizards of the Coast was willing to print cards specifically for.
A big step in this process was its adoption as a casual pick-up format among Pro Tour judges, with the late Sheldon Menery effectively serving as the format's guide and foremost advocate during this period and beyond.
The appeal here is clear; it was a more casual way of playing that could manifest at events once the more cutthroat 1v1 matches of the day had been concluded.
Even after the first precons were released, this culture of pickup games remained strong across the format.
Anecdotally, I played a decent amount of Commander during downtime at local limited and standard events in 2014 and 2015, and it just made sense as a format to play when people were looking for a bit of variety.
In his article for Polygon a few years back, Charlie Hall highlighted some of the appeals.
If a handful of people want to play a game of Magic, rather than figuring out an appropriate format to play or double-checking that a couple casual decks are built under similar ethos, Commander offers an elegant one-size-fits-all solution with a broad card pool and a slim ban list.

Though EDH has since become its own main event in most cases, played among friend groups and in dedicated leagues at local game stores, there are visible indications of the lingering influence of pickup games in the direction Wizards of the Coast has taken with the products they market to Commander players.
While they used to sell a product called Deck Builder's Toolkit as a way for casual players to build their first deck on their own time, these got phased out a few years ago to be replaced with greater Arena support and more products for Commander, the now de facto casual format of paper magic.
Relevant to my point here, most of these products are preconstructed Commander decks, which are ready to play out of the box.
If you're sitting in a game store and a few other people start to play Commander, you can walk up to the counter to buy a precon and some sleeves, and you'll be good to go in a few minutes, assuming you have a couple helpful compatriots to help you sleeve up.

Now, at this point you may be saying, "But Alex, how is this relevant?" Well, let's take a minute to think about the pickup EDH experience, where you sit down to a table and start up a casual EDH game with three unfamiliar opponents who are playing largely unfamiliar decks.

Information Overload in Pickup Games

Put simply, it can be a little overwhelming sometimes, and I'm speaking purely from an information standpoint.
With the format being slow and four-player, the board gets gradually clogged with increasing numbers of assorted permanents of various stripes, with the purpose of any single one being potentially unclear.
And this is EDH in the year of our Lord 2024, so all of these have a lot of text, too much text, some might argue.
With thousands of Commander options, there's a good chance of at least one of these at the table being one you haven't played against.
Knowledge of how your opponents plan to win will likely be incomplete at best, and next to nonexistent at worst if nobody bothered to pose that question to the table.
Pickup EDH can be an absolute information overload, and this broader problem can be broken down into two separate subproblems: "card diversity" and "gameplay diversity."

Card Diversity as a Barrier

As far as card diversity goes, the EDH format has tens of thousands of cards available to it, and a much less restrictive ban list than Legacy, which otherwise shares a similar card pool.
Not only this, but since the format is 100 card singleton, seeing any singular card in a deck gives far fewer implications for what the rest of the deck might contain compared to other formats, and also results in far more unique cards in each deck.
Multiply this by three to account for the multiplayer aspect of the format, and there will be a lot of cards to keep track of just as a baseline.
Traditionally, veterans of a given format will be better able to keep up with different things opponents are playing due to wider card knowledge, but with Wizards of the Coast increasing the number of brand new cards they print into the Commander format from the high hundreds per year to multiple thousand per year, keeping up even as an experienced EDH player can be difficult.
My friend Hans built a funky new deck recently, and even as somebody who basically thinks about EDH as a profession, there were a surprising number of cards from this deck that I had no familiarity with whatsoever.
In a game with several decks containing unfamiliar cards, just processing these new pieces of cardboard and figuring out how to play against them in real time can eat up precious mental energy that could otherwise go toward planning future turns, politicking, and performing threat analysis on other cards.
And that's with a group of experienced players.

The new player in EDH is a whole different beast.
While the number of different non-land cards in opponent's decks in a 60-card 1v1 format might be somewhere in the 15 to 20 range, in EDH that number might be 10 times that amount.
Getting a foothold in that environment will be a difficult and time-consuming process even in a setting with somewhat consistent decks and players; in an environment with rapidly changing decks and players, this is amplified.
I'm not saying it's impossible.
There are plenty of magic players who have learned the game through EDH, and I'm sure there are some who even thrived in this high-stimulation, information-dense environment.
There are also likely plenty of people who learned the format somewhat more gradually, but who had plenty of fun throughout the whole process, even if they weren't keeping up 100% the whole time.
I certainly don't think it's worth anyone's time and energy to push players away from the Commander format; it has clearly taken a hold of casual magic, and there are lots of aspects of it that are likely fun for newer players.

What I am saying, though, is that increasing the card diversity of decks does meaningfully increase the length of time it'll take for a new player to get a foothold within the format.
When I made my video about cEDH a few months back, I got a number of interesting comments from people who prefer competitive EDH specifically because there are fewer viable cards in the format that require keeping track of.
A few of these were people who had jumped straight into cEDH from a 60-card format or a different card game entirely, and this makes sense on some level.
If you're looking to play Commander seriously, and you don't want to have to bother with learning the thousands upon thousands of cards you might see in casual EDH, cEDH is a nice alternative.
It may even be, as a casual EDH player in the comments pondered, a smoother experience for those wanting to play Commander with strangers for more reasons than one.
I see this as the most reasonable response to my main gripe with cEDH.
Yes, fewer cards are viable, and that's good, actually.

Gameplay Diversity and Threat Assessment

The issue of the EDH format's card diversity is compounded when decks are equally diverse in their play patterns.
The origins of the format were people throwing together their bulk cards they couldn't play in Standard and playing them under the banner of a commander that was vaguely related at best, and this likely resulted in unfocused blobs of mid-range being a dominant deck type, maybe with some funky synergies coming to the fore here and there.
And as a casual pick-up format, this makes sense.
If decks are built more to produce relaxed games and showcase unusual cards than to win, the presence of those cards can take center stage.
Like, sure, you might have to refresh yourself on what Frailly Supplicant does, but it's probably not going to rapidly spell your downfall.
However, as decks have gotten more streamlined and have started to accomplish increasingly specific tasks increasingly quickly, lacking knowledge of the ins and outs of what your opponent's deck is doing starts to become more of a problem.
As you might expect, this effect also scales with power level up to a certain point.

If you're playing against unaltered precons, you can usually at least assume there will be some sort of synergy-based mid-range thing going on along with a couple predictable side synergy packages.
If you're playing against a handful of mid-power mid-range decks, there will likely be less certainty and more surprises, but you still can probably get at least a rough idea of what's going on without knowing the opposing decks.
However, it's the power level I'd describe as "high-power casual" where things start to feel really weird when playing with strangers.
Infinite combos, locks, and back-breakingly powerful value engines get assembled on a whim, and with only relatively subtle hints to indicate their approach.
Whereas in cEDH, there is a smaller group of viable strategies with off-meta tech being a spicy exception rather than the rule, high-power casual is packed to the rafters with off-meta because there isn't a meaningful meta beyond some archetypes being a bit more popular than others.

Now, I do love me some high-power casual, and I can even see a sort of weird allure in playing with unfamiliar people and unfamiliar decks.
It's a zany, explosive form of magic where games are often quick and where there's a ton of sleuthing the ins and outs of opposing decks that happens on the fly.
But I'd also wager that this is where a big dose of frustration comes from in pick-up magic, particularly in games that straddle the line between mid and high power and where players enter with differing expectations.
If everybody is already needing to process the cards and decks of three different opponents, and they don't know the cohesive plan of any one opponent, let alone three, well, this is an environment where it's very easy to slip through a power play that opponents just aren't equipped to challenge.
Sure, one could argue just hold up removal, but that misunderstands the nature of threat assessment.
If I know my opponent's decks, I can think to myself, "Okay, I'm 90% sure player A doesn't have the pieces to combo off on their next turn, and player B should be two mana short of a game-winning spell." And then decisions can be made on whether to hold up interaction based on if I have other useful plays to make that outweigh that risk.
But, if instead of being 85% sure it's safe to tap out, I have no firm estimate whatsoever, what exactly am I supposed to do?
Hold up mana every turn?
The answer is probably hold up mana when it's nice to do so, and don't when it's not, and maybe do my best to follow context clues; but then on some level it's just a random luck check for whether or not I lose to some right?
This effect is unfortunately amplified the more unique ways of gaining advantage and winning exist within a game.
You can keep track of some number of cards commonly associated with combos, but when decks have plans that are weird or unconventional, there will be plenty of things that slip through the cracks.
In competitive terms, you might call this "brewer's advantage," but in a casual context, it can create dynamics that aren't necessarily positive for everybody involved.

The Playgroup Contrast

With all that in mind, it might make sense that the experience that gave me my love for weird offbeat EDH decks was actually an environment that had basically none of these problems.
From the fall of 2019 to the summer of 2021, my playgroup of five people played about 200 games with a set of 10 budget decks, with each deck seeing an average of one major rebuild over the course of those two years.
Though several of these decks were quirky and evocative, and though the card choices in them got pretty funky, the fact that we were exclusively playing with these 10 guild divided decks meant that the plans for each deck and the cards used within those plans became all but public knowledge.
Multiple players basically learned the game through this pool, and despite the diversity within the pool, this was an ideal environment for that.
You could learn the game pieces and what each deck was doing reasonably easily, and even if you'd forgotten something, other players at the table were li.

#22
DeckbuildingJul 2025164K views

Reducing Dead Draws in Commander Deckbuilding

Snail's Guide to Drawing Less Junk
We explain how to minimize dead draws among non-land cards in Commander decks.We emphasize building synergistic decks, carefully evaluating subtheme support, and using a tagging system to track card interactions.The key is to ensure each card is useful in multiple situations and supported by enough other cards.
Use this in your deckTrack card synergies with a category system and cut those that will be dead draws in more than one out of four early games.
SynergyDeck ConsistencyCard EvaluationDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • Dead draw mitigation starts with evaluating a card's standalone function and its synergies.
  • Commander's higher life totals and multiple opponents make synergy essential, as cards must combine to produce sufficient impact.
Evidence
  • A deck of 35 Swamps and 64 Rat Colonies demonstrates that perfect synergy and task uniformity remove dead draws entirely, at the cost of strategic flexibility.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Label cards with categories like Flicker, ETB, or Graveyard Enabler to evaluate how many other cards they synergize with across game states.
  • For each card, consider the situations where it is powerful versus dead, and estimate its dead draw percentage based on your deck's composition.
Limitation
  • Subtheme cards like Metastatic Evangel can become dead if reliant on an easily removed commander or if they only interact with a small fraction of the deck.
Example
  • In a Kyler humans deck, Copper Coat Vanguard remains useful with any human board state, whereas Increasing Devotion requires specific enablers like Kyler or anthems to avoid being a poor draw.
More quotes
  1. In the worst case, you've got the dead card: a card that you draw and it doesn't do anything meaningful whatsoever.
  2. Drawing a land when you don't want one, or not drawing a land when you do, will always be a struggle.
  3. An exclusively rat colony and swamp deck has an exceptionally specific game plan with little to no flexibility.
  4. EDH as a format encourages synergy. And by synergy, I simply mean cards doing more together than they do on their own.
  5. This is the case because the format is 40 life instead of 20 and has three opponents instead of one.
  6. Paired with the commander, [Metastatic Evangel] can turn powerful plays into utterly earthshattering plays, but it also doesn't do much to smooth over [the commander's] weaknesses.
  7. When I'm building decks, I'll typically keep track of support for different themes and sub themes via [Archidekt's] category system.
  8. Quadrant theory exists to make sure that you're thinking about all of the situations a card might exist in, not just the ideal ones.
  9. This concept was originally designed with limited formats in mind, where cards are typically simpler and players tend to be on the lookout for narrow slivers of advantage.
  10. [Drakuseth, Maw of Flames] is a good symbol of what this deck is best at. It's just always decent.
Full transcript
The Nature of Dead Draws

Magic: The Gathering is a card game where cards are drawn from a deck, and this naturally introduces a certain amount of randomness.
Sometimes draws are good, other times they're less good; I imagine this isn't news to you if you play this game a lot, either that or you've had a luck streak worthy of legend.
In the worst case, you've got the dead card, a card that you draw and it doesn't do anything meaningful whatsoever.
To some extent, this sort of draw is just a fact of life, and it's not something you're going to be able to fully avoid.
Drawing a land when you don't want one, or not drawing a land when you do, will always be a struggle.
And though MDFCs, utility lands, and other tools can mitigate some of this unfortunate variance, it will always be a somewhat present force.
But what about among non-land cards?
Well, the number of non-land draws that end up being more or less dead can vary immensely deck to deck, and this number will often result from the specific choices that went into the deck building process.
So, here, I'm going to go through the sorts of cards that often end up being dead draws in a deck and how to make your deck play more smoothly without harming the things you enjoy about it.

A Deck With No Dead Non-Land Cards

To start with, let's talk about an example of a deck with more or less no dead non-land cards: a deck with 35 swamps and 64 rat colonies.
All of this deck's non-land cards synergize with each other, and you'll generally never draw a card and have it be entirely useless.
However, this obviously comes at a cost.
An exclusively rat colony and swamp deck has an exceptionally specific game plan with little to no flexibility; either a board with a moderate number of high power, low toughness creatures wins you the game or it doesn't.
I choose this example not as a point of aspiration, but rather as an indication of what reduces dead draws.
These rat colonies have no variation in the tasks they perform, all creatures that can beat down opponents, and they also all synergize with each other.
So, the start of dead draw mitigation starts by paying attention to these two things: what does a card accomplish, and what else does it synergize with?

Synergy and Dead Draw Mitigation

Let's start by discussing the latter.
EDH as a format encourages synergy, and by synergy, I simply mean cards doing more together than they do on their own.
This is the case because the format is 40 life instead of 20 and has three opponents instead of one, conditions that necessitate that gameplay be volumetrically larger; you simply have to have more stuff going on.
Obviously, the most simple version of this is the rat colony example: your deck is made up of a creature that will have more power the more copies of it that are in play.
But this isn't enough volume for a lot of EDH games.
So let's take the example of a somewhat larger lad: Kyler, Sigardian Emissary.
Kyler is a five mana 2/2 that gets a +1/+1 counter whenever another human enters and gives your other humans +1/+1 for each of those counters.
So, this is a commander who can dramatically power up a board of humans if left unchecked, but whose high mana cost and small starting stat line make him a bit of a removal magnet.

Naturally, the primary synergy package here is humans, but different cards have different profiles of the situations in which they're good.
Copper Coat Vanguard is good as long as you have other humans, a pretty decent assumption for a humans deck, and also gives the commander a bit of extra protection.
This card should rarely feel useless and could even be a pretty solid anthem if there are tokens out.
That brings me to another card: Increasing Devotion.
This is a large-scale token creator, and this one is slightly more complicated in its power profile.
It's fantastic if you currently have Kyler on board plus some other humans to swing with, it's decent if you have some other human ETB effects on board like Lossarnach Captain or Champion of the Parish in play, also decent if you have an anthem at the ready to pump them up in size, and pretty mediocre if you have none of these support cards.
The flashback prevents it from ever feeling truly horrible, but it might be a passable play at best if you have neither the commander available nor any of the 15ish cards in the deck that can amplify this card up to being a decent play.
Most of the popular synergy cards for a Kyler deck will do things with humans, and with some exceptions, most of these will be pretty okay.
