However, synergy can go a bit deeper; you can venture into the magical world of sub-themes.
Take +1/+1 counters.
The commander uses them, and the average Kyler deck has around 12 cards in the 99 that also feature +1/+1 counters.
So, some players choose to add Metastatic Evangel, a card that proliferates whenever another non-token creature enters under your control.
Paired with the commander, this card can turn powerful plays into utterly earth-shattering plays, but it also doesn't do much to smooth over Kyler's weaknesses; if he gets removed, the counters go with him.
Put into the average Kyler deck, this card benefits 12 other cards, which isn't nothing, but also isn't great.
Paired with its somewhat ephemeral support of the commander, I'd skip this card personally.
Now, does this mean our Phyrexian cleric friend belongs in no decks?
Not necessarily.
Some players might be running larger numbers of +1/+1 counter cards than the average player does, and others might be building the deck in a way that's likely to keep Kyler around more of the time.
This card might even be a star player in some of these decks.
It's certainly not a "bad card," but these are the sorts of considerations you should be making when you're deciding whether or not to add a synergy piece.
How often will it be in a situation to support other cards, and how substantial will that support be?

Tracking Support with Architect's Category System

When I'm building decks, I'll typically keep track of support for different themes and sub-themes via Architect's category system.
I'll have the baseline categories I put cards into, but also secondary categories for things like flicker, ETB, graveyard enabler, graveyard payoff, and so on.
When I'm adding cards and trying to flesh out a list, I'll be mostly viewing the deck on the primary categories tab to keep things visually neat.
But as I start to prune and sculpt the list, I'll start bouncing back and forth between that and the multicategory tab and see if the various parts of my deck have the right amount of support.
This multicategory approach is also nice for evaluating individual cards because I can immediately see and be reminded of all of the reasons I included a particular card, which also indirectly tells me the situations in which a card will be useful.

As an example, let's take a card in a subtheme-heavy Phyrexian deck I recently built: that of Rebirth.
The categories here tell me that it's a source of value that's payoff for having things in the graveyard.
So, as a baseline, I can check how much stuff I have that helps me get things into the graveyard with a focus on creatures since that's what this can reanimate.
However, the other categories also tell me things about this card; it will be stronger alongside counter amplifiers like proliferate cards, and it'll also be stronger when I have untap effects on the field.
It's also an artifact and a low-cost one at that, so it'll synergize with several other cards for that reason alone, and those cards are already helpfully organized into a category for me to check on.
I can think about all the other combinations of cards this card may or may not be on the field with, and based on that, I can approximate roughly how often this card will be useful and roughly how often this card will be dead.
The answer for this card is that it'll be dead some modest percentage of the time in the early game, maybe 25% of the time, but there are also situations where it'll be very powerful due to other supporting pieces being around.
So, I've currently deemed it worth staying in.
I also love the art and flavor of the card, which does end up being a non-negligible factor for a lower power deck like this.

So, that's synergies, but that's only one part of what makes a card dead or not dead.
Another big factor to keep in mind is what stage of a game a card is useful in.

Quadrant Theory and Situation Analysis

And before I get into that, I briefly want to introduce a concept: quadrant theory.
This sounds complicated, but it's basically just a way of analyzing different situations in which a card could be played.
A basic example goes something like this: we have this four-quadrant box, and in this case, we'll choose to label the top side based on the size of an opponent's board and the left side based on the size of my board.
Now, let's consider the card Teleportal.
How is this in different situations?
Well, if we both have small boards, this card sucks, not many creatures to affect and not many creatures to get past.
If I have a small board and my opponent has a large board, this is still pretty bad, but maybe there are some niche scenarios where it lets me get in for the last couple points of damage after my opponent has stabilized against my aggression and it can be cast single target for two mana in a pinch.
If I have a large board and my opponent has a small board, the unblockable isn't doing much, but at least I'm getting a power buff on all my creatures.
Where this card is a straight-up banger is when we both have large boards; this is the sort of situation where I'm probably praying for this card as a top deck since it's getting maximum effect.
This concept was originally designed with limited formats in mind, where cards are typically simpler and players tend to be on the lookout for narrow slivers of advantage.
Fundamentally though, quadrant theory has a straightforward and universally applicable purpose: it exists to make sure that you're thinking about all of the situations a card might exist in, not just the ideal ones.

A variant of this might be to analyze a card based on the stage of the game we're in.
Take a card in a mid-power deck.
How is that card in the early game?
How does it fare in the midgame?
Now, decks will typically gain more card advantage over the course of the game, so it's fine to weight your analyses a bit more heavily toward the later parts of the game as long as you have the support to get there.
But you should still consider different versions of that late game.
There will be the version of the later game where you're hitting your stride without setbacks, but what happens if a board wipe is played?
How about if you have your board, but your life total has been taxed heavily by a more aggressive deck?
How about if a mid-range opponent is pulling far ahead and you need to either kill some key permanents or heavily diminish their life total?
Different games will demand different things, and you should figure out how your different cards play in those different games.

Game Stage Analysis in the Rata Deck

For a simple example, let's talk about my Rata deck, a deck designed to be as resilient and consistent with its gameplay as possible.
It typically curves mana dork commander into four mana ramp spell into six and seven drops with the goal of continuing to ramp and eventually getting to 20 or more lands.
With that in mind, let's talk about a couple cards that are present here.
Drakuseth, Maw of Flames is a seven mana 7/7 flyer that deals four damage to one target and three damage to two targets when it attacks.
Oddly, this is a card that I'm actually most excited to see early, as landing it on turn four onward can be a pretty backbreaking opener if it doesn't get removed due to its sheer power as a removal engine.
However, it's still plenty solid as a top deck later in the game, what with its good flying statline and attack ability that remains respectable even if the creature size game has been heating up a bit.
This sort of card is a good symbol of what this deck is best at; it's just always decent.

For an example with a decidedly different power profile, let's consider a different card: Captivating Crew.
Now, this card is pretty bad in the early game.
Four mana to threaten an opposing creature is just never what I want to do with my mana in the first six turns of the game, and I'm rarely all that happy when my turn four or five cascade card pops out this treason-triggering troop rather than a ramp spell.
However, what keeps me from cutting the card is its sheer versatility in the late game.
As long as I have enough lands, it almost always feels good.
If I'm ahead on board, I can pull the last two or three blockers out from under an opponent and smack them down before they have the chance to stall things out.
If I'm behind on board, it often means that I had a more aggressive but worse scaling roll-out in the early game, in which case I can often leverage opposing life totals to get some favorable jump blocks or at least utilize some abilities on opposing creatures.
If the board is in a state of parity, it's the best of both worlds; I can nab my opponent's best threats and turn a five-attacker, five-blocker standstill into an 8/2 or 9/1 smackdown.
I was a bit hesitant to add this card to the deck initially, but the sheer consistency with which it feels great in the late game has made it one of my favorite top decks.

Playing Highly Specific Cards Responsibly

So far, the lesson of we have been more or less: be careful with cards whose use cases are highly specific.
But I can already hear some of you saying, "But Alex, I want to play those cards.
You're describing a lot of my favorite cards.
Are you telling me I need to cut those?" And the secret third part of we i that you can play those cards, and you can do it fully responsibly, but doing so requires one specific thing: card selection.
This may be achieved by playing lots of filtering or by playing a generally high-velocity deck, but the basic principle is simple; dead cards won't matter as long as you simply see more cards.
A basic example of this might be an ongoing source of draw.

#23
DeckbuildingJul 2025146K views

How to Use Card Categories in EDH Deckbuilding

The Most Forgotten Part of EDH Deckbuilding
Categorizing cards into core functions like lands, ramp, draw, protection, and interaction clarifies a deck's structure and reveals its strategic shape.A separate 'theme' category early on can mislead, as many theme cards should overlap with core roles.Ramp and interaction categories must be tailored to the deck's specific timing and goals to avoid inefficient choices.
Use this in your deckBefore finalizing your deck, ensure every nonland card contributes to at least one core function: ramp, draw, interaction, or protection.
MidrangeInteractionMana BaseCurveDeck ConsistencyDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • Categorizing cards into core functions (lands, ramp, draw, protection, interaction) reveals the deck's fundamental shape and primary game plan.
  • Ramp and interaction categories should be defined based on what they accomplish for the deck's specific strategy, not just by card type.
Evidence
  • Standard starting counts (35-40 lands, 15-20 removal, 13-18 draw, 7-12 ramp, 3-8 protection) leave almost no room for pure theme cards without overlap, illustrating the tight slot budget.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Always include a card in a core category if it performs that function, even if it also fits a theme; use subcategories for theme identity.
  • When building ramp, ask whether the deck needs to accelerate to specific mana values by certain turns or just scale into larger spells.
  • Treat interaction cards as tools to set opponents back; recognize that different interaction types (counters, fogs, removal) serve different purposes and cannot fully substitute for one another.
Limitation
  • Creating a discrete 'theme' category too early leads to overvaluing cards that do not contribute to core functions, causing decks to lack necessary mana, draw, or interaction.
  • Rigid non-overlapping categories cause bloat; overlap between categories is essential for a streamlined deck.
Example
  • A two-mana rock like Worn Powerstone functions as ramp only if the deck's acceleration goal is not to reach four mana quickly.
  • Murder can stop combos or clear blockers, but Fog excels defensively while being useless against value engines, showing why interaction categories must be tailored.
More quotes
  1. If somebody asked me for healthy starting ranges for their core categories for a bog standard EDH deck, I'd say something like 35 to 40 lands, 15 to 20 pieces of removal equivalent, 13 to 18 pieces of draw, 7 to 12 pieces of ramp, and 3 to eight pieces of protection.
  2. What you may notice about that is that if you go on the higher end of each of these values to be safe, that adds up to 98 deck slots. You got a whole one left over to work with after these core categories.
  3. The implication here is that cards that don't fit into one of these categories are inherently charging your deck an opportunity cost, and you should keep that in mind when deciding how many of them to have.
  4. I've seen a lot of guys recommending people include a category such as theme as a discrete entity alongside these other sorts of function categories, at least in the early stages of deck building, and I simply cannot recommend that as a general practice.
  5. In most successful decks, a large number of these theme cards will also be accomplishing the other core tasks I mentioned earlier: mana acceleration, value generation, protection, and ways to slow opponents.
  6. The card Murder performs one game action, destroying a creature. But this game action can accomplish a number of different tasks.
  7. [Because interaction serves multiple roles,] your instinct might be to back away from this fact and pretend it doesn't exist. But for purposes of categorization, all this means is that in lieu of perfectionism, we can focus on building categories that are flexible and work with our deck's needs.
  8. Protection plus counter spells and removal plus defense. This might sound odd at first, but it makes some sense if you think about all interaction in terms of how proactive versus reactive.
  9. I'll often include graveyard exile in a removal stacks or general interaction category because most decks won't gain anything by granting this necessary tool its own category.
  10. Worn Powerstone isn't a ramp spell if you're playing a Grenzo deck because it's fun to speak in cryptic absolutes. But what I really mean is that if your acceleration category is mainly intended to get you to four mana a turn early, Worn Powerstone doesn't fit that function and thus probably isn't worth playing unless you can find another purpose.
Full transcript
A Typical Deckbuilding Evening

Let's say it's a lovely Tuesday evening and you're out there living your best life, building a new commander deck from the comfort of your couch.
You start by finding cards; maybe they're from EDRE, maybe they're from Scrifall Searches, and maybe they're from your friend Brad, who happens to be an expert on four-toughness creatures.
Regardless, you're finding lots of cool stuff for your deck, and you're tossing them into some sort of deck building website, watching them neatly segregate themselves by card type before your very eyes.
This is fine at first, but over time, things will start to get a bit muddier.
You know the numbers of the card types, which will be helpful for some decks, but additional information is hard to come by.
What if you need to know how much low-cost ramp you have to decide on how many higher mana cards you can support?
What if you need to know how many cards allow you to cast things from outside your hand for the purposes of Flaming Tyrannosaurus?
What if you're trying to make sure that you've got the right number of things to slow down opponents and protect what you've got?
This is where categorizing starts to become really useful: you can lump a specific group of cards into their own little bucket and keep track of them that way.
Though a lovely tool on the surface, categories can range a bit in their usefulness.
They can be a helpful tool in deck building, but they can just as easily add more confusion than they elucidate or even lead players down unfortunate deck building paths all on their own.
So, here, I want to talk about what categories accomplish and how to get the most utility out of them in deck building, as well as how some people are setting back their own decks by using them wrong.
Before bumming you out with that though, I want to tell you about something exciting.

A Word from Our Sponsor

It's we sponsor, Void Monster.
Voidmonster.org is a site created by MTG artist Forest ML, who you may know from the original docside, Rest in Peace, and it features some really punchy, evocative designs for trading card game accessories.
There's the awesome cycle of mana symbol playmats, and there's also an exclusive new drop once a month that's available for a weeklong pre-order period, but gone after that.
This month is the greedy drop, a pot of greed shirt and playmat with an added goldfoiled treasure token if you get both, and you can pre-order that until July 6th.
You can check that out at the link in the description.
And thanks to Void Monster for sponsoring this piece.

What Categories Accomplish: Core Tasks

Okay, so the first thing categories do is capture the core tasks that the cards in your deck are performing and allow you to specifically look at these subsets.
I always start out with categories of card that most decks will want at least some of, principally "lands," "ramp," "draw," "protection," and "interaction." These categories are useful because, all on their own, they start to paint a picture of the shape of what a deck is doing.
If a deck runs a lot of lands and ramp, I know I'm going to be looking at a big mana mid-range deck that's looking to outscale its opponents.
If I see a deck that's lower on cards intended to interact with opposing plays and higher on protection, I know I'm going to be looking at something that's faster and more proactive.
I'm going to get more into constructing these categories and when to break them up, but first, I want to lay out a cautionary note.
I've seen a lot of guys recommending people include a category such as "theme" as a discrete entity alongside these other sorts of function categories, at least in the early stages of deck building, and I simply cannot recommend that as a general practice.
This is a somewhat misleading piece of advice because in most successful decks, a large number of these "theme" cards will also be accomplishing the other core tasks I mentioned earlier: mana acceleration, value generation, protection, and ways to slow opponents.
There may be some cards that only do their theme, such as one mana birds in a safara deck or muroke lords in a hackball deck, but a whole lot of other theme cards will often be contributing to one of these core categories.
If somebody asked me for healthy starting ranges for their core categories for a bog standard EDH deck, I'd say something like 35 to 40 lands, 15 to 20 pieces of removal equivalent, 13 to 18 pieces of draw, 7 to 12 pieces of ramp, and 3 to 8 pieces of protection.
What you may notice about that is that if you go on the higher end of each of these values to be safe, that adds up to 98 deck slots; you've got a whole one left over to work with after these core categories.
This is not to say that you necessarily need these numbers in every deck or that there won't be overlap between categories, but the implication here is that cards that don't fit into one of these categories are inherently charging your deck an opportunity cost, and you should keep that in mind when deciding how many of them to have.
With that out of the way for now, let's get into the meat of these core categories because there can be nuances in what a category should cover.

Lands: The Simple Foundation

Lands are the simplest because the deck building role lands generally matches up one to one with the card type of land.
You can make an exception for non-mana producing lands such as glacial chasm or specific centerpiece lands like Mesa's End since these won't be producing mana as their primary function.
But for most other cards, I keep them categorized as a "land" first and foremost with any other functions entered as subcategories I can look at later.
In this case, the utility of including or excluding lands from your lands category comes from how you use that category.
As long as the cards in your lands category are cards that you personally consider a land drop, you can do some math on the resulting number and figure out how successful you'll be at making it up to your higher costed spells.

Ramp: Varying with Purpose

Speaking of which, let's talk about ramp.
This is one of the trickier core categories because it can vary so much.
Most decks want some of it; some decks want a lot of it; a handful of decks won't want much at all.
So, when constructing your ramp category, you should be asking yourself, why am I ramping?
If your ramp is geared to hit specific cards a turn early, you'll want to be specific with the curve of your mana, and it even might be worth including ritual style cards that can give temporary mana in some circumstances.
By contrast, if your ramp is generally geared toward increasing the volume of your mana without specific timing goals, less card efficient options like smaller ramp and ritual style cards won't make as much sense, and bigger options will likely work out.
You'll sometimes catch me saying something like, "Warn Power Stone isn't a ramp spell if you're playing a Cranco deck," because it's fun to speak in cryp absolutes; but what I really mean when I say that is that if your acceleration category is mainly intended to get you to four mana a turn early, Warn Power Stone doesn't fit that function and thus probably isn't worth playing unless you can find another purpose for it.

Interaction: Proactive vs. Reactive

The same goes for interaction, which I choose to define here as cards that exist to set opponents back in some way.
The card murder performs one game action, destroying a creature, but this game action can accomplish a number of different tasks: killing a big attacker, clearing out a blocker, stopping a combo, taking care of a value engine, eliminating a stacks piece, and so on.
This fact gets a bit more puzzling when we consider that there are lots of cards that perform some of these tasks, often better than a murder can, but can't perform others.
Fogs are great defensively and even against some combos, but they're useless for slowing opponents down.
Counter spells will be good at protection and combo breaking, but the timing will restrict their usefulness in limiting opposing value, and they can't deal with things already in play.
Your instinct might be to back away from this fact and pretend it doesn't exist, but for purposes of categorization, all this means is that in lieu of perfectionism, we can focus on building categories that are flexible and work with our deck's needs.
One categorical starting point I've used before is having two slightly fuzzy buckets: protection plus counter spells, and removal plus defense.
This might sound odd at first, but it makes some sense if you think about all interaction in terms of how proactive versus reactive it is.
Protection is very proactive in the sense that it purely supports your own game plan and involves thinking ahead toward possible issues, and counter spells are similar but with a bit more flexibility to hit opposing threats.
Removal of all types will be a bit more reactive since it gets played after a problem card has resolved, though since it can target value engines, it could still play more of a calculated long-term role in a deck.
Defensive cards like attack and damage prevention are the most reactive in my mind since they're a thing whose only purpose is preventing a player from losing, the final line of defense in an EDH game.
With these different types of cards laid out, the two buckets approach basically just draws an arbitrary line somewhere in the middle of the spectrum of tools, though it might be useful to get more specific.
If you have a deck that cares about a particular interactive tool like defense or stacks to hinder things that might otherwise give you problems, give it a category.
If you have a deck that cares about protection, definitely give it a category; it'll be good to keep an eye on.
Putting this extra thought into categories will also give you a better idea of what your deck does or doesn't need.
I'll often include graveyard exile in a removal stacks or general interaction category because most decks won't gain anything by granting this necessary tool its own category.
This won't be the case for all decks though.
My Malfagore deck has three separate categories for his interaction sweep because they all serve different roles.
Discard effects put my opponents behind in value; removal deals with active threats on the board; and graveyard exile serves an unusually key role here in preventing opponents with graveyard oriented decks from getting use out of the cards I'm causing them to discard.
On the other hand, if different interaction pieces aren't serving clearly defined roles that you want to keep an eye on, it might be perfectly fine to throw them together in the same category; it all depends on how much you care about distinguishing somewhat different effects from one another.

Draw and Value: A Continuum

The same is true for draw to a lesser extent.
If you have a bunch of normal card draw in your deck, you might call that category "draw." If you add in some handful of draw adjacent things like Leia or Futureight, those are also probably fine in the draw category as long as you're not a quasa player, though you could also rename the category to "value" or something; the name isn't too relevant.
What is relevant is when different draw adjacent cards start to perform different tasks.
In my draw video, I laid out that various things that allow a player to see and play with more cards can be placed along a continuum based on how much of a player's library they allow you to see.
In the middle is ordinary draw, seeing as many cards as it acquires, and on the edges are pure filtering spells which generate no card advantage and pure value cards which don't even interact with the player's library.
In some cases, all of these can be lumped into the same category: tutors, card draw, recursion, and so on.
But other times, different cards might start to take on different roles.
You might run tutors or filtering spells to allow you to find the tools to build up some sort of central engine for your deck, and in that case, you might want to keep the digging cards and the value cards in different categories to keep an eye on how many of each you've got.
That's just a case where categories might be nice.
Depending on how you're building value, it might be essential to lay out more clear roles.
Some decks might run a bunch of draw, discard, looting cards as their card selection to find what they need, and also some recursion or other graveyard synergies as their sources of value to get advantage over their opponents.
In that case, those two things are going to be playing quite distinct roles in the deck to the point where they basically become a whole separate thing: a synergy package.

Synergy Packages: Setup and Payoff

This is the second major thing categories do, allowing people to keep track of synergies.
Specifically, for each synergy package, I'll typically include two categories: "setup" and "payoff." As an example, in a flicker deck I have, I include a core cards category that includes my three flicker engines plus a card that increases the overall velocity of the deck, and I also include a category called "m tasty slide" that features creatures with ETBs I might want to repeat through flickering.
Relevantly, the former category is a primary category because pushing my flicker game plan is the only thing these cards accomplish, whereas the other category is a subcategory that I don't need to see all the time.
This is because being flickerable isn't the primary role of these cards; it's simply an attribute about them.
The fact that they have an ETB that's useful to repeat is only a relevant fact when I have an astral sight effect available, and their role is typically the same in either case, just amplified if they're being flickered.
There might be other situations where both setup and payoff are in their own subcategories or where these things are layered into different categories, whatever ends up making the most sense to you.
Regardless, the utility that the categories are creating for you in this case is allowing you to keep an eye on how well supported a specific theme is.
It's easy to throw a flicker thawsa into your deck after spott.

#24
DeckbuildingJul 2025117K views

A Tool to Diagnose Commander Manabase Issues

My Brother and I Built a Tool to Yell at You About Your Manabase
We argue that manabase construction is deceptively complicated because lands must fulfill two roles: providing the right colors and hitting land drops.It introduces a new tool that uses hypergeometric distribution to model land draws and identify issues.The tool calculates odds of drawing land combinations that can cast all spells, and the difference between total lands needed and color-producing lands determines how many utility lands a deck can support.
Use this in your deckSubtract the number of color-producing lands required from your target land count to determine how many utility lands you can include.
Mana BaseDeck ConsistencyDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • Effective manabases require accounting for not only the total number of color symbols but also their intensity and when spells need to be cast.
Evidence
  • The manabase tool calculates exact probabilities for drawing every possible combination of land categories using a hypergeometric branching tree structure.
Deckbuilding Application
  • In casual EDH, prioritize consistently hitting land drops by running enough lands (e.g., 37) before adding utility lands.
Limitation
  • The tool simplifies by modeling land draws statistically rather than simulating full games, so it may miss some gameplay nuances.
Example
  • A deck that requires 37 lands to hit drops and 33 lands to produce colors can support four colorless utility lands.
  • A counterspell demands less blue mana early than a master of pearl trident because it is reactive and cast later.
More quotes
  1. One of the most deceptively complicated tasks of building a Magic: The Gathering deck is constructing a mana base.
  2. Lands are the foundation upon which a deck is built, and as with construction, a shaky foundation will lead to a shaky deck.
  3. If somebody asks you what's going on in your Prosper deck, you're not going to say, 'I'm glad you asked. It's running 35 lands, six two mana rocks, and a Sol Ring with an even blend of 21 black and 21 red symbols and an approximately 15% tap land saturation.' That would be strange.
  4. A Ball Lightning has the same total symbol requirement as three Shocks, but those two selections of cards apply very different levels of demand on a deck's red mana.
  5. Counterspell has the same mana cost as Master of the Pearl Trident or Counterbalance, but it generally applies less strain on a deck's blue mana since it's a reactive card that will likely be played later in the game.
  6. In limited, your task will generally be to create the least bad mana base you can out of basics and whatever nonbasics you were lucky enough to get your hands.
  7. The available fixing is so good in EDH that decks often include some number of lands which are lackluster in their color producing abilities, instead played for other benefits they provide. These are usually called utility lands.
  8. I actually like to think of land bases of decks as existing to hit two different goals: one is covering a deck's symbols, and the other is covering total mana values.
  9. If a deck wants 37 lands for purposes of hitting its land drops but only needs 33 lands to produce an acceptable number of symbols to cast its spells, that leaves four slots for colorless producing utility lands.
  10. There are a ton of unique ways to screw up a mana base: poor color pip distribution, inadequate overall pips, too many tap lands for a deck's intended play pattern, not enough overall lands,.
Full transcript
The Deceptive Complexity of a Manabase
Why Manabases Are Hard

The task of symbol management exists in all formats, limited, 60-card constructed, and EDH, but when it comes to how this task is accomplished, things diverge.
Whereas in limited your task will generally be to create the least bad mana base you can out of basics and whatever non-basics you were lucky to get your hands on, in EDH your options will be a whole lot less limited.
Dual lands can simply be put into your deck, and not just guildgates or gain-one lands; a plethora of different high-quality fixing lands are available, from pain lands to fetches to triomes and beyond.
The available fixing is so good, in fact, that EDH decks often include some number of lands which are lackluster in their color-producing abilities, instead played for other benefits they provide.
These are usually called “utility lands.” But how many of these is it appropriate to play?

Two Goals: Symbols and Land Drops
A Word from Our Sponsor: Dragon Shield Sleeves
Introducing the Manabase Tool
How the Tool Works

There are plenty of ways we could come up with these categories, but I decided to just have one category for each possible combination of the five colors, giving us a total of 32.
This is also the number of unique values that can be represented by five bits, which means that if we number those categories just right, the bits that store the number for each category will also store the combination of colors its lands can produce.
If we’re willing to get our hands dirty with some bit-level operations, we can get a ton of mileage out of these categories.
This is ultimately how we determine if a set of lands can cast a spell, but that process is a little too complicated to explain here.
If we test every combination of lands to see if they can cast a spell, we can get a probability for how often a given number of lands will be able to cast a given spell.
By running this for different numbers of lands, we can get an idea of how many lands we need to cast the spell, and by factoring in the land count of the deck we can figure out when we will draw those lands.
This lets us determine how often we can cast a spell on curve and how much time we spend waiting for the right lands to cast it.
If we do this for all the spells in the deck and average the results, we can get an overall representation of how well a deck’s manabase can cast its spells.
Once we have a way to score a manabase like this, we can test out small changes to find the best way to improve it.

So this is the backbone of the tool.
Average delay in casting spells is the benchmark here that is used to judge decks, alongside “cast rate,” which here means how likely it is that a card will be castable on a turn on or before its mana value.
The tool also tests small changes in the deck, seeing how it is changed by adding a single basic land for each type among the next colors as well as a Wastes.
This is helpful for pointing out which colors are most needed, and the inclusion of Wastes also gives insight about how a deck’s land count is looking.
Adding a Wastes to a deck shouldn’t really help it cast spells with less delay, or at least it should help a lot less than adding something that actually taps for colors.
If adding a Wastes reduces the average delay of a deck by a substantial amount, that’s a good indicator that it might need more lands, or at least that land count is a bigger problem for it than color fixing.

Testing the Tool: A Malagon Deck Example
Assumptions and Interpretability
#25
DeckbuildingJul 2025110K views

Rethinking Interaction in EDH Deckbuilding

The Things EDH Players Get Wrong about Interaction
We argue that EDH players often misunderstand interaction by relying on a checklist of card types.Instead, they should treat interaction as a network of overlapping solutions to problematic game states.Defensive permanents, graveyard exile, and other situational tools can be more efficient than traditional removal.The ideal interaction suite depends on the deck's strategy, draw power, and the specific threats in its meta.
Use this in your deckList the specific game states your deck struggles with and ensure multiple overlapping solutions exist in your interaction suite, not just a checklist of card types.
RemovalStaxTokensInteractionThreat AssessmentCard EvaluationMeta TrendsDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • The value of an interaction piece is determined by the specific game states it can solve, not by its ability to target a wide range of card types.
  • Relying on a mental checklist of removal types often leads to wasted deck slots and functional gaps.
Evidence
  • Graveyard exile, defensive permanents, and hate pieces are often more mana-efficient than generic spot removal for handling recurring threats.
  • Decks with high life pressure or life gain cause opponents to play cautiously, reducing the need for dedicated removal.
Deckbuilding Application
  • When tuning a deck, identify the most common problematic play patterns in your meta and select interaction that answers those patterns through multiple lines.
  • If your deck already includes defensive elements like Ghostly Prison or counter magic, you can reduce the count of traditional removal spells.
Limitation
  • There is no optimal universal count for interaction spells because overlap and deck-specific draw power vary widely.
  • An interaction suite effective in one meta may fail in another.
Example
  • Against an opponent reanimating Archon of Cruelty with Animate Dead, viable removal includes destroying the creature, the enchantment, countering the spell, or exiling the graveyard target.
  • Against a go-wide Krenko deck, a defensive card like Ghostly Prison or life gain from Soul Warden can serve as effective interaction, reducing reliance on board wipes.
More quotes
  1. In EDH, problems are often more complicated than one single problem permanent that needs to be removed, and even more complicated than just being the occasional spooky board that needs wiping.
  2. Single target removal and generic board wipes can be the solution to a lot of problems, but they're far from the only options available, and a lot of times they won't be the most efficient way to tackle a given problem.
  3. Sources of graveyard exile are generally extremely cheap per mana, as are stax cards, defensive permanents, and other things that stick around to hinder multiple opposing plays.
  4. As you get deeper into tuning an individual deck, it's generally good to move away from deciding counts of different cards using a simple template.
  5. There's no way to know exactly how many removal spells or overall interaction spells you need because different forms of removal will have overlap in the profile of what they can answer.
  6. I don't know if you've ever watched how a person with 14 life conducts themselves in a commander game, but it's generally very different than how somebody with 34 life conducts themselves.
  7. That 14 life person will often find themselves holding back on attacks and chump blocking more often than they'd like, and they're also going to be more conservative in the sorts of spells they play.
  8. For that reason, having a higher degree of life total pressure can often allow for a bit smaller of a removal count.
  9. Careful threat assessment and a few swings with a big commander can be worth countless pieces of removal.
  10. Different decks will utilize removal for very different purposes: if you're playing a combo deck, you want to focus your interaction on dealing with obstacles to your combo and spending removal on only the most imminent of threats.
Full transcript

Interaction is a well-trodden question within the EDH community, with raging debates over how much is too much, how little is too little, what types are the best, and what types are the rudest.
I've thrown my hat in this ring before, but what has struck me recently is how many people seem to not really understand interaction as a concept and what it is accomplishing for any given deck.
As with many subjects, we as the EDH community have created lots of little myths about interaction and removal.
When I say myths, I don't mean strictly untrue statements, but rather kind of true things accepted as undeniable fact or sometimes true things overgeneralized to be always true.
So, I want to tackle some of these misunderstandings and, in the process, drill into the fundamentals of what interaction does, why different decks want it, and how best to use it.

To start with, let's talk about what's worth considering alongside your removal when deck building.

Beyond the Checklist: Interaction as a Network of Solutions

I've occasionally gotten some flak because I tend to have a pretty wide scope of what I consider "interaction" and "removal," and I'll sometimes organize my interaction suites in ways that other people find unintuitive.
Graveyard exiling is a form of removal; bounce spells are a form of removal; and defensive cards like Propaganda are a form of removal, though the degree this label fits will depend on the deck.
I think the reason this rubs people wrong is because they have a very specific idea of what "removal" entails.
Removal is when you remove an opposing thing, and more specific tools like defensive cards, graveyard hate, protection, and pinpoint land destruction deserve to be placed in an entirely different mental category, their own boxes on a checklist.
In some decks, these things will warrant this sort of separation, but in many others, doing this is a waste of time at best, and in the worst cases, actively counterproductive to constructing a functional deck.
No, instead of this sort of checklist approach, the best way to think about interaction is as a network of overlapping solutions to a number of specific game states.

Let's say an opponent plays Animate Dead to bring back an Archon of Cruelty, and let's say this poses a problem to us.
How can we shut this down before they start attacking us with it?
Well, we could destroy the Archon of Cruelty with creature removal; we could destroy the Animate Dead with enchantment removal; we could counter the Animate Dead with a counterspell; and we could use instant-speed graveyard hate to exile Archon of Cruelty in response.
There are some less common approaches as well, but let's stick with these four.
In this case, all four of these options would be ways of removing this threat, dealing with this game state that I've decided is problematic to us.
I'll allow myself a single cheeky comment here and point out that, for our purposes, a Braids is not a removal spell and Cremate is.

For another example, let's say our opponent is playing a Krenko deck and is starting to pop off: several goblins, a couple sources of untapping, an Anger in the graveyard, the works.
A piece of instant-speed creature removal could deal with this so long as Krenko isn't wearing Swiftfoot Boots or something.
However, there are a number of less absolute ways to slow down or survive this sort of play.
You could have a Soul Sister route out to allow your life total to keep pace with the number of goblins on board, or you could have a defensive card like Ghostly Prison already in play to limit the number of attackers; you could even have a Thalia, Heretic Cathar to have the goblins enter tapped as punishment for their token crimes.

Now, just what point am I trying to make here, you might ask?
Put simply, in EDH, problems are often more complicated than one single problem permanent that needs to be removed, and even more complicated than just being the occasional spooky board that needs wiping.
Single-target removal and generic board wipes can be the solution to a lot of problems, and I'd never tell you to avoid these cards; but they're far from the only options available, and a lot of times they won't be the most efficient way to tackle a given problem.
Sources of graveyard exile are generally extremely cheap per mana, as are stax cards, defensive permanents, and other things that stick around to hinder multiple opposing plays.
As a result, it's beneficial to get to know these types of cards and the sorts of decks and metagames where they could play the role of more traditional removal better.

Card Types Are a Starting Point, Not the Whole Picture

When newer players are being inducted into the ways of the Commander format, discussions of interaction tend to center card types as the thing to focus on: you want a way to hit creatures, to hit artifacts, to hit enchantments, and so on.
To be clear, I have no problem with this; card types are undoubtedly a good thing to think about as a starting point.
However, as you get acquainted with more common play patterns of decks and which ones you tend to have a problem with, it becomes much more useful to focus on situations that need to be remedied.
Some decks won't care about certain types of threats as much: a go-wide token deck isn't going to be as spooky if you're playing a thick-boarded mid-range deck; a pinging engine isn't going to be as spooky if you're playing a deck with a good amount of life gain; decks with more explosive plays aren't going to be as spooky if you're already packing a handful of hate pieces, and so on.
If you're playing a deck with a focus on some defensive cards, you're already going to be somewhat shielded from a lot of plays that involve large combat steps; and if you're playing a deck that runs some counterspells for protection, you're already going to have some ways to nail critical centerpiece cards or combos.
Likewise, an interaction suite that does a good job of handling the threats of one table might not do as good a job at a different table; you need to know the environment you're stepping into.

To clarify, if what I'm saying sounds overwhelming, you shouldn't worry about it too much.
What I'm describing is what takes place during a potentially months-long process of tuning and pruning a deck, not necessarily what you should be doing when you're first putting together a deck.
It's fine if you're using templates or rough rules of thumb to start out with, but as you get deeper into tuning an individual deck, and as you get more experienced as a deck builder, it's generally good to move away from deciding counts of different cards using a simple template.
There's no way to know exactly how many removal spells or overall interaction spells you need, because different forms of removal will have overlap in the profile of what they can answer, and different interaction spells will have different degrees of how much they hinder opposing plays.
Not to mention, different decks run different amounts of draw.

Tailoring Interaction to Your Deck’s Game Plan

Some of my decks will run interaction counts in the teens, but others might run twenty-some or even thirty-some cards that interact with the resources of opponents in some way, excluding life total, and excluding life total isn't even necessarily a fair thing to do.
I don't know if you've ever watched how a person with 14 life conducts themselves in a Commander game, but it's generally very different than how somebody with 34 life conducts themselves.
That 14-life person will often find themselves holding back on attacks and chump blocking more often than they'd like, and they're also going to be more conservative in the sorts of spells they play.
Tapping out to draw a bunch of cards or play some powerful amplifier permanent is perfectly commonplace for somebody at a higher life total, but it's a terrifying proposition when finding just a little bit of extra damage could let an opponent swing lethal.
For that reason, having a higher degree of life total pressure can often allow for a bit smaller of a removal count.
All sorts of late-game permanents that would generally be good candidates for removal become a lot less of a concern if their controller is in range of being rapidly focused down.
This is one reason why Magister Sphinx is such a useful card in my Sharuum Slide deck; the deck generally looks to build toward a late-game win, so having a way to put an opponent on death's door if they're pushing out of control a bit too early is a sweet escape valve that takes a bit of pressure off the deck's control spells.

Overall, though, this life total rule is generally going to be most pertinent in aggro decks and especially in Voltron decks, which typically look to knock down players one at a time with commander damage.
Careful threat assessment and a few swings with a big commander can be worth countless pieces of removal; and for the pieces of removal that do exist in a deck, it can be beneficial to have them be targeted more towards the stages of a game that are worth worrying about.
Get rid of some blockers; kill something that can let an opponent stabilize; remove a bigger mana dork that might let an opponent contest you.
Flame Slash isn't going to be a great card in most slower decks because it misses a whole lot of stuff that you'll see in the later stages of a Commander game, but that's not super important if your most important turns are going to be happening earlier.

Ultimately, all I'm trying to say here is that different decks will utilize removal for very different purposes.
If you're playing a creature-based aggressive deck, you might want to focus on options that can clear out blockers, and you likely won't want much in the way of conventional board wipes unless you have ways to make them one-sided.
If you're playing a combo deck, you want to focus your interaction on dealing with obstacles to your combo and spending removal on only the most imminent of threats.
In either of these cases, your interaction will exist predominantly to buy yourself time, and things opponents have that require time on their end, like draw or a mana engine, might not be a concern to you at all.
By contrast, if you're playing a slower, more controlling deck, you'll want to focus more of your removal on knocking out resource generation, such as card draw engines, since not letting opponents get ahead of you will be important in winning in the long game.
You also might want to focus on interaction that targets multiple things or which accomplishes multiple tasks, since those tend to be more card efficient.
These cards do tend to cost more mana, though, which brings me to the final myth I want to tackle.

The Myth of the Universal Staple

Let's talk about the interaction staples of the format, specifically when they should be played or not played.
Typically, the forms of removal considered "staples" are the ones that cost very little mana and hit a limited range of targets, such as Nature's Claim, or which cost a bit more mana but which hit a variety of different targets, such as Generous Gift.
There are also a handful of board wipes that end up heavily played because they're just hands down the best thing available in a given color.
Now, am I about to explain to you why these cards are garbage that you need to rip out of your deck?
No. In fact, I'm about to tell you exactly why these cards are generally so good, because I think a lot of people have a tendency to accept that these cards are always the best option and treat it as dogma, a primordial and unshakable feature of our world alongside gravity and the rising of the sun, and this does these cards a bit of a disservice.

Let's start with flexible removal spells.
Why are Beast Within, Chaos Warp, and Generous Gift good cards?
Right off the bat, let's make something clear: one of these cards I'm a lot less likely to play than the other two.
Can you guess which one?
If you said Generous Gift, you're correct.
And that's not an insult to the card itself; it's functionally identical to Beast Within, after all, unless your opponent is playing an elephant deck.
No, I merely say this to gesture at the fact that Beast Within and Chaos Warp are exceptional cards.
They are hard removal in colors that don't really have access to much hard removal for creatures, and in the case of Chaos Warp, in a color that lacks enchantment removal on top of that.
There are several two-color combinations in which these will be some of the only removal for certain permanent types you'll get, and that's to say nothing of our monocolored compatriots.
So, you won't catch me wasting my breath trying to get people to take these out of their decks.

However, there are also two-color combinations where you'll have access to lots of other options.
Beast Within won't feel as essential in a green-black deck since you'll have a number of other reliable ways to remove creatures; and as I alluded to earlier, any deck that includes white is going to have ways to deal with a variety of permanent types already available.
So, does that mean these cards aren't worth playing in color combinations where they're not in some wholly unique niche?
Not necessarily.
For one, these hit lands, so the importance of that can vary a lot depending on the power level and matchup you find yourself in.
Secondly, if your interaction suite is relatively narrow, somewhere in the ballpark of 8 to 12 cards, there will be a lot of utility in having each of those cards be as flexible as possible.
If I'm packing nine pieces of interaction, after my first seven draws of the game plus my opening hand, I've on average seen around one and a quarter of these.
If I'm running 14 pieces of interaction, I've seen around two instead; and if I'm running 18 pieces of interaction, that number is more than two and a half.
If I have a diverse interaction suite and I'm seeing multiple pieces of interaction pretty early into a game, there's a good chance I'll have a way to deal with a specific problem unfolding in front of me.
But if that average is lower, running more of these versatile staples will likely be a good idea.
Personally, I prefer to run a higher.

#26
Format TheoryJul 2025106K views

Tutors and Power Level Variance in EDH

The Difficult Reality of the Tutor Question
We explain that debates about tutors in casual EDH arise from conflating card variation (seeing different cards) with power level variance (fluctuations in deck power).We argue that tutors reduce the former but increase the latter by making powerful cards appear more often.Using Sol Ring as an example, it shows that not all powerful cards inject the same amount of power variance due to contextual factors.
Use this in your deckIf a single overpowered card is your tutor target in most games, consider cutting either the tutor or that card to lower your deck's power variance.
Deck ConsistencyPower LevelDeckbuilding RestrictionsDesign IntentDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • EDH's larger deck size and singleton rule cause higher card variation than 60-card formats.
  • Card variation (seeing different cards) is separate from power level variance (how much a deck's power fluctuates game to game).
  • When a powerful card is already in the deck, adding tutors raises the frequency of that card's impact, increasing the deck's expected power level.
Evidence
  • Probability: any given card is about one-sixth as likely to be drawn in EDH compared to a 60-card deck.
  • Expected power level can be quantified: a non-tutor deck might have an expected power of 3.3 while a tutor version is 4.1, even though both have a 10% chance of a power 6 game.
Deckbuilding Application
  • To reduce power spikes, a deckbuilder can remove tutors that consistently find the strongest card.
  • Synergy-driven decks with many sub-themes can use tutors to smooth disjointedness, but omitting tutors forces greater inherent deck cohesion.
Limitation
  • Not all powerful cards cause equal power variance; Sol Ring's impact is often less dramatic because it only provides mana.
  • The abstract expected power model oversimplifies real cards, as different powerful cards accomplish different tasks and context matters.
Example
  • Sol Ring: its power is mitigated because it is low cost (falls off after turn five), it only gives mana (dependent on having spells to cast), and it is early in the winning pipeline.
More quotes
  1. The tutor is part of the immediate problem here.
  2. A game with a lot of card variation is different from a game with a lot of power level variance. You can have one without the other, and a defense of one of these things isn't necessarily a defense of the other.
  3. The deck that runs the tutors will be more powerful than the deck that does not.
  4. Soul Ring is powerful, but its power is somewhat less felt than a lot of other cards.
  5. The most problematic instances of Soul Ring tend to be where there was already a bit of power level imbalance between decks.
  6. Calls to ban Soul Ring, or now to put it on the game changers list, exist for a reason.
  7. Combos or combo-adjacent cards tend to lead to the most jarring instances of power level variance because the table doesn't have much time to react.
  8. Removal can be a tool for power level variance mitigation on the scale of a game.
  9. I've been jokingly calling [Sol Ring] my Wizards of the Coast mandated dose of variance.
  10. Power level variance will be felt more sharply the further along this pipeline a card is due to the social nature of the EDH format.
Full transcript
The Tutor Question and Deeper Disagreements
What Variance Means in Commander
Distinguishing Card Variation from Power Level Variance

When it's already a foregone conclusion that a powerful card is going to exist in your deck, the question of whether to run tutors for it then becomes the question of whether you'd prefer to have that powerful card frequently or infrequently.
On a probabilistic level, the deck that runs the tutors will be more powerful than the deck that does not.
Let's say we're using a, I don't know, seven-point power scale; that's a fun number.
If drawing into a particular power card naturally leads to a six-power game, tutoring it leads to a five-power game due to the extra cost and/or inconvenience of the tutor, and not having the card leads to a three-power game, the expected power of the deck that runs tutors will be higher than the deck that does not.
If you're figuring out what power level games to play the deck in, and you're a certified math dork running the odds, you could say, "Well, the expected power level of the tutor version is 4.1, and the expected power level of the non-tutor version is 3.3," and then this could determine the decks you choose to play this deck against.
Problem is, both of these decks are going to have that power level six game 10% of the time; it's just that one set of opponents is going to be a bit better prepared for that situation than the others.
This abstract approach demonstrates the core of the issue with decks having a higher degree of power level variance, but that abstractness makes it a bit less helpful in understanding real-world cards because different powerful cards accomplish very different tasks.

The Power Pipeline: Where Cards Fall

Following the pipeline model, what falls on the opposite end of the line?
Well, it's ways to win the game, typically ones that are near instantaneous, such as combos or combo-adjacent cards.
These tend to lead to the most jarring instances of power level variance because the table doesn't have much time to react; you go from playing the game to having it end without enough time for any sort of table-coordinated response.
I'll elaborate more on why this sort of thing constitutes a problem in a bit, but naturally when there is a problem, a card that wins the game immediately will be the problem.
Incidentally, this pipeline model also makes it clear that removal can be a tool for power level variance mitigation on the scale of a game.
Between Sol Ring and cards that outright win the game will be a bunch of cards somewhere in the realm of what might be considered an engine.
The engines that only provide card advantage will typically be closer to Sol Ring in terms of their position on the pipeline; card advantage is quite good, yes, but it can take a little while to fully pay off into a win since mana will still be a limitation.
The One Ring can feel quite powerful, but the table should still have a decent bit of time to work together and stop a One Ringing player if they start to get out of hand.
The engines that are further down the pipeline are the ones that grant mana in some form as well; think something like Bolas's Citadel or Arcane Bombardment.
When one of these hits the board, the table might be a single turn cycle away from fully losing control of the game, or even less in some cases.

A Four-Point Model of Threat Imminence
The Social Cost of Power Level Variance
#27
Community ChallengeJul 2025101K views

Deckbuilding Challenge Winners: Underused Commander Decks

I Challenged My Community to Deckbuild with Some of the Least Popular Commanders
We present the winners of a community deckbuilding challenge featuring five unpopular commanders.We analyze each winning deck's strategy, highlighting how they creatively used the commanders' abilities, and offers suggestions for improvement.Honorable mentions are acknowledged and linked.
Use this in your deckAdd recursion cards like Scrap Trawler or Myr Retriever to reuse low-cost modular creatures and maintain pressure.
Senator PeacockBlueBlackRedGreenMidrangeCountersStaxDeckbuilding RestrictionsSynergyInteractionCurveDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • Commanders with two distinct abilities should be built around synergies that use both halves to justify the card choice.
Evidence
  • The winning Senator Peacock deck combined modular creatures with her abilities; modular creatures want to die to trigger her draw and become unblockable to deal damage.
Deckbuilding Application
  • In artifact creature decks, use modular creatures to turn temporary +1/+1 counters into persistent threats through sacrifice and evasion.
  • In group-hug decks, break symmetry by using effects like phasing to deny opponents the donated resource during their turns.
Limitation
  • Modular decks are constrained by a small card pool where many creatures are overcosted, forcing inclusion of suboptimal cards.
  • Asymmetrical group-hug setups demand significant deck space for enablers like specific lands and recursion, potentially diluting the core strategy.
Example
  • The Senator Peacock deck includes Arcbound Hybrid despite its mana inefficiency solely for the modular keyword.
  • The Shizuko deck uses Talon Gates of Madara and land sacrifice effects to phase out the commander and maintain a one-sided mana advantage.
  • Inkmoth Nexus and Plague Myr are the only infect cards in the modular deck, used sparingly to avoid excessive aggro.
More quotes
  1. For this commander there was a clear winner: a deck called 'filthy Stacks players modular Beatdown deck.'.
  2. A clear goal for building a Senator Peacock deck is figuring out how to use both halves of the card.
  3. Modular is a brilliant solution to this challenging question.
  4. Infect can really put opponents on edge and attract unwanted attention.
  5. It’s very funny to play a group hug commander only to put a lot of effort into denying opponents the hug.
  6. The winner of Shizuko was a spell tax lands deck with the goal of repeatedly looping Talon Gates of Bandara to keep Shizuko phased out on opposing untap steps, preventing opponents from gaining her mana.
  7. The third commander, Éomer, Marshal of Rohan, was the card that inspired the challenge.
  8. The number one deck was 'lock fins reanimator deck' , yes, that’s mono-red reanimator.
  9. Extra combats from Éomer mean extra zombies from God-Pharaoh’s Gift, allowing the deck to build advantage as successive combat phases pass.
  10. Artifact creatures that transfer their +1/+1 counters to other creatures when they die have both a clear reason to want to hit the graveyard and a clear reason to want to be unblockable.
Full transcript

Two weeks ago, I announced a deck building challenge, and that challenge went as follows: choose a commander from among Éomer, Marshal of Rohan; Yomiji, Who Bars the Way; Egon, God of Death; Shizuko, Caller of Autumn; and Senator Peacock, and build a deck with it, prioritizing creativity, cohesion, and likelihood to produce dynamic gameplay.
Over the course of the ensuing week, an explosion of deck building occurred, far more than I expected, honestly, and at the end of this period, I was left with around 250 decks to dig through.
I went through every single one of these decks, reading primers and scoping out card choices and feeling out the vibes until I was left with a handful of finalists for each of the commanders; I then discussed these finalists with a couple friends, who helped me pick out a winner for each of the five.
I will now be announcing those winners, talking about each of those decks, and also giving some honorable mentions, which will be shown off in the “r” tab on my website.
I’ll also give some ideas for improvement where I see fit.
These are all great decks, and many are creative in ways I probably wouldn’t think to be, but the task of improving an already great deck is a fun one, and I love having fun.

Senator Peacock

Let’s start with Senator Peacock, a five-mana commander who makes all your artifacts Clues, artifacts which can be sacrificed to draw a card, and who makes a creature unblockable whenever you sacrifice a Clue.
Most decks submitted for this fell into one of two categories: “artifact good stuff” decks and “political” decks that played off the commander’s Senator status.
For a few honorable mentions, we had an artifact-based combo deck, a ninjas deck, and a stax deck, all of which can be found in a link in the description.
However, for this commander, there was a clear winner among both me and the people I discussed things with: a Modular Beatdown deck.
One of the difficulties with Senator Peacock, and a reason she likely had so few decks as of the start of this challenge, was that for each of her two abilities, there are commanders that do something similar at a better rate; this makes her hard to justify as a commander choice if you’re primarily only using one of her two modes.
So a clear goal for building a Senator Peacock deck is figuring out how to use both halves of the card: the sacrifice to draw as well as the evasion.
“Modular” is a brilliant solution to this challenging question.
Artifact creatures that transfer their +1/+1 counters to other creatures when they die have both a clear reason to want to hit the yard and a clear reason to want to be unblockable.

Now, all of this comes at a cost, and that’s that there aren’t very many modular creatures, and a lot of the ones that do exist are terrible.
This deck runs all the ones within its color identity that cost four or less mana, which ends up being a total of nine.
Some of these are still pretty bad, such as Arcbound Hybrid, a 2/2 haste creature for four mana, but the keyword is what really matters, and the rest of the deck is built out to bolster that keyword.
The deck is running a number of other cards that can grant and gain counters, as well as nine proliferate cards and eight other cards that can utilize counters in some way.
These include things like Danny Pink that provide payoffs for counters moving around, as well as cards like Giggling Skitterspike and Inkmoth Nexus that provide excellent destinations for counters to be placed on.
I like that this deck doesn’t overuse infect; it’s common for players to throw some extra infect into a proliferate deck as an alternate win condition, which works but can really put opponents on edge and attract unwanted attention.
Here, the infect cards are kept exclusively to Inkmoth Nexus as a potential one-shot with modular, and Plague Myr as a card that doubles as a dork, and this definitely is a healthy place to stop.
Overall, this deck’s beatdown game plan seems quite decent and even features Tangletrove Kelp as a top-end card that goes unbelievably hard with Peacock to bolster its artifact beats.
This deck also features a lovely suite of support cards; it’s packing plenty of interaction, with seven cards aimed at countering spells or protecting things and nine cards aimed primarily at removal or defense, and there are a handful of cards in other categories that can also serve removing purposes, such as Contagion Engine.
There’s also a quite nice card draw and ramp suite: ten cards each that I’d categorize as primarily in one of those categories, but also a lovely range of cards that serve double duty with other purposes.
Tezzeret’s Gambit draws and proliferates, Staff of Compleation ramps and proliferates, Cryptic Trilobite ramps and holds counters, Defiler of Flesh draws and ramps, and Season of Weaving does all sorts of stuff.
This overlapping of roles, along with the deck’s relatively low mana curve, likely lets it play quite smoothly.
So overall, I’m a big fan of this deck; it uses the commander excellently and features a well-chosen and well-rounded suite of cards within it.
It looks capable of putting on pressure in multiple different ways, dishing out good damage but also being resilient and able to play into a longer and grindier game.
To get to some points of improvement, I personally would try to build out more of a recursion suite: Scrap Trawler, Myr Retriever, even something like Inventive Iteration.
All of these provide nice ways to bring back some of the lower-cost sacrifice modular creatures and utilize them further, which could allow the deck to maintain pressure better.

Shizuko, Caller of Autumn

Moving along, the next commander is Shizuko, Caller of Autumn, a three-mana 2/3 that produces three green mana for each player on their untap step that sticks around for the whole turn.
This one is definitely the most open-ended out of the bunch and is also a pretty “group hug” card in general.
Most of the Shizuko submissions involved some blend of “battle cruiser,” “group hug,” and “stax,” roughly in that order of popularity; however, there were also some very different submissions as well.
Honorable mentions for this category included a storm deck, a face-down creatures deck with the goal of not tapping lands on its own turn, and a Protean Hulk combo deck sped up by Shizuko’s mana boost; you can check out all of those in the link in the description.
The winner of Shizuko was once again relatively clear to my team: Spelltex’s land deck.
This is a deck with the goal of repeatedly looping Talon Gates of Madara in order to keep Shizuko phased out on opposing untap steps, preventing them from gaining her mana.
This is a great concept I wouldn’t have ever thought to try, and fully breaking parity on Shizuko’s ability, I definitely asked myself the question of whether it’s worth the effort, and from a strategic optimization sort of standpoint that’s a valid question, but I think there’s enough there to work with.
A one-sided three-mana boost on a three-mana commander is nothing to sneeze at, and worth putting a bit of effort into maintaining, and keeping Shizuko phased out also has some side benefits like strengthening the deck’s boardwipe resistance; it’s also, if nothing else, very funny to play a group hug commander only to put a lot of effort into denying opponents the hug.
As you might expect, this deck runs all the cards needed to enable the Shizuko phase-out plan: tutors to find the Talon Gates, means to sacrifice lands to perform effects, and cards that allow the deck to access lands from its graveyard.
All of these are already great things for a lands deck to have, so the fact that they’re all that’s required to enable this deck’s devious hug denial is a big point in its favor.

As with most land decks, this is a deck that will be looking to acquire different lands for different situations.
Cavern of Souls helps get around counter spells, Boseiju, Who Endures can deal with artifacts and enchantments, Glacial Chasm stonewalls aggression, Scavenger Grounds deals with graveyards, and so on; these lands make up this deck’s toolbox, its base of cards that it can draw on through tutors to help remedy various problems that it might encounter.
In addition, a good portion of these lands have effects that involve sacrificing or discarding them, and then this allows these effects to be utilized multiple times.
Life from the Loam can repeat cycle lands or Bojuka Bog, and all manner of sacrifice land can be repeated through Undergrowth Recon, Ramunap Excavator, or Anus Gloomstalker; this allows the deck to be quite grindy, usually having something it can put its mana into.
The deck also has lots of other neat utility cards woven throughout it.
A number of different options for stax, defense, and removal can be found throughout the deck, many of them concentrated among the lands; though the deck doesn’t have a ton of options for each different type of effect, its toolbox in nature and high density of recursion likely means it should be able to repeat various interactive effects as necessary.
Finally, this deck has a few different options for winning.
The first is a classic: Thespian’s Stage can become a copy of Dark Depths, original Depths is sacrificed to the legend rule, the former Stage is sacrificed to create a 20/20 Marit Lage because it has no counters on it; rinse and repeat with land recursion if the Marit Lage dies.
The deck can also go wide with Field of the Dead or Titania, Protector of Argoth, and Helix Pinnacle serves as a last-resort mana sink win con if the board is forfeit and the deck is buckling down behind a Glacial Chasm or a Constant Mists.
The deck’s looping nature also means that there are some incidental infinite combos if it gets enough mana and pieces, but these feel more than fair; they’re more a byproduct of being a value engine rather than setting up for a combo in the traditional sense.
So overall, the deck seems well-rounded and fun.
If I were to offer a point of critique, I’d say that this deck could use some more options for extra land play; being able to loop a utility land two or three times a turn rather than one is a big deal and could allow the deck to spend its Shizuko mana more effectively.
My friend 33 Elk, who’s a lands aficionado, also suggested Nissa’s Intervention as a good card, as well as a card like Yavimaya, Cradle of Growth that could allow Glacial Chasm and Dark Depths to tap for mana, just a handful of ideas for land players out there.

Éomer, Marshal of Rohan

The third commander among the five was Éomer, Marshal of Rohan, a four-mana 4/4 haste creature that grants an extra combat once per turn when another attacking legendary creature dies.
This was actually the card that inspired the challenge; here was a commander with a wonderfully textured ability on it, and yet it only had around 100 decks on EDHREC.
There were a lot of super cool versions of this commander submitted.
A few honorable mentions were a goblin deck that paired Éomer’s ability with a variety of different goblins, a toolbox combo deck that focused on staying flexible while keeping opponents on their toes, and a hyper-budget “pingers” deck with no sacrifice outlets; all three of these can be found at the link in the description.
For the number one deck, though, we have Lockfins Reanimator deck, yes, that’s mono-red reanimator.
This deck focused around a specific critical card: God-Pharaoh’s Gift, a seven-mana artifact that reads, “At the beginning of combat on your turn, you may exile a creature card from your graveyard; if you do, create a token that’s a copy of that card, except it’s a 4/4 black Zombie.
It gains haste until end of turn.” Extra combats from Éomer mean extra Zombies from God-Pharaoh’s Gift, allowing the deck to build advantage as successive combat phases pass.
Naturally, there are a number of different components that the deck needs in order to accomplish its God-Pharaoh Gifting.

The first of these is tutors, of which the deck is running four.
One of these is Gate to the Afterlife, a card that searches up God-Pharaoh’s Gift by name, so that’s pretty cool; the other three tutors are Gamble-style effects where cards are searched up followed by a discard at random, and these are nice because even if the tutored card gets discarded, the deck has plenty of ways of reanimating artifacts.
Speaking of which, the deck runs four cards that return artifacts from its graveyard to play, including a new one that’s being printed in the upcoming Bracing-themed set; three out of the four of these cards require artifacts to sacrifice, so the deck also contains five cards that generate artifact tokens, and that’s in addition to the fact that about a quarter of the deck is artifacts that themselves can be sacrificed.
The final thing required for this deck’s reanimate plan is ways to get cards into the graveyard, and it has a wide variety of these: there are several filtering cards like Faithless Looting and Brass’s Tunnel Grinder that help the deck selectively toss stuff into the yard, as well as a handful of mill effects like Millikin or Altar of Dementia.
This list of different things required for the artifact reanimation plan may sound like a major hurdle to the deck’s functionality, but thankfully all of these different tools are helpful on their own and most serve other purposes for the deck, which is good because all of that was just to get God-Pharaoh’s Gift on board and fueled up in the first place.

From here, we need to gain some extra combats in order to take advantage of the card.
To do this, we really only need three things: on-board legendary creatures to die while attacking, and effects to ensure their death.
There are some number of fairly conventional sac outlets but also some fling effects and some cards that can copy legendary creatures.
One of these is Blade of Selves, a card that goes infinite with Éomer by creating two copies of him when he attacks that immediately see each other die to the legend rule, giving two additional combats, and this then continues until there are fewer than four players in the game.
A number of submitted Éomer decks included this card, but the fact that this deck gets additional value on each combat phase gives it a much better chance of being able to win the game right then and there rather than just deleting.

#28
DeckbuildingJul 202595K views

Peace Offering Deck: Not a Generic Group Hug Deck

Is the Peace Offering Deck Actually Group Hug?
We argue that the Peace Offering precon, commanded by Ms. Bumbleflower, is mislabeled as group hug.Her ability rewards casting two spells per turn for card advantage, but the precon includes many generic group hug cards that dilute this focused plan.We suggest cutting those cards to better align with the commander's strategy, similar to a past precon's flaw.
Use this in your deckCut the precon's generic group hug cards that do not support casting two spells per turn, and replace them with cards that enable or benefit from that pattern.
Ms. BumbleflowerWhiteBlueGreenGroup HugCountersMidrangeMana BaseInteractionDesign IntentSynergyCard Evaluation
Principle
  • Ms. Bumbleflower's ability gives opponents cards when you cast spells, but only draws you cards if you trigger the ability twice in a turn by casting two spells.
  • This design encourages a focused strategy of casting two spells each turn rather than a generic group hug approach.
Evidence
  • The precon contains many group hug and political cards, such as those with the bloom's gift mechanic, that are not directly tied to casting two spells per turn.
