  • The deck's beaters and +1/+1 counter synergies point toward a beatdown plan that the group hug elements do not strongly support.
Deckbuilding Application
  • A deckbuilder should cut defensive and generic group hug cards to make room for cards that help consistently cast two spells per turn or that benefit from doing so.
Limitation
  • Giving resources to opponents can be politically useful for deflecting pressure from struggling players, but it raises the deck's required performance threshold to compete with the advantage given away.
Example
  • The precon suffers from a similar flaw as the OTJ Gonti precon, which was built as a generic theft deck instead of one tailored to Gonti's specific ability.
More quotes
  1. Just the line 'whenever you cast a spell, target opponent draws a card' is wild.
  2. If you don't cast exactly two spells per turn, she gives your opponents more cards than she gives you.
  3. I find the aesthetic contrast between psychosis crawler and the rest of this deck very funny.
  4. It seems like Wizards added a bunch of group hug stuff because the commander does something vaguely group huggy, but Ms. Bumbleflower is not a generic group hug commander.
  5. I'll take a Manglehorn over [Bloodroot Apothecary] any day of the week.
  6. [For Tenuous Truce and Intellectual Offering,] the rate on the cards per mana just isn't that great.
  7. Octamer is a funny card, but this isn't really a token deck and five mana is just too much to pay for some vanilla stats.
  8. Coveted Jewel is funny, but this deck doesn't have enough ways to prevent opponents from snagging.
  9. Bloodroot Apothecary, though it's pre-selling for upwards of $10, seems highly mediocre to me.
  10. Hoofprints of the Stag is a decent incremental value piece if played early, but it's a very dead draw later in the game.
Full transcript

Hey folks, welcome to the cute Woodland creatures edition of my precon reviews.
A plurality of my patrons thought I should look at the group hug precon, and in we I'm going to accomplish a simple set of tasks: I'm going to lay out what I think this deck is trying to do, I'm going to describe what I like about the deck, and finally I'm going to talk about what I dislike about it and what changes I'd make to fix the things I dislike.
With that in mind, let's jump right in.

The Commander: Ms. Bumbleflower

The commander for this deck is The Honorable Ms. Bumbleflower, and the first thing I note here is that her effect is quite cool and unique.
Just the line “whenever you cast a spell, target opponent draws a card” is wild.
Part of what lets me consider this a group hug commander of sorts is the sheer amount of value you can give to your opponents.
There are general politics commanders like Breena and 2023’s Rocco that passively offer opponents resources but have a built-in way to twist things back in your favor; however, with Ms. Bumbleflower, you’re actively handing your opponent cards when you cast spells, and if you don’t cast exactly two spells per turn, she gives your opponents more cards than she gives you.
The second spell clause gives you cards when the ability triggers for the second time each turn, and this really hones the blue identity of the card: you want to cast two spells per turn, unless you just love giving your opponents cards.

Deck Contents and Game Plan

In the deck list, we can see a number of things that fit in with what this commander is doing.
For one, there are a fair number of decently chunky beaters, most of which would enjoy being given +1/+1 counters and flying; a lot of these utilize +1/+1 counters in some way, and to go with this we can see a handful of other cards with some sort of counter synergies.
In addition to the +1/+1 counter beats, there is some degree of giving other players resources present, as you might expect for a deck labeled “group hug.” There are five cards that utilize Bloomburrow’s gift mechanic, where a spell can have an additional effect if an opponent is offered some sort of resource; there are also 11 other cards I’d describe as “huggy” and six cards that are vaguely political in some capacity, though the line between these two categories I’ve constructed is a bit fuzzy.
There are also other cards whose effect depends on the actions of your opponents, and you could argue that these are political; however, this deck is relatively beatdown oriented, so I’m not convinced it will be easy to talk opponents into feeding these, results may vary.
Basically, it seems like this deck is looking to gradually develop beaters and value engines while currying favor with various opponents, right up until it starts to snowball out of control and can drop the friendly pretenses.

What I Like About This Deck

So that’s the deck, but what do I think about what this deck is doing?
Well, I’ll start with what I like, and there’s actually quite a lot of that.
This deck broadly has a decent card quality, a focused enough plan to win, and good amounts of different types of support cards.
There are several quite decent quality beaters, and Ms. Bumbleflower is solid for purposes of amplifying and pushing through damage for both these and the cards that are more in the realm of support creatures, side note, I find the aesthetic contrast between Psychosis Crawler and the rest of this deck very funny.
The +1/+1 counter package of this deck, though not super wide, has some real all-stars in it; there are several different threats that grow over time, and among the handful of counter synergy pieces, Rishkar, Peema Renegade is a pretty sweet ramp tool, and Colossus and Hydra is a counter-pumping bomb that will run away with games if left unchecked.
The deck will likely be able to politically navigate through the game using its commander, offering some opponents resources while beating down others with its suite of growing threats.

As I mentioned earlier, the quantities of support cards here are also quite decent: there are 14 cards that I categorize as ramp and 15 cards that I categorize as draw, and these should prepare the deck decently well to accelerate into a game plan and keep up momentum even if the deck hits some setbacks.
I playtested this deck a decent number of times, and the average curve out that this deck manages is far better than in either of the other precons I’ve looked at; the deck is able to ramp, build resources with the commander, and set up value engines with quite a decent degree of consistency.
Obviously, when I’m playtesting the deck, I’m kind of abstracting away the cards that opponents are being given, and I like to think that this is reasonable enough.
A lot of EDH games will have one or two players who are lagging behind a bit for one reason or another, and while giving these players resources doesn’t necessarily come at no cost, the gameplay cost will be lower than the cost of giving all opponents resources.
For that reason, I mostly think about this resource donation as being probably not an issue, as long as this deck is meaningfully progressing its own game plan at the same time.
While you could reasonably argue that this means the deck should be held to a slightly higher bar than some other precons in terms of how it plays, it most likely passes even that bar as well; this is just a deck that plays pretty smoothly.

A big part of this is likely the mana base.
The mana base of this deck is pretty similar to the mana base of the Outlaws of Thunder Junction precon I looked at a few months back, which is to say it has a healthy land count and a really robust amount of color fixing that results in quite healthy symbol counts.
Now, this isn’t at no cost: of the 38 lands, nine always enter tapped and seven sometimes do, and I’d guess that these two figures average out to a roughly one-in-three tap land quantity.
While this is a bit higher than most players will want long term, it’s far from a terrible starting point, and this tapland density doesn’t feel particularly bad in practice.
In addition, in the lens of considering this precon as a product, giving players more fixing lands to work with is undoubtedly a good thing, even if it means the deck includes tap lands that might later get cut.

Finally, this deck has an adequate amount of ways to interact with things opponents are doing: two counterspells, three board wipes, five single-target removal spells, and a smattering of other cards that play related purposes, such as slowing opponents, defending against major attacks, and protecting key pieces.
Now, one of those categories I’ve been historically less of a fan of: I find that space dedicated to defensive spells would often be better allocated on actual removal or on things that push a player’s own game plan, and I will be cutting a couple defensive cards from this deck, kind of.
But this deck’s plan of building large evasive threats means that life total races won’t be an uncommon situation, and having a “fog” around in that case might be quite helpful.
The existing political tools, namely the commander, also give it a decent option for negotiating with opponents and avoiding pressure if it seems worth the price of an opponent having some cards.
I can see the appeal of this deck’s political and defensive elements, and I imagine they work decently in tandem with the deck’s more concrete removal for keeping the game under control.

What I Dislike and How I’d Fix It

Alright, it’s time to get into what I dislike about this precon, and also what I’d change to help it out with those things.
I mentioned that the mana base of the deck reminds me of the OTJ Gonti precon, and there’s one other way that this precon reminds me of that one.
In we where I reviewed the Gonti deck, I highlighted that it feels like the deck list was designed to be a generic theft deck rather than a semi-theft deck tailored to what Gonti does.
This precon is similar, in that it seems like Wizards of the Coast added a bunch of “group hug” stuff to this precon because the commander does something vaguely group huggy; but Ms. Bumbleflower is not a generic group hug commander.
No, Snail’s Theory of Optimal Bumbleflower dictates that I want to be sharing card draw with my weakest opponent, maybe playing politics with it here and there, and also making optimal usage of the +1/+1 counters and flying that Ms. Bumbleflower gives me.
In addition, a lot of the group hug elements currently in the deck don’t even feel like they play that nicely with the rest of what the deck is doing.
Basically, what I dislike about this deck is that it’s a bit unfocused, including lots of stuff that is outside what I believe the deck wants to be: a high-velocity, politics-heavy +1/+1 counter beatdown deck.

Now, I understand if somebody wants to play harder into the group hug elements, since group hug precons don’t come that often, but in my mind there’s just not enough to work with here for that.
I will preserve the group hug element on some level, though, in that I don’t feel the need to prevent or take advantage of the cards that Ms. Bumbleflower gives my opponents; they’re allowed to have a little gift.
Another thing that might be nice is making the deck slightly lower curved to allow for double spelling with Ms. Bumbleflower earlier in the game, as the current deck is a bit high-curved for my taste.
Overall, I’m going to make 20 card swaps, and all of these will be done with a precon budget in mind, so I’m not going to add anything over $3 and I’m going to try to end up at a lower overall price for the deck.

Cutting the Hug and Underperformers

With that in mind, I’m going to start by cutting some of the hugger elements of this deck.
I like the idea of extra land plays in the deck, but I don’t really want my opponents to have extra land plays if they’re going to be getting fed cards by me, so I choose to swap out Rites of Flourishing for Wayward Swordtooth and Ghirapur Orrery for Case of the Locked Hothouse.
Case is a really powerful card in general, and Swordtooth is a nice option as a creature to give flying once Ms. Bumbleflower is in play.
I’m also choosing to cut Tenuous Truce and Intellectual Offering; these cards are doing something similar to what the commander is doing, but the rate on the cards per mana just isn’t that great on either of these.
Kwain, Itinerant Meddler and Selvala, Explorer Returned likewise get cut, because I like being able to choose which of my opponents get fed cards; if one of my opponents is ahead, I want to be able to avoid giving them extra resources while still being able to utilize my cards.
Another type of card I choose to cut is cards that cost too much mana, since I’m trying to drop the mana curve.
Octomancer is a funny card, but this isn’t really a token deck, and five mana is just too much to pay for some vanilla stats.
Another cut is Coveted Jewel; it’s a funny card, but this deck doesn’t have enough ways of preventing opponents from snagging it, and when it does get taken they don’t even need to pay that expensive six mana upfront like I did.
Most other cuts I made were cards that just don’t do enough.
Bloodroot Apothecary, though it’s pre-selling for upwards of $10, seems highly mediocre to me as anti-treasure tech; opponents can still afford to crack a few treasures at a table without other poison, and then the rest can be saved for after the Apothecary is off the board.
I’ll take a Manglehorn over this any day of the week.
Now, a cut that truly pains me is Jolly Gerbils; this card is extremely cute, but the deck simply doesn’t have enough gift cards to justify its inclusion, and even if I added a few more, I’m still not convinced it would be preferable to a better two-drop.
Another cut is Hoofprints of the Stag; this is a decent incremental value piece if it’s played early, but it’s a very dead draw later in the game.
Triskaidekaphile is another card I opted to cut; this deck wants some number of no-maximum-hand-size cards, but the deck already has several other ones, and this card just doesn’t offer enough outside of that to warrant keeping it in, in my opinion.

Adding +1/+1 Counter Synergies

What goes in in place of these cards?
Beyond the replacements I already mentioned, a big area is +1/+1 counter synergies.
A lot of these are reasonably basic: Conclave Mentor is a bear that amplifies counters; Dusk Legion Duelist is a bear that’s a draw engine; Benthic Biomancer is a similar card for one mana that loots instead; and Kami of Whispered Hopes is just a fantastic card, well worth the $2 or so price tag.
A couple others I picked out that likewise offer a bit of ramp were Incubation Druid and Freestrider Lookout.
Importantly, I tried to pick out stuff that’s good in general and relatively low cost; the issue with picking out big, fancy payoff cards is that opponents can just deny your commander with removal, and then suddenly you invested heavily in something that can’t actually materialize.
Kami and Druid in particular are especially great, because without the commander they’re still mana dorks, but with even a single targeting with Ms. Bumbleflower’s ability, either of them can suddenly tap for three whole mana, and Kami only goes up from there.

Modern Horizons 3 Gems

There are also a few more specific cards from Modern Horizons 3 that I added, as that was a fantastic set for +1/+1 counter synergies.
Dreamtide Whale is a card that feels like it was made for this deck; it proliferates +1/+1 counters, it has a high power for delivering flying beats, and this is a deck that wants to double spell anyway, so the Whale should be sticking around.
Evolution Witness is another pretty sweet card; it’s not going to be wildly powerful in the early game, but later in the game it can be an unstoppable torrent of value.
The third card from this set I added is Envoy of the Ancestors, and this is my answer to the question of defensive cards and life total races: just gain some life.
This card sets up a player for a truly impressive quantity of life gain, which will help when facing off against the final player and needing to survive long enough to get the necessary attacks in to finish them off.

Final Interaction Tweaks

Beyond these, I also tweaked the interaction suite a bit.
I added Negate as a nice general-purpose counterspell to help with avoiding board wipes.

#29
DeckbuildingJul 202588K views

Eternal Might Precon: A Disjointed Zombie Deck Review

Eternal Might is the Messiest Zombie Deck I've Ever Seen
We critique the Eternal Might Commander precon, praising its card quality and mana base but criticizing its conflicting themes.It lacks enough discard to support Hashaton and lacks burst draw to maximize Temmet, leading to unfocused gameplay.The deck is more a sampler of zombie subthemes than a functional list.
Use this in your deckAdd more repeatable discard outlets to consistently activate Hashaton's ability.
Temmet, Naktamun's WillWhiteBlueBlackTokensDiscardSynergyUpgradesDesign IntentDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • Commander precons that sample multiple archetypes without sufficient support for each can feel disjointed and fail to function cohesively.
Evidence
  • The deck contains approximately six discard enablers, which is too few to reliably activate alternate commander Hashaton.
  • Temmet incentivizes burst draw before combat, but the deck's draw spells are mostly expensive or conditional.
  • The ramp suite is larger than typical for Esper, featuring synergistic cards like Crowded Crypt and Bontu's Monument.
Deckbuilding Application
  • To utilize Temmet effectively, add burst card draw spells that can be cast before the attack step.
  • Focus on generating numerous small zombie tokens rather than investing in expensive 4/4 token producers.
Limitation
  • The solid mana base and ramp are undermined by the deck's inability to simultaneously cast Temmet and a large draw spell due to high combined mana costs.
Example
  • Cards like Forgotten Creation show how a single piece can synergize with both commanders, but the deck lacks enough such enablers.
More quotes
  1. In terms of construction this deck is a hot mess, a disgusting mishmash of conflicting game plans and synergistic dead ends.
  2. Hashaton has gotten a lot of attention as an undeniably cool secondary commander, but it doesn't even really work as a secondary commander.
  3. The deck just doesn't have enough discard to consistently enable Hashaton's ability.
  4. This deck really lacks a lot of what might give a zombie deck a sense of mechanical identity, causing both play pattern issues when disparate synergy packages don't line up and vibes issues when it plays just like a generic blob of typal midrange gameplay.
  5. There isn't a reliable way to sacrifice Corpse Augur, nor any good way to let Midnight Reaper or Undead Augur translate into Temmet triggers.
  6. The one thing working in Tet's favor here is the half dozen or so cycling cards, but at that point you're paying five mana for the commander plus two mana to cycle during the combat step for roughly an Undead Warchief worth of anthem.
  7. A blue deck that's vulnerable to removal and board wipes runs no counter spells? Come on!
  8. Just don't buy this if you're looking for a cohesive deck out of the box, because this is not.
  9. The most generous way to view this deck, in my mind, is as a sample of possible game plans and/or a toolbox of possible implements for beginning the journey of building them.
  10. This is a rare Gravecrawler L: with no sacrifice, this card just doesn't really do anything.
Full transcript
Introduction

Hey folks, it’s precon review time.
I’m going to be critiquing the Eternal Might precon for Aetherdrift and offering some potential points of improvement.
The last two precons I’ve discussed have impressed me with their construction, seeming surprisingly streamlined in their functionality and easily upgradeable into a quite decent Magic deck.
It’s actually been a bit disappointing; like, what am I supposed to say aside from “this is pretty good, cut these five bad cards and it’ll be even better”?
Thankfully, that’s not a problem with this precon, though.
Containing good card quality, lots of nice assorted zombie cards, and some nice attention to detail in terms of aesthetics, in terms of construction this deck is a hot mess, a disgusting mishmash of conflicting game plans and synergistic dead ends, and I’m excited to tackle it.

What the Deck Tries to Accomplish

To start with, though, let’s talk about what this deck is trying to accomplish.
The commander of this deck is Temmet, a five-mana 4/4 in Esper colors with vigilance and menace, as well as the following text: “Whenever you attack, draw a card, then discard a card.
Whenever you draw a card, Zombies you control get +1/+1 until end of turn.” So on the surface, this is a card that rewards token zombies or otherwise wide boards of zombies, and also a card that rewards big draw that happens before the combat damage step in a turn.
Simple effect, but there are a lot of implied features of this card that we should keep in mind.
To be maximally effective, Temmet wants a large board of zombies to attack with, as well as a large draw spell to amp up the buff it grants; it’s also a thing that is likely going to be a removal magnet if we have a large board, since it can threaten a lot of damage.
Importantly, this means that the deck will want a lot of mana, both to pay the five mana casting cost and also either draw some cards or hold up mana to protect it.

Looking at the rest of the deck, some parts of it reflect these specific needs.
There are eight cards that create small zombie tokens and three Army generators, cards that create or add counters to a single growing zombie token, and there are also a handful of cards that create 4/4 zombie tokens as well.
The deck also contains a healthy nine ramp spells to help bolster it up to larger quantities of mana.
From there, the deck also has a large number of zombie creatures in it, which range from cards that drain opponents to lords to draw spells and beyond.
An interesting additional package in the deck is the discard package.
The alternate commander for this deck is Hasan, Scarab’s Fist, which can allow a player to make 4/4 zombie copies of discarded creatures; the deck also contains a handful of other discard payoffs.
The amount of discard in the deck isn’t wildly large, though, consisting of five or six cards that allow you to discard, plus a handful of cycling cards.
There’s also a light graveyard package to go along with this.
Rounding out the deck, we have a pretty average number of removal spells.
Overall, this deck will be looking to create a wide board of zombies, utilize its cute discard and graveyard subthemes, and swing in at opponents with a Temmet-buffed board.

What I Like

I obviously preempted my overall thoughts on this deck a bit, but I’ll start with what I like about what’s happening here.
As I said earlier, it has a larger than average ramp suite for an Esper deck, which I think is appropriate for this game plan.
Crowded Crypt and Bontu’s Monument both stand out as fun ramp options with a lot of utility in this deck, offering token production and drain respectively.
This deck also contains some number of token producers, and some of these are pretty good.
I like the ones that create 1/1 white Zombie tokens, and On Wings of Gold’s flying plus anthem effect is good enough to make the card seem worthwhile, even though this isn’t a deck with a super heavy graveyard theme, at least by zombie deck standards.
The manabase here is excellent: really robust symbol counts, even with a couple of great colorless utility lands, and a moderately high but not overwhelming tap land density that also gives players room to gradually update tap lands with better untapped duals.

There are also some number of cards that slot in quite elegantly to this deck.
Forgotten Creation pairs perfectly with both Temmet and Hasan, as do the newly printed Profit of the Scarab and Rhet-Crop Mystic.
There are also gestures at a number of packages that could exist: a couple Impact Tremors, style cards paired with the token package, and a selection of different graveyard and discard enablers and payoffs to give a sampling platter of what that could look like.
That’s the most generous way to view this deck, in my mind; it is a sample of possible game plans and/or a toolbox of possible implements for beginning the journey of building them.

What Falls Short

So what about this as a deck that’s trying to play a game of Magic: The Gathering?
Well, it’s a bit disappointing.
Hasan has gotten a lot of attention as an undeniably cool secondary commander, but it doesn’t even really work as a secondary commander.
The deck just doesn’t have enough discard to consistently enable Hasan’s ability; with Hasan in the 99, you at least have the consistent discard effect of Temmet, but he’s also a five-mana card with an overrun-esque ability, so trusting him to stick around as an engine is highly optimistic.
This lack of discard likewise stands out in the case of other discard payoffs, such as Liliana’s Caress and Archfiend of Ifnir, though those at least get triggered by the deck’s cycling lands.
The deck’s graveyard package is another thing that stands out; it’s not massive, but it is decidedly present, as you’d expect with a zombie deck.
This deck is kind of thin on ways to enable it, though.
Most zombie decks would be using sacrifice abilities in addition to discard, and while this deck does have the sorts of payoffs when creatures die that those decks tend to have, it’s almost entirely lacking in ways to sacrifice creatures.
The result is that this deck really lacks a lot of what might give a Zombie deck a sense of mechanical identity; its disjointedness causes both play pattern issues when disparate synergy packages don’t line up, and vibes issues when it plays just like a generic blob of typal midrange gameplay instead of, you know, like a Zombie deck.

Does It Support Temmet?

Okay, so I obviously have issues with this deck’s handling of some traditional key zombie subthemes, but let’s set that aside.
This is a precon; this is meant to be a toolbox and/or a sampling platter.
We can give it some slack.
Does this deck at least hold up in supporting Temmet as a commander?
Are we forging an alternative zombie play style here?
In some ways the answer is yes, but in a lot of other ways the answer is no. As I said earlier, this deck’s ramp and mana are well constructed, which is good, as Temmet is the sort of card that’s going to work better when the deck has mana to play him plus something else in a turn.
The token generating suite is okay and actually contains some pretty high quality stuff, but there are fewer cards that create small zombie tokens than I’d like.
Creating 4/4 zombie tokens is cool and all, but Temmet’s design heavily incentivizes pairing him with lots of small zombies, so the bigger tokens with more rigorous requirements to be created simply feel out of place, even if they’re more aesthetically fitting.

Even more problematically, this deck doesn’t have much of the sort of bursty draw that pairs best with Temmet.
There isn’t a lot of draw overall, and a lot of the draw that is here isn’t great for taking advantage of Temmet’s ability.
There isn’t a reliable way to sacrifice Corpse Augur, nor any good way to let Midnight Reaper or Undead Augur translate into Temmet triggers, and you’re not going to tap down your zombies to Cryptbreaker if you want to swing in that turn.
Most of the other draw options are mana expensive, so you’re looking at ten mana minimum to do a Temmet plus draw maneuver.
The one thing working in Temmet’s favor here is the half dozen or so cycling cards in the deck, but at that point you’re paying five mana for the commander plus two mana to cycle during the combat step for roughly an Undead Warchief’s worth of anthem.

The final problem is this deck’s interaction suite.
It has ten cards to perform a removal role in some way or other, which might work for a faster Zombie deck, but I’m not sold on this deck’s ability to win quickly.
Worse, it has very little protection and no counter spells.
A blue deck that’s vulnerable to removal and board wipes runs no counter spells?
Come on.

Product vs. Play Experience

I don’t know.
This deck isn’t unplayably bad or anything, but it fails to meaningfully commit to any of its subthemes, and with the way it’s currently built, its command zone feels, at best, just a generic zombie guide that gives the deck its colors.
It is a bit more cohesive when we look at the visual presentation, with a clear emphasis on Amonkhet-style zombies, and there’s certainly some admirable effort put into new art for a lot of the cards present.
I don’t usually treat precon reviews as product reviews or value reviews, you know; I’m much more interested in the deck and the sort of experience that Wizards of the Coast is handing to a newer player who is pulling this out of a box to play a game of Commander with.
But I’ll make a minor exception here.
As a product, there’s a lot going for this, and if you are a zombie player who’s looking for a bundle of good zombie cards plus some cool new arts, this might absolutely be a great purchase.
Just don’t buy this if you’re looking for a cohesive deck out of the box, because this is not that.

Upgrade Path

With all that said, I still want to try an upgrade for this.
When I first saw this precon, my immediate thought was that it’d be cool to morph it into a full Hasan deck, but honestly, this deck is so far from enabling Hasan well that I’m inclined to stick with Temmet instead and see what we can do with 15 swap-outs to try and actually let him feel like a legitimate commander.
So let’s do that, and I’m going to start by talking about what we’re cutting.
Binding Mummy and Twisted Abomination aren’t great in general, and I’m also going to cut out a couple discard payoffs that aren’t adequately supported here, sorry, Hasan.
Beyond that, Mystic Nexus seems out of place here; my best guess is it’s just here to give more people access to Mystic Nexus, and I’m going to also cut out a handful of zombies that are just a bit mediocre.
This is a rare Gravecrawler L with no sacrifice; this card just doesn’t really do anything.
Nothing beyond these particularly offends me, but I will trim a handful more mediocre cards just to make space for better support.
These are cards that are just on the weaker side mechanically, or which don’t tie in particularly well with the game plan I’m trying to cultivate.

For our additions, the first order of business is that I want to add a bunch more draw and filtering, since they do double duty in smoothing out the way this deck plays and making it punchier with Temmet.
Keep Watch, Distant Melody, Pact of the Serpent, and Raffine all allow the deck to give its zombies a massive pump up for a reasonable amount of mana.
In addition to these, I’m choosing to add three cards for protection that can also draw cards, which are Change of Plans, You Find the Villains’ Lair, and Your Temple Is Under Attack, and these are nice because of their flexibility.

The other thing I’m choosing to add is cards that create zombie tokens, three total in this case.
Siphon Flesh and Dark Salvation both provide removal and token generation, and Endless Ranks of the Dead is a good singular zombie token maker.
Honestly, there are a bunch of other zombie token generators in the $2 to $10 range that I didn’t add due to price, and if you’re really trying to build a good Temmet deck, you should probably go find some of those.
Varina stands out as a card that is absurdly good in a Temmet deck, performing both key tasks I’m looking for at a good rate.

For a few final additions, Diregraf Captain is just a generically good zombie, and I’m basically having it replace a couple zombie typal cards I’m cutting.
Lethal is a removal spell that also includes a good amount of draw/discard filtering, and it’s easily payable with black zombie tokens.
Fellwar Stone and Ponder are here to give some extra ramp and filtering respectively, just smoothing out the overall game plan.
The last addition is Cephalid Coliseum, a land that can provide a burst of draw triggers at a great rate at a point in the game when the deck is looking to close things out.

Through these changes, my hope is that Temmet will be more consistently useful, sticking around longer and providing larger buffs to zombies.
The addition of more zombie tokens and removal will hopefully also direct the game plan of the deck in the right direction.
Temmet definitely isn’t my favorite commander; like, there’s definitely less depth there than with the alternate commander Hasan, but I still can’t help but respect the attempt at making a different sort of Zombie deck, one more based around drawing and discarding than around death triggers and sacrificing.
Hopefully we see more exploration in this direction in the future, and hopefully future zombie precons end up a bit more coherently constructed.

Announcement

An announcement: I have a P.O. Box now, and the address is on screen.
Feel free to send me your favorite card or a handwritten poem proclaiming your love of Brushwagg.
The P.O. Box isn’t wildly large, so don’t send me anything bulky, as there is a solid chance it will be thrown away instead of finding its way to me.
This should go without saying, but please also don’t send me anything dangerous or disgusting.
Thanks!
If I get enough cards, I might turn them into a deck or make them into a video of some sort: delicious future content for the gaping, content-hungry masses of the world.
With that said, thanks for watching, and thanks to Architect for supporting us.
I’m a fan of Architect’s UI, a thing that was on screen basically the whole time, and an especially nice feature for tweaking decks is the maybeboard categories, which let me keep thoughts and card additions nice and organized.

#30
DeckbuildingJul 202582K views

How Cheap Powerful Cards Warp Commander Deckbuilding

Let's Talk About My Most Controversial Video
We revisit our controversial video on budget cards, explaining how cards with mismatched price and power levels cause players to overvalue synergistic cards and undervalue essential effects like interaction and ramp.He argues that adding overly powerful staples can backfire by increasing threat perception without improving consistency, and advises builders to determine necessary card counts and stick to them to maintain balance.
Use this in your deckCut any staple that raises your deck's power level without addressing a specific weakness the deck already has.
BudgetCard EvaluationPower LevelFormat Health
Principle
  • Cheap cards with disproportionately high power cause players to fill the best slots and then neglect further essential effects due to diminishing returns in perceived value.
  • A deck with a wide range of power levels among its cards can cause opponents to always assume the strongest possible play, drawing disproportionate retaliation even when the average is low.
Evidence
  • Players overrate synergistic win-more cards and underrate consistent vegetables because synergies are harder to evaluate than simple interaction and ramp.
  • Upgrading a mid-power graveyard deck can backfire because opponents will likely pack silver bullet graveyard hate, leading to less consistent games.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Determine the correct number of slots for a given effect ahead of time and avoid exceeding that count even when powerful budget options are available.
  • Cut cards that increase your deck's threat level without solving a specific weakness it already has.
Limitation
  • The original video's points about social dynamics may be exaggerated, as we now acknowledge a tendency to overstate such factors in earlier work.
Example
  • Sol Ring is a cheap powerhouse because its price stayed low through reprints, but if scarce it would rival Mana Crypt in cost and be limited to power gamers.
  • We avoid Torment of Hailfire in our Glissa deck because we do not want opponents to equate having many lands with an imminent game loss.
More quotes
  1. The like-to-dislike proportion is an abysmal 77%. For context, my second lowest is around 95%.
  2. [My most controversial video] is wildly unpopular compared to literally everything else I've released.
  3. Are cheap powerful cards a problem? Was I on to something or flat out wrong?
  4. Players will add the best options within their budget, then notice the rapidly worsening power to money ratio and stop adding them, even if running more of that type of card would be better for their deck.
  5. Decks become crammed with win more cards but lack the basic building blocks that lead to consistently performing well.
  6. I don't run Torment of Hailfire in my turbo ramping Glissa deck because I don't want my opponents to equate me having a lot of lands with an immediate game loss.
  7. Less than half of people are still watching when I get to the part of we where I start to justify my soul ring slander.
  8. I don't think Sol Ring should have been reprinted in commander sets because I don't think fast mana belongs in casual Commander.
  9. I just don't like what explosive starts do to the tempo of games.
  10. Some have argued that it should be banned but that ship has sailed given the number of reprints it has seen.
Full transcript
Revisiting a Controversial Video
Summary of the Original Video's Arguments
What I Think Now About our Arguments
The Flawed Introduction
Why Fast Mana Doesn't Belong in Casual Commander
#31
Format TheoryJul 202579K views

Tutor Decisions Define Four EDH Player Types

The Simple Question that Determines What Type of EDH Player You Are
We use a thought experiment about tutoring for a powerful card to segment EDH players into four types.We explore the first two types: players who always tutor for the powerful card for fun or to enable other strategies, and players who sometimes decline to tutor for it to match the table's vibe.We explain our own philosophy of treating gameplay as a separate optimization stage, which makes intentional suboptimal play feel unsatisfying.
Use this in your deckResolve tutoring tension by cutting either the powerful target or the tutor so you never have to hold back in-game.
TokensCard DrawPower LevelDeckbuilding RestrictionsCard EvaluationDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • Powerful cards carry an allure beyond their win rate, tapping into a player's nostalgia and desire for memorable plays.
Evidence
  • The popularity of Vintage Cube on MTGO demonstrates that many players enjoy playing with high-power cards for their own sake.
  • We recount our own experience of optimizing a mono-black pile with Cabal Coffers and Necropotence, only to find the excitement diminish over time.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Tutors can serve as a bridge, allowing a deck with a weak core strategy to consistently access a single powerful enabler like Skullclamp.
  • Powerful cards can remain enjoyable when used to prop up offbeat themes, like Necropotence drawing into a casual tribal deck instead of an infinite combo.
Limitation
  • A player who consciously chooses a weaker tutor target may feel they are violating the competitive spirit of the game, leading to dissatisfaction.
Example
  • The specific scenario of having Enlightened Tutor in a token deck with Skullclamp forces the player to confront whether they always want to draw an overwhelming number of cards regardless of the table's power level.
More quotes
  1. Yes, I tutored for the Skullclamp because I like Skullclamp and I want to draw a shitload of cards regardless of the situation.
  2. No, and in fact I cut the Skullclamp because the number of cards it draws doesn't fit the vibe of my deck.
  3. No, and in fact I cut the Enlightened Tutor because I don't want the option of tutoring for the Skullclamp.
  4. Powerful cards can go from a fun thing in their own right to a way to enable some other fun things that are a bit less powerful.
  5. Trotting out the same old powerful cards in the same ways just might not remain as enjoyable.
  6. There's a reason why Vintage Cube is one of the most popular Cube formats on MTGO. Are these powerful cards more inherently fun than any others? No, but they're famous and alluring, and playing them pokes a little good card dopamine button.
  7. If I need to make a sub-optimal choice or hold back in some way, it pierces the immersion of the game and kind of takes me out.
  8. A lot of people dislike playing suboptimally, and another segment of players dislike playing against somebody they know is playing suboptimally. It almost feels like an insult.
  9. The most dominant feature of casual EDH is some degree of suboptimality, and I generally think of that as being in the deck building part of things.
  10. This game doesn't have 10 Necropotences you can slip into your deck, and even if it did, that might not even be desirable. Tutors are a nice way to work around that fact.
Full transcript

In my last video, I discussed how adding a single card to an EDH deck that simplifies or circumvents a task a player finds fun can have a negative impact on how playing that deck feels.
That video generated a lot of buzz and discussion, which was great to see, and it also helped crystallize a handful of ideas that have been sloshing around my brain for the past several months.
These ideas are complicated and span a variety of subjects, but they can be pretty straightforwardly introduced with a simple thought experiment.

The Thought Experiment

Let’s say you have a tutor and there’s a “Bonkers card” , something very powerful , in your deck.
Do you tutor for the “Bonkers card”?
Just so things are a bit more tangible, let’s say you’re Enlightened Tutoring for a Skullclamp in a mid‑power tokens deck: a decently powerful tutor and a draw engine that is probably leaps and bounds better than the next most powerful draw engine in the deck.
So, do you Enlightened Tutor for the Skullclamp?

The Four Answers

I have devised four answers to this question, which segment four different perspectives on EDH philosophy.
They go as follows: “Yes, I tutored for the Skullclamp because I like Skullclamp and I want to draw a shitload of cards regardless of situation.” “No, I might choose not to tutor for the Skullclamp because the number of cards it draws might not fit the vibe of this game.” “No, and in fact I cut the Enlightened Tutor because I don’t want the option of tutoring for the Skullclamp.” “No, and in fact I cut the Skullclamp because the number of cards it draws doesn’t fit the vibe of my deck.”

It may be clear which of these quadrants I personally fall into most frequently , the fourth one, or sometimes the first depending on deck , but I want to acknowledge that the others exist.
I’ve gotten a lot of comments from people who likely fall firmly into one of the first three quadrants, people saying something to the effect of, “But wait Alex, have you considered this?
I think you forgot to consider this.” So I now want to consider the other three of these perspectives in depth and where they come from, what core animating forces drive people to engage with this format the way they do.
I’ve split we up into two parts because I found that the first two perspectives here and the last two perspectives really demand two different lenses of analysis.
If you want to hear me discuss the final two, you can keep an eye out for that video about a week from now; for now, let’s get into the first two quadrants.

The First Quadrant: Always Tutor for the Good Card

The first one is the person who does tutor for the “good card” because they want to play the “good card.” This is, on its face, entirely understandable.
Magic: The Gathering is a card game where players try to win games, and winning games is fun.
More powerful cards win more games, so they end up being seen as especially fun in some sense.
This may be directly related to the fact that they win more games, but in a format like EDH it could also be a more indirect force: they feel good to play, they generate reactions from others, they have a mystique about them born from years of being heralded as powerful.
There’s a reason why Vintage Cube is one of the most popular Cube formats on MTGO.
Are these powerful cards more inherently fun than any others?
No, but they’re famous and alluring, and playing them pokes a little “good card” dopamine button residing in the brains of a lot of players.
The EDH player who runs good cards and tutors to find those good cards more often may be doing so because they want to win more games, but they also may just be looking to poke that button more.
And can you blame them?
It’s fun to play a Skullclamp and draw a ludicrous number of cards.
I think most players go through a stage of this at some point in time; I certainly had the experience of optimizing a mono‑black pile with Cabal Coffers and Necropotence and such like 10 or so years ago, and I remember the excitement I felt.
For some players, that excitement persists long term, and I’m happy for those players.
Maybe they make their way to cEDH, or maybe they just have a good time playing sweaty high‑power piles against other sweaty high‑power piles.
However, for myself and many others, the excitement of playing good cards for the sake of them being good does start to fade with time; trotting out the same old powerful cards in the same ways just might not remain as enjoyable, and even if you do keep playing with them, you might need to change the way you play with them to keep them fresh.

Keeping the Best Cards Fresh

One way this can be done is changing the context in which you play a powerful card.
An Ancient Tomb that curves into a Bolas’s Citadel is very different from one that curves into a bunch of Treefolk, and a Necropotence that draws into an infinite combo is very different from one that draws into a bunch of clerics from the 2000s.
Put another way, powerful cards can go from a fun thing in their own right to a way to enable some other fun things that are a bit less powerful.
This isn’t really a realm of deck building I tend to find myself in, but I can absolutely see the appeal: you get the enjoyment of playing powerful Magic cards and the enjoyment of enabling offbeat archetypes that might not be good enough with weaker support cards.
And going back to the premise here, tutors can help with this.
If you want to guarantee having a piece of powerful support to hold up your otherwise questionable game plan, tutors can be a good way to do that, to have a consistent experience with your janky deck.
This game doesn’t have ten Necropotences you can slip into your deck, and even if it did, that might not even be desirable; tutors are a nice way to work around that fact.

Okay, so that’s one way powerful cards can stay fresh: playing them in unusual contexts.
The other way is getting to the point where you don’t even feel the need to play the “good card” every time it’s available, and this brings me to the second group of players.

The Second Quadrant: Choosing Not to Tutor for the Best Card

These are the players who have the tutor and don’t tutor for the Skullclamp , or at least they don’t always tutor for the best possible option.
I found that these are players who see tutors as a wonderful and freeing tool; you can find whatever you want with them.
You can find a Skullclamp, you can find a land, you can find a Leeching Licid , the world is your oyster.
And there is a certain logic to this.
Some games you might want a power card, maybe you’re behind or maybe you want a chance at catching up with somebody running away with the game, but there also might be other games where you’d prefer to take it a little easier, play a little lower caliber, tutor for something a bit more mundane if it better fits the vibe of the game you’re playing.
I believe this to be the least popular of the four quadrants I laid out earlier, but for the people who fall into it, I’m genuinely happy for them; they are able to have fun doing something that I struggle to have fun doing: playing suboptimally on purpose.

Deck Building vs. Playing: Two Stages of the Game

See, I treat the format of EDH as a two‑stage game, as I discussed in more detail in a video about combo decks last fall.
During the first stage, that deck‑building stage, I’m in creative mode, crafting an experience for the future me to play with, thinking about the sorts of decks I might be facing and trying to fine‑tune what I’m building to create the most enjoyable games.
During the second stage, though, when I’m actually playing a game, my goals are somewhat different: I treat it as a game where my objective is winning and where I’m pursuing that under a set of constraints.
These constraints consist of the tools given to me by my deck‑building self, as well as the limitations of the type of table engagement I prefer , which is generally very open and disclosing of information I think my opponents should have.
I’m not doggedly pursuing victory at all costs, but I also don’t like playing suboptimally once I’m in a game.
If I need to make a suboptimal choice or hold back in some way, it pierces the immersion of the game and kind of takes me out of it.
I still will do this under some circumstances, but I don’t like doing it, and I certainly wouldn’t set up my deck in a way where I expect to need to do it on a regular basis.
I know I’m not alone in this either; a lot of people dislike playing suboptimally, and another segment of players dislikes playing against somebody they know is playing suboptimally.
It almost feels like an insult; you can interpret somebody choosing to make worse choices as them saying, “Oh no, I don’t need to put full effort into this game; what you’re doing there isn’t requiring that of me.” Now, realistically, the sort of player who is okay with playing suboptimally probably isn’t thinking this way, but for somebody who is in a somewhat more competitive mindset, it’s not hard to take this interpretation.
This type of player may also seem very different from most other ways of playing the EDH format , and indeed it does take a different mindset, a different perspective on the nature of winning a game, maybe even different ideas of what a game should look like.
However, going back to the two‑phase model of Magic: The Gathering, this difference could be as simple as reversing the ordering of optimization in terms of deck building versus playing.
I play in a more optimized style and deck build in a less optimized style, but it’s easy to imagine a type of player who puts more optimization effort into the deck‑building phase and is a bit more laid‑back and casual when they’re actually playing.
I might feel a little weird about somebody else making choices that notably worsen their chances of winning, but I’ve also had people complain about me making the same sorts of choices in the deck‑building stage of things.
The most dominant feature of casual EDH is some degree of suboptimality, and I generally think of that as being in the deck‑building part of things, but it seems reasonable enough that it could exist in the playing stage instead.

Two Similar Approaches, and What’s Next

As a final note, going back to the comparison between these two player types, a way that these two players are a bit more similar is in the character of their decks.
They’re generally both happy to play tutors, and they are both happy to play with powerful cards as well, maybe even when those cards feel a bit overtuned compared to the rest of the contents of a deck.
Fundamentally, these things are just game pieces, and having them in a deck isn’t an obstacle to the fun for these people.
What I’ll be talking about next week, on the other hand, is the opposite: it’s players who are much more critical of what they put in their deck one way or another, and as you’ll see when that video comes out, those particular perspectives have philosophical roots about as deep as it’s possible to be.

With that said, thanks for watching, and thanks to Architect for supporting us.
I’ve been doing a lot of brewing lately, and Architect makes it easy to keep my thoughts organized with its nice visual layout and helpful category system.
A link to my is in the description if you want to check out what I’ve been working on lately.
I’d also like to thank my patrons, some of whom are on screen now.
I just started taking submissions for this month’s deck review, so if you want a chance at having your deck reviewed by me, you can sign up to my Patreon at the link in the description.
You can also check out the podcast I’m on if you want to experience more words exiting my mouth without giving me more money; a link to that is also in the description.
All right, bye now.

#32
Community ChallengeJul 202566K views

Winning Decks from Baldur's Gate Commander Challenge

I Challenged My Community to Make Decks with Bad Baldur's Gate Commanders
We reveal the top decks from a community challenge where players built around Baldur's Gate background commanders.Judged on creativity, cohesion, and dynamic gameplay, the winning Allora deck polymorphs its background to find Sneak Attack, and the Amber deck pairs sacrifice-outlet clerics with Haunted One for repeated value.We highlight how unique synergies can turn perceived downsides into powerful engines.
Use this in your deckUse a polymorph effect on your own background creature to tutor for a key enchantment like Sneak Attack.
RedBlackBlinkAristocratsDeckbuilding RestrictionsBudgetSynergyDesign IntentDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • Evaluating decks on creativity, cohesion, and dynamic gameplay helps identify builds that are both original and highly functional.
Evidence
  • For Allora, common strategies included Ball Lightning creatures, ninjas, and recycling enter-the-battlefield effects, with around 100 total submissions.
  • Amber decks were often budget builds under $150, and black was the most popular background color due to discard synergies.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Polymorph your own background creature to consistently tutor for a key enchantment, as the background can be recast from the command zone.
  • Combine Amber with Haunted One and sacrifice-outlet clerics or dwarves to repeatedly trigger their abilities and wheel your hand each combat.
  • In a Sneak Attack deck, include flicker spells to permanently keep cheated creatures by blinking them before the end-of-turn sacrifice.
  • Include counter-removal cards like I Sand Storm or Retribution to reset +1/+1 counters on undying creatures, enabling multiple deaths and triggers.
Limitation
  • Allora’s bounce ability is only beneficial when built around ETB reuse or end-of-turn sacrifices; otherwise, it severely weakens board presence.
  • Amber’s small 3/3 body makes her reliant on Haunted One’s undying to safely attack and trigger her ability, exposing a vulnerability if the background is removed.
More quotes
  1. A spell-less spellslinger deck was a funny one and a favorite among people on my Discord server.
  2. The winning deck was a sneak attack deck with Guild Artisan chosen as the partnered background.
  3. This deck pairs sneak attack with polymorph effects, tutoring out a sneak attack effect by polymorphing its own background [Guild Artisan].
  4. Flicker cards in the sneak attack deck can flicker a creature put out with sneak attack to keep it around permanently.
  5. All of my top picks here were less than $150, some a lot less.
  6. The deck runs an eye-watering nine board wipes, which it can break parity on through Haunted One.
  7. Amber is a fundamentally janky Commander; she's a draw engine but requires a very scrappy style and doesn't reward that style heavily.
  8. Dwarves? Yeah, no. Forget about it.
  9. This deck has an undeniable thematic core, with themes of death and dying riddled throughout.
  10. The winning deck was a different sort of twiddle storm deck, focused on effects that untap all of your creatures.
Full transcript
Introduction
Allora, Merry Thief

For a few honorable mentions, we have a deck based around the card Dawn of the Dead, a deck that focuses on the card Etheral as a way to help with getting creatures back into play, and a spell-less “spellslinger” deck; that one is a funny one, and it was a favorite among people on my Discord server.
For the winning deck, though, we have a returning winner: filthy Stacks player submitted a sneak attack deck with Guild Artisan chosen as the partnered background.
Now the choice to run sneak attack wasn’t a unique one for this challenge; getting creatures out at a discount to be sacrificed at end of turn makes sense as a way to utilize Allora and was a reasonably popular choice as a result.
However, this deck had a handful of construction choices that made it stand out above the rest of the crowd.
For one, its utilization of its background Guild Artisan wasn’t a wildly popular background in the challenge, but it could be a nice treasure engine.
More importantly, though, this deck pairs it with “polymorph” effects.
That’s right: this deck can tutor out a sneak attack effect via one of two cards that remove a permanent to grab one of the same type, doing so by polymorphing its own background, which is conveniently castable from the command zone.
This can also work with the treasures produced by Guild Artisan to polymorph for artifacts, since Chaotic Transformation can target multiple permanent types and Reality Scramble can be cast multiple times via retrace.
These are supplemented with a handful of tutors that can find instants and sorceries, enabling the deck to have consistent access to its key enchantments.
One natural question for a sneak attack deck is what is being snuck out; in this case, there’s a total of sixteen creatures of mana value six or greater, a pretty robust number.
Among these, some are your typical cheat deck beaters such as Ancient Stone Idol and Cityscape Leveler, but past these there are some more interesting ones.
Balefire Dragon provides a source of large-scale removal, Knollspine Dragon can allow for some big draw with creative turn ordering, and there are also a number of ETB creatures that can get value turn over turn utilizing sneak attack, including Tyrant of Discord, Astral Dragon, and Trumpeting Carnosaur.
There’s also another key top-end creature, Nezahal, and that one can serve as a gesture to a key subtheme within this deck: flicker.
Flicker cards perform two functions here: as with the commander, they can recycle ETB creatures, and they can also flicker a creature put out with sneak attack to keep it around permanently at no additional expense.
This gives the deck a great degree of flexibility in how hard it wants to commit to the board and in how much it wants to grind value.
There’s also a good amount of redundancy in key types of effect; there are four total cards that can perform a sneak attack-like role, two enchantments and two non-enchantment cards that are similar enough, and there are likewise multiple alternative options for keeping around sneak attacked creatures outside of the command zone.
Overall, this is a super deck; great job again, filthy Stacks player.

Amber Grist O'Maul

Really, this deck focuses on one specific variety of creature: that being clerics and dwarves that sacrifice creatures to accomplish tasks.
We’ve got eleven of these along with twelve other clerics and five other dwarves.
The basic idea here seems simple: dump these creatures into play alongside Amber and Haunted One, and then swing in with Amber in order to both wheel and use sacrifice abilities on the now undying clerics and dwarves.
This can produce effects such as destroying a creature with Blind Zealot, tutoring out black creatures with Shadow-Rite Priest, and drawing cards with Skullport Merchant.
The deck also contains five cards that reanimate in some way, including three attached to a cleric, as well as three madness cards, so there’s some degree of synergy present to work with Amber’s attack trigger.
Her power and toughness aren’t great, only 3/3 on a four mana creature, but this doesn’t matter as long as the deck has Haunted One in play.
This might prompt a follow-up question: what if you want to sacrifice a creature that already has a +1/+1 counter on it?
The solution to this potential issue comes in the form of a handful of cards that remove counters from creatures; there are nine of these total, and while most of these feel more like filler than anything else, the two enchantments seem great.
I Sandstorm provides a quality source of repeatable burn damage, and Retribution of the Ancients is an even more potent tool, particularly when the deck’s board gets bigger.
The other standout element of this deck is its suite of board wipes.
The deck runs an eye-watering nine board wipes, which the deck can break parity on through Haunted One.
One of these nine is Yako Hops, which can provide a game-warping nuke once the pilot of this deck trusts their board to run to the finish line.
These board wipes seem like an appropriate tool to help the deck’s honestly mediocre creatures get around the large boards of modern-day EDH decks, because that’s the most palpable vibe this deck gives off: the jank factor.
The cards here are at best normally mediocre cards being used in their narrow use case window of being decent, and in some cases mediocre cards even in this deck; we’ve got late 90s dwarves, various mediocre commons from across the years, Grimoire of the Dead, there’s so much nonsense here united in flavor or function or both.
This feels appropriate, though, for a few reasons.
Amber is a fundamentally janky commander; she’s a draw engine, but one that requires playing in a very scrappy style and one who doesn’t even reward that style that heavily.
Her two creature types, cleric and dwarf, are not generally great ones to build around.
Clerics have gotten just enough love to make Shadowborn Apostle decks look a cut above respectable, but dwarves?
Yeah, no, forget about it.
In addition, this deck’s budget is quite low; it’s sitting below the $50 mark if you exclude Haunted One, a card which, as Lord Reverend alluded to, is basically free as long as you win this competition since the prize card is an altered art version of the card.
A wildly polished deck with top-tier card quality this certainly is not, nor was it intended to be.

As a final note, this deck also has an undeniable thematic core to it.
We have themes of death and dying riddled throughout this deck, including the Haunted One background, a whole lot of cultist-type creatures that sacrifice things to perform rituals, and board wipes that represent great dying-off events.
Lore-wise, Amber herself is a cleric with connections to the Shadowfell, carrying a certain degree of liminality with her, tugged between life and what lies beyond.
This further helps the deck in communicating itself as a cohesive unit, and the end result has an undeniable sense of style to it.

Ellyn Harbreeze, Busy Body

This is a deck focused on effects that untap all of your creatures; four of these untap all creatures on opposing untap/tap steps, eight untap all creatures once, and a few other cards untap creatures more selectively.
To go along with these, this deck has eight creatures that tap to create a token, seven that tap to add mana, and two cards that tap to create Treasures, effectively doing both of these things.
Once this deck has Ellyn and some of these effects online, it can start to do some cool stuff; the deck can use all tap abilities creating mana and tokens and filtering, and then it can play a card that untaps all creatures to refresh all these tappers.
Mana creators let the deck avoid running out of mana too fast, and creating tokens allows the deck to dig deeper and deeper with Ellyn on each round of tapping, hopefully finding additional untapping effects for as long as the Ellyn player wants to keep going.
The deck doesn’t have an explicit win condition in the 98 outside of maybe Halo Fountain, but the elegant thing here is that the deck doesn’t really need a specific payoff card for a big tapping and untapping turn.
Agent of the Iron Throne in the command zone means that a board full of tokens already contains an implied level of damage, and several Ellyn activations will mean a well-curated hand of cards when passing after a big turn.
To go along with the rest of this game plan, the deck also contains a handful of one-time token producers, some protection for the commander, and a small suite of other support cards.
What’s interesting about this deck is that I think both Agent of the Iron Throne and Haunted One work well as backgrounds, though they lend very different play patterns for finishing off opponents.
Agent of the Iron Throne gives the deck a more measured play style that focuses on optimizing twiddle turns in the moment and worrying about finishing the game later once a whole lot of tokens are in play.
Haunted One, on the other hand, is more explosive, rewarding careful planning and avoiding opposing hate with the potential for some truly disgusting alpha strikes.
Either way seems valid to me, though something nice about Haunted One is that it also gives the deck a bit of something that it’s a bit low on at the moment: protection.
Ellyn can tap herself at any time she’s untapped to grant the benefit of Haunted One even if no tokens were created during a turn, which gives is a pretty sw.

#33
Community ChallengeJul 202553K views

Commander Deckbuilding Without the Letter E

The No Es Deckbuilding Challenge
We explore a Commander deckbuilding challenge where no card name can contain the letter 'E', eliminating roughly 83% of all Magic cards.We showcase community submissions that found creative workarounds, such as utilizing the four 'Station' artifacts for an infinite combo and winning with BioVisionary.The challenge demonstrates how strict restrictions force innovation and reveal underused synergies.
Use this in your deckIf your deck's core strategy loses over 80% of its key pieces to the restriction, pivot to a self-contained combo like the four Stations that entirely avoids the letter E.
WhiteBlueBlackRedGreenComboDeckbuilding RestrictionsMana BaseSynergyDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • Strict deckbuilding constraints compel players to find unconventional synergies and hidden gems.
  • When a massive portion of the card pool is eliminated, strategies anchored by a small, resilient set of cards become the most viable.
Evidence
  • The no-E rule eliminates roughly 83% of all Magic cards, according to our analysis.
  • Despite the ban, certain card types remain largely intact: half of polymorph cards, all copies of Rat Colony, and about 11 green-producing lands for mono-green decks.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Before committing to a theme, inventory how many of its key pieces contain the banned letter; if the core collapses, pivot to a restriction-proof combo like the Stations.
  • For color identities hurt by the loss of basic lands, prioritize nonbasic lands and mana rocks since they statistically have fewer of the restricted letter.
Limitation
  • The restriction removes premium tutor targets like Darksteel Forge, forcing decks to settle for less powerful but compliant alternatives.
  • Excluding Forest makes mono-green decks especially fragile, demanding an aggressive mulligan strategy to find enough colored sources.
Example
  • One featured deck uses Arcanum Dagsson to sacrifice artifact creatures and fetch Blasting, Grinding, Summoning, and Salvaging Station, which form an infinite combo.
  • Another submission pilots Momir Vig to tutor creature copiers like Mockingbird and Sakashima's Will, aiming to assemble four BioVisionaries for the win.
More quotes
  1. A snail never tells.
  2. It's EDH, but without any E's in the names of cards.
  3. Not being able to build decks with E's eliminates roughly 83% of all Magic cards.
  4. Only around half of all polymorph cards were eliminated, leaving a respectable five left for any red-blue decks to enjoy.
  5. There were four cards printed in Fifth Dawn with station in the name, and it just so happens that none of them have E's.
  6. Something else you may note about this no E's format is that it excludes a basic land, forests.
  7. There are something like 11 lands without E's that both produce green and are legal in a mono-green commander deck, and pairing those with a modest number of green-producing mana rocks can get you up to a simple count that's an aggressive mulligan policy away from being adequate, I suppose.
  8. Kirk was canonically originally named Kerric, and his choice to remove the E from his name meshes nicely with the nature of this challenge.
  9. Iron Man is actually a great choice to go with this for a couple reasons. For one, treasures. That's an E.
  10. Artifacts for good? What a laugh.
Full transcript
The No E’s Challenge
The Struggle to Find E‑Less Strategies
Arcanum Dagsson Station Deck

Arcanum Dagsson is a spicy commander from Coldsnap, and he’s one that has the potential to be quite powerful in modern‑day unrestricted EDH.
Four mana for a creature that can sacrifice an artifact creature to find a non‑creature artifact every turn is quite potent, and the non‑creature artifacts we have available to search for in the year of our Lord 2025 are quite horrifying compared to what existed back when Arcanum Dagsson was printed in 2006.
However, what do the top‑tier tutoring targets like Darksteel Forge, Portal to Phyrexia, and God‑Pharaoh’s Statue all have in common?
E’s, of course.
So, naturally, any Arcanum Dagsson enjoyers had to get a bit more creative, which brings me to the tutoring targets of choice for this deck: stations.
There were four cards printed in Fifth Dawn with station in the name, and it just so happens that none of them have E’s.
Two of them you may know as classic combo pieces, Blasting Station and Grinding Station, whereas Summoning Station and Salvaging Station are somewhat less popular cards that only hipster YouTubers play.
However, when the four of these are in play at the same time, all of their respective abilities and untap clauses merge together to form an infinite combo with each other, so long as a low‑cost artifact for Salvaging Station is in the graveyard.
The two high‑cost stations are activated to create permanents, which untaps the low‑cost stations, and then those two are activated to sacrifice the permanents and inflict harm on opponents, which untaps the high‑cost stations; rinse and repeat until opponents are dead.
So, the heart of this deck is these four artifacts and a commander who can find them.
But what supports that?
Well, first, artifact creatures.
This is the resource that Arcanum needs to create his stations, so naturally, there are 19 of them.
These cards span a variety of purposes, with the most common being to help the deck ramp.
Some of them are a bit filler, but with a commander like Arcanum Dagsson, that’s not really a problem.
The other key thing this deck has is protection.
Arcanum is a scary commander, so to keep him alive, this deck is packing nine counterspells and five other pieces of protection.
Filling out the rest of the deck is a handful of removal spells, as well as a variety of other artifact‑related cards.
There are a handful of cards that seem here specifically to synergize with certain stations, a number of one‑cost non‑creature artifacts to allow Salvaging Station to come online, and there are likewise a couple cards that can let artifacts untap.
Trading Post also lightly synergizes with a couple different stations, yet another possible machine that can be cobbled together.
All of this has a very retro energy about it, and I enjoy that.

Green Without Forests: Momir Vig BioVisionary Deck

Lost and lacking will, Momir looks through his list of organisms.
“Nothing is worth his scrutiny,” Momir complains.
Finally, his scanning finds a grand form without fault, BioVisionary.
Thinking hard, bold but rational philosophy turns logic through airtight analysis.
Satisfactory strands of thought align into his magnum opus: Simic Assistant Tutor, a Cryptid Zoologist, a Quandrix Cultivator, an Amped Entomologist, a Bookworm.
All paths find BioVisionary.
Momir looks for mana through dirt, rocks, and things part both.
His supply is found.
A cunning trick finds its way into his mind.
Bouncing his assistants, a Portal of Sanctuary, a Shard of Crystal, and an Umbilicus fulfill his fantasy.
With BioVisionary on board, many cards copy it.
Organisms such as Mockingbird, artifacts such as Soul Foundry, and surprising magic such as Sakashima’s Will allow Momir to join in, too.
Four mirrors from a primordial first, a smiling Momir claims victory.

That story laid out a lot of what the deck is doing, but let’s do a brief rundown.
Momir Vig is a commander with two abilities attached.
Casting green creature spells lets you tutor up creatures to the top of your library, and casting blue creature spells lets you draw the top card of your library; casting blue‑green spells puts cards directly into your hand in classic original Ravnica fashion.
BioVisionary is a card that wins you the game if you have at least four copies of it in play on your end step.
In a singleton format like EDH, this means that some form of copying is generally required to make it work as a win con, and naturally, this deck has a fair amount of that.
12 cards exist to copy it in some form or other, though these span a wide range of things.
You’ve got the basic copy plan, which will take a bit of effort; you’ve got the stronger, though non‑tutorable plan of using a card like Sakashima’s Will on a wide board; and you’ve got riskier plans such as exiling BioVisionary under Soul Foundry and hoping to be able to activate it four times.
As for finding each of these components, the deck features nine Simic creatures for purposes of tutoring up clones, as well as a number of other green creatures.
There are also cards like Crystal Shard and Umbilicus, which bounce creatures to be recast, and which can also let the deck repeat ETBs on top of that.
Outside of that, this deck features a good amount of ramp, which seems like a good idea for a couple reasons.
For one, Momir Vig is a five‑mana commander with a concerning effect, so that’s basically a seven‑ or nine‑mana commander if you think about it.
Outside of commander tax, Momir Vig also feeds the deck card advantage at a healthy clip, so naturally, it makes sense to make sure to have a way of spending that.
In terms of green symbols, this deck contains 15 amongst its lands and two amongst its colorless ramp, for a total of 17 symbols, which certainly isn’t great, but can be made workable enough with a somewhat aggressive mulligan policy.
This deck certainly represents a fun navigation of this challenge’s tricky requirement.

Conceptual Decks
Iron Man and Now I Know My ABCs

“It’s missing.
How?” Tony hasn’t got an inkling.
With a total of 62 4K cams running constantly throughout this wing, saving and backing up 2 TB worth of visual data to his main SSD array hourly, no burglar of any amount of skill could slip through such vanishingly slim cracks.
Still, Jarvis had scrupulously dug through it all at his insisting, only to turn up nothing.
And what was Tony anticipating to find anyway?
It’s not a physical thing that’s missing.
No, this was a villain’s doing, not your bog standard cat burglar.
But who?
Doom?
No, no motivation.
Loki?
Now that would fit, but it’s difficult to sort out his thoughts without such a vital symbol.
And Tony Stark’s a man of action anyway.
Sitting around ruminating isn’t his strong suit.
As his armor’s hydraulics hissed into motion, Iron Man sighs and shrugs.
Just got to go out and find it, huh?

Iron Man himself is a five‑mana flying beater who creates treasures and offers a birthing pod sort of effect for artifacts each turn.
Should I have heard that sort of effect as an Oswald effect?
A repurposing effect?
Anyway, he turns artifacts into other artifacts.
The silver border card this deck features is Now I Know My ABCs, a card that wins you the game on your upkeep if you control permanents with all 26 letters of the alphabet in their names.
Iron Man is actually a great choice to go with this for a couple reasons.
For one, treasures, that’s an E.
Secondly, it allows the deck to go hunting for some of the less common letters out there that might be attached to artifacts.
Naturally, this deck has a number of artifacts with less common characters: Tamiyo’s Journal, Blazing Torch, Drivers’ Familiar, Acquisition Octopus, and so on.
There’s even Spy Kit, an equipment that gives a creature the names of all legendary creatures, which certainly simplifies the game plan a bit as long as you can keep the creature in play until your upkeep.
Even outside the artifacts, there’s a lot of variety; we’ve got Accumulox here for that lovely French Vanilla Mirrodin gameplay, as well as Grass Lak and Zorn.
The core of this deck is Now I Know My ABCs, but this deck has a good number of options to pivot into a more conventional artifact beatdown sort of plan if needed.
Sharding Sphinx and a handful of other flyers make for a solid plan alongside combat damage to player effects.
All of this and more is explained in the deck’s 700‑word primer, also including no E’s.
I have to respect the effort on display here.
I briefly toyed with the idea of writing this script E‑less, but quickly abandoned that idea when it became clear how difficult long‑form writing without E’s is, particularly when it comes to navigating around common words I might normally use, like commander or deck.

Mirrorwing Tiamat: A Cutthroat Combo Deck
#34
DeckbuildingJul 20253,260 words in transcript

What Counts as Card Draw in Commander?

The Odd Truth About Draw in EDH
We argue that so-called card draw in EDH encompasses a range of effects that either help spend excess mana or locate specific cards.We explain how mana sinks, impulse draw, recursion, and filtering can substitute for traditional draw based on a deck's needs.The main lesson is to diagnose a deck's primary bottleneck before deciding what types of 'draw' to include.
Use this in your deckDiagnose whether your deck's main bottleneck is unspent mana or missing key cards, then replace generic draw with mana sinks or selection accordingly.
WhiteBlackRedMidrangeSpellslingerCard EvaluationDeck ConsistencySynergyDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • Card advantage and card selection address two distinct deck bottlenecks: unspent mana and missing key cards.
Evidence
  • X spells and repeatable mana sinks allow battle cruiser decks to convert excess resources into board impact without traditional draw.
  • Recursion spells like Seasons Past and evolution witness effectively reuse cards, mimicking draw in decks with graveyard access.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Before adding draw, determine whether your deck primarily wants to turn mana into value or to dig for specific combo or synergy pieces.
Limitation
  • Recursion engines need self-mill or sacrifice themes to fill the graveyard before they can generate value.
  • Impulse draw effects are less effective when a deck contains many situational cards that cannot be played immediately.
Example
  • An Asheen deck prioritizes creatures with on-attack draw and utility lands over generic X burn spells, because they align with its combat strategy while still functioning as mana outlets.
More quotes
