  1. Fundamentally, the source of ambiguity here is that card draw as a concept often gets treated as one singular thing, but it's actually more like a web of overlapping solutions to achieve a handful of different goals.
  2. The goals of card draw are a little fuzzy, but broadly there are two main ones that decks will be focused on: spending excess mana and finding particular cards.
  3. Spending more mana than opponents will generally make it easier to defeat them regardless of what else is happening.
  4. [X spells and mana sinks] aren't exactly draw, and you'd get some weird looks if you tried to call them that, but they absolutely perform some of the same functions as draw in decks mostly focused on spending mana.
  5. A Steelshaper's Gift that exclusively finds a Skullclamp followed by Sword of the Animist? That's a draw spell. Get it out of that tutor category.
  6. When I'm deck building on Archidekt, I'll generally put tutors in my draw category in two cases: if they generally find a source of card advantage or if the function of tutors wholly lines up with the function of drawing cards.
  7. If you're playing a combo deck where the main thing your draw spells accomplish is digging for combo pieces or tutors, then the purpose of your draw is almost exclusively card selection and tutors accomplish.
  8. We can actually construct a spectrum and place all the types of cards I've mentioned today on it. On one side, we have pure selection effects that are all about seeing extra cards. And on the other side, pure advantage cards that turn mana into value without seeing any cards at all.
  9. Ledger Shredder: pure filtering. Tutors generally also pure filtering, though a bit flexible depending on what you plan to get.
  10. Nearing the center of the spectrum, you've got things that generate a decent bit of card advantage, but that also do a bit of extra digging. And this includes my personal favorite draw X card in most decks, Pull from Tomorrow.
Full transcript

Is a potentially repeatable recursion source like Evolution Witness a card draw spell?
How about something that draws and discards, like Matsilantly the Great Door?
Is that considered a draw spell?
What about so-called “impulse draw” options like Leia, the Blade Reforged?
I imagine you might have opinions on any one of those questions; maybe you said yes to all, maybe you said yes to none, and maybe you’re a diehard Great Door fan, no more, no less.
It would be satisfying and lovely if I could just flat out say exactly what does and does not count as “card draw” among these, but unfortunately, it’s a bit more complicated than that.
Fundamentally, the source of ambiguity here is that “card draw” as a concept often gets treated as one singular thing, but it’s actually more like a web of overlapping solutions to achieve a handful of different goals, and different decks will want different sorts of things deemed “draw” for completely different reasons.

The Two Main Goals of Card Draw
Spending Excess Mana

This is actually pretty similar to the process of learning a real-time strategy game.
Becoming an average skill Starcraft player basically just requires two things: building up resource generation and then being able to spend all the resources you end up accumulating.
We can see parallels to EDH battle cruiser decks with this.
As long as you’re completing basic tasks like hitting land drops, playing some ramp spells, and spending all your mana each turn, you’ll probably be having an okay time; and once you’re at that point, you can turn your attention to the specifics of what your mana is actually accomplishing.

In the context of this type of deck, a deck chiefly concerned with managing mana gained versus mana spent, all sorts of things can loosely fit into the category of “draw.” Adding X spells to a deck will typically reduce its need for draw since they’re very effective at spending mana, and the same goes for X abilities and other abilities that are useful to sink a lot of mana into over a turn cycle.
These aren’t exactly draw, and you’d get some weird looks if you tried to call them that, but they absolutely perform some of the same functions as draw in decks mostly focused on spending mana.
The key here is going to be making sure that mana is being spent in ways that are actually effective at pushing toward a deck’s goals.
Throwing a random Fireball in an Asheen deck might make it better at spending mana, but an X-cost damage spell probably isn’t a type of card that will generally progress the goals of a creature-based mid-range deck.
The best available ways to spend mana in a nasheen deck will typically be creatures with on-attack draw abilities that synergize with the deck’s commander, and there are also a number of utility lands in Mardu colors that can soak up mana while accomplishing useful tasks for a combat-centered deck.

Draw Alternatives That Spend Mana

Another popular non-draw source of value generation is recursion spells, cards that return things from graveyard to hand.
This could be a big one-time option such as Seasons Past, and it could also be a recursion engine such as Evolution Witness plus counter shenanigans, scrapler plus some artifacts, or even something discreet like Forexian Reclamation.
You could even include graveyard abilities such as flashback or escape in this category.
All of these options serve a similar task, allowing already played cards to be reused again in some way.
These sorts of effects are typically going to be strongest in decks that involve lots of redundancy and similar cards, since returning a common card type from graveyard to hand is going to be pretty similar in effect to drawing one of those cards.
However, these still tend to have limitations in that they require having stuff in the graveyard to function.
For a deck with lots of sacrifice or which is based around instants and sorceries, key cards will get into the graveyard all on their own, but other types of decks might want sources of self-mill or self-discard to keep their graveyard stocked with juicy options for returning.
Generally, the more self-mill or wheel-style effects you add to a deck, the more your recursion spells will stray away from the task of spending mana and start to gain some genuine selection potential.
And this brings me to the second goal.

Finding Particular Cards: Filtering

If we want to stretch the definition of “filtering” a little, and I certainly do, tutors are the ultimate example of this: cards that search a player’s library for a card that fits some set of constraints.
Solve the Equation is basically a Solundai vision that looks at unlimited cards instead of six, and Demonic Tutor is basically an Impulse that looks at unlimited cards instead of four, sorcery speed though, so it’s pretty balanced.
So, do tutors count as card draw?
Do cards like Impulse count as draw?
It depends on what your deck’s goals are with running these cards.
In some decks, it might be important to distinguish between what cards give card advantage as opposed to what cards only give card selection.
A spellslinger deck might run Impulse, and it might be a helpful card for smoothing out gameplay, but if the deck is currently running a fairly low number of draw engines, it’s not necessarily going to be a helpful addition for purposes of maintaining cards in hand.
Likewise, if you have a really impactful card you’re tutoring for, a tutor very well could feel like a draw spell, particularly if it’s a tutor that generally searches up some sort of value engine.
A Steelshaper’s Gift that exclusively finds a Skullclamp followed by Sword of Frontier, that’s a draw spell.
Get it out of that tutor category.

When I’m deck building on Architect, I’ll generally put tutors in my draw category in two cases: if they generally find a source of card advantage, or if the function of tutors wholly lines up with the function of drawing cards.
For example, if I’m playing a combo deck where the main thing my draw spells accomplish is digging for combo pieces or tutors, then the purpose of my draw is almost exclusively card selection, and tutors accomplish that.
I’ve also reached the same conclusion in the opposite direction.
My Brina deck is an aggro deck with lots of redundancy, so tutors aren’t much better than basic card draw.
When I added IR’s Oathworn because of its synergy with the commander, there was no reason for me not to group it with the deck’s draw because it’s performing the same task.
However, in other decks, tutors play a somewhat distinct role.
In my strongest deck, I run tutors to find Time Sieve because that’s what I need to win, but I also run bite and defasa effects, which generically draw cards when my creatures hit players.
This can help find Time, but it can also draw me into counterspells to protect my combo.
And if Time Sieve is up and running, but I don’t have a full combo, having an engine that gives me an extra three to four cards a turn is typically enough to turn a couple extra turns into a win by helping me find things to give extra artifacts.
Card selection and card advantage are both helpful, and having a mixture of selection-only cards and generic card draw that provides both works out to fulfill the deck’s needs in the draw department.

A Spectrum from Selection to Advantage

Nearing the center of the spectrum, you’ve got things that generate a decent bit of card advantage but that also do a bit of extra digging.
This includes my personal favorite draw-X card in most decks, Pull from Tomorrow.
It’s instant speed, reasonably costed, and the disadvantage of discarding a card is typically negligible since it’s attached to a card that draws a bunch of cards first.
You could also put something like Necropotence here, since absent a Reliquary Tower, you’ll generally be exiling some amount of what you draw, even if there’s a lot of volume of cards entering your hand.
Generic card draw leans a bit toward advantage compared to other options.
Though a lot of the strongest draw is just generic card draw, so oftentimes decks will focus on this even if they care mostly about card selection.

After that, you’ve got impulse draw and recursion engines.
While the former is relatively straightforward, the latter will vary a bit depending on how well it’s supported.
Recursion spells might lean very close to advantage, though they also might have some degree of selection as well, depending on how much self-mill and discard a deck has.
It’s often far easier to get cards to the graveyard than into hand, but the tools for hating on graveyards are far stronger and far more ubiquitous than cards that force opponents to discard are, so recursion has some natural limits in how reliable it can be.
On the far end of things, we have abilities that can soak up an unlimited amount of mana.
Kessig Wolf Run is a land with a powerful activated ability that a deck can sink as much mana as it likes into.
It will never find you any cards, but it can grant the value of giving a creature you control +X/+0 and trample, and the only cost to have this ability available is running a colorless land.
So, this is a spectrum of what different styles of draw and filtering will accomplish in terms of value and card selection.
But I worry that all this categorization and visual flourish and such like might serve to abstract the issue beyond what’s helpful.
So, how do you find the best options for your deck?
And how do you know when you have enough?
Let’s start with that first question.

How to Choose the Best Options for Your Deck
#35
Format TheoryJul 2024330K views

Why cheap staples like Sol Ring warp Commander deckbuilding

The Problem with "Budget" Cards
We argue that cheap, powerful staples like Sol Ring warp Commander deckbuilding by discouraging players from considering slightly worse alternatives.This leads to homogenization and neglect of synergy pieces because the power gap appears too large when sorted by popularity or price.Despite this, Sol Ring is unlikely to be banned due to its reprint history and format entrenchment.
Use this in your deckAdd at least two more counterspells to your blue deck, even if they appear strictly worse than Counterspell.
WhiteBlueGreenRampRemovalCountersBudgetCard EvaluationFormat HealthEDHREC Data
Principle
  • The presence of a cheap, format-defining staple causes players to undervalue slightly worse alternatives, leading them to run too few copies of that effect.
  • Sorting cards by popularity on sites like EDHREC exacerbates this by visually reinforcing a steep power cliff after the top options.
Evidence
  • Sol Ring's low price was maintained through constant Commander pre-con reprints, which cemented its ubiquity and discouraged exploration of other mana rocks.
  • Wizards included Sol Ring in the original Commander decks under the mistaken belief that its current affordability meant it should be a precon staple, ignoring that format growth would naturally increase its price.
Deckbuilding Application
  • When browsing counterspells on EDHREC, the drop from Counterspell to three-mana options leads players to skip them, even though most blue decks benefit from running at least a couple more.
  • In contrast, searching for green creature-cast triggers presents many viable cards without an obvious cutoff, encouraging players to include more of that effect.
Limitation
  • our opinion that Sol Ring should remain expensive is biased by not having a commercial stake, unlike Wizards, so it may not align with practical incentives.
  • The Commander ban list is inconsistent and unlikely to address Sol Ring, reflecting the inherent difficulty of regulating such a diverse format.
Example
  • Group playtesting often sees cards dismissed as 'worse Sol Ring' or 'worse Lightning Bolt,' a shorthand that obscures nuanced card evaluation.
  • The green value engine example shows that without an obvious top dog, players are more willing to run multiple cards like Beast Whisperer and The Great Henge.
More quotes
  1. I think it would have been fine or even advisable to let Soul Ring's price soar as the format size grows, let it be a toy for the power gamers alongside Mana Crypt, Moxen and the like.
  2. Commander's ban list is, hot take alert, kind of a stupid mess.
  3. Unless somebody goes around burning every single copy of Soul Ring on Earth released after 2010, a ban would really be the only solution.
  4. One of the most compelling issues with these cheap powerful cards is that they warp the way players look at other cards.
  5. A joke in my play group is to respond to 'what does that card do?' with 'oh yeah it's like a worse Soul Ring.'.
  6. It's like somebody asking how old a person is and you saying 'oh yeah they're younger than Henry Kissinger.'.
  7. A deck running too much synergistic stuff and not enough general stuff is at risk of getting itself into a bimodal pickle.
  8. There's an even sneakier type of budget card: the ones that cost zero dollars.
  9. You should add cards to a deck considering how they'll impact the games you play, even if you own the cards and they're free to add.
  10. [In my budget equipment deck,] when I played Sword of Forge and Frontier, it felt like a totally different deck.
Full transcript
The Legacy of Sol Ring

The reasoning for this choice on Wizards’ part was simple: Sol Ring was considered by some to be a must-include in the then-small Commander format, since, on account of it being banned in Legacy, its low double-digits price was pretty affordable for its power level, so the precon decks should also include Sol Ring.
I could say a lot about this logic, most prominently that Sol Ring’s relative affordability was a product of it not being played in large formats.
Printing sets specifically for EDH was a great way to inflate the size of the format, a process which, without a reprint, would have caused a demand spike with a fixed supply and increased the price, likely relegating Sol Ring to higher power level EDH decks.
It’s a problem that solves itself, one could argue.
Also, the pre-con Commander decks weren’t exactly well constructed, so the idea that they need to keep up with the decks of long-time Commander players is pretty laughable.
But something you might notice is that there’s a small difference between me and Wizards of the Coast: I don’t make money off of how many units of the Commander pre-cons get sold, and they do.
So I guess I’m a little biased that way, but okay.

I think it would have been fine, or even advisable, to let Sol Ring’s price soar as the format size grows; let it be a toy for the power gamers alongside Mana Crypt, Moxen, and the like.
I understand why some people might disagree, but that’s my opinion.
There are also people who have argued that it should be banned, such as a writer known as chisholicious two months after the Commander set’s release in 2011.
And, well, unless somebody goes around burning every single copy of Sol Ring on Earth released after 2010, a ban would really be the only solution, but it’s not going to happen.
Commander’s ban list is, hot take alert, kind of a stupid mess that very clearly underscores the difficulty of moderating such a large format played in so many different ways and with such a wide potential pool of cards.
On top of this, Sol Ring has been printed in over a dozen Commander sets; Wizards have done their utmost to place the card within the pantheon of EDH.
Sol Ring is here to stay.

How Cheap Power Warps Deckbuilding

As a counter scenario, one can imagine a universe in which a player’s removal options solely consist of Naturalize, Murder, Lava Coil, and Banishing Light, to be played in whatever quantities the player wants.
In this universe, a player is forced to spend more time thinking about questions like, “How much am I worried about enchantments and artifacts?
How many creature removal spells do I need to deal with opposing pressure and win conditions?” The question isn’t what specific removal spells a player needs but rather how much of these sort of stock options.
Back in the real world, things end up working out a bit different than this.
This is especially pronounced if a player is using a website like EDHREC or some other metric that orders cards by popularity or quality.
If you’re looking for counter spells in Scryfall ordered by EDHREC rank, as I have been spotted doing from time to time, you first see Counterspell, a card that’ll set you back a dollar, great, I love it.
Then you see a couple of other options that are similar with a downside, as well as a couple that cost a lot of money.
By the time you get down to your standard staples, your kitchen table kings, your Modern machinations, or whatever, it’s looking a lot less tempting.
You have your Mana Leak type cards that fall off fast in power in later turns, you have your more specialized options, and then you have your counter spells that commit the cardinal sin of costing three mana.

It’s easy to imagine a player seeing the rapid price increases and power decreases that happen when you scroll downward from Counterspell, going, “Well, okay, I guess I’ll run Counterspell and a couple of these other ones, and then I’ll have the good counter spells within my budget.” Now, it should be mentioned that Counterspell is an incredibly powerful card.
Most other counter options that players can run are worse than it, yes, but I’d guess that most blue decks want lowercase-C counter spells enough that they’d benefit from running cards that are kind of a lot worse than Counterspell.
A joke in my play group is to respond to the question of “Oh, what does that card do?” with something like, “Oh yeah, it’s like a ‘worse Soul Ring’” or “Oh yeah, it’s basically a ‘worse version of Lightning Bolt.’” The joke here is that these statements tell you almost nothing about the actual power of a card; it’s like somebody asking how old a person is and you saying, “Oh yeah, they’re younger than Henry Kissinger,” helpful, thank you.

As a point of contrast, we can consider other types of cards somebody might choose to run, ones whose power is less obvious.
It’s pretty easy to go, “Oh, Wizards’ Retort, that’s strictly worse than Counterspell,” but what about if I want, say, a card that generates value when I play creature spells?
All right, Scryfall, let’s go green cards that have “whenever cast” and “creature” in their text, and let’s rank them based on EDHREC rank.
And there, well, okay, so there’s Beast Whisperer, and there’s The Great Henge, and there’s Silverback Elder, and there’s Primeval Bounty, and there’s Glimpse of Nature, and there’s Wild Pair, and there’s Matthew Admirers, and there’s Benjamin.
So which ones are the bad ones?
Which one should I add, and where should I stop adding them?
These cards are pulled from all across the 57 ordered cards that pop up with my search inputs.
Some are more flexible, or more bursty, or lower to the ground, but analyzing them is a much trickier value proposition than looking at counter spells.
It’s possible I did the math on my deck ahead of time and went, “Okay, I need two or three of these cards, preferably at least one in the four-to-five mana range and at least one in the six-to-seven mana range,” but if I didn’t, gee, there are sure a lot of good-looking cards here.
I could run “worse Counterspell” or “worse Soul Ring,” or I could run more of these.
And counter spells aren’t the only thing to get neglected.
If I’m looking for creature removal, Swords to Plowshares and Path to Exile are both under two bucks and absurdly powerful, with other white creature removal being much worse or much more expensive.
If I’m looking for artifact removal, red has Abrade and Vandalblast, with other options once again being worse or more expensive.
If I’m looking for general-purpose draw for blue, there are a few low-budget options: Mystic Remora is fabulous, especially with its price halved by a Dominaria Remastered reprint, and Mulldrifter, Treasure Cruise, and Archmage Emeritus all have decent spots in several different archetypes.
But beyond that, things get weaker, more specialized, or more expensive.
If somebody isn’t setting out a framework for their deck, it’s easy to imagine underdoing it on the more generalist stuff and overdoing it on the more synergistic stuff, and a deck running too much synergistic stuff and not enough general stuff is at risk of getting itself into a bimodal pickle: a state where it either falls on its face after getting a key piece removed or it goes on to a Mach 10 march toward victory.

The Sneaky Problem of Zero-Dollar Cards

There are certainly powerful cards that are cheap due to wide reprints or another reason, such as Sol Ring, Counterspell, and Swords to Plowshares, but there’s an even sneakier type of budget card: the ones that cost zero dollars.
A quick anecdote: I recently built a Meria, Scholar of Antiquity deck.
It’s an equipment deck; you throw a bunch of equipments on Meria and then tap them to generate mana and value.
I kind of built it as a meme, but it’s a genuinely delightful mid-power level aggro deck.
Anyway, after ordering the forty dollars’ worth of mostly random bulk I needed for the list, I sat staring at the Sword of the Animist and the Sword of Forge and Frontier I had in my collection.
They cost no money to add and would be so good in the deck.
I put them in the deck for a few games, but they had such a warping effect that I decided to take them out.
My deck’s mana and value engines were pretty solid for an aggro deck but decidedly reasonable in scope; while Explorer’s Scope and Rogue’s Gloves both feel very appropriate within the list, when I played Sword of Forge and Frontier it felt like a totally different deck.
And I know the fact that’s within the realm of possibility naturally shifts the way my opponents will think about my deck.
Ultimately, it wasn’t meant to be a particularly high-powered deck, nor could it hope of performing like a high-powered deck on the current budget, so I cut the swords.
The point being made here is that you should add cards to a deck under consideration of how they’ll impact the games you play with the deck, even if you own the cards and they’re free to add.
My quasi-meme equipment deck has a handful of specific weaknesses, and adding power cards to the deck doesn’t help it manage those weaknesses with any consistency; it just makes people want to play better decks against it.

An even better example is graveyard decks.
Graveyard synergies are wide-ranging, fun, and potentially quite powerful, but they exist within a game that has a lot of really heavy-duty graveyard hate.
There are the options like Tormod’s Crypt and Rest in Peace, which straightforwardly demolish graveyard strategies, and even cards like Nihil Spellbomb and Bojuka Bog, which do so without even spending a card, and with the latter dodging counter spells on top of all that.
All this to say, decks heavily based around graveyards need to make a decision: accept the need to be resilient against this stuff, usually by winning quickly or being loaded with counters for the hate, or play at a power level where these hate cards are less common.
This puts a lot of mid-power graveyard decks in an awkward spot.
Adding stronger synergies will lead to crushing unprepared decks even more than before, but will still end up with getting wholesale dunked on by hate cards unless the efficiency of the deck is vastly improved to guarantee quicker or more abrupt wins.
There’s not necessarily a right or wrong answer here, but my mantra is that if you don’t want to play higher power level, don’t play higher power level.

Another example of this is cheap infinite combos, namely Niv-Mizzet and Curiosity.
I’ve known people who went, “Well, this deck is running red and blue and I’ve got the card sitting right here; why not add them?” If you’re purely looking to increase the power level of your deck, this is fine logic, but adding an infinite combo to your deck changes the whole dynamic of games once people know it’s there.
As a frequent enjoyer of aggro, I often find myself asking the question, “How will each of my opponents be trying to win this game?” If the answer goes from “I don’t know, Drakes or something” to an infinite combo that requires very little setup, I’m probably going to be hitting that player with my creatures, and they’re definitely going to deserve it.

As another anecdote about building decks for the types of games you want to play, my brother recently got his first Commander deck, a decently built Jodah.
In a night with several EDH games, everybody pulled out one of the more powerful lists every time he played the Jodah deck, resulting in games where he was doing a lot but also eating a lot of removal spells and needing to deal with a bunch of powerful nonsense from other people.
The games were close, but the only gam.

#36
DeckbuildingJul 2024263K views

Transparent Deckbuilding Avoids Small Bean Syndrome

The Curse of Smol Bean Syndrome
We argue that small bean syndrome, verbally downplaying one's board state to avoid attacks, often stems from inconsistent deckbuilding.Building decks with transparent win conditions and consistent performance through more draw, filtering, and ramp reduces the need for political manipulation.Examples like the Glissa and Meria decks show how visible threats foster less conflict.
Use this in your deckIncrease the proportion of low-cost ramp spells in your deck so that you successfully ramp to your desired mana threshold in over 40% of games.
GreenBlackRedMidrangeVoltronDiscardTable PoliticsDeck ConsistencyPower LevelThreat AssessmentSocial and Politics
Principle
  • Small bean syndrome is a political tactic where players downplay their board state to avoid being attacked.
  • Transparent deckbuilding reduces the need for small bean syndrome by making a deck's threat level obvious from its board state.
Evidence
  • Inconsistent decks have a power crater where they underperform a large percentage of the time, making small bean syndrome a rational response.
  • Players who conceal their resources and wait for others to use interaction first, playing like a nerd, may accrue social distrust.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Add more low-cost draw, filtering, and interaction spells to smooth out deck performance and reduce non-games.
  • For ramp strategies, ensure cheap ramp spells constitute a large enough proportion to reach your desired mana in over 40% of games.
Limitation
  • At cEDH, bluffing and deception are expected, but at mid-power, transparent decks promote fairer politics.
  • Consistently hiding your game state can cause opponents to overestimate unknown threats, leading to misdirected removal.
Example
  • The Glissa deck is transparent because its power derives from ramp, draw, and creatures, all visible on board.
  • The Meria Voltron deck's power is tied to visible equipment, and it runs a known 10% protection spells.
More quotes
  1. I call this Small Bean Syndrome: when a player goes out of their way to paint their deck as being in a bad spot, deserving nothing but mercy.
  2. I'm not a big fan of most forms of small bean syndrome due to their tendency to discourage attacks and artificially lengthen games.
  3. An inconsistent deck with explosive cards but no connective tissue will naturally have moments of popping off and moments of the opposite.
  4. When piloting an all-or-nothing deck, small bean syndrome is a form of power level smoothing, a way of papering over the power crater you made for yourself and bolstering your weakest moments.
  5. At a CEDH table, downplaying your game state is the norm, as it should be. You have every right to bluff and engage in low-level deception.
  6. If you're playing a deck whose wins result from weird tech pieces interacting to create unexpected avalanches of value, people might get apprehensive.
  7. Human beings have a natural tendency to fear the unknown, even if being unknown might feel like the correct play.
  8. My Glissa deck is a very consistent deck. I call it transparent: you can always approximate how well it's doing by looking at the board state, cards in hand, and land count.
  9. My Glissa deck is a powerful deck against conventionally built mid-power decks. I call it midrange crusher 5000 for a reason, but the transparency means people worry about it less than they probably should.
  10. My $25 Rakdos discard deck deletes people's hands and makes them upset, but the rest of the deck is just grindy creatures and a commander to clear the way for a small group of two-twos and three-threes to attack.
Full transcript

I think most of us have seen games like this.
It's turn five or so, and a player is getting ready to go to attacks.
Immediately, a chorus erupts from the would-be attacker's opponents: "Come on, man, I don't have anything." "Hey, don't attack me, I missed a land drop." "Really?
You're going to attack me?
But I guess I was just trying to be funny." I like to call this style of table engagement SBS, "small bean syndrome," when a player goes out of their way to paint their deck as being in a bad spot and deserving nothing but mercy.

Now, Commander's nature as a multiplayer format means the table politics naturally play a major role in a lot of games.
In addition, a lot of people play EDH specifically for a less cutthroat MTG experience; not everybody at every table is looking to win quickly or even win at all.
I'm not a big fan of most forms of small bean syndrome, due to their tendency to discourage attacks and artificially lengthen games, but I can accept that not everybody is trying to play the same kind of EDH I am.
However, there are also people who share my desire to play quicker and more cutthroat games and who employ small bean syndrome more tactically.
This may be intentional, but it also might not be, which brings me to a major reason for SBS's existence: deck inconsistency.

If somebody has an inconsistent deck, one with some powerful and/or explosive cards but a lack of support or connective tissue for such cards, there will naturally be moments of popping off and moments of the opposite of popping off.
This means that such a player can be telling the truth when they say they're having a whiff moment, but this can still be a deception in some sense of the word.
Let's try an exercise real quick: here's a graph of their deck's performance, with the Y-axis being power and the X-axis being percentile of power at a given moment (you know, 20% of the time they'll be at least this level of powerful, 80% of the time they'll be at least this much lower quantity of powerful).
Make sense?
No?
Great.
You obviously got this moderately small percentage of the time where their deck is going absolutely ham, doing whatever it is the powerful decks do, but also this gulf back here where their deck is farting around doing nothing.
Certainly, they're having a bad time here, but this is often a situation arising from deck-building choices.
A deck that dedicates too much space to the synergistic and the explosive will have some big turns and occasionally massive wins, but also a lot of moments where it withers to a single removal spell or just can't quite put the pieces of the puzzle together.
These moments could be made less frequent by running more of that connective tissue: more filtering, more small-scale draw, and more creatures and low-cost interaction spells to protect themselves.
If they're ramping to big cards, they could run a larger proportion of small-scale ramp spells in their deck so that they successfully ramp where they want to be more than 40% of the time.
When a person is piloting a sort of all-or-nothing deck, small bean syndrome is a form of "power level smoothing," a way of papering over the power crater they made for themselves and bolstering these weakest moments.
And I don't mean this as an attack necessarily; if this sounds like you, please take this as deck-building advice.

Concealing Your Game State

A related concept to small bean syndrome is people who don't verbally talk down their game state, but who do play in a way that conceals how well they're doing.
At a cEDH table, this is the norm, as it should be at that power; you're trying to win a cutthroat game of Magic against other people trying to win even more cutthroatly, and you have every right to bluff, to downplay your game state, and to otherwise engage in low-level deception.
At a more middling power level, though, this might not be the best way to go.
If you're playing a deck with a clear win con, people might know what to expect even if they're a bit scared about it, but if you're playing a deck whose wins result from weird tech pieces interacting to create unexpected avalanches of value and board state, well, people might get a bit apprehensive.
When a deck is doing a lot of things, but it's unclear how it wins, people may be asking themselves the question, "Oh God, is it that?
Or is it that?" Additionally, there are some strategic ways of playing that might involve saving resources and trying to force other people to use interaction first.
You might say to yourself, "Hmm, that card is spooky, but if I hold no removal, somebody else might deal with it, and then they'll have less removal when I want to do something spooky later." My friend Mr. Hans calls this "playing like a nerd," and though it's decent from a strategic standpoint, it's the kind of slightly baddie play style that people will eventually catch on to.
Whether or not you get punished for playing shady or playing nerdy will depend on the table, but you should keep in mind that human beings have a natural tendency to fear the unknown, even if being unknown might feel like the correct play.

Transparent Decks

For an example of a deck that performs almost the opposite way, let's talk about my Glissa deck.
I talk about it a lot, and I will do so again here.
The deck consists of ramp, draw spells, removal, and big creatures: no bursty wins, no surprises, and no weird tech pieces or out-of-place synergy cards hogging space.
There's nothing spicy with the commander either; she just adds draw to improve consistency and is a good blocker to help with survivability.
The deck simply does its battle cruiser thing.
People who've played against the deck know that's what it does, and if people ask me what it does, well, that's what I'll tell them.
If I'm currently behind while playing that deck, well, guess what?
When I get back on my feet, you can expect more ramp, draw, big creatures, and removal.
Get ready.
It's a very consistent deck; it's also what I'll call a "transparent" deck.
By this I mean, you can always approximate how well it's doing by looking at the board state, cards in hand, and land count of the deck.
If the deck has a big board, well, the threat is right there, no extreme synergies or combos to worry about later.
If the deck has a lot of cards in hand, there's a good chance it's packing a lot of removal.
If the deck has a big land count, there are all sorts of moderately scary things to worry about, but the odds of the spookiest outcomes are lessened the fewer cards in hand the deck has.
It's a powerful deck against a lot of conventionally built mid-power decks; I call it "Midrange Crusher 5000" for a reason, but the transparency and absence of sudden win cons means that people tend to worry about it less than they probably should.

Another transparent deck I have is my Meria deck.
Although that one has more unique tech and response plays, it's transparent because it's a Voltron deck.
The problem is right there on the board in front of you.
If there's a Meria on board with a lot of equipments, I'm doing pretty well.
Most of my draws will be exiles off the top, so you're seeing most of the stuff I'm seeing; the scary stuff is all visible.
There might be some protection spells in my hand, but the deck devotes a full 10% of its space to protection, so that much shouldn't be a shock.
Meria also has the advantage over Glissa that it's low to the ground.
My deck can create a value engine with its commander to exile cards, but that value is more small artifacts and creatures, and the deck doesn't do much if the artifact count is low or if the commander gets removed.
Even with a higher artifact count, the deck still only hits one player a turn and gains maybe four cards' worth of value.

My $25 Rakdos discard deck is even lower to the ground, only incrementally drawing cards with a handful of scrappy creatures, and honestly, that's the deck's only saving grace.
It deletes people's hands and makes them upset, but the rest of the deck is just grindy creatures and a commander to clear the way for my small group of 2/2s and 3/3s to attack.
People will sometimes attack it out of spite for a bit, but the non-discard portion of the deck is so utterly fair Magic that people will usually go, "Oh, you're just doing that now." Huh.
And won't worry about it too much.

Fight the Table Ideology

All three of these decks pair well with a style of table engagement I like to call "fight the table ideology." They can happily admit to exactly what they plan to do; they can do it fairly consistently, and they are prepared for the consequences if they become an enemy.
Me and Mr. Hans originally coined this phrase to describe the optimal play style for mixed power level games, or alternatively, games where one player gets a decisive advantage early.
If you're playing more powerfully than other people, you could play close to the chest before popping off with a power turn and deleting the table, but that's not really fun for anybody.
However, give your opponents the information they need to know and be open about the general shape of what your deck is doing, specifically how it wins and what cards you're playing are doing there, and suddenly you've got a dynamic game where your opponents are strategizing about how to keep you in check while managing their own individual plans.
Any deck can partake in this ideology in a game playing against lower power decks, and I encourage giving it a try.
But as a method, it also works as a more general style of deck building and play.

Now, I can already hear some of you say, "But Alex, why would I want to be the enemy?
I don't want my life total to be eaten into the sun by a bunch of quivering nerds who I've been smugly informing of their impending demise at the hands of my deck." Well, first, if you are in the dominant position, doing literally that can lead to some really fun gameplay as long as you're not an ass about it.
But more broadly, this method can actually save your deck from some hate, and let's talk about that.

Step One: Pose a Clear, Consistent Threat

This might seem odd for hate dodging, but step one of fight the table ideology is that you should pose some sort of clear threat to the table with a good degree of consistency.
This could be early damage, big mid-game beats, board wipes, stax, and so forth.
This will mostly come down to building a deck that's focused and isn't filled with unnecessary tech pieces or creatures out of sync with the game plan, and you can accomplish this step on a shockingly low budget.
The point of this step is that what you're doing needs to be visible.
To go back to an earlier point, I've seen hyper-techy mid-range decks which fill their board with unusual permanents with no clear sense of how they'll be used.
If you do this, people might feel a looming sense of threat, but with no obvious sense of what to worry about, this might simply result in them attacking you.
By contrast, if the threat is visible, people can make their own educated calculations, and that calculation might just be, "Eh, it's probably fine." Similarly, consistency is important because that will be baked into people's calculations as well.
If we take the inconsistent explosive deck I was talking about earlier and average out the power, it might very well be lower than a deck engaging in fight the table ideology, but people aren't going to be thinking about averages; they're going to be thinking about the fact that you swung at them for 90 on turn five last game.
That's a lot scarier than a deck that consistently swings for 15.

Step Two: Be Open About the Threat

Step two of fight the table ideology is to be open about the threat that your deck poses to the table.
This doesn't mean you need to overhype yourself or give people constant updates on your draws, but be straightforward with people.
Verbally, this could mean saying, "Yeah, my deck generates a lot of artifact tokens, which I can use to accomplish various effects.
I don't have very many right now, so I'll need to get a lot more and have an outlet for them before I can do anything too scary." In terms of play, being open can just mean having a thing you're clearly doing.
Don't let your opponents imagine what you're doing with all of these fishy plays; have a goal and execute clear steps along the way.
One easy example for this is hardcore ramp decks, which work well because everybody knows they're scary at a lot of mana, but there's no singular land drop that signals the deck being a problem.
It evokes more of a gentle gradient of concern from your opponents rather than the black-and-white concern of a random spooky permanent, and this can lead to the deck flying under the radar.
This is doubly true if you're not running a singular win card like "Torment of Hailfire," but rather a win involving multiple cards used in a complex way, since decks can kind of just blend together in people's minds as, "Yeah, the deck does big things when it gets a lot of mana," which is conceptually less scary than a one-shot.
This sort of ramp deck is a great example of transparency being unexpectedly effective at avoiding attacks compared to more opaque play styles.

Can Any Deck Be Played This Way?

I've laid out the basic framework, but a natural question that might arise is, "Can my deck be played this way?" I do my best to play decks that are relatively consistent, so a lot of mine do end up following this mold, but even in my case, some decks will be trickier to do this with than others.
Decks with combos are generally more difficult to do this with, as they're naturally less transparent due to the level of importance they place on cards in hand, which is by definition a hidden zone.
For this reason and others, sometimes a deck isn't a great candidate for this kind of transparency treatment.
However, I think that a good exercise for every deck is thinking about what it would have to do to become a good candidate for this.
What happens if you remind your opponents of the strongest win con in your deck along with the conditions required to get there right at the start of every game?
What happens if your opponents treat that.

#37
DeckbuildingJul 2024247K views

Choosing the Right Ramp Spell for Your Commander Deck

You're Probably Playing the Wrong Ramp (in EDH)
We argue that many EDH players blindly include Cultivate in decks where it is suboptimal.We explain how to select ramp spells based on a deck's mana curve, color fixing needs, card type synergies, and net mana cost.Different commanders require different ramp strategies, and a tailored approach improves deck performance.
Use this in your deckAsk what mana value your deck is ramping toward, and prefer ramp spells that cost less than that target.
GreenRampAggroCard EvaluationCurveEDHREC DataDeck ConsistencyDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • Ramp choices should prioritize accelerating to the deck's pivotal mana value over providing redundant fixing.
  • The trade-off between speed, vulnerability, and synergy determines whether land ramp, dorks, or rocks are best for a given deck.
Evidence
  • 60% of Toski and Tovolar decks on EDHREC include Cultivate, suboptimally.
  • Toski's innate card draw makes Cultivate's land-to-hand fixing unnecessary, wasting the three-mana slot.
Deckbuilding Application
  • If your commander costs four mana, avoid three-mana ramp spells.
  • In monocolor green decks with high draw, use two-mana ramp or dorks instead of Cultivate.
Limitation
  • Cultivate shines in budget five-color decks where fixing and a five- or six-mana payoff are critical.
Example
  • A Glissa deck skips two-mana ramp entirely, using one-mana dorks to power out the three-mana commander on turn two.
More quotes
  1. EDH is especially vulnerable to cycles of overhype about particular cards.
  2. There will be cards that are regarded in the same lens [as must-plays] but which truly don't belong in all or even most decks.
  3. I think people tend to gravitate towards certain [ramp spells] that are considered staples even when there are other options that are much better suited to their deck.
  4. Treat this piece as a guide: how to stop jamming Cultivate into every deck and figure out what your deck actually wants instead.
  5. When you're adding a ramp spell to a deck you should be asking: what is this ramping toward?
  6. If the most important mana value you're pushing toward is four, you shouldn't run a bunch of three-mana ramp spells.
  7. You're playing a monocolor deck and if you play Cultivate you're effectively overpaying for ramp that gives you an upside you don't need.
  8. Different decks will want different things.
  9. The best ramp solution isn't going to be one size fits all.
  10. I think when it comes to the traditionally simpler parts of an EDH deck (the lands, the removal, the ramp), a lot of people have the idea that they can gloss over these things and focus their efforts on the juicy synergies that comprise the spiritual core of the deck, but these parts are what let the core of the deck do its thing smoothly.
Full transcript

The other day I was exploring options for a go-wide beatdown deck, so naturally I was on the EDHREC pages of Toski and Tovolar, and on those pages I found something that bothered me: on both pages, 60% of decks run Cultivate.
Now, obviously EDHREC has some inherent flaws that get further compounded through users of the website, it would be cool if somebody made a video about that or something, but still, 60% with a commander that’s low mana enough not to be boosted out by the ramp part and who draws enough cards not to need the tacked-on land to hand; you could spend that three mana on literally anything else.
There’s also some questionable draw run in these decks whose commander is an insanely powerful draw spell.
So what gives?

Format TheoryJul 2024230K views

The Homogeneity of cEDH Decks and Why It Happens

The Problem with cEDH
Competitive EDH decks overlap heavily due to the singleton 100-card constraint, the demand for maximum efficiency, and budgetless mana bases that push decks toward four or five colors.This homogenization limits the variety of viable strategies, though the format’s social contract reduces pregame friction and salt.
Use this in your deckEvaluate whether your deck's win conditions satisfy the cEDH requirements of speed, resilience, and compactness.
cEDHMeta TrendsDeck ConsistencyWin ConditionsFormat HealthMeta Analysis
Evidence
  • The top 10 cEDH decks share 20 cards: 9 lands, 5 mana rocks, 4 counter spells, and 2 draw enchantments.
  • Seven of the ten decks run the Thassa's Oracle plus Demonic Consultation combo, and six run Underworld Breach plus Brain Freeze plus Lion's Eye Diamond.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Make your win conditions fast, explosive, consistent, resilient, and compact.
Limitation
  • The findings are drawn from a static snapshot of the top 10 meta decks and may not capture off-meta brews or a shifting meta.
Example
  • Kinnan wins via infinite mana into playing the library, and Sisay wins via flicker-based combos tutored by the commander, demonstrating win conditions outside the dominant Thoracle and Breach lines.
  • Players use partner pairings like Vial Smasher and Thrasios mainly to add colors and get an extra card, not for the partners' own power.
More quotes
  1. It seemed like all of the cards were the same: the ways they gain mana are the same, the way they draw cards are the same, the ways they end the game are the same, and the ways they stop other people from ending the game are also the same.
  2. In cEDH, you're already playing the maximum allowed number of your seventh, eighth, and ninth favorite counter spells because you have.
  3. Ad nauseam is so powerful that it blurs the line between draw spell and one-card win condition.
  4. When you look at somebody's commander and the colors they're playing, you'll often be able to immediately guess one or more of their win conditions.
  5. CEDH is an essentially budgetless format which allows for very good mana bases at very little gameplay expense to the player, barring nonbasic hate.
  6. The partner Commander pairings that people go with are pretty mediocre. They let a player run a commander they actively like, add a couple colors they want, and have an extra playable card in a pinch.
  7. A lot of meta decks end up being four- to five-color piles of the best cards in the format.
  8. [In cEDH], the difference between a well-tuned deck and a jumbled goodstuff pile can come down to a small handful of cards that the deck then tutors for, rendering these differences subtle on the scale of a deck list.
  9. As you climb to the highest power levels of EDH, the range of possible decks you can build and plays you can make contracts further and further.
  10. My list does not run Thassa's Oracle, so I'm able to win through hate like Hushbringer or Torpor Orb that people commonly play against Oracle decks.

#39
StrategyJul 2024191K views

Effective Mulligan Strategies in Commander

The Art of the Mulligan
We explain how the London Mulligan and the free first mulligan in Commander allow players to mulligan more aggressively for key cards.Higher land counts improve the odds of keeping a hand for mana, freeing mulligans to search for impactful non-land spells.For decks seeking a particular early play, running 10, 12 copies and mulliganing assertively is often more efficient than packing 20 copies and risking duplicates.
Use this in your deckIf your deck needs a specific early-game effect like ramp, run 10, 12 copies of that effect and aggressively mulligan hands without it.
RampCard DrawDeck ConsistencyMana BaseCurveDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • The London Mulligan, especially with Commander's free first mulligan, turns mulligans into a valuable filtering tool that should be used actively.
  • A strong commander effectively gives you a guaranteed play, making it safer to mulligan low-resource hands.
Evidence
  • In a 28-land deck, mulliganing to six cards provides a 95% chance of finding a two-land hand and a two-thirds chance of three or more lands.
  • A 30-land deck keeps only 66% of hands by land count, whereas a 40-land deck keeps 76%, allowing the 40-land deck to save mulligans for non-land card quality.
Deckbuilding Application
  • To reliably find a specific early-game card type, include 10, 12 copies and mulligan aggressively, as this offers a better balance than including 20 copies and risking duplicates.
  • Adopt land counts that increase the probability of keepable hands, such as the low 40s, to preserve mulligans for filtering non-land cards.
Limitation
  • Building with an extremely low land count like 28 forces you to spend your mulligans almost entirely on mana, sacrificing the ability to hunt for powerful spells.
  • Running too few lands increases the chance that a hand with excellent non-land cards must be thrown away due to insufficient mana.
Example
  • A Goreclaw deck with a specific curve can run 10, 12 ramp spells and aggressively mulligan anything that lacks ramp, trusting the commander to apply pressure once mana is fixed.
More quotes
  1. If you aren't using that first Mulligan regularly every few games at least, you're actively giving up on a resource the game is handing you.
  2. Since you get to see a full hand of seven cards with every Mulligan, it's no longer the painful choice it used to be.
  3. A six or even five card hand with one of the deck's best cards will often be a lot better than your average seven card hand.
  4. The existence of a commander creature means that decks will have a pre-selected card that's effectively in hand at all times.
  5. It's literally possible for a hand to have access to no good cards.
  6. Developing your skill in something as superficially simple as saying yes or no to a given hand will generate you tons of advantage through additional free filtering in the long term.
  7. If everybody had to keep their first hand every time, I suspect that you'd see way more decks gravitating toward land counts in the low 40s.
  8. Running fewer lands will lead to a greater frequency of needing to reject hands with desirable non-land play.
  9. A single power card like Dockside Extortionist or Esper Sentinel might redeem an otherwise painful five card hand.
  10. It's easy to see your library as a mystical black box to deify it as an entity that will either bless you with wonderful cards or curse you with all the wrong stuff, but it's not.
Full transcript

When I started playing Magic, the mulligan seemed like a largely uncomplicated subject; if your hand is okay, you keep it, and if it sucks, you don't.
For the most part, this analysis reflected the actual state of the game.
See, for the first several years I played the game, the official mulligan rule was the Paris mulligan.
If you don't like your hand of seven, you can draw a new hand of six; if you don't like that hand, you can draw a new hand of five, and so on.
In some competitive constructed applications, you might have planned out some sort of mulligan policy for yourself, but the general idea, especially in limited, was “uh, try not to mull again because it sucks,” especially with regard to mana screw.
If your deck is 40% lands, a seven-card hand averages 2.8 lands; if you mulligan after drawing too few lands, the six-card replacement hand will average 2.4 lands.
God forbid you need to go to five, because that hand will average two lands and will have a greater probability of having one land than having three lands.
Yikes.
In addition, if there are a couple of pivotal cards in your deck, every mulligan you take means a turn later that you’ll see one of those cards, meaning that your midgame potential is compromised as well.

However, this all changed in 2019.
The introduction of the London mulligan meant that, since you get to see a full hand of seven cards with every mulligan, it’s no longer the painful choice it used to be.
This is all the more true in Commander, where you get one for free.
The filtering value in being able to pitch a hand and get that same number of cards again is legitimate and shouldn’t be underestimated; taking that first mulligan has no cost to you other than that it requires taking your first mulligan, so if you aren’t using that mulligan regularly every few games at least, you’re actively giving up on a resource the game is handing you.
In addition, going down to six cards or even to five cards isn’t that big a cost for a lot of decks.
It’s a bit painful in limited, because card effects and power levels are less diverse, so drawing a different set of lands, generic beaters, and non-creature spells often won’t feel worth the cost of losing one of them.
However, in Commander, cards vary wildly.
Despite my personal wishes for the format, just about every deck runs a piece of fast mana that will dramatically warp its early turns if drawn, and there are a number of other cards that will similarly have an outsized impact if drawn in the opening hand: other low-cost ramp spells like Birds of Paradise, low-cost draw spells like Mystic Remora or Skullclamp, and high-impact creatures such as Professional Face-Breaker or Academy Manufactor.
A six- or even five-card hand with one of the deck’s best cards will often be a lot better than your average seven-card hand.
In addition, as I mentioned in my lands video, the existence of a commander creature means that decks will have a pre-selected card that’s effectively in hand at all times.
This means that when you mulligan, you know that whatever hand you draw will have access to that creature; as long as you like your commander, it’s literally impossible for a hand to have access to no good cards.
So mulligans are a great tool, and developing your skill in something as superficially simple as saying yes or no to a given hand will generate you tons of advantage through additional free filtering in the long term.

Mulligans and Land Count

In addition, not only are there relevant decisions to be made during a given game, but the influence of mulligans on your decision-making process begins while you’re still building your deck.
We can see an indication of this immediately with the number of lands people choose to run in decks.
If everybody had to keep their first hand every time, I suspect that you’d see way more decks gravitating toward land counts in the low 40s, the range that maximizes the odds of a three-land hand.
But nah, if we draw too few lands, the cost of drawing a new hand is relatively low due to the previously mentioned factors, and the odds of drawing a workable hand within the first three drawn is pretty high.
There are also people who adhere to this principle much more strongly than just absent-mindedly tossing 30 lands in all their decks.
There’s a particular person in my comments section who advocates a land count of around 28 for most lower-curved decks, with the logic being that an aggressive mulligan policy can be employed to guarantee a high probability of drawing into enough lands to function.
Let’s consider that plan for a moment.
A given hand in a deck with 28 lands will have a 36% chance of having zero or one lands, a 33% chance of having two lands, and a 31% chance of having three or more lands.
If we’re willing to mulligan into six to see up to three hands of cards, this gives us a 95% chance of seeing a hand with at least two lands and a two-thirds chance of seeing a hand with at least three lands.
These aren’t the worst odds, especially when you consider that there might be low-cost draw, low-cost tutors, low-cost ramp, land cycling, and other tools that can make a two- or even one-land hand keepable.
However, this also calls back to the idea of mulligans as a resource: getting to see more hands of cards has filtering value, and constructing a deck in the 28-land framework means that filtering resource is basically spent exclusively looking for the right land count.
Now, this can be fine if you really trust your non-land cards to be valuable and trust your mana base to give you the symbols you need, but you’re still giving up a bit of ability to hunt for good nonland plays.
As a demonstration of this, we can think of a hand that has two lands and some desirable non-land cards; a decent hand I’d happily keep.
But what if we just made some changes to the deck and one of these lands got cut for a Ledger Shredder?
Suddenly this hand has a very high risk of missing at least one early land drop, and we might need a mulligan for a different hand, a hand that will likely have worse non-land play.
The point here is that running fewer lands will lead to a greater frequency of needing to reject hands with desirable non-land play.
If we’re looking for a hand with two to four lands, running a land count of 30 will give us that 66% of the time, whereas a land count of 40 will give us that 76% of the time.
Ten percent might sound like a small difference, but think about it this way: almost a third of the hands that the hypothetical 30-land deck needs to mulligan due to land count are keepable by the 40-land deck, and those hands might contain some really great cards.
This doesn’t mean that running more lands is objectively correct; there definitely are decks where having a lower chance to top-deck a land is especially desirable, but it does demonstrate some of the trade-offs involved with running different land counts and the way that higher land counts and better mana bases, for that matter, can increase the filtering value of the mulligan step.

Filtering for Non-Land Cards

Let’s say you decide to lean more in the direction of filtering for non-land cards.
What’s a good thing to do with this filtering?
Well, a lot of decks will have a particular thing they’re looking to find; for example, a piece of ramp is a frequent want for decks with a specific curve in mind, as is the case with the Goreclaw deck I mentioned in my lands video.
In cases like this, how many of a particular type of card a deck should run often comes down to whether the deck is okay with drawing multiple copies.
In my lands video, I showed a formula for finding the probability of drawing a card of a type, and while 20 cards of a type does give a comfortable 80% chance of drawing at least one in any given hand, the flip side is that there’s also a more than 40% chance of drawing two or more, and there is actually a lower chance of drawing exactly one copy than there is running a count of 15 or even 10.
Some decks won’t mind multiple copies of whatever they’re looking for, but for a deck looking for a diverse range of cards or a deck that is tight on space, running 10 to 12 copies and mulliganing aggressively for them might be a better plan than running 15 or 20.

Another consideration that’s especially relevant for higher powers is that decks can be built with the intent to mulligan in different ways based on the opposing decks and the turn order.
In certain matchups, it might be correct to mulligan aggressively for a hand that will make a bunch of bold plays early in a rush to gain dominance, whereas a different deck pool and turn order might make a grindier hand with quality drawn interaction a much more appealing one to stick with.

Three Factors Influencing Mulligan Willingness

At this point, I think I should mention I’ve been presenting mulligans as a filtering resource, which might imply that each deck has a set quantity of this resource that it should spend all of, and this is the easiest way to think of it, but it’s not exactly correct.
Willingness to mulligan will differ person to person and deck to deck, and how tightly a given player leverages their mulligans will generally depend on three factors.
One of these factors is how much they care about the quantity of cards they start the game with.
I mentioned that constructed EDH decks have more card variation than limited decks, and this effect scales with power level.
In cEDH, a single power card like Dockside Extortionist or Esper Sentinel might redeem an otherwise painful five-card hand, and a high density of specific stax pieces means that ditching a hate card against three commanders that aren’t generally all that affected by it isn’t a large cost at all.
Another factor that will influence mulligan willingness is a player’s risk tolerance, and this actually works in both directions.
A player who wants to sit down at the table and have a game where they’re guaranteed to do something might be willing to keep a slow hand rather than risking finding a worse one, and a different player could use the same reasoning to mulligan aggressively for a great hand because they don’t trust their topdecks.
On the flip side, we can imagine daredevils of multiple stripes boldly keeping or mulliganing and believing in their deck to deliver them greatness; ultimately, this factor is a matter of personal preference more than anything and will mainly come down to what different people find fun.
The final factor in determining mulligan willingness is how confident a player feels in their mulliganing skill.
Less experienced players and players who don’t know their deck as well will often be inclined to keep a mediocre but workable hand that a more experienced player would happily toss out to hunt for a better one.
This is a skill you can work on, and the best way to do this is to understand the ratios of different things in your deck and how likely each is to get drawn, either in an opening hand or in the first several draws of the game.

Building Mulligan Intuition

It’s useful to know that your opening hands will contain an average of 2.5 lands and 2.2 cards above three mana, and that they’ll have a 70% chance each to contain at least one piece of ramp and draw.
If this feels like a nebulous process in practice, you can also do lots of play testing: draw opening hands, play out the first few turns, rinse and repeat.
And actually doing a combination of these two, pre-play analysis and play testing, is the quickest way to build intuition as to how various deck building choices will turn into practical early game results.
It’s easy to see your library as a mystical black box, to deify it as an entity that will either bless you with wonderful cards or curse you with all the wrong stuff, but it’s not that.
The way the cards in your library turn into hands is predictable in a probabilistic sense, and getting a better feel for that will improve your ability to play your deck both in the mulligan phase and beyond.

#40
DeckbuildingJul 2024185K views

How to Make Your Commander Deck More Consistent

Snail's Guide to Tuning Your Jank
We define consistency as a deck that reliably executes its game plan without wild variations in power.We teach methods to improve consistency, such as solo playtesting, understanding probability thresholds for key card types, and categorizing cards into functions like ramp and draw.Proper categorization helps avoid filler cards and ensures each slot contributes to the deck's plan.
Use this in your deckInclude at least 10 cards of a given effect type, such as ramp, to have that effect appear in your opening hand more than half the time.
RampCard DrawDeck ConsistencyCard EvaluationTable PoliticsInteractionDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • A consistent deck offers multiple play avenues that all feel valuable, reducing variance in power level across games.
Evidence
  • With 10 ramp spells, the chance of having one by turn two is 63%; with 15 spells it rises to 79%, and with 20 to 88%.
  • Running 15 ramp spells gives a 69.6% chance in the opening seven, 90.8% after one mulligan, and 97.2% after two.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Categorize your deck into functions like ramp, draw, removal, and protection to ensure you have enough of each.
  • Require at least 10 cards of an effect to see it in your opening hand more than half the time.
  • Evaluate each card's actual role: for example, Gilded Lotus counts as ramp only if your plan is to cast threats costing 10 or more mana.
  • Prefer general purpose draw over conditional draw to avoid falling behind when behind.
Limitation
  • These probability estimates ignore mulligans, specific land counts, and tutor effects, so they provide rough baselines rather than exact odds.
Example
  • A Goreclaw deck aiming to cast its commander on turn three needs a piece of ramp by turn two; probability modeling shows the required ramp count to achieve this consistently.
More quotes
  1. A consistent deck is one that consistently does something and whose things it does don't vary all that much in power level.
  2. People will remember the most powerful moments of your deck. Your spooky pop off win that you can only pull off 10% of the time will be remembered alongside a more consistent deck's solid game plan that it pulls off 75% of the time, making your deck seem disproportionately scary.
  3. From a fun standpoint, I like to have a deck be doing something every game.
  4. [Wanting my deck to perform every game] is absolutely an ego thing, but fortunately we all have human brains that do silly human brain things.
  5. A simple benchmark you can reference is that you need 10 of a card in a deck to have it show up in a given hand more than half the time.
  6. I think a lot of people tend to underestimate how many cards 100 actually is, and that tends to result in people putting in fewer cards with a particular effect than would be ideal.
  7. I've been there, and let me tell you, those decks end up turning out way worse than decks where I'm sweating and dying trying to fit everything.
  8. If a card doesn't fit into one of your key categories, you should be asking yourself what its purpose is, and the answer should be simple and straightforward.
  9. In my mind, the optimal state of a deck list is one whose components exist in comfortable tension in your mind.
  10. If you're only running the Two Boots, your odds of drawing protection in your first five draws of the game are a hair under 23%. That's not great.
Full transcript
Introduction

The three decks I mentioned in my last video, my $25 Ratos deck, my Maria deck, and my Glissa deck, all basically do variations on a theme.
With Glissa, the deck might take different opening plays depending on the game, but at the end of the day the general game plan will be ramp, play big stuff, and gain board dominance.
Similarly, with my Maria deck, the deck is always looking to play a bunch of equipments and swing big in the mid-game, but that can take a bunch of different shapes.
If you want to learn more about the Maria deck, I recently posted a ten-minute deck tech over on my Patreon that covers the process of how I built it, a breakdown of the list, and some takeaways I had from the deck-building process.

Why Consistency Matters
Roll-Out Consistency

Let’s say I want to look at how often I get a particular type of card by a particular turn, using the example of a Goreclaw deck I’ve been building.
The deck always wants to play the commander by turn three, and this means that it needs to get a piece of ramp by turn two every game.
Disregarding mulligans, lands, and other factors, the probability of this happening is modeled by this function, where X is the number of ramp spells in my deck; it can be used to graph out how likely it is to achieve this goal based on that number.
As we can see on the graph, if I have 10 ramp spells in the deck there’s a 63% chance of having one on turn two; if there’s 15 in the deck I have a 79% chance; if there’s 20 I have an 88% chance, and so on.
Alright, now let’s say instead I really want to see ramp in the opening hand and we happily mulligan to get there.
If I decide to run 15 ramp spells, then for a given seven-card hand there’s a 69.6% chance of having at least one ramp spell, and this graph lays out the probability based on the number of hands drawn: for one hand drawn it’s 69.6%, for two hands it’s 90.8%, for three 97.2%, and so on.
A simple benchmark you can reference is that you need ten copies of a card in a deck to have it show up in a given hand more than half the time.

You could also do this sort of modeling for a combo deck.
For this hypothetical persist combo deck running no tutors and no partial pieces, in order to have a better than half chance of drawing one of each piece to have the combo you need to draw roughly half the deck, and there’s about a 1 in 400 chance of drawing it in the opening hand.
Another possible calculation would be modeling land drops given a certain land count.
By showing you this kind of modeling I’m certainly not trying to say that you should go out and get a degree in math before building a deck; I almost never run this sort of calculation while deck building.
What I am trying to say, though, is that it’s good to have a rough idea of the actual numbers of the situation.
I think a lot of people tend to underestimate how many cards 100 actually is, and that tends to result in people putting in fewer cards with a particular effect than would be ideal.
This also then has other effects on the card quality.
You might build out the core of a deck and then go, “Wow, I’ve got so many slots left, I could put all sorts of stuff in here.” I’ve been there, and let me tell you, those decks end up turning out way worse than decks where I’m sweating and dying trying to fit everything in.
When slots are plentiful, filler that weakens the deck as a whole will work its way in: cards where you go, “Oh, this seems decent in a couple niche situations,” or “Oh yeah, this card has slight synergies with a couple other elements of the deck.” These cards are probably there in place of other, better-suited cards.

Categorizing Your Deck

Once you’ve figured out your categories and considered what sorts of cards should make up a given category, the next question might be how much of each to run, and this will vary deck to deck.
Things like draw and ramp can be tested via playtesting, but reactive elements will be trickier to figure out.
For removal, I’d recommend starting between 8 and 14 and adjust from there, keeping in mind that slower decks and lower-velocity decks will usually want more removal than faster decks and higher-velocity decks will.
Protection is the most variable of all: I run zero in a couple scrappy decks that don’t give a heck about anything, and 12 in the Maria deck whose supporting categories are currently on screen, where my commander staying alive an extra turn might mean a dead opponent and a bunch of value.
How much protection is correct for your deck will depend on how much it cares about its commander and other key cards surviving, and if you care about that a lot I’d like to remind you that if you’re only running the two Boots, your odds of drawing protection in your first five draws of the game are a hair under 23%.
That’s not great.
A helpful side effect of categorizing is that it will naturally leave some cards in undeniably naked view.
If a card doesn’t fit into one of your key categories, you should be asking yourself what its purpose is, and the answer should be simple and straightforward.
If the answer involves scratching your head and vaguely gesturing at a couple other cards, that’s a card that deserves a more robust evaluation.
In my mind, the optimal state of a deck list is one whose components exist in comfortable tension in your mind, where there are constantly questions on the back of your mind like, “Hm, should I swap out a ramp spell and a finisher slot for two more draw spells?” The answer to that can be no, but asking that kind of question in the first place will improve your decks and make you better at deck building.

Situational Consistency and Toolbox Decks

For a couple examples of toolbox decks at a lower power, I can talk about my Shimmer Slide deck and my friend Mr. Hans’s Vrock deck.
In Shimmer Slide, the primary objective of the toolbox is grabbing Astral Slide, but there are a ton of other options embedded into this enchantment, artifact, and wizard-based tutoring framework: board wipes, recur spells, draw spells, life gain, and so forth.
This means that the deck can flow nicely once it gets into the mid-game, even though the interesting mana base can often lead to slow early games.
In the Vrock deck, equipments are the primary card type that the tutor base is oriented around, with the equipment base containing value, protection, defense, graveyard hate, and Sunforger.
With Sunforger, the deck can hit a ton of different cards, including a convoluted infinite combo, which results in a mutate deck that’s a little strange but very fun and flexible.

A Note on Tutors
Wrapping Up
Preview: Maria Deck Tech
#41
DeckbuildingJul 2024159K views

Running More Removal Without Diluting Your Deck

How to Play More Removal (and be happy about it)
We offer two methods to run more removal without feeling like it eats deck space: use multi-removal spells or removal engines to handle multiple threats with fewer cards, and select removal that synergizes with your deck's theme, such as modal or mechanic-tied cards.This approach avoids diluting deck focus and leads to more dynamic gameplay.
Use this in your deckReplace a single-target removal spell with a multi-removal spell or a removal engine to handle more threats per card while conserving deck slots.
ControlRemovalDeck ConsistencyInteractionSynergyDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • The common ‘vegetables’ analogy for removal makes players feel they are sacrificing fun synergy cards for mandatory interaction, which discourages running more removal.
  • Reframing removal to integrate with a deck's core strategy can make it feel supportive rather than dilutive.
Evidence
  • The most popular EDH removal spells are single‑target and low‑cost, spending an entire deck slot to answer one threat, which taxes deck space when multiple threats appear.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Multi‑removal spells like Hull Breach, Soul Shatter, or Windgrace's Judgment destroy several permanents at once, providing card advantage and saving slots.
  • Removal engines (e.g., Aura Shards, Royal Assassin, or a pinger with deathtouch) remove one threat per turn repeatedly without using more cards.
  • Consider alternatives to loading up on board wipes; they destroy your own creatures and opponents can quickly rebuild, so a multi‑removal spell can be more efficient.
  • Pick removal that aligns with deck synergies: Dromoka's Command in a counters deck, Triskelion Predator in an evasive deck, or Lethal Scheme in a go‑wide deck.
  • Modal cards and MDFCs like Valorous Stance, Silverback Elder, or Angel of the Ruins let a removal spell double as land or utility, so it never feels like a dead slot.
Limitation
  • Multi‑removal and engines typically cost more mana than cheap single‑target spells, making them less viable for the most aggressive, lean proactive decks.
Example
  • A control deck like Astral Slide runs only two board wipes but leverages engines to recycle ETB removal and graveyard recursion for sustained board suppression.
More quotes
