  1. I fall firmly in the camp of incessantly telling people to run more removal. I will happily be that annoying guy.
  2. In conventional EDH deck building, ways to interact with your opponents often end up in the category of vegetables.
  3. What if you could have all the benefits of running a lot of removal without feeling like it's gobbling up your deck space?
  4. Your opponents have been through this before; you're not catching some dewy Timmy off guard with the first Wrath of God he's ever seen in his life.
  5. Player removal is absolutely on the table [for a Voltron deck].
  6. I run the low cost staples because I don't need to care what's happening on board two turns from now.
  7. Removal exists to let me stay alive and prevent opponents from winning for some unknown duration of time.
  8. Make the slots you're already spending on removal do more for you.
  9. [Blending in removal] allows it to feel less space gobbling even as the number of removal cards in your deck stays high.
  10. Modal cards allow a deck to pack a lot of removal that can be used when needed but not feel like its deck slots are being actively gobbled up.
Full transcript

A lot of EDH discourse exists around the concept of removal, and specifically around the question of whether people should be running more of it.
In that great battle of ideas, I fall firmly in the camp of incessantly telling people to run more removal; I will happily be that annoying guy.
However, I also understand why this advice might not be enjoyable to receive for a lot of people.
In conventional EDH deck building, ways to interact with your opponents often ends up in the category of “vegetables”: it’s a type of card that’s not flashy or exciting but that you need to include regardless.
If you’re building a cool EDH deck around a particular idea, sloughing off deck space to spend on interaction might feel disappointing; you’re effectively diluting the quality of what’s happening on your side of the board in order to set back opponents.
If this line of thinking sounds like you, I understand that, and I’m not here to perform the role of the chiding parent telling you that you need to grit your teeth and choke down those vegetables.
There certainly are plenty of reasons to run more ways to interact with opponents.
The two big ones are that it leaves you a better rounded deck and it leads to more dynamic and interesting games, rather than luck-based one-sided stomps, but no, I don’t want to focus on that because on some level you probably already know that.
Rather, I want to offer an alternative vision to the vegetables analogy.
What if you could have all the benefits of running a lot of removal without feeling like it’s gobbling up your deck space?

Densifying Your Removal Suite

I’m going to talk about a couple methods for accomplishing this task, and the first method is to make the slots you’re already spending on removal do more for you.
When we look at the most popular removal spells in the EDH format, it’s mostly single-target removal, typically either low-cost options or all-purpose options that can hit any target.
To be clear, these things absolutely have their place, but they are an entire deck slot spent removing one card.
What if there are two concerning cards on board?
What if somebody plays another one the turn after you remove something?
Running enough single-target removal for all potential targets can really tax your deck space, and there are two alternatives that can be comparatively compact: multi-removal and removal engines.
Multi-removal is exactly what it sounds like; it’s a spell that removes multiple potential threats.
Board wipes are one example of this, but there are plenty of other options.
Some of these are pretty straightforward: Hull Breach can destroy both an artifact and an enchantment, and Soul Shatter forces each opponent to sacrifice a hopefully valuable creature or planeswalker.
However, as you get to higher mana costs, there are some spicier options too.
Windgrace’s Judgment can destroy each opponent’s most valuable nonland permanent, and both Decimate and Casualties of War are capable of blasting cards of several card types all at once.
A really cool thing about these is that you’ll typically destroy one or two things that are currently problems but also a couple things that might cause problems down the road.
A big mana rock, a utility land, or a low-level value enchantment won’t be priority targets for removal that can only target one thing, but being able to clear out multiple of these at the same time as a higher-value target can really pay off in the long term in terms of denied advantage.

Removal engines are another way to make your interaction suite more compact, and these are cards that can destroy things over time.
Aura Shards is one of the most powerful examples of this, but there are also creatures like Royal Assassin and Trygon Predator that can remove one permanent per turn.
Removal engines can also involve multiple cards, and you’ll find this most often in control decks.
In my Astral Slide deck, the singular control deck in my current lineup of decks, there are only two cards in the list that could reasonably be described as board wipes; however, the deck frequently engages in heavy degrees of board suppression through the usage of removal engines.
Astral Slide can recycle explosive ETBs, including that of one of the board wipes, and there are also a number of ways to get control pieces in and out of the graveyard.
The deck even has a Stifle engine; what kind of goofy deck has a Stifle engine in it?
Other examples of this might include a pinger paired with a deathtouch enabler, Containment Priest paired with a flicker engine, and Muldrotha paired with a Fleshbag Marauder effect.
In general, these are great for conserving deck space.
In a toolbox control deck that has networks of ways to find different cards, a small handful of removal engines can free up a ton of deck space to go toward counterspells, tech pieces, draw, and other categories that might otherwise have to be a bit leaner to fit an adequate amount of removal.

Now, I should say in the case of both multi-removal and removal engines, you’re generally going to pay more mana for the added benefits as compared to single-target counterparts of these cards, and for the leanest, most unapologetically proactive decks out there, that extra cost will likely be an unacceptable downside.
But not all decks fit that description.
We’ve seen low-cost all-purpose removal become the “staple” choice even though plenty of decks absolutely can afford to pay a little more mana in order to get more out of their removal.
Board wipes are the model of this that most people are familiar with, and these are nice but not perfect; they often destroy your stuff as well, and another downside with them goes back to the temporality of removal.
If you purely focus on blasting all the creatures off the board, you’ll be safe the turn the board wipe is cast, but a lot of decks can bounce back shockingly quickly from a board wipe or even mitigate it entirely.
Your opponents have been through this before; you’re not catching some dewy-eyed Timmy off guard with the first Wrath of God he’s ever seen in his life.
This isn’t to say that you need to avoid board wipes , they’re a great option for a lot of situations , but if you’re considering your fourth or fifth board wipe for a deck, maybe consider that there are other options out there for accomplishing a similar task.

Blending Removal into Your Deck’s Identity

Okay, so that was the method of densifying the part of your deck that you dedicate to removal.
The second method is to widen your removal suite but to make an effort to pick out removal that blends into the rest of what your deck is doing.
This allows it to feel less space-gobbling even as the number of removal cards in your deck stays high.
One way of doing this is picking out cards that align with your deck synergies, and this is typically easier the more general your deck synergies are.
One of the most general options is card type.
If you’re running a deck based around artifacts or around instants and sorceries, I can guarantee you that there are a ton of options for interaction that will slot in nicely with the synergy core of your deck.
This is similarly doable in decks that focus around core game mechanics; there are lots of great options for decks based around counters, tokens, and graveyards.
For a few examples of synergistic picks, you could run Drown in Ichor in a deck with a focus on counters, you could run Trygon Predator in a deck that is looking for evasive attackers, and you could run Lethal Scheme in a deck that gets a wide board of creatures and wouldn’t mind a bit of extra filtering.

An extension of this principle is modal cards.
Cards like Valorous Stance, Silverback Elder, and Gix’s Command are solid for both removal and non-removal purposes.
There are also plenty of cards that are effectively modal without explicitly invoking the word “choose”: cycling cards like Angel of the Ruins as well as MDFCs come to mind.
The important thing is that these cards allow a deck to pack a lot of removal that can be used when needed but not feel like its deck slots are being actively gobbled up.
This leads to more fluid gameplay since these cards can be lands or draw or ramp or protection in addition to being removal.
This is also why counterspells are good if you can afford to hold up without falling behind, but this is a video about removal spells, so I’ll leave it at that.

As an example of removal blending in, my friend Hans has a deck he likes to call “Is It Bite?” It’s a Lilah, Undefeated Slickshot deck that runs as many Prophetic Bolt-style cards as it can possibly get its hands on; we’re talking any red-blue instant or sorcery that both deals damage and draws cards in some capacity.
As a result of this, even though the deck is packed out to the wazoo with creature removal, since almost all of that removal draws cards and works with the commander as well, it feels perfectly fine.
Another example of this is my Mayael deck, and that deck actually takes advantage of both methods I’ve mentioned here, .
For the blending-in part, all of the removal in this deck is attached to either a big creature or a cascade spell.
This is great because these are things I want anyway.
Cascade spells will usually grab me pieces of ramp to pump up my land count, and big creatures are what I use to pressure life totals and eventually win.
This is compounded by the density of these: a full seven of these are one-card removal engines, and a single one of these sticking around for multiple turns on end can wildly warp the shape of a game.
As a result of these things, the deck is very much so eating its removal vegetables, but when I play it, it just feels like a big dumb cascade ramp deck even as it eats opposing permanents at a healthy pace.

Understanding Removal’s Role in Your Game Plan

I’ve given a couple of nice options for fitting more removal into a deck.
Densifying the existing removal suite through multi-removal or removal engines can let the same number of cards do more, and blending removal into the rest of what the deck is doing through synergistic or modal options can similarly offer more removal in practice without it feeling like it’s hogging too much deck space.
However, putting these options into practice requires understanding what removal is actually doing for your deck.
Even here, about removal, I’ve so far largely abstracted away what removal is actually accomplishing in a game, letting it sit simply assumed that it’s a good thing to have.
So what is removal doing for your decks?
This is a question with a clear, specific answer.
In my Francisco-Malcolm deck, a deck that fits the lean and unapologetically proactive descriptor I mentioned earlier, removal principally exists to kill creatures that threaten my turn five to six win or that might allow opponents to win faster than that.
If I’m playing the deck and my opponents play out some scary engine creature on turn four to five, I don’t generally need to remove these as long as my combo is on track to end the game before those creatures have a chance to cause problems.
For this reason, all of the deck’s dedicated removal is two mana or less to allow it to be weaved into a turn comfortably; I run the low-cost staples because I don’t need to care what’s happening on board two turns from now.
Similarly, I run a lot of mana-efficient single-target removal in my Marisi deck as well because it’s a voltron deck.
Player removal is absolutely on the table, so my removal is there to deal with obstacles to my beatdown rather than controlling the board.

By contrast, I run removal engines in my slower, grindier decks because the time frame for winning is a lot less concrete.
I’ll usually have some tools to put pressure on opponents relatively early, but the plan to win isn’t focused enough to be relied upon as any sort of clock to calibrate the rest of my game plan against.
Thus, in these sorts of decks, my removal exists to let me stay alive and prevent opponents from winning for some unknown duration of time, and as a result I need to prepare for a wider range of situations and run removal that works better in the longer term.
There will also be the question of instant-speed removal versus sorcery-speed removal, and this will come down to power level above all else.
If you’re playing at higher powers where sudden infinite combo attempts might happen, and especially if you’re not playing blue to allow for counterspells, instant-speed removal will likely be kind of a must.
Likewise, if you’re holding up removal at instant speed just in case, you also want that removal to cost as little as possible in order to avoid wasted mana if the removal is unneeded.
Staples are staples for a reason, and this is basically the reason; you might even want to include a handful of these in a slower midrange deck just for the sake of contingencies.
They’re good cards, and if you failed to slow down a player earlier in their game plan, having that insurance policy to prevent a blowout can be important.
However, if you’ve already got your first three or four single-target staples, maybe try branching out a bit from there and consider alternatives that work better for your deck in particular.
Your deck will likely thank you.

#42
DeckbuildingJul 2024158K views

Interaction and Consistency Outweigh Power Level in EDH

Does Power Level Matter?
We argue that blowout games are usually caused by a lack of interaction and inconsistent deckbuilding rather than power level differences.Players should include enough removal and counterspells to stop opponents from snowballing and avoid overloading on payoff cards that only work in ideal scenarios.Decks built with consistency and interaction can compete against higher-power opponents.
Use this in your deckReplace three payoff cards that only work when you are already ahead with three generic removal spells or counter spells to improve your deck's consistency and interactive ability.
BudgetDeck ConsistencyInteractionPower LevelDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • Power level mismatches are often not the root cause of blowouts; what matters is whether decks run enough interaction to check a player who gets ahead.
  • A multiplayer dynamic allows multiple opponents to trade cards one-for-one with a leading player, preventing snowballing.
Evidence
  • A pool of $30 decks built for consistency and interaction remained competitive against decks with higher power levels on paper.
  • A Zakara player who overloaded on counter doublers and mana doublers but ran only a single threat was shut down by one removal spell repeatedly.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Run at least 10 to 12 pieces of interaction (removal, counters, or modal spells) rather than the typical mid single digits.
  • Cut amplification cards that double resources only when you are already ahead, as they create fragility.
  • Cut conversion cards that require having one resource to generate another, as they rely on you already performing well.
Example
  • The Dimir Rogues list exemplifies the approach: it runs 17 interactive spells and 25 evasive creatures, allowing it to generate value even with a single creature.
More quotes
  1. Do you want to know a secret? In most cases, the relative power levels of the decks aren't even the main issue.
  2. Decks with varied power levels can work fine in the same pod and avoid blowouts, but this requires decks to run interaction.
  3. A key table dynamic that keeps powerful decks in check is the ability for multiple players to trade cards one for one with the player who is ahead.
  4. A lot of decks throw in a handful of removal spells and call it good, spending the rest on engines and synergies, building their deck's ability to function against no opponents.
  5. If a deck is even a little better at what it does, generating an extra half a card per turn in value, there will be nothing to prevent that advantage from piling up.
  6. A lot of casual EDH decks put so much emphasis on the ideal pop-off scenario that 25 to 40 percent of the time they fail to perform meaningful game actions.
  7. Putting in too many payoff cards and not enough building blocks to get there.
  8. Decks can feel poorly matched and power imbalanced even when they're the exact same power level.
  9. The funny thing is, despite being built on a low budget, these decks still hold up against decks that are much more powerful on paper.
  10. A table with relatively consistent interactive decks will feel much more dynamic and balanced regardless of how powerful each of those decks actually.
Full transcript

You've probably had a game like this: somebody starts doing some nutty stuff surprisingly early in the game, really going ham with their pieces of cardboard, and by the time they've achieved a decisive win a handful of turns later, one or more other players are sitting dejectedly and grumbling to themselves that this matchup was unfair.
Power level differences within a table can be a tricky thing to navigate; it's not fun if a game ends up being an absolute blowout with some players feeling like they didn't even get a chance.
I generally encourage trying to coordinate power levels to some degree at your tables, but do you want to know a secret?
In most cases like this, the relative power levels of the decks aren't even the main issue.

Decks Should Run Interaction
The High Variance Trap

I'd categorize payoff cards into two types.
One is "amplification cards": cards that convert your tokens into more tokens, your counters into more counters, your damage into more damage, and your draw into more draw.
Some number of these cards can be decent for a deck, especially cards that can rapidly lead to a win or which synergize with a type of card you'll have especially ample access to; however, if you run too many of these and too few things to be amplified, your game plan will be fragile.
A while back I had a series of games with a Zaxara player who would play multiple counter doublers and mana doublers and various other doublers, followed by a single premium Hydra (maybe a second one if they got lucky).
If that Hydra had survived a turn cycle, they probably would have smacked down the table in short order; but in all the games this happened, a single removal spell was played.
The Zaxara player had no countermagic, and just like that, they and their pile of synergy permanents were rendered a total non-threat.
I don't want to be too hard on this player since it seemed like their deck was pretty newly built at the time and hadn't been tested much, but this is an example of a trap that's pretty easy to fall into if you're not careful and are just adding stuff that looks good without thinking about the implications of those additions.

The other main type of payoff card is what I'll call "conversion cards": ones that give you one type of resource as a result of having or spending a different one.
For example, spending mana to draw cards when you've gained life, drawing cards based on having a bunch of creatures of the same type, creating wolves based on how many cards are in your graveyard, and creating Angels when you play enchantments.
As with amplification cards, these aren't inherently bad and are indeed a perfectly solid type of card in moderation; but running too many of these resource conversion cards will have problematic results for deck consistency.
If your deck's core building blocks already require you to be doing well in some capacity, you're limiting your ability to get your game plan going in the first place.
Pair this with running an excessive quantity of amplification cards as well, and you'll find that your deck will struggle to get into gear consistently, will struggle to play through interaction, and will struggle to get up off the floor after it's been shut down.
Sure, you'll have the crazy pop-off games from time to time where things line up just right, but the overall result of a high-variance deck like this is this sort of all-or-nothing dynamic that I find very negative.
If the whole table is playing this sort of deck, differences in deck power levels will naturally be amplified wildly when one player gets the lucky pop-off whereas the others get stuck with dead hands full of unmet payoff cards; and especially when nobody has interaction to close this sort of gap in performance, decks can feel poorly matched and power imbalanced even when they're the exact same power level.

Lessons from a $30 Budget Meta

The funny thing is, despite these decks being built on a low budget and designed to play exclusively against each other, most of these decks still hold up in games with decks that are much more powerful on paper.
A big piece of this is because of their relationship with interaction.
Take a Dimir Rogues list I built: it runs six counterspells, six removal spells, two modal cards that can counter or remove, and three stax cards.
In addition, the proactive cards in the deck don't rely on it being ahead or avoiding opposing interaction; even something like Bident of Thassa generates acceptable amounts of value with a single evasive creature, of which the deck is running 25.
These factors work together to make a deck that is very flexible and which can perform actions like hindering its opponents, chipping in damage, and drawing extra cards even when it's behind.
Not only this, but the high water mark of the deck is genuinely pretty high, with 30-plus damage combat steps, massive draw, extra turns, and forcing opponents to discard their hands all options on the table for the deck; and these plays are important for allowing the deck to get to the finish line once it's ahead.

A Rakdos discard deck from the same pool is similarly heavy on interaction: it's running 10 cards plus the commander for removal, as well as 25 cards that cause opponents to discard cards.
The deck's cards also work well on their own, as a matter of necessity.
When a deck's game plan involves forcing the table into a hellbent state, it's not going to play a bunch of stuff that requires lots of setup, because that's the exact sort of deck it's looking to disorient and hinder through discard spells.
Both of these decks are consistent, grindy, and interaction-dense, leading them to fit in quite well at a variety of different tables and always feel like they're contributing something to the game, even when other decks have significantly stronger cards.

When I reflect on games with these old decks, there's something almost paradoxical at play.
These highly optimized decks, decks that are overwhelmingly dedicated to nuts-and-bolts functionality over all else, led to gameplay that was way more fun to me than a lot of games I've played with more conventional EDH decks that have wacky card choices and perform lots of big, splashy plays.
Playing decks that are packed with interaction and also resilient to setbacks leads to fewer feel-bad "I'm doing nothing" type moments and way more moments where it feels like you're actually playing a multiplayer game where different players are trying to navigate their way to a win while also keeping their opponents in check.
This is certainly not to say that playing with a pool of hyper-optimized $30 decks is the only way to achieve this; there are plenty of things that people find fun about EDH that won't be found in an environment like this, and there's a reason my playgroup doesn't play exclusively with these decks anymore.
However, I think it's worthwhile to consider these sorts of decks as an example of how EDH can be played.

Reducing Swing Through Interaction and Consistency
#43
DeckbuildingJul 2024145K views

Why Board Wipes Polarize Commander Games

The Problem with Board Wipes (in EDH)
Board wipes disproportionately crush casual midrange creature decks and fail against combo, aggro, and non-creature strategies.Overloading on board wipes leads to polarized games where the deck dominates or flails.Deckbuilders should assess their interaction's weaknesses and make them intentional.
Use this in your deckAssess which strategies your interaction package is weak against and decide if those gaps are intentional.
Child of AlaraWhiteBlueBlackRedGreenControlMidrangeComboInteractionDeckbuilding RestrictionsPower LevelFormat HealthDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • Board wipes and stax effects are both asymmetric but in opposite directions: board wipes punish creature-based midrange while stax punishes carefully planned burst strategies.
Evidence
  • The child of Alara deck included only two counter spells, leaving it unable to interact with sudden combo or instant-speed wins.
  • The Glissa deck, with many board wipes and sorcery-speed removal, was effective against midrange but helpless against combo and countermagic.
Deckbuilding Application
  • When building a deck, identify what your interaction suite is weak against and ensure any holes are intentional.
  • Run three to five board wipes with tutors instead of eight to avoid over-committing to a polarizing strategy.
Limitation
  • Board wipes fail to stop combo wins, aggro life-total pressure, and instant and sorcery value engines.
  • A board-wipe-heavy control deck cannot outrun a combo deck in the long game.
Example
  • The child of Alara gate deck ran 55 lands, sacrifice outlets, and only five interaction slots, creating highly polarized games.
  • The Marriott equipment aggro deck intentionally lacks big creature removal, relying on early pressure and politics to compensate.
More quotes
  1. I am a cruel control player at heart.
  2. Board wipes won't stop combos, they won't prevent aggro decks from abusing your life total, they won't slow down large land counts, and they won't shut down your opponents generating a lot of instant and sorcery based value.
  3. When I tell you I want a commander that just repetitively board wipes to be a good idea, I mean it. But it's not a good idea.
  4. This deck will ruin the day of casual synergy decks but against a less board dependent deck it'll putter around and be a mild nuisance before getting ultimately crushed with little to no chance of actually pulling off one of its wincons.
  5. Board wipes are an asymmetric effect: they impact different players massively differently.
  6. Years spent as a mono black player forced me into the hard truth that unconditional tutors are bland as hell so I don't play them.
  7. Ideally it should be well balanced and if there are holes they should be intentional rather than incidental.
  8. You can build a deck with a bunch of stax effects and a win plan that can outrun a mid-range deck but you can't build a deck full of board wipes with a win plan that can outrun a combo deck.
  9. If you build a deck that massacres casual mid-range decks but flails around against everything else you're going to be struggling to create interesting and dynamic games.
  10. There's a reason why running a bunch of stax effects is a better idea than running a bunch of board wipes: the source decks shut down by an Archon of Emeria are generally stronger and more carefully constructed than the source of decks shut down by a Plague Wind.
Full transcript

A few months ago I had a brilliant idea: I’d make a tribal gate stack with Child of Alara as the commander.
I’d run 55 lands along with a bunch of drawn ramp, with Maze’s End, Crackling Perimeter, and a handful of beatdown options being my wincons.
A plethora of ways to sacrifice Child of Alara means that I should have board control mostly handled, so I only need a mere five slots of the deck dedicated to interaction of all types, how efficient.
Finally, a few options for big life gain means that once my land count is up where I want it, I can heal up the damage it took me to get there.
It’s not a high caliber deck, but it certainly scratched my goofy deck itch, and it seemed pretty solid for what it was.
Unfortunately, it was a terrible idea, chiefly because of the commander.

Now allow me to lay my credentials on the table.
I am a cruel control player at heart.
My first commander deck was mono black “ruin your day” with Children as the commander; my first deck I played with at sanctioned events was a standard Grixis control deck I built just after the release of Born of the Gods; and my favorite draft I ever played in was the Dragons of Tarkir draft where I picked up five Flattens and seven other removal spells and spent my night gleefully watching the joy drained from my opponents’ eyes as creature after creature bit the dust immediately after it was played.
So when I tell you I want a commander that just repetitively board wipes to be a good idea, I mean it.
But it’s not a good idea.

The Asymmetric Impact of Board Wipes

In the context of Commander, “board wipes” are a type of effect that I would call “asymmetric.” This is confusing on the surface because they’re generally literally symmetrical effects, you know, they destroy everything, but what I mean here is that they impact different players massively differently.
Somebody playing a low- to mid-power synergistic mid-range deck will feel them very brutally, but there are a lot of types of decks that just won’t really care as much.
Board wipes won’t stop combos; they won’t prevent aggro decks from abusing your life total; they won’t slow down large land counts; and they won’t shut down your opponents generating a lot of instant- and sorcery-based value.
The best option my Child of Alara deck has for dealing with somebody suddenly winning is using one of the two counterspells within the deck or preparing to instant speed sacrifice Child of Alara and hoping I don’t encounter interaction of any kind.

Okay, so the Child of Alara, dependent game plan has some holes.
Big whoop, I’m playing a gate stack, man, no, my deck has some weaknesses.
However, this is a bigger problem than it seems.
Going back to the idea of the card being “asymmetric,” we can think about how it is experienced by the decks that are more vulnerable to it, and the answer is it’s hell.
It sucks so hard for these decks; they can barely even start to build a board before they’re staring down the barrel of another Child of Alara sacrifice.
Even if they deal with one of my wipes, as long as my ramp game worked out as intended there will be another one immediately following it.
This is similar to the graveyard decks analogy from my last video: this deck will ruin the day of casual synergy decks, but against a less board-dependent deck it’ll putter around and be a mild nuisance before getting ultimately crushed with little to no chance of actually pulling off one of its wincons.

Now, Child of Alara is the most extreme example of board wipes creating highly asymmetric game experiences, but this is an issue with board wipes as a whole.
I talked a bit about my glitter deck in my mid-range video, and that deck operates as a kind of homage to the mono black Sheoldred deck I played when I was 15: get card advantage on a ton of mana and proceed to throw bombs and one-sided board wipes at the table until my opponents fold.
Now, that deck is much more well-rounded than the theoretical Child of Alara deck; it has a lot of single target removal to deal with key threats and a variety of wincons which can all eliminate a threatening player much faster than Maze’s End or Crackling Perimeter.
Nonetheless, the profile of how it fares against different decks looks very similar.
I try not to pull out the deck against the low- to mid-power mid-range decks that make up a decent portion of the decks in my play group because it generally stomps on these decks in theatrical fashion.
However, when my opponents have pressure to prevent me from fully greeding out or counterspells for my massive mana investments or combo wins to catch me and my mostly sorcery speed removal suite off guard, it’s much worse; against these sorts of plays my deck ends up looking like one of the clunky mid-range decks that I usually crush.

Board Wipes Aren’t Inherently Bad, But Require Mindfulness

Before going further, I should take the opportunity to state that board wipes aren’t bad overall.
A lot of decks are heavily board based, and it’s good to have a solid way to deal with that.
A better version of my Glissa deck would probably be running three to five board wipes instead of eight, with tutors to bridge the gap, but years spent as a mono black player forced me into the hard truth that unconditional tutors are bland as hell, so I don’t play them.
Ultimately, the deck is what I want it to be, and I’ll happily let it serve as a teaching point as happily as I’ll play it.
The real thing I’m trying to convey here is the need to be mindful when constructing your deck; the “interaction package” of your deck and what things it is particularly good or bad against is something you should think about.
Ideally, it should be well balanced, and if there are holes they should be intentional rather than incidental.

As an example, my Marath equipment stack has very little hard removal, burn, and non-creature permanent destruction, yes, but not much for big creatures.
This is mostly a matter of the fact that I’m playing an aggro deck and I’m looking to get a substantial board presence early and be threatening life totals.
The next removal serves to slow down my opponents and deal with pesky cards like Dragon Predator, but ultimately the next solution to later threats is to simply deal with the player or politic around the threat for a while.
My deck’s removal suite has a hole, but it’s a calculated one.
Another common hole in an interaction suite would be fast blue decks that choose to almost entirely run counters that don’t target creatures, a choice motivated by the fact that most forms of interaction from their opponents are non-creature, and in essence Scatter isn’t going to help protect a Turn 4 combo win.

A Final Note on Stax and Asymmetric Effects

As a final note, let’s return to the idea of asymmetric effects.
A lot of “stax” effects and hate cards also fall under this label, but stax effects generally operate the opposite way as board wipes, choking out bursty strategies that carefully plan out their resources to execute a specific game plan while having less of an effect on a deck whose game plan consists of “play Treefolk” or something.
There’s a reason why running a bunch of stax effects is a better idea than running a bunch of board wipes: the sorts of decks shut down by an Archon of America are generally stronger and more carefully constructed than the sorts of decks shut down by a Plague Wind.
You can build a deck with a bunch of stax effects and a win plan that can outrun a mid-range deck, but you can’t build a deck full of board wipes with a win plan that can outrun a combo deck.
And if you build a deck that massacres casual mid-range decks but flails around against everything else, you’re going to be struggling to create interesting and dynamic games.

#44
DeckbuildingJul 2024142K views

How Combo Pieces Make Your Commander a Removal Target

Stop Turning your Commander into a Combo Piece
We argue that inserting a powerful combo involving your commander into a deck with a weaker primary strategy causes opponents to treat your commander as a constant threat, leading to excessive removal that derails your gameplan.This misalignment of power levels creates strategic confusion and an unfun experience for both you and your opponents.We advise such combos should only be included if they match the deck's overall power level or are the main strategy.
Use this in your deckCut any compact combo piece from your commander deck unless your primary gameplan is to execute that combo.
WhiteBlueBlackGreenComboMidrangeThreat AssessmentEDHREC DataDesign IntentDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • Decks that mix weak and powerful strategies without consistency cause strategic whiplash for opponents, making threat assessment erratic.
  • A commander known for a combo will be treated as a kill-on-sight threat even if the player built a different, weaker theme.
  • Including an out-of-place combo in a synergistic deck draws disproportionate removal to the commander, undermining the intended play pattern.
Evidence
  • EDHrec data shows 46% of Zaxara decks include Freed from the Real, which combos with the commander for infinite mana.
  • 72% of Tivit decks run Time Sieve, and even among voting-themed builds, 63% include this one-card combo.
  • In a Zaxara deck without the combo, the commander is a moderate value engine; with the combo threat, it becomes a high-priority removal target.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Only include a commander-based combo if it is the deck's primary gameplan, ensuring consistent power throughout.
  • If combo pieces naturally align with the deck's strategy and the combo's power is comparable, the risk is reduced.
  • Evaluate how opponents will react to your commander based on popular combos, and cut pieces that provoke unwanted removal.
Limitation
  • This advice does not apply to dedicated combo decks where the commander is intentionally part of the win condition.
More quotes
  1. If you build a deck that's mainly doing one thing but has some other much more powerful thing it can draw into 10 to 20% of the time, that's going to lead to some table whiplash.
  2. When a deck does a mixture of relatively weak things and wildly powerful things with no rhyme or reason, it's much more difficult for people to conceptualize and strategize around.
  3. The second you throw some poison counters on your opponents, [your commander] immediately reads: each turn, I get a poison counter for all your opponents, and their removal choices will reflect.
  4. Poison counter proliferation is about as visible and visceral as setup gets. Your opponents will be kicking and screaming against the lurching inevitability of your game plan, even when it's no stronger on paper than a good old-fashioned tokens plus overrun.
  5. I'm not trying to give any hard and fast rules here. I'm just trying to make sure that players have their expectations well calibrated.
  6. If you're prepared to fight ruthlessly to keep your commander in play, that's great. On the contrary, if you're just here for a good time and don't want to fight that battle, don't build your deck in a way that gives other people extra reasons to kill your stuff.
  7. What you do with your commander will inform how your opponents react to that commander.
  8. Stretching your commander to fill more roles can be a great way to up your overall efficiency and consistency, but you do need to keep an eye on what exactly those roles are and whether they pose dramatically different threats to your opponents.
  9. At the end of the day, the main thing that all of this really comes down to is knowing what kind of magic you want to be playing.
  10. I know exactly how my opponents are going to be feeling, and I'm prepared to say no if they send their removal spells in the most logical direction.
Full transcript

A lot of the discussion around Commander deck building often revolves around the commander, and this makes sense; it’s right there in the name of the format.
Specifically, how to best utilize your command zone is a big area of discussion, and it’s certainly one that I’ve dabbled in plenty.
It’s good when you can get more out of a card in your deck that you’ll always have access to.
However, there’s a specific deck-building choice I’ve noticed that results from this goal, and it’s one that kind of bothers me: that choice is when a player takes their lovely “synergy engine” commander and turns it into a “combo piece.”

What I’m Not Talking About
The Combo Tacked On
The Vibes Issue
How Your Commander Informs Opponents’ Reactions

As another example, let’s talk about Tivit, Seller of Secrets.
Now, Tivit is a bit different from Zaxara in that its combo plan is often the primary plan.
While I’d estimate the number of real Freed-from-the-Real Zaxara players who would consider their deck a genuine combo deck is pretty low, Tivit’s one-card infinite combo with Time Sieve is the first thing a lot of people think of when they think about the commander; indeed, we can see 72% of players are running this combo.
However, if we dig a bit deeper, we can also see that about 45% of players are playing a Tivit deck that is based around voting.
Sorting by inclusion of Brokers Representative, we can see that people building this sort of deck are running lots of voting-oriented cards, as well as some flicker and artifact synergies; it seems like a pretty reasonable midrange deck.
Let’s say that it’s turn five or six and this sort of Tivit player is casting the commander, and let’s also say I’m holding up a counterspell.
Well, Tivit is certainly a bit concerning: the card is a strong engine, and him sticking around to get multiple triggers could lead to the Tivit player pulling ahead.
However, there are plenty of cases where I’d be fine with Tivit resolving, especially if there are more opposing turns before mine.
What if another opponent is pulling ahead and I want a chance at nailing a critical spell of theirs?
What if I have a big board I want to protect from potential board wipes?
What if I’m holding up a board wipe and have the ability to clear out the Tivit later with counterspell backup anyway?
In those cases, I might just let the player have their Tivit and get a bit of mana and card advantage.
But wait: I forgot to mention, 63% of voting-oriented Tivit decks run Time Sieve.
Statistically speaking, no shot that’s resolving; get your one-card infinite combo deck out of here, nerd.
Once again, this Tivit player could just be looking to do fun voting things and have a grand experiment in mildly rigged democracy, but unfortunately for them, they decided that grand experiment in democracy also needed a one-card infinite combo, so no, I think I’ll pass on that one, thank you very much.

Poison Counters and Inevitability
Knowing What You Want to Play
#45
Meta CommentaryJul 2024119K views

Gavi, Nest Warden Erased Cycling's Strategic Depth

How WotC Made Cycling Bland in EDH
We critique Wizards of the Coast's design of Gavi, Nest Warden, arguing that her free cycling ability removes the strategic resource management that makes cycling decks interesting.It contrasts her with Astral Slide, which fostered complex decision-making, and laments that Gavi encourages a linear, value-oriented playstyle common among 'designer commanders'.
Use this in your deckBuild your cycling deck with Astral Slide as the primary engine and avoid commanders that automate cycling benefits to maintain the mechanic's inherent strategic tension.
Gavi, Nest WardenWhiteBlueRedMidrangeTokensCard DrawDesign IntentFormat Health
Principle
  • Cycling forces players to weigh the immediate utility of a card against the potential of drawing a different card plus a mana cost, creating constant strategic tension.
  • The value of a cycling card changes dramatically with game state, making each cycle a meaningful decision with high-impact consequences.
Evidence
  • Astral Slide cycling decks require players to manage whether to use slide triggers aggressively for small value or conserve them for critical disruption.
  • In those decks, mana management becomes a probabilistic exercise where players calculate the odds of drawing additional cycles or answers based on available resources.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Avoid commanders like Gavi that give free cycling, as they eliminate the crucial trade-off between casting a spell and cycling it for a new card.
  • Select cycling cards with varied cycling costs, ensuring that the decision to cycle carries a significant mana commitment, to replicate the strategic depth of older cycling builds.
Limitation
  • Gavi's free cycle each turn encourages a repetitive pattern of exactly one cycle per turn, squandering the mechanic's potential for explosive or reactive sequences.
  • Her design leads to decks that default to passive value engines like Astral Drift, resulting in a formulaic midrange playstyle that lacks the nuance of earlier cycling decks.
Example
  • Decree of Annihilation cycled with Gavi exemplifies the failure: it resets mana but the Gavi deck lacks a quick win condition, prolonging games without strategic payoff.
  • Decree of Silence as a free counter with Gavi reduces meaningful interaction to a thoughtless safety net, stripping away the tension of resource planning.
More quotes
  1. Cycling is a beautifully simple mechanic: you can pay an alternate cost on a card and discard it to draw a card.
  2. Astral Slide decks have a super interesting play style that revolves heavily around strategic resource management, in a way that feels very in line with the fundamental nature of the mechanic.
  3. Free cycles on each turn removes an entire dimension of the cycling mechanic.
  4. Gaby destroys most of the cool strategic decision making generally involved with playing a cycling deck and replaces it with yet another do the thing value pile mid range deck.
  5. [With Gavi,] this cool mechanic with a rich history and tons of synergy potential is reduced down to yet another value pile Commander.
  6. I hate it because it's a total betrayal of one of my favorite mechanics.
  7. Wizards has a habit of printing designer commanders that take a particular mechanic and fully dedicate themselves to it, dictating a very specific and often very boring type of deck.
  8. A popular format for this sort of Commander is one with two lines of text: one that says whenever X happens Y happens, and another that says whenever enough Ys happen a Z happens. This is a template that builds its own deck.
  9. Gaby into decree of annihilation, while certainly unusual, still kind of fails to be interesting.
  10. My deck ended up with a foundation that includes the commander but doesn't wholly consist of the commander.
Full transcript

This is a video about a mechanic I love, a commander card I hate, and a bizarre deck I built out of spite.
Our tale begins in 1998, with Urza’s Saga bringing all sorts of horrendous to the world and also a gift: the cycling mechanic.
It’s a beautifully simple mechanic; you can pay an alternate cost on a card and discard it in order to draw a card.
This began in simple fashion during the Urza block, but five years later Onslaught block added a ton of dimension to the mechanic: varied cycling costs, triggers attached to cycling abilities, cycling for basic land types, and six cards with triggered abilities when other cards were cycled.
These six were of varying power levels, with one in particular being just excellent, but we’ll talk about that a bit later.
Cycling has seen additional prints every few years since, and for a simple reason: people like this mechanic.
As an alternate cost ability, it adds a lot of flexibility to cards, which leads to interesting decision-making.
Every cycling card a player draws prompts the question, “Is this card better than a different card plus a small mana commitment?” This is nice in aggro when running out of gas might be a concern, and it’s even more useful in a control deck, where that mana cost can be spent on the end of an opponent’s turn or even in response to things to dig for answers.
On top of this, since cycled cards are discarded and go to the graveyard, they work well with graveyard synergies, providing a near effortless way to put cards in the yard; and that’s only the play styles helped out by the mechanic innately.

The Strategic Depth of Astral Slide

Remember that cool Onslaught card I mentioned earlier?
Astral Slide has seen a ton of experimentation ever since it was printed, as it synergizes with many different cards and offers a ton of flexibility.
As a result, it was kind of understood as the core of any cycling deck before 2020.
Astral Slide decks have a super interesting play style that revolves heavily around strategic resource management, and in a way that feels very in line with the fundamental nature of the mechanic.
The number of cycling cards in hand constantly dictates the strategic direction of the deck: when you have more, you can get bolder and set up for more daring turns, as well as using Slide triggers more liberally as blocker elimination, to net small value off ETBs and so forth, or casting a card normally.
By contrast, when you have fewer cycling cards in hand, each one is pure gold; you always want to save them for protecting a critical creature or disrupting a dangerous play from an opponent, but if you feel the game slipping away, you can also choose to gamble that lifeline on an end step for a little bit of value.
In addition, mana is a super important resource for both Astral Slide and the mechanic as a whole, as land counts dictate how safe you feel playing setup pieces and how bursty your turns can be.
A more controlling cycling deck constantly forces a player to think probabilistically: “If I spend mana to play a spell on my turn, what are the odds I’ll be able to handle a key threat on my opponent’s turn if it gets played?
What are my odds of drawing an additional cycling card or an answer off the top?
How much mana would I need to have the best chance of averting death in a worst-case scenario?” These questions are also fundamentally tied to deck construction, since a player must always weigh the strength of a cycling card versus the cycling cost of it as a card.
Akroma’s Vengeance is wildly more powerful than Winged Shepherd, but if I’m sitting there on an opponent’s turn with four mana open, looking down the barrel of defeat, I know which of the two I’d rather have in hand.
In short, the cycling mechanic creates and promotes far more textured and strategic gameplay than the vast majority of mechanics in Magic: The Gathering.

Gavi, Nest Warden: Boring by Design

With all of that deep strategic texture in mind, let’s talk about Gavi, Nest Warden.
It’s a five mana 2/5 who makes the first cycle each turn free and who creates a cat when you draw your second card each turn.
This card is strong enough to be a natural choice for a cycling commander and also deathly boring.
Free cycles on each turn remove an entire dimension of the cycling mechanic, and on top of that, it highly encourages a stable quantity of cycling: one per turn, maybe two on an opponent’s turn sometimes.
Every cycle past that is a waste, since you have to pay for it and you don’t even get a cute little Dino cat, and every turn you fail to cycle is also a waste; come on, it’s free real estate.
This ability and the associated incentives remove the status of cycling cards as an important resource to be saved and used carefully, and also in turn leave players with a lot of extra mana that they didn’t have to plan out.
What does that mana get spent on?
Well, permanents that generate value when cards get cycled, of course.
Gavi destroys most of the cool strategic decision-making generally involved with playing a cycling deck and replaces it with yet another “do the thing” value pile midrange deck.
The design of a Gavi deck is such that if you do the thing, cycling in this case, you get an effortless mixture of card advantage and board advantage spread out over time, just like the other 80% of EDH decks that exist out there.
And this isn’t because Gavi players are people who like especially boring decks; the card is precision-designed to encourage exactly this type of gameplay, even if you build it from scratch rather than buying a precon.
It’s a betrayal on the part of Wizards of the Coast of everything cool about the cycling mechanic, and it creates totally predictable gameplay.

Well, except for one thing.
You see, the other thing Gavi incentivizes is cards with high cycling costs.
This is maybe the one thing about the card that’s not dead boring, but not really in a good way.
Okay, so this incentivization is most relevant for one specific cycle of cards from Scourge, and since Gavi doesn’t pay the bonus cost of Decree of Justice, it’s actually mainly relevant for two cards.
The first is Decree of Silence; big whoop, it’s a free counter spell you’ll have sometimes, not much to say here.
The other is Decree of Annihilation.
I’ll here deliver a hot take: nuking lands is maybe okay, maybe pretty cool, if your playgroup is down for that and also if you actually have a way of ending the game pretty soon after using that wild swing of momentum.
But nothing else about Gavi really promotes that sort of deck style; more likely, you’ll keep slowly getting cats, everybody’s mana base will gradually recover, and congratulations, you’ve just doubled the length of this game.
Gavi into Decree of Annihilation, while certainly unusual, still kind of fails to be interesting.
So we have here this pretty bland commander, and if you ask an EDH player what they think about cycling, she’s what they think of: this cool mechanic with a rich history and tons of synergy potential reduced down to yet another value pile commander.

Now, to any Gavi players out there, I want to say this is not an attack on you, and Gavi is more interesting than a lot of commanders out there.
There are still some interesting synergies and fun instant speed plays to be had piloting a Gavi deck.
I don’t hate it because it’s a uniquely bad design; it’s not.
I hate it because it’s a total betrayal of one of my favorite mechanics.

The Problem with Designer Commanders

And this isn’t just about Gavi either.
Wizards has a habit of printing what I’ll call “designer commanders,” which I hear used to mean commanders that take a particular mechanic and fully dedicate themselves to it, dictating a very specific and often very boring type of deck based around that mechanic.
Another example is Eligeth, Crossroads Augur, taking scry, a neat top-of-library manipulation mechanic, and literally just replacing it with value in a way that doesn’t synergize with anything related to the original mechanic.
Cool.
These commanders push players toward less interesting decks, and their specificity causes them to offer a narrow range of possible decks to be built.
A popular typographic format for this sort of commander is one with two lines of text: one that says “whenever X happens, Y happens,” and another that says “whenever enough Y’s happen, a Z happens.” This is a template that builds its own deck; you run some cards that do X and Y, and maybe some Z payoff if you’re feeling nutty.
Some people might enjoy this type of deck, and to those people I have to say that’s great.
There are certainly people out there who want these kinds of cards: newer players, people who don’t want to spend too long on deck building, or people who like to make unexpected versions of seemingly straightforward commanders.
And I think it’s cool that Wizards of the Coast has given them lots of options to work with.
But if you want to try out an alternative form of commander deck building, I’m here to offer you a vision of what that could be.

Building a Truly Bizarre Cycling Deck

For the rest of this piece, I’m going to talk about how I took one look at Gavi and then turned around and built a truly bizarre cycling deck.
It started with me absent-mindedly cruising through random Onslaught cards looking for deck ideas, as one does, and as expected I saw Astral Slide but also another enchantment I wasn’t familiar with: Oversold Cemetery.
These seem kind of neat together, right?
One promotes cycling, the other works well after I cycle creatures into the graveyard.
With these in my pocket, I decided to include blue as well, since it’s a very useful color for both cycling and instant speed shenanigans, and I started shopping around for a commander in Esper colors.
My hope was to find a commander that could be a flexible value engine when I flickered cards with Astral Slide, and this was my short list.
Eventually I decided all of them were either too generic, too niche, or not the right vibe; all except good old Sharuum.

With my commander selected, I set some healthy ground rules: no Sculpting Steel-type combos and a $100 budget.
I’m not building a high-power Sharuum deck; I’m building an Astral Slide deck.
A good place to start is always win conditions, and one that leapt out immediately was Magister Sphinx, which combined with Sharuum can one-shot a player.
Sharuum’s nature as a reanimation tool means that a beatdown suite with a number of big flying artifact creatures is likely to be a good bet at $100 cycling deck power levels and will hopefully offer enough beef to win even when Magister Sphinx isn’t available.
However, this beatdown step will likely be tricky unless I’m mostly stabilized against hate and attacks, which can be accomplished through my core card of Astral Slide.
The card offers so many use cases; it’s a reanimation engine with Sharuum, it can be a value removal, ramp, or life gain engine with other artifact creatures, and that’s to say nothing of the built-in protection and disruption utility of the card.
Since Astral Slide is so key to the deck and I can only run two in the deck, enchantment tutors were a must, so I started out with Idyllic Tutor and eventually settled on Zur as my other tutor.
His wizard tag is oddly important, as Wizards of the Coast has printed two wizardcycling cards, basically instant speed wizard tutors that count as a cycle.
Out of these strange cards organically grew a toolbox that expanded as I found new hurdles my deck needed to overcome.
Wizardcycling grabs a tutor for Slide, a tutor for my top-end boys, a recursion spell for my Slides, and a Stifle effect that’s also an extra cycle for my big turns.
A mixture of tutoring, discard outlets, and my recursion spell commander means that my deck’s key pieces generally get into play mana-efficiently.
The deck does a great job scratching my itch to play a complex and strategic deck, and it does so at a middling power level that feels good in quite a lot of pods.

Lessons from the Deck Building Process

A key takeaway from this deck building process for me was the strength of flexible, ground-up deck construction.
By starting with a mechanic and a couple key cards and selecting a commander afterward, my deck ended up with a foundation that includes the commander but doesn’t wholly consist of the commander.
It’s built to follow best practices for a cycling deck, and though it does include the occasional stormy element or value engine, each of these pieces are intentional and most of them are tutorable if I decide that’s what a given situation demands.
The most important thing I prefer to avoid is having the entire deck stand on the shoulders of the commander; at least, not unless you’re ready for that kind of responsibility.
I could have made the commander Zur, but then the issue of not having a consistent value source combined with Zur being a removal magnet would have rapidly turned my deck into one more Zur than Astral Slide, as draw enchantments and protection spells gobble up my deck space.
It’s good to keep in mind the sort of deck building incentives a given commander pushes onto you, as even one that seems like a good idea on paper might make your deck take a turn that isn’t what you were looking for.

If I was to wrap this up with a bow, I’d go with this: when you’re building a deck, figure out what the deck wants to do and try to turn that into a somewhat focused plan, except that you won’t nail the first version of the deck and that there’s room to adapt as you encounter further problems.
I’d say there are four key versions of a single deck: the first-principles version in your brain, the draft version after you actually hammer out a 100-card list, the streamlined version completed after you solo playtest a bit, and the final version completed after you play against other people for a little while.
Each of these steps will solve a different set of problems that the deck has, and that’s a strength.

#46
DeckbuildingJul 2024116K views

When to Stop Upgrading Your Commander Deck

When (not) to Upgrade Your Decks
We argue that after reaching a smooth middling power level, further upgrades can backfire due to natural bottlenecks, cost inefficiency, or unwanted play patterns.We advise assessing whether a deck's core concept is fundamentally weak or strong before investing in better cards, and using card quality upgrades strategically to match your desired power level.The goal is to enjoy the deck without forcing it into a role that undermines your fun.
Use this in your deckIdentify whether your deck's game plan collapses to common hate pieces like graveyard exile or repeated commander removal before buying upgrades.
BlinkVoltronComboBudgetPower LevelDeck ConsistencyUpgradesDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • Card quality upgrades alone cannot fix decks with natural bottlenecks, such as heavy reliance on an expensive commander or a graveyard dependent strategy.
Evidence
  • our Astral Slide deck is not upgraded because it is vulnerable to enchantment removal, artifact wipes, and graveyard hate, which become more common at higher power levels.
  • Feather, the Redeemed decks struggle to improve past a certain point because they already run the best cheap instants; replacing them with generic value cards weakens the commander synergy.
  • We keep Gisa at a middling power level to preserve battlecruiser wins through board superiority rather than adopting combo finishes or explosive spells.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Before upgrading, check if a single piece of hate (like Rest in Peace or Drannith Magistrate) can cripple your deck's primary plan.
  • Ask whether raising the power level will push you into unenjoyable play patterns, such as consistent turn four combos or stax suppression.
  • Use card quality upgrades to lift a janky premise to a playable mid power level, but expect diminishing returns if the core idea is inherently limited.
Limitation
  • The advice does not apply to commanders with scalable, efficient engines like Yuriko, the Tiger Shadow, which can be upgraded smoothly without hitting early bottlenecks.
Example
  • Feather, the Redeemed depends on cheap instants that are already optimal; swapping them for generic staples provides negligible improvement and weakens the deck's focus.
More quotes
  1. I understand this impulse: you've got your baby and you want it to fly.
  2. Some decks have natural bottlenecks in their functionality that make it difficult to give them a cohesive upgrade.
  3. Trying to improve a deck with a bottleneck will generally lead to some very good games but much worse consistency.
  4. The cheap deck is a deck that works pretty well at low budgets but is very logarithmic in terms of how it translates additional budget to additional power.
  5. In a Feather deck, one to two mana instants and sorceries that target creatures are cheap, so you're already running the best ones.
  6. You also might need to slip combos into your deck instead of winning in ways that play more to your deck strengths, which a lot of players might not find satisfying.
  7. With my Gissa deck, I play to do beefy battle cruiser things and become the big bad of the table, not to pull off a sneaky win when my opponents aren't looking.
  8. We as Magic players tend to overwhelmingly focus on card quality when we think about a deck's power level.
  9. Upgrading your deck's card quality is a tool for obtaining the deck's desired power level.
  10. There's a culture among a lot of EDH players of higher powered decks being considered cooler or more interesting, which leads to a rat race of chasing generic staples.
Full transcript

It seems like a lot of people have a particular experience while getting into the EDH format, and that experience goes something like this: they find a commander and a deck they really gel with, like it speaks to them on some deep level, and then they embark upon a great optimization quest, pouring increasing amounts of time and money into making the deck as good as it possibly can be.
I went through this process about 10 years ago with my first commander deck, and I understand this impulse; you’ve got your baby and you want it to fly.
This process can work to some extent, especially if you have big piles of money to throw at upgrading a deck, and I encourage everybody to put effort into making their decks as enjoyable as possible for themselves, which may mean power upgrades.
That said, there are actually some pretty compelling reasons not to upgrade a deck further once it plays smoothly at a healthy middling power level; so here, I’m going to go through each of these reasons using one of my mid-power decks to exemplify each of them, and then I’ll be talking about what decks are the best candidates for an upgrade.

Natural Bottlenecks

The first reason not to upgrade a deck is that some decks have natural bottlenecks in their functionality that make it difficult to give them a cohesive upgrade.
The most common bottlenecks here are being vulnerable to a particular type of hate or having a game plan that is fundamentally difficult to speed up.
If your deck relies heavily on the board presence of a commander that’s more than three mana, your opponents only need to hit out your command zone a couple times early in order to shut you down.
If your deck heavily relies on building up a graveyard over the course of several turns, a single piece of graveyard hate can totally blow you out; and card quality improvements alone won’t generally change this fact.
If your deck heavily uses board wipes to maintain control over the game, or something like goad for that matter, you’ll struggle against decks that don’t rely on combat damage or which are more explosive, which get more common at higher powers.
Ultimately, trying to improve a deck with a bottleneck like this will generally lead to some very good games with the deck but much worse consistency, with the deck getting dragged down to the dirt by bottlenecking factors a decent portion of the time.
This is the reason I don’t upgrade my Sherm slide deck.
There are ways to make it much more efficient at generating value and controlling the game, but the deck has several vulnerabilities: its Astral Slides getting hated out, its artifacts getting wiped, and/or its graveyard getting exiled.
The deck can play through these things happening a little bit, but decks generally run much more of these things at higher powers, and if it encounters too much of these sorts of hate, the deck will fall flat on its face regardless of card quality upgrades.

An extension of this problem is what I’ll call the “cheap deck.” This is a deck that works pretty well at low budgets but is very logarithmic in terms of how it translates additional budget to additional power; it gets rapidly more expensive to improve it past a certain power level.
The best example of this I can think of is Feather.
It’s a commander that has a decently beefy Voltron game plan if you build your deck specifically around it, which usually means running a lot of one- to two-mana instants and sorceries that target creatures, along with some cards that work well with these sorts of spells.
It’s hard to end up with a Feather deck that’s bad, but it’s also hard to build a Feather deck that’s really good.
One-mana instants and sorceries that target creatures are cheap, so you’re already running the best ones, meaning that the only option for trying to improve the deck is cutting some of these for generic value cards in the colors; and the weakening of the Feather game plan that this results in will negate some of that increase in power.
This reason is also why I don’t try to improve my Maria deck.
One- to two-mana equipments are the deck’s bread and butter, and there just aren’t that many options in this category of card outside of the dirt cheap ones I’m already running and ones I consider prohibitively expensive.

Enjoyment and Power Level

The other reason not to increase the power level of a deck is if you simply don’t enjoy the power level you end up at.
If you’re playing in a group of beatdown decks and everybody is relentlessly tuning the effectiveness of their beatdown, that might feel okay in that context; but your deck might end up on the power level of a deck that consistently combos off on turn five, or Voltron kills you on turn four, or forcefully suppresses your deck into the dirt with an unending stream of hate.
Even if you’re still pulling off your fair share of wins against these sorts of decks, as a player you’ll need to ask yourself the question of if that’s the sort of MTG you want to be playing.
You also might need to slip combos into your deck instead of winning in ways that play more to your deck’s strengths, which a lot of players might not find to be a satisfying way to go with their deck.
This reason is why I leave my Gissa deck at a middling power level.
It’s a deck that wants to build up resources and then play a bunch of big stuff, winning through gaining board superiority and beating down its opponents rather than playing some sort of explosive card like Torment of Hailfire or tutoring up a combo.
I play the deck to do beefy battle cruiser things and become the big bad of the table, not to pull off a sneaky win when my opponents aren’t looking.

What Does “Upgrading” Really Mean?

Okay, so the point I’m trying to make here is that some decks are less ideal candidates for upgrading past a certain point; you should maybe call them good enough.
I do fully believe in this idea, but I think it’s also useful to consider the terms here: what do we mean by upgrading a deck?
I’d say there are two main factors that determine a deck’s power level: card quality, how good the cards are, and cohesion, how well the cards are put together.
I’d say we as Magic players tend to overwhelmingly focus on the first one when we think about a deck’s power level, particularly when it comes to the question of upgrading.
And I actually think this makes perfect sense to do; when you’re asking whether or not you should improve your deck’s card quality, you’re talking about an unambiguous exchange of resources, money for power level, whereas cohesion is a much more challenging factor to modulate, particularly for a deck that’s already fully built with that in mind.
I’d like to offer an additional framework: you can think of upgrading your deck’s card quality as a tool for obtaining the deck’s desired power level.
If you have a fundamentally janky premise for a deck that you want to end up decently powerful, running good card quality can accomplish that goal to some extent, as long as you tread carefully around potential bottlenecks.
If you have a fundamentally powerful deck concept and you want to play in a group of more middling power decks, you can restrict your card choice by budget or theme or some other factor.
But to this you might ask: what does it mean for a deck concept to be fundamentally powerful or fundamentally weak?
This takes me back to the core question here, the question of when a deck should be upgraded with better card quality.

The Core of Upgradeability: Yuriko vs. Feather

I mentioned Feather as an example of a deck that generally performs solidly on a low budget but which could be difficult and cost-inefficient to improve past that point.
As a point of contrast, I want to talk about a different popular commander that, like Feather, tends to convert a specific card type into resources for a fast game plan: Yuriko, the Tiger’s Shadow.
Yuriko decks span a range of power levels and contain a lot of variety in subthemes and card choices, but all of them share a fundamental game plan: play evasive creatures and/or ninjas and utilize these cards with Yuriko to whittle down your opponents through a mixture of combat damage and Yuriko triggers, generating card advantage in the process.
Yuriko just doesn’t have the same ceiling as Feather because she’s fundamentally a faster, more resilient, and just simpler card.
Commander Ninjutsu at two mana immediately puts her head and shoulders above Feather in terms of raw power; and instead of being fiddly and requiring constant upkeep or extra steps to reach her full power, she converts low-cost ninjas and evasive creatures into very fundamental resources of the game, card advantage and life loss for opponents, and can do so with minimal mana investment.
Decks can vary in how many ninjas they run and what sorts of evasive creatures they play, but these cards are basically guaranteed to be effective due to the speed, resilience, and simplicity of Yuriko; and as long as the deck is above a certain minimum number of these supporting creatures, these cards combined with Yuriko form a very powerful core to the deck.
It’s this strong core for generating generic resources that makes a deck especially upgradeable.
There’s a good amount of diversity in what a successful Yuriko deck can look like, and there are lots of piecemeal card quality upgrades and packages that can be added to modulate the deck to whatever power level the Yuriko player wants to be playing at.

I think it’s worth clarifying here that there’s nothing wrong with commanders that are better suited to mid-power, nor is a commander like Yuriko or Korvold that inherently pushes a higher power necessarily more interesting or more worthwhile.
There are also the players who like the challenge of trying to boost a commander or theme that isn’t inherently high power up to that point, and that can be a fun process.
I personally like the challenge of building powerful decks on a low budget, as it forces you to put a lot of effort into the cohesion of a deck since the card quality is naturally going to be a bit lacking.
At the end of the day, I just want people to know what they’re getting into when they decide to pour time and money into a deck.
If you have a limit on how much you want to spend on decks, it’s valuable to know when you’re dealing with a commander who will naturally take more money to boost from a mid-power deck to a high-power deck; and as stated earlier, this will fundamentally come down to how powerful a deck’s core resource-generating and game-winning pieces are.
Even if budget isn’t a concern, it’s worth keeping an eye on a deck’s weak points and making sure that they aren’t going to be exacerbated by playing against more powerful decks.

The Upgrade Culture and Final Advice

I feel like there’s a culture among a lot of EDH players of higher-powered decks being considered cooler or more interesting, which leads to this sort of rat race of chasing generic staples and applying formulaic rules to every deck to bump up its power level.
I don’t want to tell anybody not to improve a deck they want to improve, but I just want to tell you right now that if you have a mid-power deck you really like, you don’t necessarily need to change it.
If you want a higher-power deck, you can pick out a couple things you like from an existing deck you enjoy, such as a card or a theme, and then build a new, more powerful home for it.
This also goes for putting pet cards in decks, but that’s a topic for another day.

#47
DeckbuildingJul 2024113K views

Self-Balancing a $25 Commander Deck Pool Over Two Years

I Spent Two Years Playing EDH With a Pool of $25 Decks
We describe a two-year experiment of building and maintaining a pool of ten $25 Commander decks.By tracking win rates and making iterative adjustments, we achieved a balanced metagame where most decks had win rates between 18-33%.The process showed that aggressive decks require slower decks to include early blockers and life gain, and that combo decks suffer if they lack defensive speed.
Use this in your deckIf a deck achieves a 40%+ win rate, retrofit other decks with early blockers, life gain, and board wipes rather than nerfing the dominant deck.
WhiteBlueBlackRedGreenAggroControlComboBudgetDeckbuilding RestrictionsMeta TrendsPower LevelBudget Building
Principle
  • Iterative tuning based on win-rate data enables a self-correcting metagame even under strict budget limits.
Evidence
  • The Orzhov control deck had a 54% win rate initially, but after meta adjustments it won zero games in the next period.
  • The Selesnya cats aggro deck reached 42% win rate, prompting other decks to add early defensive tools.
  • In the final 86-game sample, win rates ranged from 18% to 33%, showing substantial balance.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Add cheap creatures and board wipes to combat-oriented decks when a fast aggro deck threatens balance.
  • Ensure combo decks include enough early interaction or blockers to survive until they can go off.
Limitation
  • The pool's metagame was shaped entirely by iterative play within a closed group, so results may not transfer to random playgroups or broader formats.
Example
  • The Dimir Lazav deck exploited infect via Vector Asp but eventually underperformed as opponents learned to pressure it early and disrupt the combo.
More quotes
  1. The Orzhov deck garnered an eye-watering 54% win rate.
  2. The pool began with two control decks; that factor shouldn't be overlooked.
  3. A lot of the decks just felt kind of flat and lifeless, generic mid-range decks doing generic mid-range things.
  4. The Cats deck ended up being a truly ruthless aggro deck, putting on heavy pressure as early as turn two.
  5. A meaningful metagame had been developed where lacking ways to hold off creature-based aggression would cause a deck to win fewer games.
  6. This process of building decks formulated a lot of my understanding of the commander format.
  7. I had a rude awakening where most of the games I played were way less dynamic and interesting than the games with these $25 decks.
  8. The decks ended up grouping into three clusters in terms of win rate.
  9. [The Lazav deck] had become what it had tried and failed to survive against.
  10. If you build a set of decks like this, don't let any of them be overly weak or overly boring, and have the courage to dismantle a deck you like if it ends up being one of these things.
Full transcript
How It All Started

The deck building began in an interesting place.
Though I knew the Sheoldred deck of my youth had problems, I was still a black player wanting to do black player things, so I opted to build an Orzhov control deck.
Putting Kambal in the command zone offered a great way to keep the deck’s life total up while chipping away at opposing life totals and allowed the rest of the deck to focus on removal, draw, and various life-gain-related combos rather than trying to develop beaters.
