Likewise, my friend Hans built a pretty brutal Azorius control list with Noyan Dar in the command zone, and the fact that the pool began with two control decks is a factor that shouldn’t be overlooked.
As other decks got flushed out, this influence can be seen: of the pool’s five mid-range decks, three were some form of aristocrats and another was a land-focused deck that didn’t rely on keeping non-land permanents in play.
The pool also ended up containing a few more explosive, combo-oriented decks, and these were likewise well situated to handle control, consisting of a graveyard-centric artifacts deck, a big mana deck, and a deck with a number of its own counter spells.
Finally, we have what ended up being the hardest color combination to build a $25 deck for: Dimir.
For my first attempt, I opted for an artifact tokens list with Rona as the commander, a mid-range deck that could hopefully be flexible enough to survive in a wide range of scenarios.
So this was the first pool we had, and we played about 30 games with it before going back to make major changes.

Early Performance and the First Wave of Changes
The Second Round and a More Balanced Metagame
A Six-Month Groove: 86 Games and a Stable Metagame
Final Adjustments and the Finished Pool

And the funny thing is, the fact that I had this major period of EDH deck building during my college years almost certainly contributed to my decision to begin making videos about the format.
So, in a sense, Salubrious Snail is brought to you by the COVID-19 pandemic.
Garbage sponsor, honestly, would not recommend.

Lessons and the Philosophy of Deck Building

As a final point, this process of building decks formulated a lot of my understanding of the Commander format, and when I started building decks outside this pool in the first half of 2023, I had kind of a rude awakening where most of the games I played were way less dynamic and interesting than the games with these $25 decks.
My first Magic: The Gathering video was partially inspired by this experience of playing against bland, formulaic decks without enough interaction to make for a dynamic game.
And while I’ve had plenty more interesting games since then, I try to keep the mindset that I had when building these $25 decks, which is to say: try to present a specific problem for your opponents and be prepared for when your opponents present a problem for you.
To me, multiplayer games are more fun when people have to think about their opponents rather than when everybody is just racing engines against each other like we’re all playing some kind of second-rate Euro game.

Thanks for watching.
I linked a folder in the description that contains the final versions of all 10 of these decks, though some cards have gone up in price in the past few years, as cards have a tendency to do.
I’m also releasing an end note over on my Patreon where I discuss the Orzhov deck in a bit more detail, so if that interests you, you can head on over there and throw some money at me.

#48
DeckbuildingJul 20241,796 words in transcript

How to Fit Pet Cards Into Commander Decks

Your Pet Cards Deserve a Better Home
We explain that players often want to include favorite 'pet cards' in many decks, but this isn't always optimal.It categorizes pet cards into three types and suggests two main strategies: building a dedicated deck around a pet card, or using a flexible deck shell that can house multiple niche cards.For moderately powerful but specific pet cards, finding the right home can make them more enjoyable.
Use this in your deckEnsure your deck has a solid core of lands, ramp, and draw before sprinkling in niche pet cards.
MidrangeControlTokensPet CardsBudgetSynergyPower LevelDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • The act of building a deck that a pet card fully belongs in is the greatest act of love for that card.
Evidence
  • Cards like Shigeki feel useful in any green deck because its instant-speed uncounterable recursion is always relevant.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Before adding pet cards, ensure the deck has a solid core of lands, ramp, and draw to function smoothly.
  • For a single niche pet card, build the entire deck around supporting that card's unique ability.
Limitation
  • Highly focused decks, such as Voltron or dedicated combo, cannot include random pet cards without losing consistency.
Example
  • Astral slide, Maze's End, and Time Sieve each got a dedicated deck where they shine.
  • The Skeleton Ship proliferate control deck runs quirky pet cards like Overwhelming Intellect and storage lands.
More quotes
  1. The act of building a deck that a pet card fully belongs in is the greatest act of love you can show your favorite piece of cardboard.
  2. You can throw these sorts of cards into any old deck whose color identity they fit, but this doesn't necessarily mean they'll feel satisfying.
  3. These sorts of cards won't reach their full potential if you're playing them in a deck they only tangentially connect.
  4. At the worst, you might gradually grow resentment for them if you keep drawing them rather than a card that is more useful.
  5. The more generic a deck's general game plan is, the more niche and varied the specific card choices can be.
  6. It may be tempting to disregard [interaction, protection, and removal], but if you're making a funny deck, how well-rounded it is may determine whether it ends up being a deck you want to keep playing with or whether it ends up getting set aside as that gimmick deck that was fun for a little while.
  7. I could throw $500 at a fancy deck, but for that same amount I could build a half dozen decks at lower budget points that can also serve the interests of different gameplay styles and different cards I love.
  8. If I'm throwing together a $50 list based on a card I really like or a funny premise, I can just order that sucker with no second thoughts, knowing that if I get half a dozen fun games out of it I'll be happy.
  9. Exercising a bit of discretion in where these cards are played can lead to them being more enjoyable.
  10. Building a deck around a single card or building a deck that's general enough to fit a number of these specific cards are two options for fitting them into decks.
Full transcript
The Temptation of Pet Cards
Handling Bad or Hyper-Specific Pet Cards
When Pet Cards Are Just Good
The Tricky Middle Ground
Option A: Build a Deck Around a Star Pet Card
Option B: One Deck to House Many Niche Cards
Finding a Home for Many Pet Cards: Flexible Themes
Don’t Neglect the Fundamentals
The Bottom Line
A Final Note on Budget Deck Building

Thanks for watching.
If you’ve got some pet cards you really like, you can let me know in the comments, both because I like hearing about fun cards people like and also because it serves my algorithmic overlords.
That’s a win-win if I ever heard one.

#49
StrategyJul 20242,466 words in transcript

Analyzing a $20 Zyrus Buff Spells Commander Deck

My Playgroup's Best Deck is $20
A budget Zyrus the Writhing Storm deck uses buff spells to win via commander damage while appearing friendly by giving cards to opponents.The transparent game plan and cheap cards cause opponents to underestimate its snowballing resources.Snake tokens from Zyrus compound the threat and make combat tactically difficult for others.
Use this in your deckBuild a Zyrus deck with about 20% buff spells, 10% protection spells, and 10% removal spells.
Xyris, the Writhing StormBlueRedGreenGroup HugVoltronTokensBudgetTable PoliticsThreat AssessmentSynergySocial and Politics
Principle
  • A commander that provides mutual card draw can mask an aggressive game plan, allowing a stealth snowball strategy.
Evidence
  • In practice, opponents will protect the Zyrus player, even countering removal aimed at Zyrus, to continue drawing extra cards.
  • The deck's consistent card draw leads to hands filled with multiple protection and buff spells, making it hard to disrupt.
Deckbuilding Application
  • To consistently draw into interaction, dedicate about 20% of your nonland cards to buff spells and another 10% to protection.
  • Keep the average mana value low to hold up multiple spells during combat and maintain unpredictability.
Limitation
  • The deck is vulnerable to board wipes, which bypass most of its single-target protection spells.
  • Forcing the deck to use buff spells defensively instead of offensively delays its critical turn and reduces its threat.
Example
  • A mana-screwed player once countered a removal spell on Zyrus just to keep receiving three cards per turn.
More quotes
  1. I need to make a buff spells deck now.
  2. The deck is friendly and unassuming, right up until the point where it starts deciding to stop being those things and start murdering players.
  3. Because fun fact: letting you and an opponent each draw 15 cards is great when that opponent simultaneously dies to commander damage.
  4. This deck gets underestimated game after game, and by people who have lost to it half a dozen or more times.
  5. The deck's lack of spicy plays and absolute transparency in what it's trying to do in a given game play to its strengths.
  6. The threat profile of the deck typically lags behind its actual power at any given moment.
  7. Do I look like somebody who throws my threats in the garbage?
  8. The deck gains piles and piles of potential attackers and blockers that could get larger and gain all manner of potentially problematic keywords at any moment.
  9. This deck doesn't seem very good, and that perception is exactly what makes it so good.
  10. Discarding to hand size is the final dynamic that causes people to underestimate the deck.
Full transcript
The Buff Spell Disagreement and the Birth of a Deck
How the Xyris Deck Operates: Friendly Card Draw and a Deadly Trap
Small Bean Syndrome and Predictable Transparency
The Snowball Principle and Lagging Threat Profile
The Combat Fear Factor: A Stream of Buff Spells
Snakes, Snowballing, and the Board Wipe Question
The Aeri Kyler Scale and Resource Permanency
Velocity: High Turnover of Hand Resources
Deceptive Strength in Low- to Mid-Power Games
Conclusion: Dynamic, Fair, and the Highest Win Rate

Thanks for watching.
I had planned to release a video about navigating power levels this week, but I couldn’t quite get the script where I wanted it in time, so I decided to make we instead.
You can expect that power level video next week, though.
I will also be posting an end note to we tomorrow over on my Patreon that discusses a particular deck building style that was used in making this deck, so if you aren’t giving me money yet, now is a pretty cool time to start.

#50
Meta CommentaryJul 20242,103 words in transcript

Channel Updates and Commander Q&A

Some channel updates + Q&A
We outline a new weekly posting schedule and plan for patron deck reviews.In a community Q&A, they discuss the importance of rule zero conversations to align deck power and fun expectations.They also argue that the EDH ban list faces tension between competitive balance and casual fun, advocating for social communication over heavy-handed bans.
Use this in your deckBefore your next game, ask the table what power level their decks are and if any strategies or cards would make the game less fun for them.
ControlLifegainRemovalRule ZeroPower LevelFormat HealthSocial and Politics
Principle
  • Before a game, verify that all decks operate at comparable power levels by discussing their speed and primary strategy.
  • For casual tables, also ask each player about strategies or cards they find unfun to avoid, such as heavy stax or combos.
Evidence
  • The EDH ban list must serve two conflicting goals: banning competitively overpowered cards like Dockside Extortionist and banning casually unfun cards like Armageddon, leading to tension.
Deckbuilding Application
  • If you build a particularly oppressive or 'mean' deck, expect it to see less playtime because it can reduce others' enjoyment.
Limitation
  • You cannot demand that opponents remove every card or strategy your deck is weak against; such requests are unfair and undermine the spirit of compromise.
Example
  • We regularly ask a friend not to play a specific Turrid deck, showing how targeted deck exclusions can be negotiated in a friendly playgroup.
More quotes
  1. I had the idea to do a 10K Q&A when I was at 9,000 subscribers, and somehow I'm now creeping up on 15K.
  2. I needed a username for some website, and my two second on the spot smash together of words was salubria snail.
  3. My first deck was a sort of black life gain deck. It ran a fair amount of removal because that's just who I am as a player.
  4. Mark Rosewater gave a career day presentation at my middle school and handed out starter decks from M12.
  5. My favorite Magic card is [Ashiok, Weaver]. I'm a big horror fan, and the idea of an unknowable otherworldly entity weaponizing the fears of its foes to destroy them is just incredible.
  6. If I had to ban one card for very petty personal reasons, it would be farewell. I hate the card. I think it's very lazy design.
  7. The EDH ban list is tricky because it's really trying to be two different things: it's trying to dictate what's too strong for cEDH and it's also trying to dictate what's not fun for casual.
  8. I actually really like explicitly political cards. Council's dilemma and will of the council are both cool mechanics with a lot of cool design space.
  9. The most memorable deck I've played against was a Tymna Jeska tribal Ball Lightning deck, and that deck's vision is splendid to me.
  10. I have a friend who has a Tergrid deck, and three nights out of four I don't want to play against.
Full transcript
Channel Updates

I had the idea to do a 10K Q&A sort of thing about a month ago when I was at 9,000 subscribers, and somehow the passage of time took place and now I’m creeping up on 15K, I don’t really know how that happened.
In all seriousness, I appreciate all the support.
It’s just cool to make things and have a lot of people enjoy those things, so that’s been cool; thank you.

Part of the reason I’m making this is because I want to share some updates about what’s going on with us and what I want to do with it.
You may have noticed that I am posting more often; indeed, I am posting weekly at the moment, on Thursdays.
I’m currently trying to alternate between the higher-effort content, the kind of deep dives into a particular deck-building subject, and the more fun content where I, for example, analyze a precon or just talk about whatever it is that I want to talk about that day.
One particular type of off-week content that I’m going to start next month is Patron Deck Reviews.
These deck reviews are going to be very much in the style of the precon review, so I’m going to try and figure out what a deck is trying to do, how well it’s doing at that goal, and some potential routes for improvement.
As I said, that’s going to be starting next month; I’m going to be picking decks through a lottery system, and you can go check out my Patreon for more information about that.
Also on my Patreon, I am going to be posting a Glissa deck tech very soon, so that’s two different reasons to check out my Patreon, that’s pretty cool.

With that out of the way, let’s get to questions.
These are going to be mostly taken from a post I made on Patreon recently.

What’s the Story Behind Your Username?

About a year ago, I needed a username for some website or other, and just my two-second, on-the-spot smash-together of words was “Salubrious Snail.” Cut to a few months later, I was trying to come up with a name for my YouTube channel, and that one popped back into my brain.
I kind of liked it; it’s snappy, it’s alliterative, has a fun word, seems pretty cool, so that’s what I went with.

What’s Your MTG Story?

I learned to play Magic in 2011, and the way I got into the game is that Mark Rosewater gave a career day presentation at my middle school.
At the end of it, he handed all of these eleven- and twelve-year-olds starter decks from M12, and I got the red one and then just went from there.
I started playing Limited in 2013, so my first draft was Dragon’s Maze, and then in early 2014 I really got into the game; I started playing Standard and EDH.
I’ve played off and on since then, picking back up very heavily in early last year and starting to build a whole bunch more Commander decks.

What Was the First Card, Deck, or Mechanic That Excited You?

I’ve always been a black player, and my first deck that I can remember was a sort of black life-gain-ish deck.
It ran a fair amount of removal, because that’s just who I am as a player.
The specific interaction that I can remember between two cards is it had Tormented Soul, which is an unblockable creature, and Vampire Outcasts, which is a bloodthirst creature, meaning it’s bigger when it enters if an opponent was dealt damage, so it almost always came in as a four-mana 4/4 lifelink, which is honestly not bad.
Lifelink is one of the best keywords; pretty solid.

What Is Your Favorite Magic Card?

Jace, Unraveler of Secrets has always been a card I love, and it’s funny because I feel like it’s known for Cube, you know, you do the splash Jace, but I’ve never played with it in Cube.
The place I’ve played with it was in Standard Grixis Control; I ran three of it, and it was fantastic.
In the matchups, it soaked up damage for my deck, which sadly did not run life-gain, and against the control matchups, of which there were many, because that was Standard at the time, all the abilities are great.
The plus-two lets you exile cards, which is great against Azorius control that kind of cycles through the deck repeatedly; the minus-ten, if your opponent lets you get up there, just blows out a control deck.
So just a great card all around, and I also just love the flavor.
I’m a big horror fan, and the idea of this kind of unknowable, otherworldly entity who’s weaponizing the fears of its foes to destroy them, that’s just incredible to me, I love it.

What Does Your Rule Zero Conversation Look Like?

This is a good question.
I think for starters it’s good to make sure that decks are at similar power levels, so figure out what decks are doing, how fast they’re doing them, and just make sure there’s not going to be any crazy discrepancy.
Then, for middling to lower power levels, it’s good to check in with what people’s intentions are for the game and what they find fun.
There are things like board wipes or combos or heavy-duty stax pieces where some people might not have as much fun if they’re playing against those; that might not be a thing that they want to see a lot of in their game, and I think it’s fair to try and navigate that to some extent.
I will sometimes ask somebody not to play a particular deck, even.
For example, I have a friend who has a Tergrid deck, and three nights out of four I don’t want to play against a Tergrid deck, not really, no. I think that’s fair, although there are some limits to what you can request of somebody, and it’s a tricky thing to navigate.
You can’t just ask that every deck your deck is bad against isn’t in the game; that’s not really fair.
At the same time, when I build a deck, if it’s a particularly feisty or mean or suppressive deck, I have some understanding that it might see less action than a deck that is, you know, nicer or offends people a little less.

If You Were in Control of the EDH Ban List, What Would You Do?

The EDH ban list is tricky because it’s really trying to be two different things: it’s trying to dictate what’s too strong for cEDH, and it’s also trying to dictate what’s not fun for casual.
I think answering these two questions at the same time is difficult.
We can think about some different cards that we might think about banning.
Let’s say Dockside Extortionist, very good card, maybe it should be banned.
But that would be mainly a ban for cEDH and the top levels of high-power casual, because it doesn’t matter for random midrange beats decks: there aren’t that many artifacts at that power level, you’re not going to be doing the same sort of value loops, and it’s just not going to have the same horrifying power level as it does in other EDH contexts.
Similarly, we could think about a card like Armageddon; you can ban that because it’s not fun, but that’s not a ban in the conventional sense of a card game.
You’re not banning it because it’s too strong, you’re banning it because it’s unfun, and I think there is a little bit of tension there, that you’re trying to solve these two different problems.
So really, I probably wouldn’t be that comfortable dictating a whole bunch of new card allowances or disallowances for EDH, just because I think it’s a really hard thing to do.
What I would encourage is players having more of a Rule Zero conversation, more of a conversation about what they find fun and what they don’t want to see in their game.
You shouldn’t roll up into a game with strangers and destroy all their lands if they’re just trying to play their upgraded precons or something.
I think it’s good to have an understanding about that, and I think that’s an okay solution because EDH fundamentally is a social format.
If I had to ban one card for very petty personal reasons, it would be Farewell.
I hate the card; I just think it’s very lazy design.
It’s a six-mana board wipe that has no downsides other than that it’s a six-mana board wipe.
It’s probably not broken from a power-level standpoint, but I just hate to see it, it’s lazy, don’t like it.
It also kind of crowds out other cards like Merciless Eviction or Austere Command, so I don’t know, not a fan, not a fan of Farewell.

What’s Your Opinion on Explicitly Political Mechanics?

I actually really like explicitly political cards.
Council’s dilemma and will of the council, I think, are both cool mechanics with a lot of cool design space.
I think it’s unfortunate that the two cards from those that actually ended up being good in EDH are some of the less interesting ones, but I think there’s plenty of other design space; I think it’d be cool if they printed more of those type of cards.
I think goad is actually a super cool mechanic, I love goad, and I think it would be neat to expand the mindset of goad outside of creatures, just because I think goad has kind of a natural power limitation where it’s not going to be good above the low-midrange level of power.
I think that’s valid for a type of card to be, but it would be neat to see more cards that are explicitly political but play around with other resources like cards in hand and that sort of thing.
I don’t remember what the mechanic is called, demonstrate, maybe, the one where you can copy a spell if you let an opponent copy it as well; I think that one’s kind of neat, that’s cool.
So yeah, I think more stuff like that would be neat.

What’s the Most Memorable Deck You’ve Played Against?

Sometime last year, I played against a Tymna / Jeska tribal Ball Lightning deck, and that deck’s vision is splendid to me.
I love decks that take a really juicy, powerful commander or pair of partner commanders and use them for a ridiculous end; I just love that.
I love the idea of swinging with a Ball Lightning for eighteen at somebody’s face, that’s just a vision I can appreciate, let me tell you.
So I’d have to go with that deck; that’s the only one that comes to mind.

The Cat

I’ve gotten a handful of comments asking about the cat post and the implied cat that goes with it.
Thankfully, he made an appearance for this.
This is my boy Nico; he loves to bounce off the walls when I’m trying to record.
So yeah, he’s a great guy.

#51
DeckbuildingJul 20243,945 words in transcript

Deck Focus: Winning Commander with Cohesive Plans

Snail's Guide to Winning an EDH Game
We argue that categorizing specific cards as win conditions is less useful than ensuring every card contributes to a focused plan.It introduces a framework of inputs and outputs to analyze deck cohesion, identifying unfocused inputs and outputs as common pitfalls.The solution is to evaluate each card's function, reliability, and contribution to a cohesive strategy.
Use this in your deckAsk yourself for each card in your deck: What does it do, how often will it fail, and does it contribute to your deck's cohesive plan?
MidrangeTokensWin ConditionsDeck ConsistencySynergyCard EvaluationDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • A constructive way to evaluate an EDH deck is through its inputs (conditions to meet) and outputs (results that advance the plan), rather than focusing on individual win condition cards.
Evidence
  • Unfocused inputs in a deck lead to a high number of dead draws and inconsistent board states.
  • Unfocused outputs cause a deck to generate generic value without a clear, reliable path to ending the game.
Deckbuilding Application
  • For each card in your deck, determine its role, its failure rate, and whether it directly supports your deck's unified strategy.
Limitation
  • Decks that pivot between multiple plans can succeed, but they require careful construction to ensure the different components connect meaningfully.
Example
  • The Missy deck illustrates unfocused inputs: it contained artifact creature, face-down creature, and narrow sub-synergy packages that did not connect well.
  • Gavi, Nest Warden decks often suffer from unfocused outputs, generating tokens, removal, and damage without a dedicated way to win.
  • Moon Shaker Cavalry demonstrates a focused output for Gavi, turning a wide board of tokens into a lethal attack.
More quotes
  1. The important question is what cards in my deck are the ones that don't win the game.
  2. Anything can win a game if your opponents play no cards; a 1/1 squirrel is a quality win condition if your opponents are playing nothing but archers and bears.
  3. The number one criticism I have for people when they ask me to look at their deck is 'this looks unfocused.'.
  4. Most decks involve a system of inputs and outputs, with inputs being conditions that need to be met and outputs being the results of those inputs that contribute to the deck's game plan.
  5. The goal of a parity breaker should be to turn a game state that's not quite winning into one that wins, but some cards will only win with a board state that was probably already about to win.
  6. If something is a dead card two-thirds of the time, that other third had better be really good.
  7. Mass calcify is the best evasion spell.
  8. If your opponents are playing nothing but archers and bears, a single Siege Dragon is a great win condition.
  9. Conventional EDH wisdom might have you gesturing at various things as a win condition: Craterhoof Behemoth is a win condition, Torment of Hailfire is a win condition, Purphoros, God of the Forge is a win condition, Sanguine Bond is a win condition.
  10. It's a generally toothless type of deck that will struggle to win against decks that have more refined win plans, even if those decks generate a smaller quantity of total resources.
Full transcript
What Is a Win Condition?

When I got into Standard ten years ago, the question of what constitutes a "wincon" was one with a single standout answer: Aetherling.
It was a control meta, no doubt; if you weren't playing a devotion deck or attempting in vain to play green, you were playing one of the many variants of control, and you were almost certainly running one or two copies of Aetherling as a way to close out the game.
For a relatively new player, it was a comforting type of card: you input a bunch of mana over two or three turns, and in return it reduces your opponent’s life total to zero.
So that’s what a "wincon" is, right?
Or maybe a wincon is when my brother played an Overwhelming Stampede into a cluttered board state to cut through my modest suite of blockers like a hot knife through, I don’t know, a handful of 4/4 vampires.
Or maybe it was a wincon when I played Angelic Gift on a 4/4 land at the Battle for Zendikar prerelease and smacked my opponent four times for the win.
Above average two-mana enchantment, honestly; some might even call it a wincon.

We all come across the concept of a "win condition" somewhere along the line in our existences as Magic players, and this is for a reason.
MTG’s defense-friendly combat system and hand-emptying mana system means that it can’t always be assumed that the game will end naturally; there are all sorts of game situations that might sprawl out and demand some sort of remedy.
There are lots of situations where the phrase "win condition" has a fairly understandable meaning; however, as with most pieces of terminology, it becomes a lot more challenging in Commander.
Sextupling the life total of your opponents makes a lot of things challenging, to be honest; not only does it take more to win an EDH game, but the concept of what winning means shifts wildly.

Win Conditions in Commander

Conventional EDH wisdom might have you gesturing at various things as a "wincon": Craterhoof Behemoth is a wincon, Torment of Hailfire is a wincon, Purphoros, God of the Forge is a wincon, Sanguine Bond is a wincon.
And sure, this classification does work on some level; there are lots of situations where these cards will win you the game, and they each gesture at a different plan for winning the game.
But is this a useful category of card?
I don’t think it is.
Anything can win a game if your opponents play no cards; a 1/1 squirrel is a quality wincon if your opponents are playing nothing but archers and bears.
A single Siege Dragon is a great wincon.
You shouldn’t ask the question, “What cards in my deck are the ones that win the game?” because the important question is, “What cards in my deck are the ones that don’t win the game?”

Now, let me clarify that last statement.
What I don’t mean to say is that you should cram your deck full of big, splashy bombs; quite the opposite, even having too high a density of splash in your deck will leave you too little room for the building blocks that actually lead to victory.
What I mean here is that if you’re building a deck with the intent of winning a game of Commander, that goal should be present in some form or other throughout each and every one of your card choices.
The number one criticism I have for people when they ask me to look at their deck, a criticism I give about half the time, is “this looks unfocused.” This phrase can mean a couple different things in practice, and to start thinking about those, it’s useful to first lay out a framework for how EDH decks work fundamentally.

The Focus of Your Deck: Inputs and Outputs

Most decks involve a system of inputs and outputs, with inputs being conditions that need to be met and outputs being the results of those inputs that contribute to the deck’s game plan.
My Sharuum deck’s primary inputs are Astral Slide and cycling cards, and its primary outputs are flickers, which allows Sharuum to recur large artifacts onto the board.
My Marad deck’s primary input are equipments, and its primary outputs are mana value and my commander hitting your face for 14 damage.
I could give more examples, but long story short, inputs and outputs are the name of the game for decks with any amount of synergy, and this framework itself points us to the two places a deck can stumble with regard to focus: it can have “unfocused inputs,” and it can have “unfocused outputs.”

Unfocused Inputs and Outputs

For an example of a deck with unfocused inputs, a viewer emailed me about their Missy deck that contained both artifact creature and face-down creature synergy packages, with a couple narrower sections of sub-synergies within these.
Within the deck there are the components to make some decently powerful board states, but the issue is that there are too many different types of card and ability present, and they don’t really connect with each other outside of all being related to the commander in some form or other.
Ultimately, a deck like this with unfocused inputs will naturally have a lot of dead draws and will struggle to put together a cohesive board state with any consistency.
It’s possible to build a deck that can pivot between a few different plans, but these decks are tricky to build and typically involve paying close attention to the points of connectivity between various types of card.

Some examples of decks with unfocused outputs would be conventionally built variants of what I like to call “value tribal” commanders, and I’ll use Gavi, Nest Warden as an example since I’ve already talked about her before.
Your average Gavi deck is pretty good at maintaining cycling-based value loops, but when we look at the actual payoffs for cycling aside from draw, it’s a lot of small token creation, some larger token creation, some soft creature removal, some incremental damage, some creatures getting bigger, and so on.
Theoretically you’re getting a lot of stuff when you add the input of cycling, but those outputs are so diverse that these decks don’t end up actually doing any one thing in particular; it’s just a flood of generic Magic: The Gathering gameplay.
This is probably better than a deck with unfocused inputs, since it’s actually doing something pretty consistently, but it’s a generally toothless type of deck that will struggle to win against decks that have more refined win plans, even if those decks generate a smaller quantity of total resources.

Building a Cohesive Game Plan

Both unfocused inputs and unfocused outputs are problems that will lead to challenges in ending the game, but for different reasons.
Despite this, the solution to the two problems is pretty similar: you should be asking what each card in your deck does, how often it will fail to perform its intended function, and whether it contributes toward a cohesive plan for your deck.
A common first step in building Commander decks is to lump together a bunch of cards that synergize around a particular one or two things, and while this will lead to a deck that does do something, if you’re trying to win games, it might take a bit of tuning beyond that to make sure the deck can actually make it to the finish line.
And for a bit, I want to zoom in on a particular type of deck that often falls just a little short in this regard.

All right, so this is a transition point in a longer video where it might make sense to put a mid-roll ad, but I don’t like mid-roll ads, so I don’t run them on my videos.
That said, if you like what I do and have a spare three bucks a month to support me in making high-effort videos like this one, there’s a link to my Patreon down in the description.
As I was going through the EDHREC page for Gavi while writing the previous section, there was one standout exception to the lack of good options for closing out the game: Moonshaker Cavalry.
The card is a brutally efficient finisher, capable of converting a board full of tokens into a bunch of dead opponents in short order, and its presence here gestures at a specific type of card: mass buff cards, whose usage could lead to a Gavi deck with a more definite identity.

Midrange and Parity

This brings me to the subject of midrange, which I’ll define here as decks that want to get a big board of creatures and then attack with them.
Now, I’m sure when playtesting we all like to imagine the ideal scenario for a beatdown deck: we play out a bunch of creatures and swing into the big empty boards of our quivering hypothetical opponents.
And this might occasionally be the case, but in most games your opponents will have creatures as well, even if their deck isn’t a purely midrange deck.
So here I’m going to introduce an important phrase: “parity.” Parity means a state of standstill, basically, and in the midrange context it’s usually when multiple players have decent-sized boards but can’t swing in for large damage due to a lack of evasion or a risk of counterattack.
In a game like this with multiple midrange decks, we can divide the game into two key steps for a given player: create parity and break parity.
Creating parity just means getting your beefy midrange board up and going and not falling behind.
This sounds simple, and indeed it’s not too hard in the abstract, but there are lots of places to go wrong.
You can fail to draw mana, fail to draw beaters, fail to draw the right combinations of beaters, and even if you succeed at these three things, you can still get board wiped or lose to a non-combat wincon before you’re able to do an adequate quantity of beating.
For this reason, lands, ramp, draw, protection, and a varied mixture of creatures might all be parts of how you get into a state of parity with other decks.

Breaking parity is where you do something to the board that allows you to deviate from the state of parity, which generally means letting your creatures hit for a lot of damage.
The most popular “parity breakers” in Commander are overrun effects, but there are a myriad of other potential options: targeted buffs, extra combat spells, mass evasion spells, and cards that disable potential blockers or even remove them altogether.
I am on the record saying that Mass Calcify is the best evasion spell.
Now, if I were to rank these in terms of raw power, the bigger overrun effects like Craterhoof Behemoth and Finale of Devastation would be easily top of the pile, with Moonshaker Cavalry being a similarly powerful option in white; however, I’d argue that these are also probably the least interesting options as well, given how straightforward and hard to interact with they are.
So if you’re not playing at a super high power level, you should go with whatever parity breaker feels most appropriate to you.
The important thing is that you should have at least something for this: some number of cards played with the goal of converting that cool board you worked hard to build up into a win.

With all of that said, and to crib shamelessly from EDHRECast, one challenge in navigating parity breakers is being able to distinguish a card that helps you win from a “win-more” card.
The goal of a parity breaker should be to turn a game state that’s not quite winning into one that wins, but some cards will only win with a board state that was probably already about to win.
For example, Epic Struggle wins the game outright if you have 20 or more creatures on your upkeep, but there are tons of cards that can win off of a board that massive, and most will be useful in situations with fewer creatures as well, rather than being a four-mana enchantment with no text.
Crucible of Fire is a less extreme example; it’s an anthem for a creature type that’s generally already quite big, and most Dragon decks would probably be better served by running an additional dragon with useful abilities instead.
Ultimately, the way to navigate these sorts of cards is just to think about all the situations in which a given card is useful and also the situations where it’s not useful.
If something is a kind of dead card two-thirds of the time, that other third had better be really good.

Parity Beyond Midrange

Parity also exists as a relevant concept outside of midrange decks, specifically for aggro and control decks.
For an aggro deck, parity is what you want to avoid, and if your momentum does come to a bit of a stall as your opponents catch up on board, you at least want them to be low enough on life or other resources to be deeply uncomfortable in their position within the game.
This is the general conceit of a lot of 60-card burn decks: throw creatures at your opponent’s face until you can’t anymore, and then hope you got your opponent low enough to finish them off with burn in time.
Control, on the other hand, is similar to the classical midrange parity plan in the sense that it’s looking to create and then break it, but it is very different in that control decks will often create parity by using board wipes or stax pieces that slow down the table, and then break parity using carefully picked out cards that are less affected by these tools.
Control decks are also typically chiefly worried about resource management; compromises must be made between how scary the board is allowed to be and how many control spells are kept in hand for contingencies.
For these reasons, control is sometimes easier to finish off opponents with than midrange, since small boards and suppressive methods mean that less potent finishers can still do the job and that winning over the course of several turns is acceptable.
You do still need to think about the deck’s win plan, though, and balancing that with the deck’s controlling element is naturally going to be part of the challenge.

The Role of Removal

On that note, let’s talk about removal in a more general sense.
Based on the amount of removal people seem to run in casual EDH, it seems like a lot of people might not be aware of this, but if one of your opponents wins the game, your odds of winning that game actually drop quite precipitously; you might have a bit of a hard time pulling off that W.
So, seeing as how this is Snail’s Guide to Winning an EDH Game, it is my duty to tell you that preventing your opponents from winning the game is an important concern that you shouldn’t take lightly.
However, despite my smartassery, I understand why people tend to run low removal counts.
Let’s assume for a moment that we’re thinking about the archetypal removal spell: a spell that destroys a spooky thing on our opponent’s board that may or may not lead to our demise if left unchecked.

#52
Meta CommentaryJul 20242,190 words in transcript

How to Decode EDHRec Synergy Data

The Hidden Lore of an EDHRec Page
We argue that EDHRec's percentage display hides how cards are grouped into archetypes.By selecting indicator cards from high synergy lists and using filters, you can estimate the sizes and overlaps of different deck types.Three commanders are analyzed to reveal distinct player groups, helping builders understand card inclusion and power level.
Use this in your deckIdentify two high-synergy cards representing opposite strategies, then filter EDHRec for decks containing both to estimate archetype overlap.
BlueBlackGreenComboMidrangeCountersEDHREC DataPower LevelSynergyBudgetMeta Analysis
Principle
  • EDHRec's percentage inclusion flattens the relationship between cards, hiding whether they appear in the same or different decks.
  • High synergy cards that are disproportionately played can serve as indicator cards for distinct archetypes.
Evidence
  • Francisco/Malcolm decks: 58% run Thoracle combo, 36% run midrange pirates, and only 7% run both.
  • Skeleton Ship decks split into commander-ability, proliferate, and skeleton tribal, with the tribal group being most distinct.
  • Aros Lagoon Mystic page lacks clear clusters, showing many small strategies like turtles and untap, making interpretation difficult.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Select a high-synergy card from each suspected archetype and filter for decks containing both to calculate overlap.
  • Use the filter tool to exclude indicator cards and discover unexpected themes like pirates in Skeleton Ship.
Limitation
  • The method depends on choosing representative indicator cards, which can miss smaller archetypes.
  • Overlap percentages are only estimates based on the chosen cards and may not reflect true deck distributions.
Example
  • In Skeleton Ship, excluding the main indicator cards revealed a hidden theme of pirates and ship-themed cards.
More quotes
  1. EDH Rec is a fascinating tool that has grown to monolithic stature eclipsing most other resources in the EDH ecosystem.
  2. Displaying cards by percent inclusion in decks flattens a lot of information about the decks they're played.
  3. Almost 60% of Francisco Malcolm decks run Thassa's Oracle and Demonic Consultation, an instant win combo requiring a mere two cards and three mana.
  4. Ramirez and Hostage Taker are both fun cards but they're decidedly not mana well spent in a cEDH Thoracle deck.
  5. The skeleton ship page was fascinating for the opposite reason as the Francisco Malcolm one. This EDH Rec page looks something like a tour of low power EDH.
  6. The process of making this piece and returning to the glorious EDH Rec page has only increased my amount of love for all my bone homies out there living their best skeleton ship lives.
  7. EDH Rec is a good tool but it's not one you can go on autopilot.
  8. No matter how snappy the UI is, the stuff you're seeing isn't magical. It's just data slurry.
  9. One of the biggest pitfalls I've seen EDH Rec users fall into is when they add cards that look good without thinking about what deck those cards belong in or how many of a single card type belong in a deck. This results in decks which have some amount of synergy in theory but which in practice feel disjointed and clunky, containing a Frankenstein-like mishmash of components from various decks.
  10. I'd advocate spending an extra moment trying to parse what specific types of deck are actually being built with the different cards.
Full transcript
What Percent Inclusion Hides

EDHRec is a fascinating tool that has grown to monolithic stature, eclipsing most other resources in the EDH ecosystem, and this is not for no reason.
A neatly organized, ordered list of what cards other people play is a useful tool; other people put thought and effort into crafting decks, and you get a summary of that effort delivered directly to your eyes via a snappy UI.
However, it’s not a perfect tool, due to a variety of factors.
In the first Magic: The Gathering video I posted to this channel, I made a point to discuss some downsides of EDHRec that I hadn’t seen mentioned before, and while it’s a cool video that you should definitely go watch, the contents of that video aren’t what I want to talk about today.
Instead, I want to talk about a point brought up in the comments to that video: that displaying cards by percent inclusion in decks flattens a lot of information about the decks they’re played in.
If there are three cards each played in a third of decks, it’s difficult to tell whether they’re all played in the same third of decks, if they’re all played in different decks, or something in between.
This is a flaw pretty inherent to the construction of the website; it’s not some emergent problem that popped up and is awaiting a solution.
But it got me thinking: is there anything that users of the website can do to adapt to this fact?
When I look at a card on EDHRec, can I figure out what it’s doing there, what other cards go with it, and what all that says about the decks it’s played in, or even the players that built those decks?
The answer to this question is complicated, but as a process it’s taken me down a series of rabbit holes as useful as they are bizarre, and I want to share that journey with you today.

Francisco and Malcolm: A Combo-Midrange Split

As a fairly straightforward example, in late December I was looking to make an aggressive partner commander deck, and I was intrigued by the commander pairing of Malcolm, Keen-Eyed Navigator and the freshly printed Francisco, Foul Marauder.
The appeal to me was strong: two low-cost partners, one of which ramps and the other of which filters, which can be paired with a further slate of low-cost pirates to enable all sorts of shenanigans.
EDHRec had a mere 310 decks for this freshly minted pairing, so a question we can ask is: who else jumped on these two as a worthwhile commander pair?
Looking at the ten high synergy cards, the ones disproportionately run in this commander pair compared to the color as a whole, offers a clear picture.
Almost 60% of Francisco/Malcolm decks run Thassa’s Oracle and Demonic Consultation, an instant-win combo requiring a mere two cards and three mana.
Looking further, we can see a bunch more high-powered staples also at around 60% usage, but scroll down a bit beyond that and we can see a very different type of card at approximately 35% usage: a couple four-mana pirates with abilities useful in slower, more midrangey decks.
Ramirez DePietro and Hostage Taker are both fun cards, but they’re decidedly not mana well spent in a cEDH Thoracle deck.
For this reason, I’m going to call these two cards, along with Thoracle, “indicator cards”, cards that give you a rough idea on the upper bound of players playing a particular type of deck.
If I use the indicator cards of Thoracle and Ramirez, I can start to construct a Venn diagram with a combo circle containing 58% of decks and a midrange circle containing 36% of decks.
I wouldn’t expect a ton of overlap, and indeed a search with EDHRec’s filter feature plus some quick math tells me that there’s about 7% of decks that run both cards and about 13% of decks that run neither card.

Now, what does this sort of analysis tell me?
Well, my first takeaway from seeing the 58% Thoracle play rate was “goddamn, I guess I’m on the right track if I’m looking to make a high-powered deck.” To be clear, Thassa’s Oracle plus Demonic Consultation will be run in some percentage of any deck with blue and black in it, but 58% is a lot of decks.
While it’s not a wincon I’d go for, the fact that such a high percentage of people building Francisco/Malcolm run Thoracle communicates to me, somebody who’s building on a budget, that I’m probably getting a lot of bang for my 84 cents from the command zone.
For my part, I ended up including none of the indicator cards I mentioned in the deck I ended up building, because I’m a unique and special boy.

Skeleton Ship: Proliferate, Skeletons, and Memes

For another example, about a year ago I was building a Skeleton Ship deck.
It was intended to be a glacially slow proliferate deck, and it had all the fixins: the Parallax enchantments, some impressively slow wincons, and the original storage lands that enter tapped and don’t tap for non-stored mana.
The commander was a card that could be played as a mediocre removal engine, but most games it just sits there as a meme, a perfect encapsulation of the slow mass of nothing I was doing.
So what do other Skeleton Ship players build?
Looking at the high synergy cards, the top five are all cards that pair with Skeleton Ship’s ability, so already other people are deviating from my game plan by actually caring about the commander.
Next up, in roughly 50% of decks, are a bunch of proliferate cards, which seems sensible enough to me, that’s what I’m doing.
After that, in about 30% of decks, are a bunch of skeletons, and in about 15% of decks are a handful of questionable but undeniably skeleton-themed cards.
Excellent.
Okay, so these are going to be the three archetypes, with our indicator cards for each being Freed from the Real, Thrummingbird, and Reassembling Skeleton.

We can now make a triple Venn diagram and fill it out by simply plugging in every combination of these three cards in EDHRec’s filter tool.
Upon doing this, the first thing I noticed was that “cares about commander ability” and “cares about counters” seem almost like one diverse super-category, whereas skeletons were definitely more distinct.
If you’re a freak like me, you’re probably also wondering: what is that other 15%?
I did another filter excluding these indicator cards plus an additional one for each category to account for slight variances in card choice, and the result is pirates and cards with ships on them.
Respect.
Honestly, the Skeleton Ship page was fascinating for the opposite reason as the Francisco/Malcolm one.
This EDHRec page looks something like a tour of low-power EDH: there’s the players who are earnestly building around the glorious card known as Skeleton Ship, the players who took it as a jumping-off point for some strange quest into the unknown, the people who are here for a commander for their janky skeletons deck, and the people who just appreciate the card for all its skeleton and ship goodness.
I picked out Skeleton Ship as the commander for a bad meme deck, and it looks like I’m in good company with that fact; but the interesting thing is how many different types of meme are present here.
Honestly, the process of making this piece and returning to the glorious EDHRec page has only increased my amount of love for all my bone homies out there living their best Skeleton Ship lives.

Arixmethes, Slumbering Isle: Turtles, Gates, and Confusion

For our third example, let’s talk about Arixmethes, Slumbering Isle.
This page has a lot more decks than the previous two I mentioned, and yet somehow it’s honestly the most confusing EDHRec page I’ve ever been on.
High up on the page we get some stuff that makes sense: things that tap and untap the commander, and lands-matter cards.
Sure, that makes sense; cards that lead to lands entering tapped are good when the commander has them come in untapped.
We also get Villainous Wealth, okay, yeah, I guess if you’re playing a lot of lands you may as well have an appropriately colored outlet.
And then you get down to stuff like King Macar, the Gold-Cursed.
Are people playing him because Arixmethes has a “may tap” ability and thus lets you get an inspired trigger a turn early?
Maybe, but there’s also a lot of tap/untap synergy sprinkled throughout the page, not enough for me to get a confident idea of what people are doing, but certainly a handful seemingly present in a hair over 20% of decks.
Next up is tribal turtle decks, and for this we can see a bunch of turtles played in roughly 15% of decks, with about half that many decks also running a bunch of questionable turtle-themed cards.
We’ll call these “turtle players” and “dedicated turtle players.” Finally, let’s talk Gates.
The indicator cards I’m going to use here are Baldur’s Gate, Nine-Fingers Keene, and Maze’s End, cards played in a decent portion of decks that you’d probably only want in a Gates deck.

Now, these play rates confuse me a little, because in my opinion as a professional Gates enjoyer, Baldur’s Gate is a much worse card than these other two for early game purposes; it’s a waste in a three-color deck, and the limitation to three colors means that even if you run every single in-color gate, only a third of your lands will be Gates.
I don’t love Keene either; its triggers will whiff more than a quarter of the time in a three-color deck, but at least it’s a decently statted creature.
Maze’s End is my personal favorite in an Arixmethes deck: it comes in untapped and can find you an untapped gate immediately, and can be paired nicely with extra land play effects, and that’s not even the strongest way to use Maze’s End in an Arixmethes deck.
I recently posted a video to my Patreon discussing what ended up being my highest-effort deck, and you can stick around to the end for a preview of that.

Why This Detective Work Matters

On some level, these adventures are cool simply because I enjoy them; I enjoy a good puzzle, I enjoy putting myself in somebody else’s brain and asking why they decided to run a particular card.
In theory, EDHRec has an archetypes feature that gives breakdowns on different deck types, but it’s not very good.
“Wow, you’re telling me there are Francisco/Malcolm decks that involve Pirates, treasure, and sacrificing?
That’s crazy, I never would have guessed.” So, in absence of a feature like this, I enjoy the challenge of snooping around and figuring things out.
However, this is also the part of we where I tell you that this goofy little thing I like to do while using EDHRec is actually a fantastic exercise that will prevent all sorts of mistakes down the line.
One of the biggest pitfalls I’ve seen EDHRec users fall into is when they add cards that look good without thinking about what deck those cards belong in, or how many of a single card type belong in a deck.
This results in decks which have some amount of synergy in theory, but which in practice feel disjointed and clunky, containing a Frankenstein-like mishmash of components from various decks.
For this reason, I’d advocate spending an extra moment trying to parse what specific types of deck are actually being built with the different cards; even just keeping in mind that there are different decks doing different things within a given commander will likely be a great help to newer players.
EDHRec is a good tool, but it’s not one you can go on autopilot with.
To echo the conclusion of the EDHRec Effect, you should try to keep in mind where the cards you’re looking at are coming from, and that no matter how snappy the UI is, the stuff you’re seeing isn’t magical, it’s just data slurry.

Preview: Breaking Maze’s End with Arixmethes

With that said, thanks for watching, and here’s a preview of a bonus video I made about the Arixmethes deck I ended up building.
I had begun to mentally catalog this deck idea as a failure when I once again stumbled upon Maze’s End, and this time the gears really got turning.
Fundamentally, the issue with Maze’s End is that it’s both incredibly visible and glacially slow; that slowness is due both to the extremeness of the win condition and the requirement of running a ton of taplands, downsides which exist in a state of tension.
Every turn of playing a non-gate land means an extra Maze’s End activation that’s required down the road.
However, these downsides can be mostly eliminated by simply playing all of the gates at once through an effect like Scapeshift or Reshape the Earth.

#53
DeckbuildingJul 20242,996 words in transcript

Optimal Land Counts for Commander Decks

Your Manabase Deserves Better
Many Commander players treat lands as a necessary evil and run too few.Frank Karsten's simulation model suggests 37 to 42 lands for optimal curve consistency.High-cost draw commanders and multicolor decks require even higher land counts unless using expensive mana bases.
Use this in your deckStart by running at least 37 lands in your Commander deck, and add more if your commander draws cards or your deck needs multiple colors.
Mana BaseDeck ConsistencyCurveBudgetDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • Many players treat lands as a necessary evil and aim to run the minimum possible, which leads to deck-building mistakes.
Evidence
  • Frank Karsten's EDH-specific simulation model recommends 37 to 42 lands for a smooth curve.
  • The model assumes the commander is an extra non-land card always available and optimizes for cast mana values across turns.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Commanders that draw cards, especially those costing four or more mana, require higher land counts to ensure they can be cast reliably.
  • Multicolor decks need more lands if not using expensive fetch and shock lands to consistently hit color requirements.
  • Medium-to-large draw spells can support higher land densities because the draw helps find action cards.
Limitation
  • Karsten's model treats all cards as identical apart from mana value, ignoring unique effects that could influence land needs.
  • Players often struggle to cut non-land cards, leading them to accept suboptimal low land counts.
Example
  • A Rona deck with many cheap legendary creatures can run fewer black sources and slightly fewer lands if it can guarantee casting Rona on turn two.
  • We run few fetches and shock due to budget and compensates by running more lands.
More quotes
  1. If your hand was missing something else you find vital to your game plan, an artifact, a creature, a piece of ramp, you wouldn't say 'Wow, this opening hand would be great if it had an artifact in it' because that's a silly statement to make.
  2. I think a lot of people have a mindset of lands being a sort of necessary evil, a card type that dilutes the gas of a deck and one that should be played in the lowest quantity that a player can get away.
  3. I suspect the reason a lot of people end up running low land counts isn't because they've done a lot of field testing and decided that they need to be drawing more gas. It's because they get finished adding cards to the deck and feel attached to the non-land stuff they put.
  4. If your commander costs four or more and draws cards, not only should you not run less lands than you otherwise would, you actually should run more.
  5. That's a draw spell you'll have every single game, and your only barrier between yourself and those sweet, sweet cards is drawing enough lands or ramp to get there.
  6. The annoying truth is if you want to run a lot of colors and you don't want to run a whole lot of tap lands in order to actually get the colors you need, you either need to spend hundreds of dollars on a mana base full of fetches and shocks or you need to run a lot of lands.
  7. If you're willing to spend money you can run a lot of colors with basically no downside, and if you aren't willing to spend money you can't do.
  8. I don't run many fetches and shocks in my decks due to their cost, and I honestly find the effectiveness of them to be a little upsetting.
  9. 30 symbols in your mana base will give you great access to all but the most symbol intensive cards. 25 symbols will give you great access to single symbol cards and decent access to double symbol cards. 20 will give you decent access to single symbol cards and mediocre access to more symbols.
  10. If you're like me and you can't justify that purchase, you should either play fewer colors or run more lands.
Full transcript
The Nine-Card Hand and the Land Mindset

A now infamous sentence in my playgroup is, “Wow, this opening hand would be great if it had more lands.” Whenever it’s said, either me or my friend Mr. Hans will sit up straight, get a devious glint in our eye, and say, “Yeah, nine-card hands are pretty good.” This is a joke that will never stop finding funny, but the frequency with which people express the original sentiment also speaks to a broader point about lands: despite their centrality to the game, a lot of people don’t really see lands as a part of their deck in any meaningful sense.
If your hand was missing something else you find vital to your game plan, like an artifact, a creature, or a piece of ramp, you wouldn’t say, “Wow, this opening hand would be great if it had an artifact in it,” because that’s a silly statement to make; an opening hand without an artifact in an artifact deck is a bad hand, not a mildly flawed good hand.
To take this a step further, I think a lot of people have a mindset of lands being a sort of necessary evil, a card type that dilutes the “gas” of a deck and one that should be played in the lowest quantity that a player can get away with.
I don’t necessarily want to invalidate the desire to draw gas, but I do think that what I just described is a mindset that will naturally lead to deck-building mistakes.

A Framework for Land Count: Frank Karsten’s Formula

So to start with, I want to lay out a basic framework for the approximate number of lands that you should be running in your deck.
I’ve seen a lot of numbers paraded around by various people as general reference points, and to be frank, I think a single number does a poor job of answering the question.
Speaking of Frank, let’s start there.
Frank Karsten has done an admirable job of answering questions of mana and mana fixing, and his analysis on the subject of land count is as good a place to start as any for the topic.
The format-agnostic version of Karsten’s land count analysis goes something like this: he takes a whole bunch of match data from across a couple years of online MTG events and excludes any deck that didn’t have more wins than losses.
He then does a regression analysis on the remaining decks, which sounds complicated but is actually pretty simple in concept.
A regression analysis basically just cares about how a bunch of predictor variables correlate with an output variable.
In this case, the question Karsten’s analysis is asking is how do mana curve, quantity of cheap draw spells, and quantity of cheap ramp spells affect how many lands a successful deck plays?
The result of this question is then turned into a formula that can be used to determine the number of lands that a given deck should play, and as a starting point I like this.
I’d argue that average mana value, as well as quantities of cheap draw and ramp, should be major factors used to decide land count in a deck, and I’ve left a link to that article in the description if you want to give Karsten’s formula a try.

Karsten’s EDH Model and Its Assumptions

However, since it was constructed from 60-card deck data, Karsten’s analysis here misses out on some specific factors involved in EDH, and he himself is aware of this.
Another article of his lays out a different set of criteria for mana curve, ramp, and land count in an EDH-specific context.
Due to the absence of good data on EDH games, this article obtains its findings through simulated games against a passive opponent, with the model optimizing for the highest quality curve out.
This quality is defined here as a single number being composed of the mana value of each card played, repeated on each turn thereafter to simulate the value of having played a spell that influences the game.
The model factors in a number of Commander-specific assumptions, such as the free mulligan and the commander being effectively an extra non-land card available at all times, and honestly, I like this model a lot, not because it’s perfect but because it’s simple and specific.
It gives land count, ramp count, and mana curve recommendations based purely on the goal of curving out.
Cards aren’t “Necropoent” and “Rewind” and “Aquaa”; they’re hypothetical pieces of cardboard with mana value three, four, and two respectively, and to assume that they’re different from each other beyond this information would introduce an unreasonable level of complexity to the model.
So as with the land count formula, it’s a good starting point.
It offers some neat recommendations on optimal land counts, mana rock counts, and mana curve, and comes to the conclusion that for purposes of curving out properly, decks would be well served to run at least 37 lands and as many as 42 in some cases.
This is good information, but the simplicity and specificity of the model mean that to get the full picture we need to venture a bit beyond its scope.

Commanders That Draw: Lower or Raise Your Land Count?

Karsten’s assumptions themselves offer a neat place to start thinking, and I’m most interested in his assumption about the commander.
His model assumes it to be effectively a non-land card like any other, but one you have available all the time.
This is a decent shortcut for purposes of putting together a model, but the commander of a deck can have a wildly important impact on the curve and desired land count of the deck beyond just occupying a mana slot.
Specifically, there are commanders that offer some form of draw or filtering, and these will influence the number of lands a deck is looking to run.
If you’re building a Rona deck with lots of one- and two-cost legendaries, as long as you run enough blue sources to guarantee playing Rona on turn two, your black sources and land count as a whole can be a bit lower since you know you’re going to turn through a lot of cards on turns three and four.
Similarly, a Yar deck can go a bit heavier on ramp and a bit lighter on lands, knowing that as long as it’s able to get up to Yar, it’ll have its next couple land drops for free.
These examples make sense as there are cases when a player will have guaranteed access to a high-quality tool for drawing into additional lands.
However, I also want to talk about a different type of commander that I think will be more controversial, that being the high-costed commander that draws a lot of cards.
If your commander costs four or more and draws cards, not only should you not run less lands than you otherwise would; you actually should run more.
That’s a draw spell you’ll have every single game, and your only barrier between yourself and those sweet, sweet cards is drawing enough lands or ramp to get there.
Ditto if the deck is very heavy on draw that costs a lot of mana: medium to large draw will cut through that slightly higher density of lands with no problem, so running a higher land count won’t hurt you; it will only serve to lessen the chance of screw.
And I’ll go a step further here.

The Temptation of Low Land Counts

I suspect the reason a lot of people end up running low land counts isn’t because they’ve done a lot of field testing and decided that they need to be drawing more gas; it’s because they get finished adding cards to the deck and feel attached to the non-land stuff they put in.
Making cuts can be a somewhat painful and difficult process, so if EDH Fan99 tells you that you can run 33 lands, a lot of people will take that advice very eagerly, and then down the road they’ll curse the luck gods when they get stuck on four lands in their deck whose average mana value is 4.5.
I say this with a heart full of empathy; I’ve been there.
But sometimes you just got to bite the bullet and make those cuts.

Colors, Budgets, and the Trade-Offs of Manabases

Another factor that influences land count is colors, both the number of colors and the symbol density.
Well, kind of.
See, this is a subject that’s a bit harder to solve with big-brained deck-building tips.
The annoying truth is, if you want to run a lot of colors and you don’t want to run a whole lot of tap lands in order to actually get the colors you need, you either need to spend hundreds of dollars on a mana base full of fetches and shocks, or you need to run a lot of lands.
I do not say this from sitting on top of my throne of paper-made copies of “brisk study.” I don’t run many fetches and shocks in my decks due to their cost, and I honestly find the effectiveness of them to be a little upsetting.
If you’re willing to spend money, you can run a lot of colors with basically no downside, and if you aren’t willing to spend money, you can’t do that.
But I’m not here to get upset; I’m here to give you actionable advice.
That advice is, if you can justify buying fetches and shocks, or if you feel like proxying, you should run them.
If you’re like me and you can’t justify that purchase, you should either play fewer colors or run more lands.

A Budget Manabase Case Study

As an example of this principle, I built out four manabases for a hypothetical four-color deck with blue and green being dominant colors.
The first one was 30 lands, $300, and a 10% tap lands allowance, a decidedly solid mana base if you’re okay with the low overall count.
Symbols-wise, it’s 22 each for green and blue and 16 each for red and black, pretty good numbers for coming from just 30 lands, not even including any sort of ramp.
The second one was 30 lands again, but $50 this time and a one-third tap land allowance.
The symbols for this deck are 20 each for green and blue and 13 each for red and black, definitely worse but not horrendously so; as long as you’re okay with tap lands, a well-built ramp suite should be able to patch up those numbers to something that ends up being pretty reliable.
The third one was 30 lands and $50 once again, but going back to the 10% tap land allowance.
This is where things get painful: 16 symbols each for green and blue and 10 each for red and black.
This is rough.
Our core colors aren’t even well supported, and 10 is a low enough count for our off colors that we’ll quite frequently be missing one or both of them.
However, if we dial the land count up to 40 with equivalent per-card budget, our count jumps back up to 20 for the core colors and 13 for the off colors, the same as the more tap-land-oriented 30-land option.
I should reiterate that this still isn’t amazing, but it’s a good demonstration of the trade-offs involved with building a mana base.
I could go into specific cycles of lands and when they should or shouldn’t be used, but we i long enough already, so I won’t.

Counting Symbols: How Many of Each Color You Need

Instead, I want to talk more about the mana symbols and how to know how many of each you’ll need.
This is another area where lots of people have posited various counts and formulas and such like, and to start with I’m going to stick with a familiar face.
Frank Karsten also posted an article offering an analysis of the number of each symbol a deck should run in order to be able to consistently play a card when they have enough total mana, with consistently being 89 plus m percent of the time, where m is the mana value of the card.
One application of this formula and resulting table would be to apply it to every single card in your deck and then build a mana base that gets as close as possible to satisfying this for every single card.
This works in faster formats where most or all cards want to be played on curve, but it works a bit less well at EDH.
I do think it’s appropriate to apply this strict 89 plus m percent standard for pivotal cards you want to land on curve, such as a commander or ramp spell.
However, for stuff you need a little later, such as high drops, combo pieces, or removal, you can put them behind a substantially lower symbol count as long as you’ve got enough draw and ramp to help you get the needed symbols by the time you need those cards.
This doesn’t necessarily mean you should totally let your guard down on that sort of stuff, though; certainly if you’re running a lot of colors or if you’re running symbol-heavy cards, it’s worth taking a careful look at your manabase to make sure things will work out.
I recommend checking out the chart in Frank Karsten’s article, which I linked in the description, but as a bit of shorthand: 30 symbols in your mana base will give you great access to all but the most symbol-intensive cards; 25 symbols will give you great access to single-symbol cards and decent access to double-symbol cards; 20 will give you decent access to single-symbol cards and mediocre access to more symbols; and 15 symbols will give you okay access to single-symbol cards but will be unreliable beyond that.

Deck Examples: Applying the Principles

For an example of one of my mana bases, my Maria deck runs 37 lands with 23 red symbols and 22 green symbols.
The deck is pretty low-curved and has a three-mana commander that ramps, so the lands and symbols are mostly to make sure that I can drop the commander on curve consistently.
When I run the deck through Frank Karsten’s land count formula, it’s right on the money, which makes sense to me since the commander ramps, which would make me okay with fewer lands, but also draws, which would make me fine with more.
For my Gissa deck, I’m running 41 total lands with 31 green sources and 22 black sources.
This count might sound high, but it’s totally justifiable; it’s a high-curved deck, it has card draw in the command zone, and its ideal mana curve is Elf on one, commander on two.
Having mana issues in my first three turns will dramatically slow down the deck as it won’t be able to get its draw and ramp online, so I’m pretty comfortable with a high land count.
In fact, Karsten’s formula generates an even higher count of 43 and some change, which might partially be because the deck’s average mana value of 4.71 would likely be an outlier in Karsten’s data set, but there are legitimate arguments for adding lands to the deck regardless.
A general pattern across my decks is that even when they’re relatively low-curved, I still generally run a high-30s quantity of lands in them as long as they can draw a lot of cards.
Mana screw is optional, and if you run a deck with big draw, running more lands is your way of unticking that box.

Conclusion: Be Intentional with Your Lands

I’ve covered a lot of stuff here, and I’ve meandered a little bit, so let me clarify what I’m actually trying to say here.
For one, you should be intentional with the number of lands you run and keep in mind that the optimal number might be higher than the sorts of numbers that get thrown around on the internet.
Frank Karsten’s formula for the ideal count is a good start, though, since it

#54
DeckbuildingJul 20241,072 words in transcript

Slot Compression: Fitting More Functions into Fewer Cards

The Way to Fit More Cards into your Cards
We explain slot compression, the practice of choosing cards that fulfill multiple roles to fit more tools into a deck without sacrificing essential functions.It shows how this applies across midrange, combo, and synergistic strategies, and recommends using MDFC lands to boost land counts.The key caution is that smoothness should not replace substantive win conditions.
Use this in your deckAdd MDFCs or utility lands to your deck until your land count reaches at least 35.
MidrangeComboStaxDeck ConsistencyMana BaseWin ConditionsCard EvaluationDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • Aim for each non-land card to serve at least two distinct purposes, such as draw, removal, or board presence, to maximize slot efficiency.
Evidence
  • Decks using slot compression can include more interaction and lands, leading to fewer non-games and increased consistency.
  • Multi-role creatures in midrange and combo decks provide both threat density and utility, reducing the need for dedicated spell slots.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Swap basic lands for MDFCs to raise your functional land count without cutting spells, aiming for 35 to 40 total lands depending on curve.
  • In tribal or power-matters decks, ensure your draw and removal sources share the relevant type to free slots for more synergy pieces.
Limitation
  • A deck that maximizes slot compression may still fail without enough explicit win conditions; after building, verify you have multiple reliable ways to end the game.
Example
  • In the Rose deck, creatures that draw cards or force discards serve as the draw engine, and Blood Artist effects on finishers deter board wipes.
  • The Gly deck relies on high-cost creatures that are removal spells and draw engines, eliminating the need for separate wincon cards.
More quotes
  1. The idea of slot compression is basically this: you can fit more stuff into your deck, more things of each category like finishers or draw or removal, if you pick out and play cards that do multiple things.
  2. My deck cashes in a hefty 20 or so slots up front for ramp, but it is rewarded for this with a creature base consisting of powerful removal and draw spells stapled to 5/5s and 8/8s and 10/10s, which means that the deck ends up being able to win games without a single dedicated wincon slot.
  3. By running stax effects that are also creatures, these decks are able to heavily oppress their opponents' game plans while still having a board state to block and pressure life totals.
  4. If you can make cards needed for your combo also valuable in their own right in the deck, the combo will feel much less space-gobbling.
  5. If you're building a Goreclaw deck and your deck's draw and removal mostly consists of creatures with power four or greater, suddenly you can fit a whole lot more beef into the deck while still having it feel smooth and well-rounded.
  6. I sometimes see people run land counts in the low 30s in decks that aren't that low-curved, and while I appreciate the desire to draw gas, I echo Interesting Failures' thinking here: at least do yourself a favor and throw in some MDFC or utility lands to bring your count up to 35 or 36.
  7. This goes for high-curved decks running 36 lands as well; don't be afraid to MDFC your way up to 38 or 39, or even break into the 40s.
  8. Lands are good and people often don't play enough of them in casual EDH.
  9. At its core your deck still needs to do a thing that wins.
  10. Playing smoothly and having interaction isn't a substitute for having a good way to end the game.
Full transcript

Hey folks, happy Wednesday, or Sunday, or whichever day it is you're watching this.
