I figure instead of spending days engineering the perfect 15-minute video script while I could be spending time with my family, I'm just going to spend five minutes shamelessly riffing on a concept brought up in my comment section: that being slot compression.

What Is Slot Compression?

The idea of slot compression is basically this: you can fit more stuff into your deck, more things of each category like finishers, draw, or removal, if you pick out and play cards that do multiple things.
For midrange decks, an easy way to do compression is to run decently statted creatures that do other things your deck wants to do.

Midrange Decks and Multi-Purpose Creatures

In a dirt cheap Rose deck I built a while back, the creature count is decently high, but the vast majority of creatures in the deck either draw me cards or make my opponents discard cards.
Even among the more finisher-oriented creatures that don't fit into these two categories, most are blood Iris effects, which still perform a secondary duty of discouraging board wipes; so the compression is still there.
My Gly deck is a more high-curve example of this principle.
Fun fact: if you pay a lot of mana for a creature, it will often be great for multiple things.
My deck cashes in a hefty 20 or so slots up front for ramp, but it is rewarded for this with a creature base consisting of powerful removal and draw spells stapled to 5/5s and 8/8s and 10/10s, which means that the deck ends up being able to win games without a single dedicated wincon slot.

Hate Bears and Stax Creatures

Another example of this is hate bears decks.
By running stax effects that are also creatures, these decks are able to heavily oppress their opponents' game plans while still having a board state to block and pressure life totals.
Sometimes this pressure will be pivotal to winning, such as in my Liesa deck, and other times it will serve more as an alternative wincon or as hate toward life pay; regardless, this flexibility is very useful.

Combo Decks: Pieces That Pull Double Duty

For more combo-oriented decks, one way to have plenty of space for both pieces and supporting cards is to pick cards that do both.
If you need a sac outlet for your combo, you'll naturally run into the issue that some games you won't draw one and other games you'll draw multiple.
You could run only a couple and some tutors, but you could also run a bunch of Altars in a deck with some artifact synergies, or sac outlets that get buff if you want an alternative beatdown wincon, or cards like Fanatical Devotion and Greater Good, which might just be generically good cards in some decks.
There are also combo decks looking for a lot of certain types of cards to go off, such as with a Midow Nila deck; there, a reasonable plan might be throwing in low-cost warriors you like from any color, which will further the beats and combo potential offered by the commander while also letting you have high-quality creatures.
The general gist here is that if you can make cards needed for your combo be also valuable in their own right in the deck, the combo will feel much less space-gobbling.

Synergy and Card-Type Overlap

This logic also applies for heavily synergistic decks.
If you have a deck focusing on a particular card type, such as equipments or auras, having a lot of your supporting cards also fit into those types means that your wincons will pack a ton of power, and it will also have the added benefit that conditional tutors will give you access to a good portion of your deck.
The same goes for decks that focus on a particular creature type or particular attributes: if you're building a Goreclaw deck and your deck's draw and removal mostly consists of creatures with power four or greater, suddenly you can fit a whole lot more beef into the deck while still having it feel smooth and well-rounded.

Lands and MDFCs: Don't Skimp on Mana

Finally, let's talk lands.
MDFCs, utility lands, and other dual-mode cards that get you lands are great, and I highly encourage using these.
I sometimes see people run land counts in the low 30s in decks that aren't that low-curve, and while I appreciate the desire to draw gas, I echo Interesting Failure’s thinking here: at least do your future self a favor and throw in some MDFCs or utility lands to bring your count up to 35 or 36.
This goes for high-curve decks running 36 lands as well; don't be afraid to MDFC your way up to 38 or 39, or even break into the 40s.
I plan to do a longer video about lands and mana bases sometime soon, so I'll save further discussion of the subject until then, but long story short, lands are good and people often don't play enough of them in casual EDH.

Balancing Synergy and a Path to Victory

I've discussed some different ways to compact your slots, you know, to run cards to satisfy multiple things your deck wants, and I think a logical question to ask regarding this is exactly how valuable it is to have compacted slots.
I like to think I've laid out a decent case for doing such a thing: you can run more of each supporting card type while still maintaining a solid curve to your deck, and you can run a higher land count for purposes of not getting screwed while mitigating some of the downsides of mana flood.
However, an important thing to remember is this: at its core, your deck still needs to do a thing that wins.
In the past, I've sometimes fallen deep into the hole of synergy and support cards and built a deck that runs very smoothly and feels good at the table but ultimately lacks substantive wincons and usually just succumbs to getting outscaled by somebody else.
I advocate for people to put more support cards in their decks because I think the average EDH player is on the opposite end of the snappy-to-smooth spectrum as me, but it's good to keep in the back of your head that playing smoothly and having interaction isn't a substitute for having a good way to end the game.
You should try to have both of these things in your deck.

#55
Format TheoryJul 20241,466 words in transcript

How Aggro Works in Commander: Life Pressure and Commander Damage

Aggro Ideology: A Guide to Spee-DH
We explain how aggro strategies must adapt to Commander's 40-life format.It promotes 'aggro ideology,' the practice of pressuring opponents by lowering their life totals to force conservative play.We emphasize Commander damage as a powerful tool that effectively reduces the needed damage to 21 per opponent.We categorize aggro decks into Voltron, pseudo-Voltron, and disruptive aggro, and offer practical tips on evasion, board wipe resilience, and target prioritization.
Use this in your deckAdd Selfless Spirit to your white aggro deck to protect against board wipes.
AggroVoltronStaxResilienceThreat AssessmentDeckbuilding RestrictionsStrategy
Principle
  • The core 'aggro ideology' is to keep threatening opponents at or below 15 life, where they must play conservatively and cannot safely commit to setup turns.
Evidence
  • In a 40-life format, players often neglect blockers and rely on life as a cushion, but aggressive attacks expose this vulnerability and make value engines like Phyrexian Arena detrimental.
  • Life-payment cards such as fetchlands and Dismember are nearly costless until opponents start attacking; afterward, they become risky.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Add Selfless Spirit to white aggro decks as a permanent-based answer to board wipes.
  • Run Blood Artist variants in black aggro to convert board wipes into direct damage, punishing opponents and preserving your aggression.
  • Ensure your creatures have built-in evasion or use equipment and enchantments like Wonder to bypass blockers.
Limitation
  • Aggro decks often face a social stigma in casual groups because eliminating a player early can lead to a long wait, potentially discouraging this playstyle.
  • Balancing speed and resilience is difficult: including protection spells slows the deck, but a lack of protection leaves it vulnerable to board wipes.
Example
  • A budget deck using Malagor as a commander showcases an alternative to evasion by using discard to clear blockers before attacking.
More quotes
  1. A mere 39 lightning bolts are needed, presuming each player shocks themselves with a land. Or 39 swings with a Delver of Secrets, if you prefer. Or 13 swings with an elite squad of three Delvers. Perplexing. The answer is that it doesn't work.
  2. Paying one life for one card is an even more delicious bargain than usual. These effects, worthwhile but noticeably painful in a 20-health format, are now nearly painless.
  3. Blocking isn't as necessary. decks are empowered to constantly use tap abilities, swing in for value with a Bident of Thassa effect, and eat 5 to 10 damage hits rather than blocking with an important creature.
  4. Suddenly, that Phyrexian Arena is a liability. That Dismember is close to regaining its status as a three-mana black card. Your commander might need to be a blocker.
  5. Spending a turn playing setup cards for an approaching big turn goes from an exciting proposition to a terrifying one when you might be the one to get knocked out if you skimp on blockers.
  6. Your hands are tied, your knees are quivering, and your deck was decidedly not built for this situation.
  7. My mantra in a game where somebody has a deck that is concerning and unpredictable but not necessarily totally dominant is that they should be at 15 life.
  8. When somebody is at 15 life, they have to play much more conservatively. Having safe setup turns isn't a given, and they're generally one round of attacks away from death if they start doing concerning things.
  9. Aggro ideology: this is not the beginning and end of what aggro means in EDH, but simply a phrase I use to gesture at the benefits of having opponents who are scared about their life total.
  10. If your deck is commander-based, you're playing a 21-health format instead of the much more challenging 40-health format. Neat.
Full transcript
Conventional Aggro
Why It Fails in Commander
What 40 Life Really Means
Aggro Ideology
Commander Damage and Deck Types
Creature-Based Aggro: Goals and Challenges
Speed and Resilience
Target Prioritization

#56
DeckbuildingJul 20242,653 words in transcript

Building Combo Decks for Commander

EDH’s Combo Conundrum
Combo decks are a healthy part of Commander metas because they can check greedy value strategies.Pure combo decks that rely on a single combo are often too fragile and uninteractive for multiplayer.We advise building combo decks with alternate win conditions and ensuring combos blend naturally with the deck's overall game plan.
Use this in your deckAdd an alternate win condition to your combo deck to avoid folding to a single piece of interaction.
ComboWin ConditionsInteractionFormat HealthSynergyDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • Combo decks break conventional Magic rules by winning regardless of board or life, which can police slow value decks.
  • EDH's multiplayer nature and 120 life total make pure combo decks more vulnerable to interaction than in 1v1.
Evidence
  • The cEDH Dargo/Thrasios deck runs multiple combo lines and a combat damage plan to beat heavy stax.
  • The casual Vohdro deck integrates combos through its natural mutate and equipment synergies.
Deckbuilding Application
  • When a combo is central, build redundancy and protection to consistently execute it against light resistance.
  • When a combo is an add on, use cards the deck already wants to tutor for or play.
Limitation
  • A sudden infinite combo in an otherwise fair deck creates jarring gameplay and frustrates opponents.
Example
  • The modern Splinter Twin deck adapted for EDH often fails because it lacks backup plans after disruption.
More quotes
  1. Combo holds an interesting position within the landscape of Magic: The Gathering. Is it a type of deck? A type of win condition? A flavor of play style? Yes, it is indeed these things and probably many more.
  2. The spooky scary version is a two or three card combo that wins on the spot, such as Splinter Twin Pestermite.
  3. A lot of players have a negative association with it, and I think it's useful to explore why that might be.
  4. It's pretty common for combo decks to feel uninteractive and like they win out of nowhere.
  5. You can be doing your own game plan and watching with dread as your opponent spends very little resources on board state and interaction, instead relentlessly digging their way toward a one turn win.
  6. A lot of combo decks break the rules of the game as understood by most players. They throw this playbook in the toilet.
  7. The fact that Commander is a format with 40 health per player and three opponents rather than one encourages some styles of play which are too risky in 20 health 1v1 formats.
  8. A game where everyone always has big piles of value generating permanents and where turns involve five minutes of abilities and triggers with little movement or strategy or actual action isn't one I enjoy finding myself.
  9. Pure combo actually works better in conventional formats, and it is indeed very common for combo decks in these formats to have no other win conditions.
  10. If you put a two card infinite combo in your low power midrange deck, your deck is going to be a low power midrange deck 90% of the time and an unprotected infinite combo deck 10% of the time. That's going to give your playgroup some serious whiplash.
Full transcript

Picture this: it's an average Thursday night, and you're just doing your thing, that thing being playing your groovy blue-red combo deck in a game of Commander with all your cool friends.
You're out there drawing the cards, making the plays, living your best life.
The pivotal moment fast approaches; a pesky Thalia hits the board.
Not to fear, you've got a counter.
Your Kiki-Jiki meets a Dissipate, and though the ensuing counter war is lost, you've got a backup plan.
You throw down that Splinter Twin.
What follows is those dreaded words: “in response, it’s a removal spell.” Suddenly this game has become a lot less cozy.
What’s the backup plan, champ?
You keep cycling through your deck, finding plenty of draw and mana but nothing much of use.
Things are desperate.
Where do you even go from here?
Well, you don’t go from here.

Defining Combo

I mean, combo holds an interesting position within the landscape of Magic: The Gathering.
Is it a type of deck, a type of win condition, a flavor of play style?
Yes, it is indeed these things and probably many more.
The basic gist of what combo is is exactly what it sounds like: a combination of cards that together do something much stronger.
The spooky, scary version is a two- or three-card combo that wins on the spot, such as Splinter Twin and Pestermite, but there also exist infinite mana and infinite life combos that need additional cards to win, as well as less game-ending combos that only win situationally or even just generate a lot of value.
Though combo is wide-ranging and has undoubtedly been part of the game for basically its whole existence, a lot of players have a negative association with it, and I think it's useful to explore why that might be.

The Negative Perceptions

To start with, it's pretty common for combo decks to feel uninteractive and like they win out of nowhere.
You can be doing your own game plan and watching with dread as your opponent spends very little resources on board state and interaction, instead relentlessly digging their way toward a one-turn win until either they find it or you reduce their life to zero.
This kind of Magic isn't particularly fun for anybody; it's just another kind of solitaire.
The other thing I've seen complaints about is the fact that, in a way, a lot of combo decks break the rules of the game as understood by most players.
Games that involve conventional aggro, midrange, and control decks feature a common language: board presence is a thing to be fought over, and cards in hand and life total exist as resources to be taken into consideration and leveraged.
By contrast, combo decks throw this playbook in the toilet.
They operate using additional, more subtle resources, and they can win even when they are behind in board state, life total, and hand size.

Now, there are a lot of things to be said about these points, but I'll keep it to this for now.
The volatility and uninteractivity of combo decks can indeed be negatives, and I will be talking about how to minimize them; however, breaking the conventional rules of the game can be a good thing, particularly in a format like EDH.

Why Combo Can Be a Good Thing

The fact that Commander is a format with 40 health per player and three opponents rather than one encourages some styles of play which are too risky in 20-health, 1v1 formats.
These empowered styles tend to be slower and greedier than a lot of what you find in other formats.
In mid-power EDH, it's very common to see decks that pile on heaps upon heaps of creature-based value, and then you get decks like my Gissa list, calling card “Midrange Crusher 5000,” that make it their whole goal to demolish these sorts of decks by piling on an even larger amount of greed.
And though I enjoy playing Midrange Crusher 5000, I know that this environment isn't always a very dynamic one.
A game where everyone always has big piles of value-generating permanents and where turns involve five minutes of abilities and triggers with little movement, strategy, or actual action isn't one I enjoy finding myself in.
The fact that there are decks that can cut through the slow, greedy, board-centric decks is a good thing.

The Problem with Pure Combo in EDH

Now that I've laid out why I think combos and combo decks occupy a useful niche, we can ask the core question: where do they usually go wrong in EDH?
I think a lot of it comes down to the structure of the decks.
The example I gave in the intro is a deck that's basically an EDH adaptation of 2015-era Modern Splinter Twin, a pure combo deck, and the operative word for why that doesn't work so well is “pure.” So, okay, there's this weird paradoxical thing: as you get to higher and higher powers in Commander, combo becomes more and more ubiquitous, for the simple fact that everybody's got 120 health to chisel through, right?
And it takes a lot of time to fair-Magic your way through that level of meat.
But what this also means is that stax cards to slow down and break combos also become ubiquitous, much more ubiquitous than they are in a 20-health, 1v1 format.
The result is that pure combo actually works better in conventional formats, and it is indeed very common for combo decks in these formats to have no other win conditions.
By contrast, for a high-power Commander player who must always contend with the possibility of encountering a quagmire of stax effects and counterspells, it's a good idea to run alternate win conditions just to be safe.

A High-Power Example: Dargo and Thrasios

As a high-level example we can consider Dargo and Thrasios, a partner commander pair that is sometimes played in competitive EDH.
Thrasios is a beast of a card; it's a generally decent value engine and, more importantly, it's an outlet for infinite mana combos that's in the command zone.
You combo off to get your infinite mana, and then you play Thrasios and draw through your deck to find the kill.
Good stuff, Thrasios is real good.
Dargo is there to add a bunch of utility: he can be used with Life's Legacy or Greater Good to generate cards, and he can also be sacked to Neoform or Pod to grab Tidespout Tyrant for one of the deck's combos.
He also, more pertinently to the point here, is a beefy boy who can threaten your opponents, and that can be a useful thing to have in your pocket if your combo plan gets shut down somewhere along the road.
The multiple different combo win conditions, along with the backup beatdown win condition, make the deck exceedingly flexible and adaptable and help it to weather a lot of challenging situations.

Building for Fun and Lower Power

However, I hear a clamoring.
“But Alex,” I hear some of you say, “I'm not a cEDH player; I'm not going to be facing a wall of stax high enough to be an eighth wonder of the world.” And you know what, that's fair.
So for now, let's stop considering what's good and switch to considering what's fun and what works well within a somewhat lower-powered group.
For starters, I'm going to have to crush your dreams a little.
Combos are powerful and can be quite fun, but it's important that the combos be well situated within a deck.
This means that if a combo is central to the deck, the deck should be able to pull it off reasonably consistently, with the assumption of at least light resistance from your opponents.
If the combo is less central to the deck, it should blend in naturalistically with the deck, requiring mostly cards and types of setup the deck achieves through its normal game plan and not feeling wildly out of sync with the normal game plan.
If you put a two-card infinite combo in your low-power midrange deck, your deck is going to be a low-power midrange deck 90% of the time and an unprotected infinite combo deck 10% of the time.
That's going to give your play group some serious whiplash, and it's decidedly not an example of fun and dynamic gameplay.
See part two of my budget cards video if you want to hear me waffle about this topic for another 5 minutes or so.

An Elegant Execution: Vadrok Combo

For an example of an elegant execution of a lower-power combo deck, I'm going to use a deck from my friend Mr. Hans, because he's much more of a Johnny than I'll ever be.
He built a Vadrok deck; it's a toolbox mutate deck that tries to generate value through its commander and a suite of other value engines throughout the deck.
An equipment subtheme with several tutors also exists within the deck, giving it access to several reconfigure cards which offer various forms of utility, as well as the central pillar of Sunforger.
With Sunforger, the deck has access to yet a further suite of options: assorted interaction, draw spells, and burst damage, and also a couple of distinctly spicier cards.
Glorious End can put an immediate stop to a problematic combat phase or effectively counter a concerning spell, and Chance for Glory is an extra turn spell that also doubles as a protection spell.
For both cards, Angel's Grace can be played on the following turn in order to prevent the “lose the game” clause.
This is a decidedly useful tool on its own, but it is also within the interaction between these cards and the mutate mechanic that the combo exists.
Vadrok can repeat low-cost noncreature spells from the graveyard when creatures mutate onto it, and since the mutate stack counts as a single creature, a bounce spell returns the entire stack to hand.
So the combo goes something like this: get an extra turn via Chance for Glory and prevent loss on that turn via Angel's Grace, then repeat this on each subsequent extra turn by mutating three creatures onto Vadrok , one for Chance, one for Grace, and one for a bounce spell.
If you have the correct setup, this means infinite turns and infinite combat steps, swinging with a body that rocks.

Something you may note about this is that, as combos go, this one is very convoluted and difficult to achieve.
In addition to the three cards required for the combo, there is also the requirement of having 14 mana and three mutate creatures, as well as the commander.
However, this is actually okay for a couple of different reasons.
For one, Vadrok isn't a pure combo deck.
Maintaining a solid mutate stack with the commander leads to having a decent beater that deals commander damage and generates a lot of value.
The deck isn't playing at high power levels, and it can and does win games simply by hitting people, drawing cards, and holding up enough protection to prevent the stack from dying.
A common strategy for the deck is to build the stack on top of a manland, which allows it to dodge sorcery-speed removal in general and board wipes in particular, and the pivotal Sunforger boosts Vadrok's power to seven, the exact amount you need to kill a player in three hits.
It's a very real win plan with a solid shell for generating value and controlling things.
The other reason the difficulty of setting up the combo is okay is that all three of the combo pieces have their own utility, and more importantly the requirement of having a lot of mutate creatures and mana is achieved by the deck just generally doing what it wants to be doing.
Generating a lot of value leads to drawing into mutate creatures and mana rocks, and successfully protecting its ever-important Sunforger allows the deck to grab its combo pieces.
Everything needed to set up the combo is a solid goal in its own right, so simply playing the deck in ways that are generally effective also naturalistically leads into having the combo.
Sometimes the combo will get stopped, but if it does, guess what?
The deck has been doing a good job of pursuing its general goals, so you're still going to have a decent shot of winning via commander damage.

This idea of combo fitting naturally into the deck is the biggest thing I want to convey here.
A combo you run shouldn't feel abrupt or out of place; it shouldn't feel like the deck does nothing or everything depending on if it draws a certain three or four cards.
If you've got a deck with a combo as the centerpiece, you should run tutors or big piles of draw in order to get there, with control cards used as a sort of extending element to buy the deck time.
If you have a deck where it's going to take a long time to put together the combo, you should fill up space in the deck with a game plan that works concurrently with that setup process.
That can be ramping in a deck where the combo is mana intensive, or running lots of draw in a deck where the combo involves looping with a number of different cards, or running tutors in a deck where getting combo pieces together is a challenge.
And then once you've constructed a framework for piling on particular resources, you can devise how to fit in an alternative win plan that also takes advantage of those resources.
Don't trust your combo to win you the game, because guess what: removal, counters, and stax exist, and optimizing your combo deck is going to put you at power levels where increasing quantities of these roadblocks exist.

The Backup Combo in Beatdown Decks

As a final point, I want to discuss the opposite of a combo deck with an alternative beatdown win condition: that being a beatdown deck with an alternative combo win condition.
Now, this is a little more unusual, but hear me out here.
Sometimes a beatdown deck is able to get ahead on resources, but then it gets locked out of swinging for on-board damage for some reason or other.
For example, a couple years back I built a $25 Grismold, the Dreadsower deck, a fast midrange deck.
Plan A of the deck is the pseudo-Voltron beatdown plan, where the deck creates a bunch of tokens on board and then deletes said tokens in order to swing in for lethal amounts of commander damage.
You might note, however, that the deck is not running blue, and a $25 budget is too small to afford nice shoes for my Grismold.
As you might expect, there are games where Grismold kind of just gets bullied out of existence by removal.
So what else can you do with a lot of tokens dying?
Run some Blood Artist, of course.
The win plan of generate tokens, play your Konrad, and then kill the tokens isn't as effective at killing players as a pure Voltron Grismold plan, but relying on the Grizzy plan alone is a bad idea, and having a backup means that the deck can win even if it just gets a couple medium-sized Grismold swings rather than living the dream and one-tapping three players back to back.
The important point here is that both of these win conditions use the same basic resource: tokens.
So the deck doesn't really need to do anything different to have both of these win conditions, and this is the crux of things, really, at low power.

#57
DeckbuildingJul 2024917 words in transcript

Build Midrange Decks Around a Commander Solving a Weakness

A Weird Trick to Make Cool Midrange Decks
We argue for an underrated deckbuilding approach where the commander is chosen to solve a glaring weakness, allowing the rest of the deck to focus on its strengths.By using the commander to cover a specific deficiency, like early blocking or finishing power, the deck can be built greedier and more consistently.Examples include a Glissa Sunslayer battlecruiser deck and a Liesa, Shroud of Dusk hatebear deck.
Use this in your deckWhen designing a midrange deck, ask: "What is my deck's most glaring weakness, and how can my commander consistently solve it?"
WhiteBlackGreenMidrangeStaxDesign IntentBudgetDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • A commander can be used to patch a specific deck weakness, enabling the rest of the deck to be built more focused or greedy.
  • This approach differs from the conventional method of building a deck purely around a commander's synergies.
Evidence
  • The commander is a card you will have every single game, providing a reliable solution to the deck's identified hole.
  • The singleton nature of the format creates high variability; a commander that fixes a weakness reduces that inconsistency.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Identify a gap in your midrange deck, such as early defense or a way to end the game, and pick a commander that directly addresses it.
  • Free up deck slots by letting the commander handle a key function instead of dedicating many cards to it.
  • Use the commander's guaranteed availability to avoid agonizing over how to consistently fulfill a role that the commander can cover.
Example
  • A Glissa Sunslayer deck was built as a battlecruiser with ramp, removal, and massive threats, relying on Glissa for blocking and card draw.
  • A Liesa, Shroud of Dusk deck used hatebears to slow opponents, with Liesa serving as the recurring beater to break stalled boards.
More quotes
  1. You're building a 99-card singleton deck, something with a naturally high degree of variability, but you have an ace up your sleeve: a card that you will always have, every single game.
  2. However, my favorite form of this relationship is where the commander does the exact opposite of the thing that the rest of the deck is trying to do.
  3. Basically, you pick out a commander and then you build a mid-rangey deck with a glaring weakness solved by that commander.
  4. I have a top-notch blocker that's also a draw engine every single game, and that lets me use my deck slots to focus on being the greedy [deck I] truly want to be deep inside.
  5. I built a hate bear stack. I crammed as many hate bears as I possibly could into the deck, just truly an upsetting wall of little guys trying to ruin the day of my poor opponents.
  6. The cards in the deck chip in damage, prevent damage coming at me, and generally hinder my opponents in all sorts of ways, and then [Liesa] cuts through the resulting board quagmire to reduce my opponents to a paste-like homogeneous [mass].
  7. By design, it's a deck with roughly a [Liesa]-shaped hole, and I'm able to play [Liesa] for five mana anytime I want in any game I want.
  8. Running a commander that covers a particular element of the deck allows a player to build a deck that puts that element on the back burner, instead focusing all its effort on the things it knows it can succeed.
  9. This way, you can effectively just solve the problem that you know your deck will have ahead of time, instead of agonizing over how many deck slots to devote.
  10. Mid-range is often something that people look down on for being boring or bland, but with some creative usage of the rules of the format, you can build a really fun deck that exists within the mid-range realm.
Full transcript
Introduction

Howdy, folks.
My last video got a lot more attention than I was anticipating, and I have a couple of non-Magic: The Gathering videos that I’m currently doing research for, so I figured I may as well put out a short little video with a few more thoughts on the EDH format.
Today I’m going to be talking about a couple different ways of orienting a deck in relation to its commander, and one in particular that I find to be underrated.

Conventional Deck Building

To start with, let’s talk about a pretty conventional way of constructing a commander deck.
You find a commander that looks cool, find cards that synergize well with said commander, and then fill in the gaps with other things the deck needs to succeed.
This is a simple and malleable format, but there are other options out there.

Rethinking the Rules

To talk about those, a useful exercise is to think about the rules of the Commander format as if you’ve never seen them before.
You’re building a 99-card singleton deck, something with a naturally high degree of variability, but you have an ace up your sleeve: a card that you will always have, every single game.
You could make that card Krenko, Mob Boss and then play a Goblins deck, and that’s a perfectly valid plan.
But something you might notice about that plan is that the commander and the deck do basically the same thing: play goblins.

The Opposite Approach

A lot of strong decks will opt to have the commander do something tangential to the rest of the deck, tying in with some key cards but broadly offering something different in nature than the rest of the deck.
However, my favorite form of this relationship is where the commander does the exact opposite of the thing that the rest of the deck is trying to do; basically, you pick out a commander and then build a mid-rangy deck with a glaring weakness solved by that commander.

Example: Glissa Sunslayer

An example that comes to mind is a Glissa Sunslayer deck that I built several months back.
The card is a fantastic creature for three mana: an absurdly effective blocker, a draw engine, and a source of endless sadness for decks running enchantments.
The EDHREC page seems to consist mostly of decks trying to capitalize on +1/+1 counters from your cards, but I instead opted to build a hardcore battlecruiser deck.
The commander’s flexibility and daunting presence mean that I can fully forget about early game creatures and not worry as much about draw, instead jamming my deck to the brim with ramp, removal, and massive haymakers.
I have a top-notch blocker that’s also a draw engine every single game, and that lets me use my deck slots to focus on being the greedy deck that I truly want to be deep inside.

Example: Liesa

Another example of this is a Liesa deck I built recently.
When I first saw Liesa, I went, “God, that card could absolutely beat the tar out of people.” All I want in this world is to mercilessly beat my opponents with that angel.
So great, my commander is selected.
How to build a deck around it?
Well, judging by the EDHREC page, most people’s answer to that question is to put it in a lifegain deck.
Now, I don’t hate this approach; you run a bunch of cards that gain life, some lifegain synergy, and a commander that will sap life out of both you and your opponents.
It makes sense, and you could even argue that it sort of fits my template here, but that’s not the direction I went in.
Instead, I decided to build a deck that’s just dying, craving for a beater to cut through a stale board state.
I built a hatebears deck.
I crammed as many hatebears as I possibly could into the deck, just truly an upsetting wall of little guys trying to ruin the day of my poor opponents.
And the deck has ended up working exactly as intended in a good portion of games.
The cards in the deck chip in damage, prevent damage coming at me, and generally hinder my opponents in all sorts of ways, and then Liesa cuts through the resulting board quagmire to reduce my opponents to a paste-like homogenate.
By design, it’s a deck with roughly a Liesa-shaped hole, and I’m able to play Liesa for five mana anytime I want, in any game I want.

The General Formula

There’s a lot of room for variation here, but this gives a decent idea of the basic formula.
Running a commander that covers a particular element of the deck allows a player to build a deck that puts that element on the back burner, instead focusing all its effort on the things it knows it can succeed at.
This way, you can effectively just solve the problem that you know your deck will have ahead of time, instead of agonizing over how many deck slots to devote to it.
I certainly don’t like the prospect of figuring out how to kill my opponents consistently with a low- to mid-budget hatebears deck, so I just avoided that question.

Midrange Can Be Fun

Midrange is often something that people look down on for being boring or bland, but with some creative usage of the rules of the format, you can build a really fun deck that exists within the midrange realm, and I think more people should give that a try.

#58
Format TheoryJul 2023226K views

EDHRec's Data Feedback Loops Distort Deckbuilding

The EDHRec Effect
We argue that EDHRec's decklist aggregation creates a feedback loop where decks built using its recommendations are scraped back as new data, artificially inflating the popularity of already-common cards and burying less-played ones.This makes the metagame sluggish and especially amplifies questionable 'win-more' cards that rely on a commander staying on the battlefield.
Use this in your deckWhen evaluating a card with 10, 50% inclusion on EDHRec, ask: 'Would this still perform well if my commander were never allowed to stay on the battlefield?'
EDHREC DataCard EvaluationSynergyDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • When players build decks using EDHRec and then post those decks, EDHRec scrapes its own aggregated data, creating a feedback loop that skews card popularity.
  • EDHRec treats all scraped decks equally, so decks built largely from its own recommendations are treated as independent data, further entrenching the loop.
Evidence
  • A player building an Arcades deck with EDHRec might include Overgrown Battlements (95% inclusion) and post the list, which then reinforces that card's dominance in EDHRec's data.
  • Decks built using EDHRec are faster to construct, so their proportion among posted decklists balloons over time, accelerating the feedback loop.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Question any card recommended in the 10, 50% range that assumes your commander will remain on the battlefield; consider whether it performs well even without the commander.
  • Actively scroll past the top recommendations to find cards with lower play rates, as they may be overlooked gems suppressed by the feedback loop.
Limitation
  • EDHRec's feedback loop mainly distorts the status of cards in the 10, 50% inclusion range; top-staple cards are still largely reliable picks.
Example
  • In the Arcades, the Strategist example, cards like Overgrown Battlements and Shield Wall Sentinel appear prominently because they fit the defender theme, and their high percentages are self-reinforcing through the loop.
More quotes
  1. It's also a video about Magic: The Gathering, although hopefully I've made it somewhat accessible to people who haven't made the terrible mistake of playing this game.
  2. In theory, a website that aggregates deck lists allows sorting through a ton of potential card choices very efficiently. We aren't trusting the card analysis of just a single person, but rather the analysis of the collective, thereby reducing the risk of bias.
  3. Instead of getting data from independently operating deck builders, EDH Rec is receiving a regurgitation of its own aggregated data.
  4. You could also call this a win more card: a card that's bad when a player is behind or when a game is tight, but strong when a player is already ahead.
  5. Cards that didn't quite make it into the EDH Rec list or which are lower on the page get artificially tanked further.
  6. The cards already high up in the EDH Rec recommendations are bolstered regardless of how useful they actually.
  7. EDH Rec can tell people what cards are being played, but it can't tell them why or what they contribute towards a well-rounded deck.
  8. A lot of players add [interaction] cards out of a sense of obligation rather than any clear vision.
  9. Casual EDH decks don't run nearly enough of two key things: pressure and removal.
  10. Large sections of the casual Commander format become akin to multiplayer solitaire, with each player racing toward their own win plan and just praying that they can outrun their opponent.
Full transcript
Introduction: Data Scraping and Feedback Loops

Hey there, my name is Alex.
This, fundamentally, is a video about data; more specifically, it's a video about data scraping and the sorts of feedback loops that can occur sometimes when using scraped data over long periods of time.
It's also a video about Magic: The Gathering, although hopefully I've made it somewhat accessible to people who haven't made the terrible mistake of playing this game.

Magic: The Gathering and the Need for Multiplayer

Magic: The Gathering was released in 1993 and followed the template of a lot of card games: there was an opponent to defeat, and defeating them requires some little dudes that do things, as well as other types of cards.
It's a game whose play formats are mostly designed for two players, which always brings up the natural question: well, what do my other one to four friends do, just stand there looking sad on this Flooded Strand?
I certainly remember being twelve and sitting on the floor with five other kids playing a game that gets abandoned after 90 minutes, but that's not ideal.

The Birth of Commander

Enter Elder Dragon Highlander, known more commonly now simply as EDH or Commander.
EDH is a format with four players where each person picks one of their little guys to be a commander that they can play multiple times at escalating cost.
With origins in Alaska in the late 1990s, the format gradually gained popularity over time, with Wizards of the Coast finally printing a set specifically for the format in 2011.
This set was more of a toolbox than anything else; the pre-built decks were technically playable but not very good, containing a mishmash of themes and random unrelated cards.
They were seemingly intended to be hammered out with other cards into a more functional deck.
This is probably a good thing, as it gave newer players a gentle push toward building their own deck.

The Intricacies of Deck Building

Building a deck is one of the most fundamental processes in any collectible card game, but people don't often think about the intricacies of how decks actually get built.
It usually starts with formulating basic ideas and a bunch of rummaging, whoa, Plumon Spontaneous Flight, that's a wombo combo, by rummaging I mean looking through cards.
This can be looking through physical cards in a collection, or looking through card galleries, or even performing searches with websites like Gatherer.
With these various methods of rummaging, you're going to be asking questions like: Is this card good?
Does it fit into my deck?
How many cards of this type should I be running?
How will this card work against the people I'm likely to play against?
Does this card fit the aesthetic of my deck where all the facial expressions in the art are funny?
Yes, yes it does.
Different players will be asking different questions, but every player uses some criteria or other.

Looking at Other Decks and the Birth of EDHRec

Another major piece of deck building is looking at other people's decks, either physically or through websites where people post their decks.
This could be the last step in deck building to find cards you might have missed, the first step where you're trying to get a bit of inspiration, or somewhere in the middle where you're trying to hammer out the structure of a deck.
Now, this is all well and good, but let's try out a little thought experiment and say that we're trying to use the power of technology to simplify deck building.
A lot of the rummaging, either digital or physical, is hard to directly replace, but the act of looking at other people's decks theoretically could be.
Looking at individual decks is relatively slow, since each individual deck has the biases of its deck builder; meaning that unless you really trust the deck builder, it's a good idea to look at multiple different decks.
What if we could look at all similar decks at the same time, seeing cards based on how often other people choose to run them?
This is where EDHRec comes in, for the Commander format.

How EDHRec Aggregates Decks

In theory, a website that aggregates deck lists is going to allow sorting through a ton of potential card choices very efficiently; we aren't trusting the card analysis of just a single person, but rather the analysis of the collective, thereby reducing the risk of bias.
Now, something to watch out for would be if these decks are influenced by each other, which could compound bad card decisions by people.
This effect will probably always exist to some extent, since people look at each other's decks and generally exist in the same ecosystem, but by default it likely isn't going to be pronounced enough to undermine the idea behind an aggregating website.
The fact that people aren't perfect analysts of cards and sometimes trust other people's imperfect analyses isn't a particularly devastating critique; rather, it's more along the lines of saying that we live in a society.
Everything I just said applies to EDHRec as it existed at its launch in 2015.
As a website, EDHRec scrapes deck lists from websites such as Archidekt and MTGGoldfish, where players post their deck lists to receive feedback.
These deck lists are then organized by Commander, as well as by other categories like price and sub-themes, and then within each tab cards are displayed by the frequency with which they appear in decks.
If I'm trying to build an Arcades, the Strategist deck, I don't have to scroll down far to find Overgrown Battlements, a card that is found in 95% of the Arcades decks that EDHRec has scraped.
Nice, that seems good; I should put that in my deck.
Ah, Shield Wall Sentinel, that goes too.
Wow, I've got all these great cards for my Defender deck and I didn't need to put in very much effort to find them.
Time to log into my favorite website and post this deck so I can send it to all my friends.

The Feedback Loop

It's at this point that EDHRec goes, "Wow, a new deck was posted.
Time to add this useful data to my Arcades, the Strategist page so that other players can benefit from this original card analysis." You might be able to see the problem here.
In this case, instead of getting data from independently operating deck builders, EDHRec is receiving a regurgitation of its own aggregated data, plus maybe a bit of the deck builder's discretion on which specific cards to add.
A little bit of this type of deck doesn't really change EDHRec's methodology, but since all posted decks are treated equally and decks made heavily using EDHRec are much quicker and easier to build than decks built from the ground up, the proportion of decks built mainly with EDHRec naturally balloons over time.

Compounding Effects and "Win More" Cards

As this proportion increases, we get two compounding effects.
For one, cards that didn't quite make it into the EDHRec lists, or which are lower on the page, get artificially tanked further; this, in turn, makes the EDHRec card suggestions artificially sluggish to changes in how decks are played and which decks are played, since for every time a player thoughtfully puts a less played card in their deck, there's another player building a deck straight off the EDHRec page that doesn't include that card because they weren't presented with it.
Secondly, the cards already high up in the EDHRec recommendations are bolstered regardless of how useful they actually are.
The cards at the very top are generally good, but there are a lot of cards that get played in 10 to 50% of decks that are peculiar.
When I think of the archetypal overplayed card in the format, it's one that is questionable on its own but is good in a game where the deck's commander sticks on the battlefield untouched by other players at the table; you could also call this a "win more" card, a card that's bad when a player is behind or when a game is tight but strong when a player is already ahead.
Commanders are the flashiest and most visible part of Commander decks, so even without something like EDHRec, players tend to overrate cards that are better when the commander sticks to the board.
EDHRec compounds this, as these questionable synergy cards are pushed high up in the results and tell players that they're played in 40% of decks, thereby amplifying the deck-building mistakes of EDH players.

As an example of this, a friend of mine was building a Volo, Guide to Monsters deck recently; let's call him Bartholomew.
Bartholomew sent me the list asking for suggestions, and it had several of these "win more" cards, with the two that caught my eye being Second Harvest and Junk Winder.
These cards aren't bad necessarily, but he was building a deck with a number of powerful format staples like Rhystic Study and Consecrated Sphinx, and by comparison these "win more" cards are really only good when Volo sticks around for multiple turns in a row; and if you're that far ahead that's the case, there are much stronger things that you're probably able to do.
I told Bartholomew that he should cut these cards and I wondered to myself why he'd included them.
When I went to the EDHRec page for Volo out of curiosity a bit later, I got my answer: the website is here to quickly inform me that 48% of Volo decks run Junk Winder and 47% run Second Harvest, proportions high enough to make even an experienced player think twice about calling them "bad cards."

The Broader Issue: Inexperienced Players and Card Evaluation

This also speaks to a broader issue with giving high-powered deck-building tools to inexperienced players.
EDHRec can tell people what cards are being played, but it can't tell them why or what they contribute towards a well-rounded deck.
There are some pieces of a deck that are clear as to why they run: I want to run Axebane Guardian because my commander, Arcades the Strategist, encourages me to have lots of Defenders, and I run Slaughter the Strong because most of my creatures have zero power.
However, there are other cards that aren't as obvious.
Why run Path to Exile or Negate?
They don't relate to my commander in any direct way; they just interact with my opponent.
A lot of players don't understand why these cards are good, and I'd speculate that this results in these players adding only a handful of these cards and adding them out of a sense of obligation rather than any clear vision.
This results in a casual EDH format where most of the decks are stuffed with "win more," only-good-with-the-commander cards, with these cards taking up space that could have been occupied with cards that are better in the deck but whose purpose is less immediately obvious.

The Missing Ingredients: Pressure and Removal

A general trend I've noticed is that most casual EDH decks don't run nearly enough of two key things: "pressure" and "removal." Pressure, essentially, is just things that make your opponent's life totals go down, usually creatures that can attack them.
If you know your opponent is playing a deck stuffed with "win more" cards, one that will do nothing and have no defenses until later, then snowball out of control, a natural response should be to turn your creatures sideways and punish that deck before they randomly pop off.
Removal is similar: if you know your opponent is running a deck loaded up with cards that do nothing when their commander doesn't stick around, then all you have to do to keep that player in check is make sure a Doom Blade gets tossed at their commander whenever it's played, and that's a small price to pay to basically shut down a player.

The Result and a Call for Critical Use

So what does it mean that people don't run enough pressure and removal?
It means that the sorts of decks that are stuffed with "win more" cards go unpunished, and large sections of the casual Commander format become akin to "multiplayer solitaire," with each player racing toward their own win plan and just praying that they can outrun their opponent.
I don't love this fact, and this is where some people might say, "But Alex, EDH is a fun casual format; play with your friends." This is true, and honestly, if doing wacky hijinks with your commander and playing against other people doing the same is something you enjoy, I'm not here to tell you're doing anything wrong.
I'm also not telling you're doing anything wrong if you use EDHRec to help you build your decks; I use it pretty regularly when building Commander decks, typically in the later stages of deck building where I'm looking for cards I might have missed in my earlier stages.
It's a good resource as long as you treat the cards that it shows you with the same criticality as cards you find anywhere else, and also recognize that there are cards it's not showing you.
If there's any message I want people to take away from all this, it's just that EDHRec isn't a magical website; it's a website with all the advantages and all the flaws of anything in our modern age that exists through the wonders of sifting through data slurry, and that fact should form the way we use it.

#59
Deck TechMay 202680K views

Lorehold Precon Issues and Deckbuilding Fixes

The New Lorehold Precon Has a Bit of an Issue
The Lorehold precon relies heavily on its planeswalker commander for graveyard filling and value, leaving it vulnerable when the commander is removed.Its low land count of 37 causes frequent mana screw, and its value engines are too slow or conditional.To improve the deck, players should add more lands and early graveyard enablers while delaying the commander until they can protect it.
Use this in your deckIncrease the land count to at least 39 to avoid missing early land drops.
Quintorius, History ChaserRedWhiteMidrangeTokensMana BaseDeck ConsistencySynergyDesign IntentDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • Planeswalker commanders in multiplayer EDH are more vulnerable than in 1v1 because multiple opponents can attack them without spending resources, making board protection essential.
Evidence
  • In 20 test games, 5 missed early land drops because the deck runs only 37 lands and lacks early draw or filtering.
  • Only 5 of the 99 cards are effective graveyard enablers, putting a heavy burden on the commander to stock the graveyard.
  • Seven of the twelve value generating cards cost five or more mana, require setup, or depend on the commander staying in play.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Raise the land count to at least 39 to reduce the chance of mana screw in the early game.
  • Delay casting the commander until you have blockers or synergy pieces to generate immediate value and protect them.
  • Add cheaper graveyard enablers to lessen reliance on the commander's plus one ability.
Limitation
  • The mana base has high colored mana symbol counts, making it harder to consistently meet casting costs.
  • Without the commander in play, the deck's value engines and overall game plan feel clunky and conditional.
Example
  • An opponent uses removal on a blocker and then attacks the now unprotected planeswalker, turning the removal into a two for one against the Lorehold player.
More quotes
  1. This is definitely one of the weaker precons I've reviewed.
  2. In Commander, attacking a planeswalker can mean eliminating an opposing threat without needing to spend a card.
  3. I've also been in a lot of situations where an opponent will use a removal spell on a chunky creature I have in play to block for my planeswalker, which then ends up making that removal spell a two-for-one since it lets my opponents collectively thwack my planeswalker to death as well.
  4. I honestly don't understand why we're still getting precons with fewer than 39 lands. It seems like we're just dooming newer players to have unnecessary mana screw games.
  5. There are some baffling inclusions like Mistveil Plains, Study Hall, and Lotus Field that add to the awkwardness.
  6. This mana base is honestly the most disappointing precon mana base I've reviewed since Blame Game.
  7. If you're going to have a phantom in your deck, you could at least have the decency to pair it with an indestructible land and try to convince me it's ramp.
  8. The average synergy core the deck had put together by the end of turn five was actually better than it was for the blight curse precon I reviewed a couple months ago, even if the number of total dud games was a bit higher here.
  9. This deck broadly seems designed to be grindy and increase in power over the course of a game, but graveyard hate will also increase in frequency as opponents access more of their libraries.
  10. This version of Quintorius is much more powerful than the previous creature iterations if you can keep him around on board.
Full transcript
Introduction and First Impressions
The Deck's Game Plan

Well, as far as synergies go, there are 19 cards that recur, exile, or otherwise remove things from the graveyard, along with five things beyond the commander that are payoffs for cards leaving the graveyard.
To pair with these, there are 12 cards outside the commander that help cards get into the graveyard.
Though, seven of these are pretty insubstantial, so I guess we could say that there are five cards that are proper graveyard enablers.
As an added layer of synergy, there are some discard payoffs with Currency Converter, Conspiracy Theorist, and Containment Construct all letting the deck accumulate extra resources off of discarded cards in some form or other, and these compare nicely with the commander.
As a final bit of cohesion, the deck has 19 creatures that are spirits, five other cards beyond the commander that create spirit tokens, and another few cards that synergize with spirits in some other way.
Between this wide-ranging synergy core, moderate counts of draw and ramp, and a nice though somewhat narrow span of cards to interact with opposing plays, this deck is looking to out-grind opponents and assemble an army of spirits before swinging in big.
So, that's the plan.
But, how is this plan likely to work out in practice?

What I Like About This Deck
The Core Problems: Over-Reliance on Quintorius
A Disappointing Mana Base
Vulnerability to Graveyard Hate
Interaction Issues
How to Fix the Deck: Cuts
Suggested Additions
#60
Card AnalysisMar 202671K views

Why Sol Ring Should Not Be Banned in Commander

A Lukewarm Defense of Sol Ring
We defend Sol Ring's legality by arguing its play pattern is less problematic than other powerful cards.Its impact fades if not drawn early, allowing opponents time to collaborate against the accelerated player.While it can cause blowouts, these are not the norm, and the card does not warp the format like banned cards such as Primeval Titan.
Use this in your deckAsk yourself if your opening hand can capitalize on Sol Ring beyond the first three turns.
Card EvaluationFormat HealthPower LevelThreat Assessment
Principle
  • A card should be banned only when it has both a high power level and a bothersome play pattern.
Evidence
  • Sol Ring is most powerful when played early, but its impact diminishes rapidly if drawn later in the game.
  • Late game bombs like Bolas's Citadel are harder to answer through social means because they can win the game immediately upon resolution.
  • Many players who keep a hand solely for a fast Sol Ring lack a follow-up and fizzle after a few turns.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Always pair Sol Ring in your opening hand with a clear plan to convert the early mana into a sustained advantage.
Limitation
  • Sol Ring can still lead to non-games when combined with the strongest deck at the table or when opponents stumble, though this is not typical.
Example
  • Bolas's Citadel exemplifies a card that, once resolved, often makes social tools useless because it threatens victory the same turn or after one untap.
  • Banned cards like Primeval Titan and Prophet of Kruphix warped games around their resolution, a level of dominance Sol Ring does not reach.
More quotes
  1. I find the play pattern of Sol Ring less bothersome than that of Rhystic Study.
  2. Sol Ring is strongest when it comes earliest in the pipeline toward victory, but it falls off fast compared to a lot of other especially powerful cards.
  3. If a Bolas's Citadel hits the table, the game will usually be over if its controller is allowed to untap with it, and it might even be over the same turn it was played with the right setup.
  4. [Because Bolas's Citadel can end the game so quickly,] the social tools of the EDH format, the fact that you have two other opponents in the game, aren't very useful at dealing.
  5. Sol Ring is humble and task-oriented. It enables a player's game plan early on, but the card lacks any sort of identity or scaling, so after a few turns, it often slips into the background.
  6. To me, getting to be the big bad is one of the defining experiences of playing EDH, and it's an experience that everybody deserves access.
  7. Increased variance won't necessarily create fairer games, nor is fairness a particularly good metric for analyzing Commander games in the first place.
  8. Being strong early means [Sol Ring] can be considered strategically right from the outset of a game, and this feature also offers plenty of time for players to collaborate in dealing with a Sol Ring fueled game plan.
  9. A deck that drew Sol Ring will usually feel much faster, but it will still feel like a version of itself, rather than transforming into a combo deck or something.
  10. The biggest remaining issue with Sol Ring is that sometimes the strongest deck at the table will get it, and that deck will then wipe the floor in a way it might not have otherwise.
Full transcript
The Question of Sol Ring

After my recent video about Rhystic Study, I got a lot of comments saying one specific thing: What about Sol Ring?
Why is it that Rhystic Study should be banned, but not this all-powerful fast mana piece?
Bits and pieces of my answer to that question are sitting around in plain sight.
As I mentioned in that video, the reason to ban things is having a high power level plus having a bothersome play pattern, and I find the play pattern of Sol Ring less bothersome than that of Rhystic.
Not a very high bar, I know, but the ring has cleared it.
I also had a segment in a 30-minute defense of tutors I put out last year where I discussed what causes cards to feel problematic and why a very early game threat like Sol Ring feels less game-warping than other very powerful cards that exist.
Not un-game-warping, but less game-warping.
Still, I think it's worth returning to the question of Sol Ring and asking some questions about it and giving the question of whether it should stay legal a fair shake.
I still firmly lean on the side of not banning it currently, and I'll be laying out my reasons for feeling that way, but the arguments in favor of a ban are also fascinating, and I love being fascinated.

Sponsor

Sol Ring is a card that can really jump-start an aggressive game plan, and if you're looking for other ways to get your speed on, look no further than the red collection from our sponsor, Void Monster.
voidmonster.org is a TCG-inspired accessory and apparel shop started by artist Forest Uml, and every month there's a new drop featuring some fantastic original designs that's only available to pre-order for 1 week.
This week is the red collection, which features a deck box, sleeves, a playmat, and a card binder, all with variations of this red pattern with these sort of rocky magnet details that I really dig.
As always, these are some super cool accessories available only for a limited time, and if you're interested in them, you should follow my link in the description and pre-order by March 1st at noon PST.
Thanks to Void Monster for sponsoring this piece.

The Play Patterns of Sol Ring

Okay, so we're going to start with the play patterns of Sol Ring and why I find them sometimes problematic, but unobjectionable a lot of the rest of the time.
The card's most obvious feature is that it warps early games.
Turn one Sol Ring, as well as its mean older brother, turn one Sol Ring or Arcane Signet, can really set a player up for a nasty turn two and turn three.
I think most casual EDH players have been flattened by a killer Sol Ring roll out in their time, but that earliness of Sol Ring is actually a reason why I don't always mind it so much.
Turn one Sol Ring is amazing; turn two Sol Ring is also pretty juicy, but what if you draw into it on turn three?
How about turn four or five?
At a certain point you might not even want to draw into this little guy, and that points to something important about the card: its ephemeral nature.

To briefly return to a metaphor I used last year, Sol Ring is strongest when it comes earliest in the pipeline toward victory, but it falls off fast compared to a lot of other especially powerful cards.
Smothering Tithe is another card that offers a lot of mana for its cost, but in that case the mana is dynamic in how quickly it gets generated, it's storable, and since it's in the form of treasures, it can also enable some powerful artifact synergies.
It's still just mana, but it's a decidedly more scalable and flexible form of mana.
Next up along the pipeline might be a powerful draw engine like Rhystic Study or The One Ring.
A strong flow of cards will almost always be useful, whether that be in an early setup turn or when a player is drawing their way toward a kill.
The potency of one of these could be limited by the amount of mana a player has, but drawing cards can also draw into ways to solve a player's mana issues, so for most decks this will be a temporary limitation, if even one at all.
Finally, we could talk about a powerful end game card that will usually spell out victory if a player is ahead, and could give a shot at swinging the game back around if they're behind.
Bolas's Citadel is a very powerful card, and the point in a player's game plan where it fits in is often quite far along, just before a player is looking to win.

All right, so what am I trying to say by constructing this scale?
Well, it's a useful data point for analyzing cards that most people would consider powerful.
Let's consider how often a Bolas's Citadel will feel especially powerful, maybe even too powerful for some games.
The answer to that might vary a bit by table, but I'm willing to bet it's reasonably frequent that this card feels out of place.
That's not any sort of complaint about the card; I love Bolas's Citadel, but it is an interesting fact about it, and it's a fact that has a lot to do with the point in the game at which this card is powerful.
If a Bolas's Citadel hits the table, the game will usually be over if its controller is allowed to untap with it, and it might even be over the same turn it was played with the right setup.
This means that the social tools of the EDH format, the fact that you have two other opponents in the game basically, aren't very useful at dealing with Bolas's Citadel.
If people happen to have big boards right now and are able to get an opportunity to swing big at the Bolas's Citadel player, that could be a good way of containing the card, but it's kind of rare that your options will be this good unless the Citadel haver played it when they were pretty far behind.
More realistically, the other players at the table will get maybe a single turn cycle to either come up with a piece of artifact removal or have their own crazy pop off turn, and if neither of those things happen, you're kind of cooked.

This stands in contrast to the way things play out with a card that is powerful earlier in the game.
A powerful draw engine like Braids or Toski can generate a lot of card advantage if not addressed, but these are significantly more manageable through the social nature of the format since they won't be typically winning the game super imminently.
If somebody is getting a lot of cards off of one of these, their opponents can attack them and or interact with some of their stuff in order to slow them down.
This is even more true for a mana tool like Sol Ring that comes into play early.
It will absolutely accelerate the game plan of its caster, but in a bracket two or three game, there will typically still be a lot of turns for the table to work together against the Sol Ringing player before a win is threatened.
To be clear, this is not to say Sol Ring will never be a problem.
If the player who draws it is playing the strongest deck, if that player draws other powerful stuff in conjunction with the ring, if one or more of their opponents has a rough start, all of these could result in Sol Ring causing a blowout, turning an uphill battle into a non-game.
This absolutely does happen sometimes, but it's not the norm of the card in my experience.
More frequently, players do get ample opportunity to gang up on the Sol Ring player, and that's assuming it's a problem at all.
Many a player has kept a questionable hand with a Sol Ring in it, and in those cases, it's pretty common to see a scary first three turns followed by a rather un-concerning turns four, five, and six.

How Much a Card Dominates the Game

Another factor that's worth considering with regard to banning a card is how much a card dominates the landscape of a particular game it's in.
If the legends about Primeval Titan are to be believed, it basically transformed the Commander format into the Primeval Titan format for a year or so, with everybody playing it and stealing it and copying it in order to get their favorite lands out onto the board as fast as possible.
The same goes for Prophet of Kruphix, instantly transforming a game into a Prophet of Kruphix game the second it hit the field.
This can even be the case for Rhystic Study; a player getting to draw a lot of extra cards might trivialize any other set of value engines they were playing, turning their game plan from insert complex synergy web here into Rhystic Study, end of story.
In contrast to these examples, Sol Ring is humble and task-oriented.
It enables a player's game plan early on, but the card lacks any sort of identity or scaling, so after a few turns, it often slips into the background.
I've had multiple games recently where I entirely forgot it had been played, only remembering about it after a moment of marveling at how sleek and tuned an opponent's deck had seemed.

To be clear, this isn't necessarily a perfect defense of a powerful card.
You could make similar arguments about Mana Crypt and Jeweled Lotus, and I'm perfectly happy with them staying banned.
Still, I do see some appeal in the fact that this is a universally available tool that can give players a chance to play an accelerated version of their game plan.
To me, getting to be the "big bad" is one of the defining experiences of playing EDH, and it's an experience that everybody deserves access to.
Now, just to be clear, I have seen some bad defenses of Sol Ring that argue that it's a "tool of the people," an equalizing factor between the stronger players and the weaker players out there, or the stronger decks and the weaker decks.
These are bad defenses because anybody can draw Sol Ring, including the strongest player, and I prefer a modified defense of the card that focuses on its ability to increase early game variance.
Increased variance won't necessarily create fairer games, nor is fairness a particularly good metric for analyzing Commander games in the first place, but what it can do is create more balanced play experiences on the aggregate, offering exciting games to players playing decks that might otherwise be slower.
And that's worth something.

My Stance on Sol Ring

So, ultimately, I've grown a bit more tolerant of Sol Ring over the past couple years, even as I remain a fairly variance averse player.
Random power spikes in a game plan can make for frustratingly unpredictable play experiences, making it impossible to predict opposing decks and strategize.
A deck looking very different power-wise game to game can also make it tricky to set the table up for success, to find a pairing of decks that will result in something satisfying for everyone involved.
In principle, this logic could provide a reason for me to hate Sol Ring, but the profile of the card manages to minimize some of the issues that significant power level variance can cause.
Being strong early means it is a thing that can be considered strategically right from the outset of a game, and this feature also offers plenty of time for players to collaborate in dealing with a Sol Ring fueled game plan.
Being a background enabler whose presence in a game diminishes over time does mitigate some of the power spike it provides; a deck that drew Sol Ring will usually feel much faster, but it will still feel like a version of itself, rather than a mid-range deck that it's transformed into a combo deck or something.
The biggest remaining issue I see with Sol Ring is that sometimes the strongest deck at the table will get it, and that deck will then get to wipe the floor in a way it might not have otherwise.
This undeniably does add to the difficulty in constructing successful Commander games, adds to the uncertainty of how much power level difference between decks is too much.
Whether that fact overshadows the other aspects of the card I brought up is up to you.

The Logistics of Banning Sol Ring

All right, let's move on to the logistics of banning Sol Ring.
I wanted to separate the theoretical case for banning Sol Ring from the practical case for banning it, in large part because I find the practical case for banning Sol Ring almost impossible to really consider, an insurmountably challenging proposition.
It's been printed in every single precon since 2011, and it's played in over 80% of decks according to EDHREC.
Banning Sol Ring would be a massive and monumental undertaking, requiring a prolonged communication campaign to reach all sectors of the player base.
I think we as EDH players on the internet have a tendency to assume the community as a whole is far more cohesive and engaged than they really are, like surely these spicy musings on Reddit or YouTube are an accurate reflection of the player base.
But no, the EDH format is full of people who don't consume much content about it, who haven't heard the latest announcement about the bracket system, who don't pay much attention to current sets, who don't spend a whole lot of time building decks, and who don't get together to play more than once a month, if that.

Take a moment to think about it.
For a 60-card format, the people playing it competitively will be actively listening for ban updates because that affects the meta game and also seems to happen a lot now.
We can also think about the average Commander player who ran Jeweled Lotus or Mana Crypt or even the still legal Rhystic Study.
These players will be relatively in tune with the Commander format compared to most players and will be able to actively receive any news about these cards getting banned.
Meanwhile, if Sol Ring was banned, the median Sol Ring player would probably hear about it 3 months later and go, "Oh, why?
Okay, guess I have some swaps to make." In addition to being challenging to fully convey to the player base, banning Sol Ring would also fundamentally muddy all Wizards of the Coast communication about the Commander format.
Precons serve as an example of what Commander deck building looks like, the tools it should use, the features it should have, and on a fundamental level, how the format works.
To ban a card that WotC has chosen to put in every precon would destroy any sort of messaging consistency about what the format means and would make any future messaging power significantly weaker.
In this way, I do think part of the discussion.

#61
Card AnalysisFeb 202695K views

Why Rhystic Study Deserves a Ban in Commander

Rhystic Study and the Dark Side of EDH
We argue that Rhystic Study should be banned in EDH because it combines a high power level with frustrating play patterns.We explain that the card is powerful even when opponents play optimally, and its taxing effect creates negative social dynamics.The widespread play and high salt scores make it a frequent source of bad experiences, meeting the criteria for a ban.
Use this in your deckInclude one-mana removal spells to answer Rhystic Study efficiently without granting extra cards.
Rhystic StudyBlueCard DrawStaxCard EvaluationFormat HealthPower LevelTable Politics
Principle
  • For a card to warrant a ban in casual EDH, it must be both powerful enough to see widespread play and create frustrating experiences that degrade game quality.
Evidence
  • Rhystic Study is a staple in cEDH, demonstrating its power even against skilled opponents.
  • Opponents often cannot pay the one mana tax when casting pivotal spells or interaction, leading to unavoidable card draw for the Rhystic Study player.
  • The card taxes removal spells and incentivizes counter wars that generate even more card advantage.
  • EDHREC salt polls rank Rhystic Study as the second most hated blue card, reflecting its negative impact on play experience.
Deckbuilding Application
  • To minimize Rhystic Study's advantage, run one-mana instant-speed removal so you can answer it without giving the opponent a card.
Limitation
  • In lower-power brackets, decks often have more spare mana and cast fewer spells per turn, which can somewhat reduce the volume of cards Rhystic Study draws.
Example
  • Banned cards like Hullbreacher, Leovold, and Mana Crypt share the problematic combination of high efficiency and gameplay patterns that frustrate opponents.
More quotes
  1. At the end of the day, Rhystic Study's power level is certainly quite high, but the destructive social dynamics it creates are the more pernicious element of the card, and these two factors combine to make it a card that absolutely warrants a ban.
  2. People will inevitably end up giving the studying player cards for any number of reasons. Just pay the one can be a sensible strategy when a deck features high quality plays at multiple mana values, but if your deck has a wider spread of mana values and card effects, paying the one could keep you away from a critical play that you really need on a particular turn.
  3. Anyone who misses land drops or fumbles their setup turns is going to be much less able to spare mana for Rhystic Study, and being pressured to do so on a critical turn might, in one fell swoop, destroy any chance they had of catching back up.
  4. The players most harmed by needing to pay an extra one for their spells will be on average the less experienced players piloting the less powerful decks and getting less lucky.
  5. In a weird way, arguments in defense of Rhystic Study often end up very similar to conservative economic arguments in favor of the status quo.
  6. [The Rhystic Study defender's] implied message is that there is something wrong with the people who are currently poor, that they are less deserving, that they have been given opportunities and squandered them, that they have earned their shaken life.
