  7. It is an argument in favor of human suffering based on the idea that those people have earned that suffering and no longer deserve our consideration. [Applied to Rhystic Study, it suggests players who feed the draw deserve the consequences.].
  8. When somebody says Rhystic Study isn't that good, people just need to pay the one, they're making a very similar argument, if a less consequential one.
  9. Rhystic Study would be an extremely powerful Magic card if it was a one-sided Sphere of Resistance, but it is not.
  10. Just remove it is a strategy you can employ, just as making somebody the archenemy is a strategy you can employ, but neither of these things are arguments to keep a card legal on the scale of a formatwide ban list.
Full transcript
Introduction

In an October 2025 announcement, Wizards of the Coast floated the idea of a change to the way hybrid mana is treated in the deck building rules of EDH.
This caused quite a lot of discourse, which I laid out some thoughts to in a previous video that was uncontroversial and universally beloved by the EDH community.
However, that October 2025 announcement also featured some other things, one of which was the proposed banning of Rhystic Study, the powerful blue draw engine.
That particular card has been in a perpetual state of low-level controversy for some years now, being both the third most widely played blue card, but also the second most hated blue card according to salt polls conducted by EDHREC.
I won’t bury the lede here: I don’t like this card, and I absolutely think it should be banned.
However, my reasons for thinking that are different than those of a lot of people, and I actually think that this card and the discourse around it is a fascinating window into some of the grittier instincts of EDH players, the ones we don’t like to think about or talk about.
At the end of the day, Rhystic Study’s power level is certainly quite high, but the destructive social dynamics it creates are the more pernicious element of the card, and these two factors combine to make it a card that absolutely warrants a ban.

What Warrants a Ban in EDH?

To start with, though, we need to understand what it is we’re talking about.
Why is it that a card should be banned in EDH?
After all, it’s a casual format; a lot of the usual rules of tournament frequency and meta dominance don’t really apply.
We can look at the current ban list as well as other actions taken against specific cards to start to paint a picture.
I choose to begin in a familiar place.
Yuriko, the Tiger’s Shadow spent seven months on the “game changers” list last year; it was eventually delisted under the reasoning that a “rule zero” tool like the game changers list is far less necessary for policing commander cards, which are visible at the outset of a game.
But Yuriko still works nicely as an example of a card that sometimes feels problematic in EDH.
As I laid out a couple months ago, Yuriko feeling problematic comes down to two things.
For one, she is efficient and powerful, being very cheap to play and providing a ton of utility through her mixture of damage and card draw.
Secondly, her play pattern is frustrating; since her commander ninjutsu ability dodges commander tax, it is quite hard to keep her off the board, and it often feels like the Yuriko player effectively gets to cheat the normal rules of the format.
So these are typically the two things required to make a card truly problematic: a high power level and a play pattern that is frustrating.

We can see this echoed in some cards on the ban list.
Hullbreacher and Leovold are efficiently costed creatures, and they also easily combine with wheels to blast away opposing hands.
Primeval Titan and 15-mana Emrakul are both high-quality bombs that would see wide play if legal, and their abilities also let their controller rapidly snowball and end up playing a lot more Magic than opponents.
We can even see this in the fast mana bans of 2024: Mana Crypt and Jeweled Lotus are obviously very powerful, and the fact that they allow such explosive turn-one plays can be frustrating and disempowering for opponents in a way that some other powerful cards might not be.
Not everything currently on the ban list or the game changers list perfectly fits this framework, but this power-level-plus-play-pattern pairing is still a decently reliable template.
For something to be worth banning in EDH in the year of our Lord 2026, it needs to be frustrating in a way that creates bad experiences for people, and it also needs to be powerful enough to see wide play and make that bad experience commonplace so as to actually warrant a ban, rather than just being an occasional annoyance.
As we will see shortly, Rhystic Study checks both of these boxes.

The Power Level of Rhystic Study

To start with, let’s talk power level.
On an objective level, Rhystic Study’s power level is quite high, and this is evident in the fact that it’s one of the best cards in cEDH.
Surely these expert gamers know how to play around this bad boy, right?
But no, Rhystic Study is quite powerful even if all your opponents are being tactical about paying the one.
If somebody wants to cast a piece of interaction that spends all their mana, they’re not going to be paying the one; if somebody has a pivotal play on their turn and no extra mana to pay, the correct choice is to play the card and let the Rhystic Study player draw, not to skip your own turn.
There are all sorts of cases in which it is absolutely the correct choice not to pay the one, and that makes Rhystic Study a pretty great draw engine on base.
Now, as you move down in power level, decks will get a bit less lean, have spare mana a bit more often, and cast fewer overall spells each turn.
If I’m being generous, I think this is where a lot of people get the idea that Rhystic Study isn’t really problematic; after all, it does scale with power level to some extent, especially when we consider that the maximum card draw in a game with three spells per player per turn cycle is significantly more than in a game with one to two spells per player per turn cycle.
However, this still misses some things.
For one, at bracket 3, cards are going to cost more mana typically: whereas an engine in cEDH might be two or three mana, it’s pretty common to see engines in bracket 3 that cost four or five mana, and if you only have four or five mana to work with, one of these cards is going to be much more possible to pay the tax on than the other.
This is true for engines, and it also certainly applies to removal.

A lot of people will make the argument that Rhystic Study can be answered by simply removing it, but this misses two key things.
For one, you can say this about basically any problematic permanent, but there are still very good reasons to ban permanents; Prophet of Kruphix is a piddly little creature that can be removed far more easily than Rhystic Study, but there are still very good reasons for it to stay banned.
“Just remove it” is a strategy you can employ, just as making somebody the archenemy is a strategy you can employ, but neither of these things are arguments to keep a card legal on the scale of a format-wide ban list.
Secondly, any removal spells cast on Rhystic Study are themselves Rhystic-taxed: best-case scenario, it costs an opponent a mana, but if the removal isn’t super mana-cheap, there’s a good chance that the Rhystic Study player will get a card as well.
And that’s all assuming that the blue player isn’t prepared to play counterspells in defense of their copy of the most powerful draw engine in the format; in the land of reality, counterspells frequently are present, and any multi-spell fight over whether a Rhystic Study stays on board will often itself draw the studying player a lot of cards.

There are also people who will downplay the severity of a Rhystic Study sticking around on board.
A lot of pro-Rhystic folks will casually present Rhystic Study as a “one-sided sphere of resistance”, like, “Come on, bro, if you just pay the one, it’s only three mana to make all opposing spells cost one more, a tax tool more powerful than any other that has ever existed.
It’s not that bad.” This is just patently absurd; a three-mana one-sided sphere of resistance is obviously good, and there’s a reason Grand Arbiter Augustin IV is such a salty card even as a four-mana multicolor card.
I think all of what I just said is pretty reasonable, but I also worry that my engaging with the one-sided sphere of resistance talk legitimizes it in a way that it absolutely does not deserve.
Rhystic Study would be an extremely powerful Magic card if it were a one-sided sphere of resistance, but it is not that, and this is where we i going to really start to diverge from most critiques of the card I’ve seen.

The Social Dynamics of “Just Pay the One”

To start with, let’s just establish that people will inevitably end up giving the studying player cards for any number of reasons.
I laid out a couple reasons earlier that frequently apply to cEDH, and these reasons arguably strengthen at lower levels.
“Just pay the one” can be a sensible strategy when a deck features high-quality plays at multiple mana values; like, if you’re forced to play a three-mana ramp spell instead of a four-mana amplifier, that might not really be a huge hit to your game plan.
On the other hand, if your deck has a wider spread of mana values and card effects, paying the one could keep you away from a critical play that you really need on a particular turn.
This effect is further amplified when there are power level differences present: if one player has a deck that is less tuned, whose ramp isn’t as well calibrated, and whose plays are less mana-efficient, that player is going to be hit harder by the Rhystic Study tax.
Ditto for somebody who gets unlucky or keeps a subpar hand; anyone who misses land drops or fumbles their setup turns is going to be much less able to spare mana for Rhystic Study, and being pressured to do so on a critical turn might, in one fell swoop, destroy any chance they had of catching back up.

I say “pressure” because Rhystic Study, esque effects frequently come with a dose of social pressure not to feed the studying player too many resources.
This fact can feel pretty reasonable on the surface, but this pressure to pay will be experienced very differently by different players.
The players most harmed by needing to pay an extra one for their spells will be, on average, the less experienced players piloting the less powerful decks and getting less lucky, and whether or not these players are pushed into ruining their own game plan may come down to how they react to the social forces at play.
With this in mind, the “just pay the one lol” crowd starts to appear in a somewhat different light.

In a weird way, arguments in defense of Rhystic Study often end up very similar to conservative economic arguments in favor of the status quo.
Hear me out on this one.
Let’s imagine somebody trying to introduce a policy in a society with a lot of economic inequality.
John Allocation says, “We should implement my tax credit plan, as it will give all the people of this great nation more of a fighting chance at succeeding in this economy and joining the middle class.” This is an economic argument, and whether it’s likely to do what John says it will do can be determined by digging into the details, running some models, and whatever else economists do.
Now let’s imagine John’s political rival has something to say about that.
Jeff Business says, “This economy already gives citizens a fighting chance at joining the middle class; just look at all the hardworking people who have already succeeded in a society with a lot of inequality.” This is not an economic argument, this is a moral argument.
The people currently below the middle class indisputably exist, and rather than asking why that is or what can be done to help them, Jeff Business is instead choosing to turn them into an out-group to be ignored.
His implied message is that there is something wrong with the people who are currently poor, that they are less deserving, that they have been given opportunities and squandered them, that they have earned their shaken life.
It wouldn’t matter if John Allocation’s tax credit had been highly successful at helping people elsewhere in the world; here it would be disrupting a divine order that has put this pathetic out-group in their correct place.
It is an argument in favor of human suffering based on the idea that those people have earned that suffering and no longer deserve our consideration.

When somebody says, “Rhystic Study isn’t that good, people just need to pay the one,” they’re making a very similar argument, if a less consequential one, not saying they’re identical, nor that these are patterns of thought held by all Rhystic Study defenders, or all conservatives for that matter, but there’s a strong resemblance.
What I mean by that is this: if telling everybody to just pay the one was actually a successful strategy for making Rhystic Study a fair and enjoyable Magic card, we wouldn’t be having this conversation.
The fact that it’s not a solution, that it’s an unhelpful thing to say, is already proven by the observable facts that surround us, namely, that Rhystic Study is a very powerful card at all levels of EDH.
Every day, thousands of Magic players don’t pay the one, and there are a variety of different reasons for that.
As I suggested earlier, some of them are playing a weak deck or having a weak game with their deck, and playing a couple cards into the Rhystic Study is the only way to keep up.
Or maybe they’re newer to the game and don’t have a firm grasp on the power level of an engine like Rhystic Study, as suggested by the Trinket Mage in his video on the subject.
Maybe they’re overwhelmed by the current state of the game and choose to go about their turns ignoring Rhystic Study for that reason, only paying when doing so is a convenient afterthought.
Maybe somebody sees other players not paying the one for any of the previous reasons and decides that it’s time for them to stop paying as well.
This is the reality of Rhystic Study, not the quippy hypothetical of a smug power gamer.
You can make some sort of argument toward educating people about playing against the card, just as an economic conservative might meekly gesture toward the importance of teaching people to budget, but all four of the situations I described earlier will continue to exist no matter how high a rooftop you yell “just pay the one” from.
“Rhystic Study is fine, people just need to pay the one” is not an argument about game balance; it is a moral argument about the people currently not paying the one.

Why Defend Rhystic Study?

So, if saying “just pay the one” isn’t a helpful solution to the problem of Rhystic Study, why do people say it?
Well, one

#62
Card AnalysisNov 202561K views

Why Yuriko's Commander Ninjutsu Frustrates Players

A Tale of Two Commander Tax Evaders
We examine why Yuriko, the Tiger's Shadow, feels uniquely frustrating in Commander.We argue that her commander ninjutsu avoids commander tax while offering immense card draw and burn for two mana.Other tax-evading commanders like Hogaak and Liesa still impose scaling costs or perform a gesture of tax, which Yuriko lacks, making her feel like a design oversight.
Use this in your deckWhen building against graveyard-based commanders, include exile removal to force them into the command zone and reimpose commander tax.
Yuriko, the Tiger's ShadowBlueBlackcEDHFormat HealthPower LevelDesign IntentCard Evaluation
Principle
  • Tax evasion on commanders only feels fair when accompanied by a scaling cost that acknowledges repeated recasts.
Evidence
  • Yuriko draws a card and deals roughly two damage to each opponent per ninja connection, providing high value for her static two-mana cost.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Include instant-speed exile removal like Swords to Plowshares to permanently answer graveyard-recurring commanders and enforce commander tax.
Limitation
  • Even a trivial scaling cost like Liesa's life payment can preserve the feeling of fairness by performing the gesture of a tax.
Example
  • Derevi's flash ability costs four mana, which is a notable increase from her three-mana base, making her repeated casts less offensive than Yuriko's two mana.
  • Hogaak uses convoke and delve to bypass mana payments, but still spends resources from the graveyard and board, which scales with game state.
More quotes
  1. Yuriko has been singled out as a design mistake, including by Gavin Verhey, who worked on the original card.
  2. The fact that commander tax wasn't a part of commander ninjutsu seems like an obvious oversight in retrospect.
  3. Broadly, there are two things that contribute to Yuriko having a uniquely frustrating play pattern: her vibes and her power level.
  4. A big part of what makes Yuriko frustrating is that it feels like the Yuriko player is exempted from a core rule of the commander format.
  5. It's not significant, but it's something: an acknowledgement that my commander has died, and this is not the first time I'm casting.
  6. Derevi's four-mana ability doesn't perform the gesture, and it does kind of totally cheat command tax.
  7. Four is a lot more than two. Paying two mana for a commander repeatedly is quite inherently powerful.
  8. Yuriko's text box does such a large amount for two mana that it's almost absurd.
  9. A whole lot of tribes would swoon at receiving [Tovolar's] level of utility from the command zone.
  10. Power is still strongly felt in how upsetting a card design is; a lot of designs that are unobjectionable now would become extremely objectionable with a single tweak.
Full transcript

Yuriko, the Tiger's Shadow, is a pretty unique card.
It's the most popular Dimir commander by a country mile, one of only two creature-type based commanders that sees common cEDH play (the other being Magda, of course), and Yuriko does not pay commander tax.
I guess if you're casting her for her base cost she does, but more realistically, Yuriko almost always gets cast for a commander ninjutsu cost, which is forever pegged at two mana.
Last week, I talked about Eminence as one of the two major exceptions to the way commanders work, not even requiring a commander to be on the board to have an initial effect; this is the other, not costing additional mana for subsequent commander casts.
Yuriko, in particular, has been singled out as a design mistake, including by Gavin Verhey, who worked on the original card, and I agree with that assessment.
Commander ninjutsu is a bespoke ability created with the one task of bringing ninjutsu into the command zone, and the fact that command tax wasn’t a part of that ability seems like an obvious oversight in retrospect.
The results of this have bled into the player experience, with Yuriko commanding a similar salt score to that of Edgar Markov, the most contentious of the Eminence commanders.
People are bothered by this card, and they dread playing against it.
However, I think it’s interesting to dig into why Yuriko is a problem for people and how important her tax evasion is in that equation, because I think the line for not paying command tax is actually blurrier than it seems, and more sensitive to the specifics of a given card, with Yuriko’s other features being equally important as her unique method of entry in creating her infamous status.
Broadly, there are two things that contribute to Yuriko, in particular, having a uniquely frustrating play pattern for a lot of players: her “vibes” and her “power level.”

Vibes: The Gesture of Commander Tax

By “vibes,” I mean that the way Yuriko cheats commander tax feels uniquely bad among commanders.
To demonstrate that, we should talk about some other tax evaders and why they don’t drum up the same sort of ire.
Firstly, let’s talk about the Squee of the world, legendary creatures that can be cast from the graveyard.
On the surface, these may seem similar to something like Yuriko, since you can continue to cast them for some fixed cost after they die, but the comparison quickly falls apart.
Firstly, all but one of them will be whisked back to the command zone by any now commonplace exile-based removal.
But more importantly, they obey the rules of 60-card magic.
These cards are designed with the idea of them being cast over and over baked into the design, and their function in a 60-card format will be identical to their function in commander after the first cast.
The uniquely potent thing about commander is the command zone, and these are skipping out on a large portion of the benefit of that zone.

One step up from these would be Hogaak, a commander with the two different discount keywords of convoke and delve.
Hogaak also shares being castable from the graveyard with the previous example, but perhaps more relevantly, regardless of where the card is being cast from, you won’t be spending mana.
If it gets exiled a couple times, the command zone cost will go up accordingly, but paying in exiles from the graveyard or tapped creature tokens will often feel much less meaningful than paying in mana.
You won’t be experiencing paying for Hogaak as a linear barrier in the same way you would with most other commanders.
Nonetheless, Hogaak still feels fine because its payment method performs “the gesture” of paying command tax.
A big part of what makes Yuriko frustrating is that it feels like the Yuriko player is exempted from a core rule of the commander format.
When other commanders get removed, they’re harder to cast, but Yuriko simply isn’t.
Even if Hogaak bucks some of the conventional wisdom, it still doesn’t feel like a major outlier because you’re still expending additional resources.

This idea comes into even sharper focus when we consider Liesa.
Liesa, Shroud of Dusk, is a five-mana commander who, instead of requiring two more mana for each subsequent command zone cast, requires paying two life for each previous cast.
As a Liesa player, I will tell you right now, that is not a meaningful cost.
In the 40-life format of commander, most players are plenty willing to fork over three life in exchange for one mana, and two life for one mana, as seen in something like a Phyrexian mana symbol or an untapped shockland, is pretty much a no-brainer.
Cutting that no-brainer rate in half, one life for one mana, makes it basically free and will rarely feel even a little bit relevant.
So, why doesn’t Liesa come to mind as a chronic tax evader?
The answer, along with other factors we’ll get to later, is “the gesture.” If my Liesa dies, when I cast her again, I adjust my life total by down two numbers; if I need to cast her again after that, four numbers.
It’s not significant, but it’s something, an acknowledgement that my commander has died and this is not the first time I’m casting her.
I’m not playing by the exact same rules as everyone else, but I’m still playing the same format, and I recognize the existence of that format’s rules.

This brings me to an example that, while less problematic than Yuriko, is still a bit hairier than the other ones I’ve mentioned so far: Derevi.
She is a three-mana Bant commander with the ability to pay four mana to put her from the command zone into play at instant speed.
This ability doesn’t perform “the gesture,” and it does kind of totally cheat command tax.
So, what makes this different from Yuriko?
This is where the details get a bit more granular.
You could argue that four mana is larger than three mana, so Derevi’s cost will usually go up at least once, though it’s not hard to imagine somebody arguing that flash and uncounterability mitigates this difference, making the four-mana cast option an appealing one in its own right.
To me, the more potent element is the raw number.
Four is a lot more than two.
Paying two mana for a commander repeatedly is quite inherently powerful; you hit the field early, and then subsequent plays will leave boatloads of mana open for follow-up plays or counterspells or so on.
By contrast, paying four mana repeatedly for Derevi, or five mana repeatedly for Liesa, feels like a more meaningful cost.
It’s still quite nice to have spare mana later in the game when you’re on seven or eight lands and not needing to worry about having an uncastably expensive commander, but that dynamic exists on a decidedly lesser caliber than something like Yuriko.

Power Level: The Value of Two Mana

So far, I’ve been ignoring the text boxes of all the cards I’ve been talking about, which has its upsides in isolating specific design elements.
However, now it’s time to talk about the other reason Yuriko feels so frustrating: her “power level.” What I mean by power level is simply how much power you get based on the amount of mana you pay.
Yuriko’s text box does such a large amount for two mana that it’s almost absurd.
The actual text reads, “Whenever a ninja you control deals combat damage to a player, reveal the top card of your library and put it into your hand.
Each opponent loses life equal to that card’s mana value.” If we wanted to grossly simplify this, we could replace it with the text, “Whenever a ninja you control deals combat damage to a player, draw a card and each opponent loses two life.” Trying to find an equivalent card led to Sisay, the Anointed, a six-mana Selesnya commander who could realistically be more like four mana given the niche position of snakes, and Tovolar, a three-mana wolf and werewolf commander with a bit of added utility for flipping werewolves but no equivalent damage ability.
Tovolar is a pretty good tribal commander; a whole lot of tribes would swoon at receiving that level of utility from the command zone.
So, we’ll say that Yuriko is along the lines of a powerful three-mana commander or a moderately powered four-mana commander, and it’s castable for two mana as many times as you like, as long as you’re able to pay the ninjutsu cost.

So, how hard is that?
Well, a quick visit to the Yuriko EDHrec page makes that somewhat clear.
Ornithopter exists as an evasive zero-cost creature that can get Yuriko into play, and there are a ton of great evasive one-mana creatures that can do the same.
Thousand-Faced Shadow, Changeling Outcast, and Mothdust Changeling are all also ninjas, and there are other cards like Fairy Seer, Siren Stormtamer, and Hope of Ghirapur that have meaningful other upside with Yuriko’s game plan.
The ninjutsu element of commander ninjutsu decidedly is a cost, but given that other ninjutsu creatures also make use of these evasive little dudes as well, it’s hard to call it too dramatic a cost.

By comparison, Derevi’s untapping effect for three-ish mana is far from bad, but it doesn’t give direct value or direct damage, unlike Yuriko, which gives both for two to three mana.
Derevi is a good commander, and you can do a lot of things with her, but those things require the convergence of other elements of your deck, and it’s perfectly possible to stall out, even if Derevi is generating you a lot of mana.
The power level point comes into even sharper focus when we consider Liesa, whose ability drains a player for two life whenever they cast a spell.
An equivalent effect is on a pretty fairly costed three-mana red enchantment called Spellshock, so for two extra mana, we’re slapping that on a 5/5 angel with a couple keywords.
The result is fine; it’s okay, an adequate overall package.
Liesa commands a relatively modest salt score of 0.8, even as a group slug commander that effectively cheats command tax.

One interesting exercise might be to imagine a world in which Liesa drains players for four, or even five, when they cast a spell instead.
That design wouldn’t be overpowered beyond the realm of possibility, but it would be pushing toward the upper reaches of what you might want to put on a five-mana card, and for that reason, I think that version of Liesa would be absolutely despised, a monstrous commander that players shudder in fear thinking about.
Barring counterspells, the best case scenario there is your opponent slams it down, you spend five life and some mana to remove it, and they spend some life to bring it back out again the following turn, without any commander tax, nauseating stuff.
Obviously, this is more of a hardcore group slug design, but nevertheless, this is the sort of power territory Yuriko lives in.
We could alternatively imagine if Yuriko had a commander ninjutsu cost of four, with everything else about the card remaining the same.
It would remain a fairly decent ninja commander, still a more powerful card than current Liesa, but it would lose its status as an unstoppable freight train of damage and value, instead being costed similarly to a Coastal Piracy plus a creature bounce.

How Power Drives Salt and Frustration

This elucidates a weird quirk of the commander format.
Although it’s a casual format where power is modulated by social factors and rule zero tools such as the bracket system, power is still strongly felt in how upsetting a card design is.
A lot of designs that are unobjectionable now would become extremely objectionable with a single tweak, à la five-damage Liesa, and plenty of currently objectionable designs would suddenly cease to be problems at all with a nerf to their power level.
This power relationship can also be seen in the salt scores that are compiled by EDH Track by asking players how much they’re bothered by various cards, which I’ve already referenced a couple times here, .
The first couple dozen saltliest cards all have some clear gameplay reason for bothering opponents, but what about Gaea’s Cradle?
What about Necropotence?
What about Mana Vault or The Great Henge or Roaming Throne?
These cards are purely proactive, and none of them do anything that unique, but they just so happen to be at the highest power level among all equivalent options, so they end up high in the salt charts regardless.

This relates to “fairness.” Even in a casual format, there might be a sense of something an opponent does being unfair outside the bounds of ideal gameplay.
I’m not immune from this either; I dislike the design of Rhystic Study in Commander intrinsically for a lot of reasons I’ll probably get into in a future video, and I also dislike playing against it.
Relevant to the point here, would I dislike Rhystic Study less if it was a five-mana card rather than a three-mana one?
Yeah, I would.
It wouldn’t solve my core gripes with the card, but it would bother me less if it cost more mana.
We can kind of see how this concept of “fairness” applies to Yuriko: a full command tax cheater will garner some baseline level of frustration for opponents, and that frustration will then be amplified depending on the power level of the card it lives on, very high in the case of Yuriko.

What This Means for Future Design

So, what does all this mean for the future?
Should Wizards of the Coast avoid cards with alternative command tax structures or should they print more of them?
My attitude here shares some similarities with my attitude about Eminence cards, in the sense that they’re both motivated by a sense of curiosity and a spirit of play.
I like it when the mechanics of the EDH format are tinkered with, utilized, and recontextualized in ways that make for interesting gameplay.
I enjoy designs that reference commanders and involve the number of times a commander has been cast from the command zone, both on commanders themselves and on supporting cards.
I also like alternative costs like the ones I’ve discussed earlier and ways of interacting with command tax in some way.
This choice of wording may hint at the line I draw, though, and that’s that commanders need to make “the gesture.” They need to acknowledge in some form or other that they exist within a format where your comman

#63
Deck TechSep 202539K views

Optimizing a Multi-Subtheme Glissa Deck for Bracket 2

Why I Built a Deck With Way Too Many Subthemes
We build a Bracket 2 Commander deck around Glissa, Herald of Predation by intentionally including many subthemes, similar to preconstructed decks, and then optimizing the list.The deck combines incubate tokens, artifact synergies, +1/+1 counters, token copying, and graveyard recursion.Despite the overloaded themes, careful card selection creates cohesive interactions and a satisfying, lower-power deck.
Use this in your deckIntentionally overload your deck with multiple subthemes, then optimize each card choice to create cross-synergies, to build a cohesive Bracket 2 deck.
Glissa, Herald of PredationGreenBlackTokensCountersReanimatorPower LevelDeckbuilding RestrictionsDesign IntentSynergyDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • Deliberately including an excessive number of subthemes, like precons but taken further, can cap a deck's power at Bracket 2 even when individual cards are optimized.
Evidence
  • The Glissa deck's subthemes interact: incubate tokens are artifacts that carry counters, enabling multiple payoffs simultaneously.
  • Clock of Omens demonstrated the potential for explosive turns by untapping mana rocks with counters, turning artifact tokens into a resource engine.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Pair graveyard payoffs like Dollhouse of Horrors with incidental self-mill from Millikin or Sarinth Steelseeker to ensure consistency.
  • Use Liquid Metal Torque or Phyrexian Scriptures to convert non-artifact creatures into artifacts, allowing them to benefit from artifact untap synergies.
Limitation
  • Early versions struggled when Dollhouse of Horrors had too few creatures in the graveyard, showing that payoffs need dedicated enablers.
  • The deck's focus on artifacts can leave non-artifact tap abilities without support unless specially addressed.
Example
  • Glissa, Herald of Predation generates two incubator tokens per combat, which are non-creature artifacts with +1/+1 counters, feeding both artifact and counter themes.
  • Nexus of Becoming and Dollhouse of Horrors produce token copies that are artifact creatures, which can then be copied by populate effects like Esika's Chariot.
More quotes
  1. What sort of idea is so inherently mediocre that optimizing it ends up at the power level of a precon?
  2. What if I built a deck with way too many sub themes, like a truly irresponsible number of sub themes, and then tried my hardest to optimize it into a workable deck list?
  3. The fact that such an absurdly complex and multifaceted deck typically wins by swinging in with a bunch of vanilla 6/6s is a pretty juicy morsel of irony.
  4. I have not once had a win with this deck that didn't heavily involve Glissa, and that puts a natural cap on things.
  5. As long as [Glissa's] out, I get two incubators per turn, but that's all I get. I can make them bigger, but I'm still going to be winning the game with a fairly ordinary and extremely predictable number of most likely vanilla creatures.
  6. A deck that tries to cram in as many sub themes as possible is naturally going to struggle with dead cards when the cards intended to work with those cards decide not to show up.
  7. The only deck I have that comes even close to this one in terms of complexity is my [Sharuum] deck. That deck is a toolbox deck that runs 11 conditional tutors to help it piece together engines, whereas this deck has two tutors.
  8. I really like the process of tightening up a deck's cohesion, of finding the perfect cards for its mechanical needs. I like to play decks that feel like they're performing the role I want them to perform as well as they can.
  9. Immediately, this is a bit of a conflict. This preference for optimization works fine for bracket 3, but bracket 2 is harder.
  10. Once [Glissa's] up and chugging, I can just focus on cobbling together whatever stupid little engine I have the tools to create.
Full transcript

Earlier this year, the bracket system was rolled out in the Commander format, providing guidelines for separating decks into different rough groupings to be played together.
Soon after, though, I noticed a problem: I didn't have any bracket 2 decks.
I had a lovely variety of different bracket 3 decks, from battlecruiser to aggro to control to low-slung staxy mid-range, but nothing I'd feel comfortable consistently pulling out in games against three precons or equivalent decks; at least not without the expectation of playing a 1v3 archenemy sort of role.
So, that seemed like a problem worth remedying.

Now, I had built a lower power deck in the past.
A couple years previously, I'd built a Skeleton Ship proliferate deck, and that was kind of funny, but it was lower power purely because it ran a hot garbage commander and a number of hot garbage cards in the 99, such as Shrine of Piercing Vision, Mindless Automaton, Time Bomb, and a whole four of the '90s storage lands that couldn't tap for non-stored mana.
It was funny, but the play pattern was jarring, with the deck being glacially slow to get going, but potentially quite oppressive in the late game if it was left alone to fully build up an engine.
You can probably imagine why I mostly stopped playing this deck.

One alternative for having a bracket 2 deck would be just buying a precon and maybe doing a slight round of upgrades or sidegrades, but I wanted something that felt more my own than that.
There's also another problem with both precons and the Skeleton Ship deck: they feel kind of sub-optimal.
This probably sounds like a very silly criticism of a deck intended to play in relatively low power games, but I really like the process of tightening up a deck's cohesion, of finding the perfect cards for its mechanical needs; the process of turning a deck into a machine that does something.
I like that process, and I like to play decks that feel like they're performing the role I want them to perform as well as they can.
Immediately, this is a bit of a conflict.
This preference for optimization works fine for bracket 3, because there are a whole lot of somewhat funky ideas whose optimized version ends up in that sort of nice midpower zone.
But bracket 2 is harder: what sort of idea is so inherently mediocre that optimizing it ends up at the power level of a precon?
In this case, I actually took inspiration from precons themselves.
See, preconstructed commander decks are typically unfocused by design; they feature slightly too many themes and elements because they're trying to give players a taste of different play styles and also give some easy paths to upgrade a given deck, such as cutting a singular subtheme for some more relevant stuff.
So, what if I did the same thing but on overdrive?
What if I built a deck with way too many subthemes, like a truly irresponsible number of subthemes, and then tried my hardest to optimize it into a workable deck list?
Thus began the process of building "Farrexian flavored subthe soup."

Selecting the Commander

Longtime snail heads out there might remember that I hated a Glista Sunslayer battlecruiser deck a while back.
And while I love Glya as a character, both that deck and that Glyissa ran a bit generic for my tastes.
Glya the Traitor could be fun as an old-school sort of commander choice, but that one is actually a bit too specific for my purposes here, with a setup and payoff that don't offer a super wide variety of options.
However, there's another green-black option, and that one actually lands right in the sweet spot for me: Glya, Herold of Predation.
This is a five-mana 3/5 that offers a choice at the beginning of combat on your turn: incubate two twice, flip all of your incubator tokens, or give your Phyrexians first strike and deathtouch until end of turn.

Incubate as a mechanic is pretty unique.
It creates a non-creature artifact token with +1/+1 counters on it that can later be flipped into a Phyrexian artifact creature by paying two mana.
Obviously, this lends itself to a deck that cares about counters, so we'll call that subtheme number one.
But honestly, the first thing I noticed about this Glissa is, wow, this card generates a lot of artifact tokens.
There's a number of fairly straightforward ways to take advantage of having artifact tokens: cards like Terrion's Journal and Aviscerator's Insight sacrifice artifacts to draw; Monumental Corruption can either be a big draw spell or a player-killing spell depending on the artifact count; and Throne of Gath is a potent one-card engine with the commander, also boosting the counter theme.
So, for our purposes here, having a lot of artifacts is subtheme number two, and it's one of the more central themes here.
It also contains arguably the deck's most powerful card and one that inspired much of the rest of the list: Clock of Omens.
This is a four-mana artifact that allows a player to tap two artifacts to untap a different artifact, and while it's not quite a combo piece here, I think it still has the potential for some big turns.
The most basic version of that is with some of my bigger counter-based mana rocks: repeatedly tapping and untapping Everflowing Chalice, Astral Cornucopia, or Empowered Auto Generator can really push the deck's mana into new territory and let it do some big plays.
There are a lot of other options as well, though: Idol of Oblivion for card draw, Staff of Completion for proliferates and more, and both Twitching Doll and Lux Cannon as cool cards that also use counters.

The deck contains a couple other options for untapping, but it does sometimes run into a problem: not all of the cool tap abilities I want to run and repeat are on artifacts.
I have cards like Plaguema Beast and White of the Requary that take advantage of my tokens, and I'd quite like to activate them repeatedly after flipping a bunch of incubators with Glissa; so, what if I just turned them into artifacts?

The Liquid Metal Package

Enter the Liquid Metal package.
This features Liquid Metal Torque and Phyrexian Scriptures, both cards that turn a creature into an artifact creature, as well as being a mana rock and a board wipe, respectively.
This package also contains two other cards, and these are actually my two favorite cards in the deck: Dollhouse of Horrors and Nexus of Becoming.
Both of these cards create token copies of cards that are also artifact creature constructs, with Dollhouse doing this by exiling creatures from the graveyard and Nexus doing this by exiling artifacts or creatures from a player's hand.
The downside of exiling the copied card is certainly real, but the potential volume of liquid creature creation is really potent here.
And due to a particular quirk of these cards, they're able to form the bedrock for yet another one of the deck's subthemes: "token copy populate."

Token Copy Populate

Since the artifact creature copies created by Nexus and Dollhouse are tokens, they can be further copied by cards like Selesnya Eulogist and Esus chariot, cards that copy tokens.
This can allow for some nutty levels of advantage building.
And to improve the odds for things to come together, I threw in a few extra token copy generators as well.
This still isn't a huge package, just five setup and four payoff, but most of these cards are fine enough on their own, and the payoff for putting them together can be pretty huge.

The Graveyard Subtheme

A late addition to this deck was a graveyard subtheme.
I adore Dollhouse of Horrors, and on paper, it's perfect for this deck, but too often in my early games, I was finding myself without much to do with it, making copies of maybe one or two creatures, but not feeling like I was getting anywhere close to the full potential of the card.
This was also my experience with another card I like, Vat of Rebirth.
So, I decided to help these cards out with the addition of some other graveyard payoffs, and especially some more graveyard enablers.
I went with mostly flexible options here: Millikin as a card that's also a mana dork; Sarinth Steelseeker as a sort of value engine that works well with my artifact tokens; Old Rustine as a card that also itself pushes my tokens plan; and Tyar as a planeswalker that also ties into activated abilities.

Minor Packages and Weird Synergies

Okay, so with the graveyard package covered, we've capped off the list of all the major subthemes, but there are a few more minor packages as well.
The deck runs a constructs package with Scrapyard Recombiner, which gives it access to a removal spell and a few synergy options of different calibers.
There's also five cards that create powerstones, which can pay for one of the next 31 artifacts, one of the deck's 23 cards with a colorless mana payable activated ability, or any one of the incubator tokens Glissa makes.
For the weirdest little tidbit here, the deck can actually populate incubator tokens.
This might seem bad, since the copy won't enter with counters and will instantly die when flipped, or instantly, instantly if it's already on the creature side; but this actually allows for on-demand usage of on-death trigger cards like draw scorpion and Morbid Opportunist without needing a sacrifice outlet, sort of like aristocrats without the aristocrats, I guess.

How the Deck Plays: Glissa as the Anchor

So, this deck kind of does a little bit of everything.
And all of that everything is anchored by Glissa.
The artifact tokens she provides certainly are a big help for sacrificing and whatnot, but the biggest thing Glissa does is provide the deck with a reliable win condition.
Typically, I'll try to ramp out Glissel on turn four and start gradually accumulating incubators, maybe sacrificing one here or there.
Once that's up and chugging, I can just focus on cobbling together whatever stupid little engine I have the tools to create, whether that be a value engine, a ramp engine, a removal engine, or some combination of these things.
What's important is that I eventually find my way to some proliferate, at which point I can use my resource advantage to amplify the counters on my accumulated tokens before having Glissa flip them, allowing me to swing big at an opponent.
The fact that such an absurdly complex and multifaceted deck typically wins by swinging in with a bunch of vanilla 6/6s is a pretty juicy morsel of irony, and it really lets the deck feel truly belonging in bracket 2.

Bracket 2, the Infinite Combo, and Natural Limits

And actually, this feature about the deck is part of what helps it stay comfortably bracket 2.
In theory, this deck has some gross plays.
First of all, there's an infinite mana combo that involves popping out Empowered Autogenerator as a creature with Nexus of Becoming before repeatedly untapping it with Voltaic Construct the following turn.
It's a rather strange one and requires a non-trivial amount of setup, but it still is a three-card infinite combo.
In addition, the deck features some pretty powerful cards like Clock of Omens, Kudo the Forgemaster, and Staff of Completion, and these can help the deck assemble some pretty disgusting engines.
Despite this, I have not once had a win with this deck that didn't heavily involve Glissa, and that puts a natural cap on things.
As long as she's out, I get two incubators per turn, but that's all I get.
I can make them bigger, in some cases a lot bigger if I have a proliferate engine really pop off, but I'm still going to be winning the game with a fairly ordinary and extremely predictable number of most likely vanilla creatures.

So, that's the deck, and I do find it quite fun, but I worry that the process of describing it might be making it seem a bit more functional than it actually is.
A deck that tries to cram in as many subthemes as possible is naturally going to struggle with dead cards when the cards intended to work with those cards decide not to show up.
The only deck I have that comes even close to this one in terms of complexity is my Sharuum deck, but there's a pretty clear difference between these two.
That deck is a toolbox deck that runs 11 conditional tutors to help it piece together engines, whereas this deck has two tutors.
I don't know if there's been an exact tutor limit given for bracket 2, but I've heard the number three floated around and that felt like a nice number to shoot for here that would force some creative workarounds without being an outright barrier against tutoring.
All this to say, with the daring vision and self-imposed restrictions I had going into this deck, dead cards were inevitably going to be a challenge.
So, how does this deck mitigate that?

Mitigating Dead Cards with Role Overlap

A big one is that I try pretty hard not to let a card perform only one task for the deck.
There is no generic removal here.
When I highlight the cards in my interaction suite that proliferate, use counters, or are lands in architect, that excludes only five cards, and all of those either fit into another subtheme or have some sort of useful synergy in a removal engine.
The same goes for ramp.
Of the next 19 cards that perform some sort of ramp function, the two most generic are Golgari Signet and Talisman of Resilience, with every single other one evolving counters, producing powerstones, or otherwise slotting into one of the deck's themes.
Likewise for draw, with Morbid Opportunist being by far the most generic of the deck's 17 sources of value, and that's even considering the weird populate trick I mentioned earlier.
Something else you might notice here is that the number of ramp and draw spells are fairly high, and this is another pretty essential feature for letting the deck run smoothly.
Since there are single-digit counts of setup and payoff for a number of different subthemes throughout the deck, it becomes very important to see more of the deck in order to piece together engines.
I actually bought a couple draw engines on the convention floor at MagicCon Vegas because I was noticing some light stallouts in the games I was playing, and sure enough, that helped out the deck quite a bit.
This also applies to ramp, since there's no guarantee that the low-cost cards I have available will be what I'm looking for in a given moment.
Having some extra ramp to let me use activated abilities.

#64
Deck TechAug 202585K views

World Shaper Precon Review: Land Sacrifice Engine Breakdown

This Land Sacrifice Precon is Great Aside From One Thing
We review the World Shaper Commander precon from Edge of Eternity, explaining its land sacrifice and recursion engine.We highlight the deck's strong mana base with many fetch lands and duals, consistent land graveyard filling, and powerful payoffs from landfall and sacrifice triggers.The deck can ramp explosively and generate significant value, but we note an unspecified weakness.
Use this in your deckSet your land count to 42 in a land-sacrifice deck that relies on a commander granting extra land drops to fuel consistent landfall and recursion.
Hearthhole, the World SeedBlackRedGreenLandfallAristocratsRampCard EvaluationMana BaseUpgradesWin Conditions
Principle
  • A land-sacrifice deck thrives when it combines a commander that turns land sacrifice into card draw and extra land plays with mass land recursion and multiple payoff types like landfall and sacrifice triggers.
Evidence
  • The precon runs 42 lands, which is high but justified by the commander's land-sacrifice and extra-land abilities, ensuring consistent land drops and recursion targets.
  • It includes 10 budget fetch lands and 13 cards that sacrifice lands, consistently filling the graveyard with lands for recursion.
  • Six cards can recur multiple lands, with three providing mass recursion that can easily grab four or more lands, even early in the game.
  • The payoff suite comprises four landfall cards, nine permanent sacrifice payoffs, and four cards that scale with the number of lands in graveyards, offering diverse routes to capitalize on the engine.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Incorporate a suite of fetch lands and typed duals to maximize synergy with land recursion spells that return lands tapped, as these lands still trigger landfall and sacrifice payoffs.
  • Include multiple mass land recursion spells to convert graveyard lands into explosive ramp, and ensure a mix of payoff types that trigger off lands entering or being sacrificed.
Example
  • Tireless Tracker is a standout landfall value engine in the precon, creating Clue tokens as lands enter to maintain card velocity and pressure.
More quotes
  1. I was nothing short of electrified to see this sort of effect printed in a precon in the first place.
  2. You want to give newer players the chance to pop off and be the big bad sometimes, but you also want their deck to chug along and make them feel like they're getting to play, even if they're not having some sort of big gamer turn.
  3. This deck is obviously a big Mayhem Devil dub, but I also love Windgrace's Judgment and Pest Infestation.
  4. I'll admit that I was a little skeptical of the two tapped fetches and especially the three Capenna fetches here, but these start to make a lot more sense when you remember that the four most powerful land recursion spells in the deck return the lands to play tapped, which isn't a downside for any of these five.
  5. I ran a number of play tests with this deck out to the end of turn five, and there were a surprising number of them where I finished with 10 or even 12 lands in play, which can set the deck up for some serious midgame shenanigans.
  6. The commander team at Wizards is continuing in their quest to flesh out dual land cycles in precons, and I'm super here.
  7. It lacks early game utility plays and it has too much land sacrifice and sacrifice payoff, especially considering that the commander is both of these things already.
  8. Sacrificing lands to perform various effects before spitting those sacrificed lands back out into play.
  9. Landfall is a great tool because it doesn't even require much setup.
  10. Lone Crafter Fawn: unless you just want an outlet to discard lands, which I don't think this deck really wants, the two requirements to make the ETB good won't be lining up often enough.
Full transcript
Introduction and Overview

Hey folks, I’ve got a bunch of projects in the works cooking up for later, and I’ve been loving Edge of Eternity so far, so I figured this was a lovely time for a precon review.
I put it up to a vote, and my patrons narrowly chose the World Shaper precon, so that’s what we’re going to look at here, .
Here’s the format: I’m going to tell you what I think this deck is trying to do, I’m going to talk about the areas it succeeds in, and then we can talk about its weaknesses and a set of card swaps that could help out with those.

Commander Analysis

To start with, let’s talk about the commander.
Hearthhull, the Worldseed, the World Seed is a four-mana spacecraft in Jund colors, spacecraft being a new artifact subtype that’s being introduced in Edge of Eternity.
These are artifacts that can basically be leveled up by tapping creatures to gain abilities similar to crewing a vehicle, and most of them eventually become creatures themselves as well.
This set also brings a rules change to the Commander format: any legendary vehicle or spacecraft with a power/toughness box can be in the command zone.
Over on the podcast I co-host, we just did a tier list of all the vehicle commanders, which will be linked down in the description.
Okay, so that’s spacecraft.
But what does Hearthhole in particular do?
Nothing at first, but once it’s had two power put into it, it gains the ability to tap, pay one, and sacrifice a land to draw two cards and gain an additional land play this turn.
At eight charge counters, it becomes a 6/7 with flying, vigilance, haste, and “whenever you sacrifice a land, each opponent loses 2 life.” Initial impressions: this seems like a solid value engine with some explosive endgame utility.
If we squint a bit, we can kind of think of its first ability as having a floor of tapping to draw a card, since we’ll probably draw and play another land from those two cards pretty often, repaying the one mana if it entered untapped.
This is a fine starting point, but obviously there are a whole lot of things that amplify the power of this.
The biggest one is things that can utilize lands in our graveyard if we’re able to get them back out into play in some form; this card represents a fair amount of ramp potential in addition to the value it provides.
Another amplifier would be landfall effects that benefit from additional lands entering, and a third would be payoffs for permanents being sacrificed.
If we can get some number of these online, the two-plus ability on Hearthhole will pack a ton of utility.
The eight-plus modality is a bit less appealing to me to get early on, since it makes our commander more removable without putting on much pressure in most cases, but it’s a nice tool to have in our toolbox as something that can be turned on right before a big land sacrifice play.
Overall, a nice package.

How the Deck Works

So, how does this deck work with this commander?
Well, there are six cards that can recur multiple lands from the graveyard, three instantaneous and three more gradual, along with a number of other cards that can sacrifice lands to perform effects.
This, to me, represents a core ramp engine of the deck: sacrificing lands to perform various effects before spitting those sacrificed lands back out into play, building a lot of value and mana in the process.
Mana and value don’t win games though, so what are our payoffs for futzing with lands?
Three main things: four landfall cards, nine payoffs for sacrificing permanents, and four cards that check the number of lands in graveyards, with most of these payoff cards amplifying a player’s board or pushing damage in some way.
To support this game plan, there’s a pretty wide and diverse removal suite as well as a deliciously high land count of 42.

Game Plan Recap

So, to recap, this deck is trying to build mana and value through the process of sacrificing and replaying lands before turning things up to 11 with a suite of permanents that perform powerful effects when parts of this cycle happen.
The endgame plan is likely going to be a mixture of large creatures, moderate-sized creature tokens, and potentially an explosive burst of life loss from the commander if things line up right.

What I Like About This Deck

So, what do I think about this game plan?
Let’s talk about what I like about this deck.
And since this is a lands deck, I’m going to mix things up a bit and talk about the deck’s mana base first.

Mana Base

Overall, there’s quite a lot to be excited about here.
First of all, 42 lands is a fair amount, but with a commander that both sacrifices lands and gives extra land plays, this number is perfectly reasonable.
In terms of raw mana base quality, things are pretty great here: great symbol counts in black and green and a fine enough symbol count in red, given that it’s a secondary color.
Like the last precon I reviewed, we have a moderately large count of taplands here, but given the high total land count, I don’t really see this being an issue.
More to the point of what’s cool about this manabase, there’s an impressive density of budget fetches here, a whole 10.
I’ll admit that I was a little skeptical of the two tapped fetches and especially the three Capenna fetches here, but these start to make a lot more sense when you remember that the four most powerful land recursion spells in the deck return the lands to play tapped, which isn’t a downside for any of these five; and the upside of these is immense.
These tie into all three of our main payoff types: lands-in-graveyard payoffs, permanent-sacrificing payoffs, and landfall effects.
The amount of utility these subtly add to the deck is really cool.
Finally, let’s talk about the typed duals.
We have all three of both tango lands and bicycle lands, which can be fetched with any card grabbing a basic typed land, and yes, that includes the Golgari ones as well.
The commander team at Wizards is continuing in their quest to flesh out dual land cycles in precons, and I’m super here for it.
These are lovely for any players who want dual types and don’t want to shell out for one of the more expensive options; even just having a little upside over a basic tap land is really nice.

Core Engine

Okay, so how does this deck build off of this strong foundation of lands?
Let’s talk about the core engine here.
This deck is looking to sacrifice lands, oftentimes recur those lands, and generally get payoffs from the act of playing and sacrificing lands.
In practice, this mostly works out.
If there’s one thing this deck can do, it’s get lands into the graveyard.
Between the commander, 10 fetches, and 13 cards in the 99 that sacrifice lands, this deck is very consistent at finding ways to fill its graveyard with lands should it want to do such a thing.
For recurring lands, there’s a somewhat lower quantity, although this is to be expected for a payoff card; and the deck, for the most part, seems high-velocity enough to find these, it tends to churn through a lot of cards.
When they are found, the impact can be big.
Even relatively early in the game, finding one of the deck’s three mass land recursion cards can routinely grab four or more lands.
I ran a number of playtests with this deck out to the end of turn five, and there were a surprising number of them where I finished with 10 or even 12 lands in play, which can set the deck up for some serious midgame shenanigans if it can keep finding ways into non-land plays.

Payoffs: Landfall and Sacrifice

How about payoffs outside land recursion?
As I mentioned earlier, two major payoff types are landfall and permanent sacrifice, and these both play pretty cool roles here, particularly the former.
Landfall is a great tool because it doesn’t even require much setup.
With the next 10 fetch lands and an extra-land-play commander, you’re already looking at anywhere from two to four lands entering per turn, and that’s before taking into account ramp or land recursion.
There are three powerful finishers utilizing this effect as well as Tireless Tracker as a lovely value engine.
Permanent sacrifice payoffs exist in a somewhat similar realm; the commander sacrifices a land every turn, and tack a single fetch land per turn on top of that and you’re looking at two easy triggers every turn before considering the 13 nonland cards that can help with this task.
A lot of these payoffs are pretty sweet.
You’ve got classic Jund powerhouses like Korvold and Mayhem Devil as well as some neat new ones printed for this precon.
I have opinions about the exact quantity of these that I’ll get to a bit later, but the bottom line here is that I really like the structuring of this deck.
This deck will be at its best when it’s able to perform land recursion, but most of these landfall and sacrifice payoff cards have a high enough floor that they’ll feel perfectly fine even if the deck is currently operating at a somewhat lower caliber.
This, to me, is ideal for a precon: you want to give newer players the chance to pop off and be the big bad sometimes, but you also want their deck to chug along and make them feel like they’re getting to play, even if they’re not having some sort of big gamer turn, and most of this deck’s payoffs accomplish that goal.

Removal Suite

Moving on to this deck’s removal suite, things continue to look pretty solid.
Five pieces of instant-speed single-target removal, five pieces of multi-removal, and then another seven cards that perform some sort of hybrid role, being a removal engine or performing another task as well.
I’ll highlight some favorites here, but the key takeaway is that this removal suite is just pretty great, and there’s only one card here I find remotely worth cutting.
This deck is obviously a big Mayhem Devil dub, but I also love Windgrace’s Judgment and Pest Infestation as powerful multi-removal options in decks that can afford to pay a bit of mana.
There are two pieces of graveyard exile here, but it might feel like a lot more in practice since this deck is high-velocity and has the capability to loop Bojuka Bog to its heart’s content.
We also have to talk about Planetary Annihilation.
This is a wild new design, dealing six damage to each creature and forcing each player to sacrifice down to six lands.
To discuss the card’s merits here, it works satisfyingly with both the commander and this deck’s sources of mass land recursion; and outside of that, I was nothing short of electrified to see this sort of effect printed in a precon in the first place.
I wear the conflicting hats of big mana player and advocate for incomplete mana wipes such as this, and while I don’t want to derail this piece by jumping away to talk about that for 5 minutes, I did discuss my thoughts on this card and some others from Edge of Eternities in a segment available to patrons and YouTube members, and there will be a preview of that at the end.

What I Dislike About This Deck

So that’s what I like about this deck a fair amount.
But what do I dislike about this precon?
Only really two things.
It lacks early game utility plays, and it has too much land sacrifice and sacrifice payoff, especially considering that the commander is both of these things already.
So, the way we’re going to go about improving this is that we’re going to cut some of the trickier payoffs and replace them with other payoff cards that are more flexible, and we’re also going to add a handful of cards for purposes of smoothing the deck’s early game and helping with setup.
Unlike basically all of my previous precon reviews, I’m not really going to be introducing any new elements to this deck; the ideas are already here, we just need to commit a bit harder to the better choices in this deck.

Card Swaps: Cuts

Let’s start with cuts first.
Lone Crafter Fawn: unless you just want an outlet to discard lands, which I don’t think this deck really wants, the two requirements to make the ETB good won’t be lining up often enough.
Scouring Swarm is honestly just a pretty mediocre card; obviously, flying is nice and there’s a bit of explosive potential, but each of the conditions on the card are too difficult to meet to justify their respective payoffs, and it doesn’t even have two power to get Hearthhole juiced up in one go.
Worldbreaker costs way too much mana for the utility it provides to this deck, so it’s also got to go.
Sprouting Goblin at least accomplishes a couple tasks the deck vaguely wants, but the overall package here is still a bit meh.
Likewise, I sort of see the vision with Groundskeeper, and I’d be okay with it if it was a 2/1, but as things stand, the use cases for it just aren’t wide enough.
Uurg, Spawn of Turg sadly just doesn’t do enough for my liking, being neither a great early game play nor a particularly ideal later play.
Finally, this deck is just a bit overloaded on higher-cost and trickier-to-use payoffs, and the two I’m choosing to cut here are Mazirek, Kraul Death Priest and Centaur Vinecrasher.
If you love these cards, I don’t know, pretend I named two other payoff cards you like a bit less; the specifics aren’t that important for these last two cuts, we’re just trying to rebalance the ratios a bit.

Card Swaps: Additions

Now, on to the additions.
First of all, and by orders of magnitude most importantly, Spelunking.
Land untapping effects are very powerful in this deck, and the options out there other than Spelunking are quite pricey.
Next up, I did cut some land-sacrificing payoffs, and the logical replacement to me is landfall.
Lotus Cobra and Tireless Provisioner are both great mana engines, and Valakut Exploration is a great value engine that doubles as a damage wincon in a big land-fiddling turn, potentially working in tandem with the commander to one-shot opponents from shockingly high life totals.
Next up, I want to get some higher-utility two-drops in here.
This deck has a lot of late-game potential but is a little clunky right now and hungry for early smoothers.
Deeproot Wayfinder is a pretty sweet little guy that can filter and ramp a bit early and maybe even a bit later, too, if we can keep a path clear for him.
House Cartographer is a neat card that can help us keep a steady flow of lands to hand, and our commander’s st.

#65
Deck TechJul 2025331K views

Building a Discard-Focused Aggro Deck for Commander

I Built a Deck That Turns EDH Games into Hellbent Cagefights
This deck tech explains how to build an EDH deck that creates low-resource cagefights using heavy discard and scrappy creatures.We emphasize compact card choices where creatures also provide draw or removal, and selects Malfegor as a commander to clear blockers and exploit opponents' empty hands.The deck runs 20 discard spells for consistency and avoids cards that rely on specific board states.
Use this in your deckRun 20 discard spells, prioritizing creatures that also discard, to consistently draw two by turn five and strip opponents' hands.
MalfegorBlackRedAggroDiscardBudgetResilienceDesign IntentDeck ConsistencyDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • Compactness is key: use cards that serve multiple purposes, like creatures that also discard or draw, to efficiently spend deck slots.
  • Resilience is essential; the deck must function with few cards in hand and recover easily from discarding its own hand.
Evidence
  • The deck runs 20 discard spells (11 creatures), 14 draw spells (11 creatures), and 14 removal spells (5 creatures), demonstrating compactness.
  • A count of 20 discard spells gives a 75% chance of drawing two within the first five draws.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Choose a 'counterweight' commander like Malfegor that covers your strategy's weaknesses, such as clearing blockers for an aggro-discard deck.
  • Run many utility spells as creatures to maintain a high creature count for pressure and synergy.
Limitation
  • Avoid non-compact cards like anthems, protection spells, and temporary ramp, as they are unreliable without specific board states or follow-up.
Example
  • Malfegor discards your hand on entry and forces opponents to sacrifice creatures, turning discard pressure into board control and enabling your small attackers.
More quotes
  1. What if I played a pile of little scrappy dudes and then dragged the rest of the table down to their level?
  2. Discard doesn't say yes or no; it strips away resources, forcing opponents to pick and choose what to keep, disproportionately hindering synergistic or complicated decks.
  3. Compactness means decks should use their slots as efficiently as possible, not overcommitting resources to accomplish tasks.
  4. [Malagor] is a counterweight commander: he subverts the weaknesses of a discard deck full of grindy creatures, doing the opposite of what the deck does.
  5. This deck takes a baseball bat to its own kneecaps: over a third of the discard spells affect me, and the commander makes me discard my hand.
  6. Nobody at the table is allowed to keep cards in hand, not even me. We're all getting dragged down into the mud for some rough and dirty Magic gameplay.
  7. Most of the draw spells are discrete engines: they don't rely on other cards to generate card advantage, and they provide cards over time.
  8. I frequently get a visceral sense of mixed feelings from opponents: they wish I was the unambiguous threat, but I'm often barely ahead.
  9. What do you do with a player who isn't an imminent threat, isn't comboing, and isn't scaling up to an overwhelming late game?
  10. [Malagor] is the key to turning games from an epic wizard duel into a knife fight in four-inch deep mud.
Full transcript

One of my favorite ways to build EDH decks is to chase a particular vibe, a feeling that I want to infuse as much of as possible into the DNA of a deck.
A big instance of this was chasing the feeling of raw power and inevitability granted by big lands decks, ramping myself into being the untouchable god-king of a particular EDH game.
However, equally alluring has been the exact opposite type of deck: a deck that can cultivate the sort of low-to-the-ground, cutthroat games that often characterize other formats but which are frequently absent in EDH.
On my end of things, it’s absolutely possible to play that kind of magic; I can play a combat-focused deck full of scrappy two- and three-drops.
The issue is that this frequently doesn’t play well with the sorts of decks that dominate most of the format.
Life totals are too high, ramp is too plentiful, and decks are designed to get too many resources for a scrappy little pile of dudes to keep up.
Aggro can work, but most often it involves either Voltron-esque methods or a strong commander such as Torbrand, Jetmir, or ANM Pacal as a core that binds together an otherwise disjointed game plan.
That would be fine, but it wouldn’t be quite the sort of cage-fight magic vision I was going for; it would be me racing a bunch of normal game plans using my powerful commander.
So what if instead I went a different direction?
What if I played a pile of little scrappy dudes and then dragged the rest of the table down to their level?

Discard as the Great Equalizer

This is where discard comes in.
Discard doesn’t necessarily say yes or no; rather, it strips away resources, forcing my opponents to pick and choose what they want to keep in hand and disproportionately hindering decks doing more synergistic or complicated things.
When I first conceptualized this deck as a $25 budget deck four years ago, I went with a bit of an oddball commander choice: mala gor.
What’s he doing here?
Well, the logic is that with a 99 that is focused on flexible two- to three-drop creatures and three- to four-cost discard spells, there really wasn’t a need to fit a commander into my curve, so a higher-cost utility option works just fine.
As for the specific appeals of mala gor, well, I’ll get back to that bit.

When I went to finally update the 2020 version of the deck this summer, I started with a full 20 discard spells.
The goal is to get the table hellbent, and you need a lot of discard spells to do that consistently.
Two spells ends up being a good number to shoot for, and a count of 20 allows me to draw that within the first five draws of the game approximately 75% of the time, which is good enough for my purposes.
This gave a good starting point for the deck but simultaneously posed a problem: 20 slots is a lot to spend on a category of card that broadly doesn’t directly impact the board.

Compactness and Efficient Slot Use

This brings me to a key feature of the deck: “compactness.” Compactness is a feature I find important in general, and on a basic level it just means that decks should use their slots as efficiently as possible so as not to overcommit resources to accomplish tasks.
In this case, the deck manages to pack decent quantities each of cards that consider draw spells and cards that consider removal spells in addition to the discard.
This compactness can be seen when I sort my categories by card type on Archidekt: 11 of the deck’s 14 draw spells are creatures, as are nine of its 20 discard spells, and five of its 14 removal spells.
This is critical because it allows the deck to put on pressure and keep a high creature count for purposes of cards that care about that while still spending a full 20 slots on cards that force opponents to discard and 14 slots each on removal and draw.
My Rata deck does something similar: I cash in around 20 slots up front for ramp, and then the deck is as dense as possible with card utility from there.
Specifically, these decks both place a high priority on draw and removal, running as many cards as possible that give one of these things while also in a different purpose, such as being a beater or an acceleration spell or a land.

This is not done at no cost; there are lots of cards that are quite decent generally in EDH that don’t fit well into this outline.
Protection spells aren’t very compact because they need something to protect in order to gain value.
Anthem-style cards aren’t very compact because they require a board of creatures in order to gain value.
Escalation cards that amplify the power of certain effects aren’t very compact because they require something to amplify to gain value, and mana acceleration that is efficient but temporary isn’t very compact because it requires a specific follow-up plan to gain value.
None of this is to say that these cards are bad; some of these are really fantastic alongside other cards.
But that’s the issue: they’re not reliably helpful on their own, and the more of them you put in a deck, the more fragile it will be.
Each of these decks I just mentioned mostly avoid these cards, making an exception for a small quantity of life gain because staying alive over the course of a long game can be a challenge for a deck that is grindy and compact.

With the idea of compactness in mind, let’s take a look at potentially the greatest enabler of this concept in EDH: the eighth card in hand, so to speak, the commander.

Malagor as a Counterweight Commander

Malagor is a six-cost 6/6 flyer who discards my hand and forces each opponent to sacrifice a creature for each card discarded.
This is an odd choice of commander on the surface; when we look at popular commanders for discard decks, almost all are either an enabler for making opponents discard or a payoff for making opponents discard.
Come on, you’re playing the commander format, the card that’s always available to you should relate to what you’re doing with your deck, right?
However, malagor has proved to be an excellent commander choice for a few different reasons.

For one, malagor fills a specific strategic niche here.
One of the main disadvantages of running a pile of grindy creatures with discard effects and draw effects attached is that these creatures don’t generally attack well into chunky midrange boards.
Malagor serves as a solution to this issue, providing a way to blast away blockers and allow me to keep the pressure on.
Malagor’s effect also pairs fantastically with discard effects.
Normally, opponents might avoid overcommitting to the board if they don’t have tokens to soak up some of the sacrifice triggers from malagor; however, with a stream of discard spells, opponents are pressured into making a terrible choice: either keep that awesome creature in hand and risk discarding it, or play that creature and risk losing it to malagor.

In this way, malagor is an example of what I’ll call a “counterweight commander.” He subverts the weaknesses of a discard deck full of grindy creatures, thereby making the deck better rounded.
Instead of doing the same thing as the deck, he does basically the opposite, and the resulting tension created by this allows for an almost call-and-response-esque play pattern.
My board and discard spells prompt certain play patterns for my opponents, and malagor exists to be my follow-up to those patterns.

Resilience and Embracing the Hellbent State

With a commander that discards my hand on ETB, this deck naturally ended up needing to focus on another factor that’s related to compactness: resilience.
What this means is that a deck can recover from setbacks readily; challenges thrown its way can be shrugged off with relative ease.
This is a good deck attribute to go for in general; it creates more positive play experiences for everybody at the table when a deck can recover easily from setbacks, and most of my decks feature this as a critical feature in some way or other.
However, this deck takes it to a whole different level because it doesn’t wait for opponents to set it back, oh no, this deck takes a baseball bat to its own kneecaps.
