Of the 20 discard spells, more than a third affect me as well, and as I’ve mentioned, the deck’s commander literally makes you discard your hand.
Nobody at the table is allowed to keep cards in hand, not even me.
Step one of this deck is that we’re all getting dragged down into the mud for some rough and dirty Magic: The Gathering gameplay.

The natural follow-up to this is that this deck is specifically engineered to be unusually good at playing under exactly those circumstances.
I mentioned the next draw being mostly creature-based, and the other important fact about the draw suite here is that most of the draw spells are what I’d refer to as “discreet engines.” By “discreet,” I mean that these cards largely don’t rely on other cards to generate card advantage, and by “engine,” I mean that these cards give card advantage over time.
One of these requires a player to be hellbent to be online, which is helped by playing discard spells or malagor, and four others require dealing combat damage to players, which can be assisted by removing opposing creatures, but the rest kind of just hang around and provide a stream of cards with minimal extra input required.
This makes these a wonderful type of card: they’re great as a topdeck, they’re great as a last card kept in hand while playing a big discard spell like Delirium Skeins, and most are also great to land on curve as well.

The other thing that makes this deck resilient is its graveyard package.
There are five different cards that can recur things from the graveyard, and there are also five cards that can be cast from the graveyard or that can otherwise get value from being in the graveyard in some capacity.
There are an additional five cards with Madness and another two that provide benefits as a result of the deck discarding cards.
All of these components combined allow the deck to somewhat mitigate the downsides of discarding large quantities of cards, not fully mitigate, mind you; the deck still gets to a hellbent state and feels it, but it has at least some tools to allow it to play through that hellbent state better than a lot of decks can.

Social Dynamics and Threat Assessment

In addition, an unexpected feature of the deck has been that discarding one’s hand isn’t entirely a bad thing.
See, malagor’s “One with Nothing” ETB also has a social benefit.
The deck’s early turns are often spent stripping away opposing hands, and this is a frustrating experience for my opponents, an experience that is only amplified when malagor comes down to demolish some creatures as well.
Looking at the person who cleared out your hand and killed your creatures would be pretty upsetting if they still had a full grip of cards, and they’d be a magnet for attacks and removal.
However, if they’re also hellbent and have a board entirely consisting of a 6/6 flyer and some utility 3/3s, well, things aren’t as clear.
I have frequently gotten a visceral sense of mixed feelings from my opponents while playing this deck; my opponents wish I was the unambiguous threat so they could have a good excuse to attack me, but I’m often barely ahead at all, and sometimes it’s entirely unclear to my opponents who is ahead.
The threat profile of a low-slung, grindy deck is just not one most EDH players are used to needing to consider.
What do you do with a player who isn’t an imminent threat to your life total, isn’t setting up for a combo, and isn’t scaling up to an overwhelming late game?
The deck is perfectly capable of winning games through a mixture of attrition and beatdown, but it manages to fly under the radar much more than you’d expect, even after doing a bunch of nasty stuff to its opponents.
In this way, malagor is critical to the deck for vibes reasons; he is the key to turning games from an epic wizard duel into a knife fight being fought in four-inch-deep mud for myself and my opponents alike.

The Vibe Check and Rejecting a Central Engine

Speaking of vibes, let’s talk about one of the oddest conclusions of the deckbuilding process.
One of the exciting things about upgrading this deck over the summer was that I could finally play with Waste Not, since the previous version of the deck was an ultra-budget list and since there was now an Enchanting Tales reprint that was both cheap and super cool looking.
I’m playing a discard deck, so a card that gives me various assorted benefits when opponents discard is a perfect fit, right?
However, one of the first games I played with the deck was an utterly disgusting game for the card: turn two Waste Not, turn three Arterial Flow, and continuing onward and upward from there.
It felt great from a power level perspective; the deck was keeping a pretty full grip and accelerating forward in its game plan even as the rest of the table was struggling to keep up under the weight of its discard.
But something also felt very wrong about it.

After a bit of reflection, it became clear what the problem was.
The whole point of the deck was to play scrappy, dragging the rest of the table down into a fight in the mud and then being really good at winning mud fights.
A big piece of what made the deck work on a vibes level was that the deck had no central engine like Waste Not; it had a bunch of little individual ones, but none of them were enough to be a load-bearing pillar for the deck’s game plan.
The fact that advantage is fought for tooth and nail with a bunch of disconnected little engines is a defining feature of the deck, and it’s what makes the deck so grindy and flexible.

Central Engines vs. the Scrappy Grind

This approach contrasts with that of many EDH decks.
It’s quite common to feature a central engine in the command zone; the commander is the center of a synergy pillar, and it has a number of other cards that generate some form of value when played alongside it.
When I go to the top commanders, around half of them follow this mold: Atraxa generates value with counters, Miirym with dragons, Yuriko with ninjas, Miirym also with dragons, Agrus with attack-ability creatures, Jodah with legendaries, you get it.
In the best cases, an engine commander is one that plays to the strengths of a card type, serving as an anchor for that type that hopefully passes the vibe check.
However, not all engines pass that vibe check.
One example of an engine I have mixed feelings about is Angie Falcon Wrath because she doesn’t really play to the strengths of Madness; she can enable them, but the design of t

#66
Deck TechJul 2025173K views

Building a Toolbox Astral Slide Cycling Deck

How I Built My Most Difficult Deck
We explain how they built an Esper cycling deck around Astral Slide, requiring a high density of cycling cards and creatures.They use a network of conditional tutors to form a toolbox that finds key enchantments and artifacts, making the deck flexible and consistent.This approach pushed their deckbuilding beyond normal comfort zones, emphasizing cards that serve multiple roles.
Use this in your deckAim for a high 30s count of cycling cards and at least 30 creatures to enable Astral Slide and Oversold Cemetery.
Sharuum the HegemonWhiteBlueBlackBlinkReanimatorControlSynergyDeck ConsistencyDeckbuilding RestrictionsDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • High-synergy decks require maximizing card utility by ensuring each piece contributes to multiple overlapping needs, especially when slot space is limited by mandatory card counts.
Evidence
  • The deck needed at least 30 creatures, over 30 cycling cards, and seven enablers for graveyard filling, leaving under 40 flexible slots.
  • A network of conditional tutors lets a player access any of the three Astral Slide effects via six other cards or find Fluctuator/Oversold Cemetery via five.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Include enough cycling cards (high 30s) and creatures (≥30) to reliably trigger synergies every game.
  • Build a toolbox by including cards with abilities like wizardcycling or transmute that can fetch specific subsets of your core pieces.
Limitation
  • The interconnected nature makes the deck resistant to easy modification; swapping cards without analyzing the tutor web can break key lines of play.
Example
  • Vedalken Aethermage cycles to find tutors like Zur or spell disruption, itself an instant-speed tutor that counts as cycling for Astral Slide triggers.
More quotes
  1. Every card was meticulously chosen to perform hyperspecific roles and mesh with as many other cards as possible.
  2. I actually left the deck unchanged for about a year starting in mid to late 2023 purely because it was functioning okay and I was terrified that any changes I haphazardly made would lead to the deck falling apart.
  3. The deck needs a lot of cycling cards for purposes of reliable Astral Slide triggers. I ended up deciding that a high 30s count was about right.
  4. This deck ended up being easily my most difficult deck to build, thrusting me well outside my normal comfort zone of deck building.
  5. There's nothing like the rush of tutoring to find a tutor to find a tutor to find an Astral Slide.
  6. This deck is a control deck through and through. You might not be able to tell this by looking at the deck.
  7. My ideal game is one where all of my energy can go into building up my own engine of ETB creatures and cycling spells as well as neutralizing opposing engines and threats early in their lifespan.
  8. Threat evaluation is key to keeping opponents in check without losing too much momentum.
  9. As with most control decks, this deck is trying to reach a state of stability where it has a lot of agency over the state of the game.
  10. A big part of this deck's game plan is repeating value generation and removal, and that repeated removal can be some heavy duty hate over the course of multiple turn cycles.
Full transcript
A Deck That Defied My Norms

Just over a year ago I made a video about the cycling mechanic; that video featured a brief history of the mechanic and associated decks, as well as a discussion of why I dislike a particular Commander printed for the mechanic a few years ago.
That video also features a brief description of my own cycling deck, a Sharuum deck built around the card Astral Slide.
That deck has endured more so than basically any other I’ve built; it’s the only deck built before I started making YouTube videos that both still gets played regularly and also hasn’t changed dramatically.
However, the process of building it was also way different than that of the other decks I’ve built.
My decks broadly tend to be less synergy-focused and more play-pattern-focused, with deck dynamics being unusual but the cards themselves being often somewhat more generic budget staples rather than coming from a particular synergy package.
This deck was a massive exception to that: every card was meticulously chosen to perform hyper-specific roles and mesh with as many other cards as possible.
For that reason, this deck ended up being easily my most difficult deck to build, thrusting me well outside my normal comfort zone of deck building.
I actually left the deck unchanged for about a year starting in mid-to-late 2023, purely because it was functioning okay and I was terrified that any changes I haphazardly made would lead to the deck falling apart, its mysterious special sauce getting tainted.
However, late last year I decided that it was finally time to update the deck, both adding newly printed cards and smoothing out a handful of longtime issues; and with the success I’ve enjoyed with the deck since then, I figured I should give a full overview of how I built the deck, what makes it work, and what it says about how we as EDH players build decks.

Origins of the Deck

To start with, let’s talk about the origins of the deck.
The starting point for building it was browsing a card gallery of Onslaught cards and having a couple particular cards stand out to me.
One of these was Astral Slide, a three-mana white enchantment that flickers a creature whenever a card is cycled, and this offers an effective way to reuse ETBs and temporarily remove opposing creatures.
The other card was Oversold Cemetery, a two-mana black enchantment that returns a creature from its controller’s graveyard to their hand on each of their upkeeps as long as there are four or more creatures in that graveyard; this effect can act as a recursion engine enabled by cycling and can also be used to simply keep up Astral Slide fodder.
From there, I decided that I also wanted blue, since that’s a color that shares a lot of synergy elements with what I was building and which also gives access to a nice range of commanders.
After a brief commander hunt, I ended up settling on Sharuum as an Esper commander with an impactful graveyard-oriented ETB.
These three cards alone already paint a decently clear picture of the sort of deck I was going to be building: an ETB-centered cycling deck with artifact and graveyard subthemes, some key enchantments, and enough creatures to get Oversold Cemetery online.

The Challenges of a Specific Game Plan

If that sounds specific, it’s because it is.
Immediately a handful of challenges presented themselves, different conditions that my deck would need to fulfill in order to be even remotely functional.
For one, the deck needs a lot of cycling cards for purposes of reliable Astral Slide triggers; I ended up deciding that a high-30s count was about right and have stuck with around that number through various iterations of the deck.
Secondly, a reasonably high creature count for Oversold Cemetery, at least 30.
Thirdly, a way to find key cards: the deck relies on Astral Slide for its game plan, and there are only three cards that perform the function of Astral Slide, so some tutors to find them will be very helpful.
Finally, the deck needs ways to get cards in its graveyard, with draw and discard effects seeming most appropriate; I found that seven was basically as low as I could go for these.

Okay, so this is immediately placing a lot of requirements on the deck, with the high-30s count of cycling cards being perhaps the most rigorous.
Even if we consider the 14 available cycling lands in Esper colors and run all of them, which I do, that still means that we’re looking at less than 40 available slots for non-land, non-cycling cards, which means that cards need to perform multiple roles or else be utterly fantastic at their main job.
For ETB creatures, the Mages were a great starting point: three-mana 2/2 Wizards that tutor for an artifact of a specific mana cost, and Sphinx Summoner was a similarly sweet inclusion.
Tribute Mage finds Fluctuator, which is a key card for the deck since it gives great mana savings on cycling back-to-back cards, but aside from that, these cards mostly find secondary cards for the deck.
So what about tutors to find the deck’s key enchantments, Astral Slide and company?

A Toolbox of Conditional Tutors

Since I was already in Esper colors, a natural choice was Zur, a creature who can tutor out low-cost enchantments whenever he attacks; conveniently, he’s also a Wizard, which brings me to a key piece of this deck: Wizardcycling.
There are two different Wizardcycling cards in existence, which end up being solidly costed instant-speed tutors that also count as a cycle for Astral Slide purposes.
As an added bonus, one of these is a flash creature, which is grabbable with the newly printed MDFC Waterlogged Teachings.
I got a chance to do this a couple weeks back, and there’s nothing like the rush of tutoring to find a tutor to find a tutor to find an Astral Slide.
Now, this may seem a bit deranged, and yeah, it kind of is, but it also points to a key facet of this deck: its status as a “toolbox deck.” This phrase means that the deck has a network of conditional tutors that can each find a certain selection of cards that the deck might need in various situations.
For example, the deck’s Wizardcycling cards can find four tutors as well as an excellent recursion card and three cards for purposes of countering opposing spells and abilities; worth pointing out is that all of these work with Astral Slide in some way, gaining additional value off of Slide or finding Slide or triggering Slide.

Delving deeper into the toolbox, the most useful of the three artifact Mages, Treasure Mage, can find life gain, a finisher, multiple types of removal, and a couple value sources, and the fact that it’s repeatable with Astral Slide is a big deal since it lets me load up on top-end threats that I can discard and then Sharuum back out.
Sphinx Summoner can find most of the stuff that Treasure Mage can, since most of them are artifact creatures, and it can also find a board wipe and an angsty bird.
In addition to the selection of conditional tutors, there’s also a couple cards from one of my favorite mechanics in all of Magic: transmute.
These are cards that allow their controller to pay three mana and discard them to tutor for a card of the same mana cost.
I originally had the three-mana enchantment tutor Idyllic Tutor in this deck, but honestly, a three-mana transmute card does a similar job of giving me access to key enchantments and also gives me access to other useful things like the recursion of Teshar, the graveyard hate of Boggart Trawler, the graveyard feeding of Thirst for Knowledge, and so on, as well as being a very funny counter spell on top of it all.
The cumulative effect of all of these individual highly conditional tutors is striking: I have six other cards that can lead me to one of my three key Astral Slide enchantments or one of my top-end artifact creatures, and I have five other cards that can lead me to Fluctuator or Oversold Cemetery.
I also have a lot of cards that can be grabbed by just one or two tutors, meaning that this deck ends up being a massive web of different tutors which can each prompt me with interesting decisions to make.
This quantity of tutors might sound objectionable to some, but, well, hear me out, I’ll get back to that objection.

How the Deck Plays in Practice

With the key cards and an overview of the toolbox out of the way, let’s get to how this deck plays in practice.
Early turns are spent sculpting a hand, ramping, and beginning to assemble value engines.
There are a lot of cards that can be played out early and then Astral Slid later to reuse the ETBs: the artifact Mages, my Sad Robots, my angsty bird, and so on.
This early game can also be a time to feed the graveyard; being able to Sharuum out a massive artifact on turn five or six can offer a nice tempo swing that forces my opponents onto the back foot and gives me room to develop my advantage.
I talk about this deck in terms of tempo and advantage because this deck is a control deck through and through.
You might not be able to tell this by looking at the deck: it’s running a relatively high creature count of 35, and its commander is a generic artifact recursion card from Alara block; its quantity of counter spells, while probably higher than average in raw terms, is nothing crazy and consists mostly of spells which can also either be transmuted or cycled; it’s also not running a single conventional board wipe.
Sure, there’s an incomplete wipe, a sleep effect, a multi-creature edict, and a couple things that reduce power and toughness based on the amount of mana put in, but nothing that says “destroy all” anything.
So what makes this a control deck?
Fundamentally, it comes down to this deck’s play pattern.

Highlighting my deck’s Astral Slide targets in Archidekt immediately gives an indication of this: a big part of this deck’s game plan is repeating value generation and removal, and that repeated removal can be some heavy-duty hate over the course of multiple turn cycles.
More broadly, at any given moment in a game, this deck will be balancing three different goals: stay alive, suppress opponents, and build value.
Staying alive means keeping my life total up and preventing opponents from winning; suppressing opponents means taking actions that worsen my opponents’ position in the game; and building value means doing things that improve my position in the game.
The desirability of working toward these different goals runs from right to left: I love to build up my own value, I’m happy enough to take actions that slow down my opponents and make them less of a threat, and I don’t really like needing to spend time and resources staying alive, but that one’s pretty unnegotiable if I want to keep having a shot at winning a game.
My chances of winning go up and up the more I get to focus on these right two; my ideal game is one where all of my energy can go into building up my own engine of ETB creatures and cycling spells, as well as neutralizing opposing engines and threats early in their lifespan.
So that’s the biggest feature of this deck: balancing these different elements.
Early turns are spent building resources, getting blockers on board, and getting ready to answer anything my opponents are doing that is an existential threat to my position in the game.
Since this is a toolbox deck, every suppression tool that I don’t need to grab means a piece of value or ramp instead, and once I have Astral Slide in play there will often be choices between flickering creatures that are suppressive or defensive in some way and flickering ones that boost my game plan.
As a result, threat evaluation is key to keeping opponents in check without losing too much momentum.
The deck can make more daring early plays, but these often come at the cost of long-term value, so it’s important to weigh the pros and cons and to have follow-up plays in mind.

One natural question might be, well, what do I mean by follow-up?
How does this deck win?
Well, as with most control decks, this deck is trying to reach a state of stability where it has a lot of agency over the state of the game; this state will look different depending on the game because different tools will be required to keep different opposing decks in check.
Here are a handful of things that might be true in the endgame scenario, though, in no particular order: one, a lot of mana, either through land counts built up by flickered robots or Powerstone tokens that can pay for cycling costs; two, a healthy hand of cards, especially one that includes multiple cycling cards as well as things like counter spells and recursion pieces, Nimble Obstructionist is especially nice, as it deals with a lot of pesky abilities that most control decks don’t have a way to handle; three, utility permanents that keep the deck chugging along, such as engines that build value turn over turn and permanents that can disrupt opposing attempts to regain footing; four, a high life total that puts me out of range of dying to any number of common threats, as well as enough blockers to handle any sudden attacks.
Once enough of these conditions are met, winning the game from there is usually straightforward.

A key type of creature for this deck is medium to large flyers, a type which includes the commander, and these constitute a decent threat to life totals given enough time; as long as I’m able to give them that time, they’ll usually win me the game.
However, there will be some games where an opponent will get a very high life total or, alternatively, will be in a highly threatening position with a normal life total, so I run a couple finisher cards as well.
One I alluded to earlier, Magister Sphinx, is a fantastic card, giving me a way to rapidly burst down a problem player; this can be extremely sudden for my opponents.
On the second main phase before my turn, I can play a draw-discard effect to discard Magister Sphinx and then cycle something to flicker Sharuum and bring back Magister Sphinx, turning an opponent’s life total to 10; then on my turn, I can cycle a couple more things to clear out any flying blockers before one-shotting the problem opponent, who could have been at any life total a single turn earlier.
The other finisher I run is Herald of the Forgotten; this can bring back any of my 14 cycling creatures in my graveyard, and importantly it can also bring back any of my 14 cycling lands.
This is great; it’s usually a burst of creatures to contest the board.

#67
Deck TechJul 202584K views

Endless Punishment Commander Deck Review

Snail Reviews a Slug Deck (+ some ban opinions)
Reviewing the Endless Punishment Commander precon, a Rakdos group slug deck, we explain how Valgavoth's ability demands a high density of enablers that cause opponents to lose life on their turns.The deck provides 11 consistent triggers and 15 occasional ones, with a strong removal suite and mana base.We recommend minor upgrades to increase speed and diversify triggers.
Use this in your deckTo consistently trigger Valgavoth by turn five, ensure your deck includes at least eleven cards that cause opponents to lose life on their turns.
Valgavoth, Harrower of SoulsRedBlackMidrangeUpgradesDeck ConsistencyMana BaseSynergyDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • A group slug deck aiming to abuse Valgavoth must incorporate a diverse range of life-loss enablers rather than stacking only repetitive pings, to avoid becoming a linear and easily disrupted archenemy.
Evidence
  • The precon contains 11 cards that reliably cause opponents to lose life on each of their turns, yielding a 78% chance of having at least one by turn five, but only four of these cost three mana or less.
  • It also includes 15 cards that trigger life loss occasionally, such as Blood Artist effects and removal spells, providing backup triggers and utility.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Build your deck with a balance of consistent every-turn damage sources and versatile cards that can trigger life loss incidentally through removal or creature deaths.
  • Include a robust removal package, ideally seven or more targeted removal spells and four board wipes, to manage threats while your slug effects drain opponents.
Limitation
  • The precon's limited number of low-cost enablers (four at three mana or less) may delay explosive starts, as faster decks could outpace the value engine.
  • Blood Artist-style triggers can be inconsistent without dedicated sacrifice outlets, so consider adding sacrifice synergies or replacing them with more reliable pings.
Example
  • Sulfuric Vortex and Underworld Dreams represent the ideal every-turn damage sources that consistently trigger Valgavoth.
  • Blood Artist is a classic example of a creature that can trigger life loss on opponents' turns through removal or combat, showcasing the value of incidental enablers.
More quotes
  1. Pro tip: doubling your opponent's mana is generally not recommended.
  2. Star Athlete commits the cardinal sin of giving opponents choices.
  3. If you think you can bribe opponents to be nice to you with cards, I think you're underestimating how much your opponents hate seeing their life total spiral downward, especially if it's in tandem with you drawing cards.
  4. This deck will make opponents square the hell up.
  5. Cramming as many every-turn pings as possible into one deck flattens the play pattern into one that is predictably brutal for opponents.
  6. There's a reason why my Rakdos group slug deck does exactly the opposite of giving opponents cards.
  7. Vial Smasher's ability doesn't deal enough damage to make up for his otherwise meager body.
  8. Treasonous Ogre is a reasonably popular card, and our Gremlin friend here gives a three times better rate for our opponents to translate life into additional mana.
  9. You don't want to be giving your opponents resources to fight you with because you will be attracting the eye of the whole table.
  10. Enchanter's Bane is theoretically an enchantment hate option but in practice it seems undertuned and gives opponents way too many ways to get around being harmed.
Full transcript
Introduction
The Deck’s Game Plan

Going into this deck, I was kind of curious how much of this sort of thing the precon would be packing, and the answer is honestly more than I expected.
There are 11 different cards that I’d expect to trigger life loss more often than not on each opponent’s turn, and an additional 15 cards that will at least sometimes trigger life loss on an opposing turn.
Beyond these, there are an additional 10 cards that will cause some sort of life loss to opponents at sorcery speed, and it seems like this sort of incremental chip damage is how this deck is looking to close out the game.
A small part of this package seems to be a combination of cards that draw opponents additional cards and cards that damage opponents when they draw; this is backed up by a BR removal suite that packs answers for a wide range of threats.
Finally, a small package worth of life gain is here to keep the deck alive when the heat turns up.
Overall, this deck is looking to build advantage with its commander while gradually knocking down opposing life totals through a broad range of different cards that can dish out incremental damage.

Assessment: What Works

Looking at the other 15 Valgavoth enablers, there’s a wide variety of different effects here.
Six of these are Blood Artist effects, which make a decent bit of sense; the deck packs a good bit of removal and it also runs a relatively high creature count of 32.
This deck doesn’t have much self-sacrifice, but I expect that it should still manage to get triggers often enough for these to feel useful.
The rest are a mixture of different things that will lead to occasional life loss on opposing turns; overall these have a good bit of variety, and oddly, I feel like that’s a good thing.
With a commander this potent, a lot of players building Valgavoth are going to default to cramming as many “every-turn pings” as possible into one deck, and though this does maximize the possible Valgavoth triggers, it also flattens the play pattern of the deck into one that is predictably brutal for opponents; this will lead to the deck being Enemy Number One at all times and not having many cards dedicated to the backup plans that will inevitably be necessary against competent opponents.
By contrast, having a more diverse range of Valgavoth enablers means that this deck’s play patterns and relationship with its commander are more textured to opponents, and a lot of these enablers can also serve useful purposes outside of enabling Valgavoth.
So the instant-speed ping plan looks pretty good to me.

As I mentioned earlier, this deck’s removal suite is likewise pretty good.
It has seven pieces of targeted removal, four board wipes, three removal engines, and one Kardur, he’s not exactly removal, but he removes some scary things from my presence by setting them at a different player for a turn, so that’s basically the same thing, right?
There’s also Brash Taunter; I guess that’s a removal engine if your opponent’s creatures are puny.
Beyond removal, this deck also has moderate amounts of ramp and non-commander draw.
This is a deck that’s going to be drawing a lot of cards and wanting to pay a lot of commander tax, so having a healthy collection of mana rocks is absolutely a good thing.
Non-commander draw is also good; expecting to have the commander in play at all times is highly optimistic, and fitting in a healthy share of other draw is good.
Finally, I support the choice to have some amount of life total stabilization here as well, which includes a few different sources of life gain; this is a deck that’ll attract attacks, and it’s good to be able to weather a bit of that.

The mana base here looks quite solid, if a little tapland-oriented.
In the lands alone we see 25 symbols each for red and black, which are fantastic counts.
There’s a lovely selection of different dual lands: a tango, a cycler, a check land, a pain land, a fast land, a reveal land, an Odyssey filter, and most exciting of all, a Graven Cairns future sight art.
This is great variety in lands for a two-color precon.
As far as utility lands go, the deck runs Leechridden Swamp and Shivan Gorge to go with Valgavoth, a Witch’s Clinic to grant lifelink, and Spinerock Knoll as a hideaway land.
There are some minor tweaks that could be made to tune up this mana base, and I’ll be doing one of them in my upgrade to this deck, but honestly this mana base is going to play just fine right out of the box.
I playtested the deck 20 times to get a feel for it, and in the vast majority of these the deck felt pretty great, curving out relatively smoothly, setting up threats and engines pretty consistently, and seeming generally well poised to push later into the game.
In some ways I find this deck tricky to evaluate because it seems like a strong deck but also a deck that will be a lightning rod for opposing removal and attacks; however, keeping that in mind gives us a good idea of where to start with upgrading.

Upgrading the Deck

A number of other cards I cut because they simply don’t do quite enough to warrant keeping around.
Vial Smasher’s ability doesn’t deal enough damage to make up for his otherwise meager body.
Star Athlete commits the cardinal sin of giving opponents choices, and Tectonic Giant is fine but a bit underwhelming here for four mana.
Grab the Prize and Light Up the Stage are fine as filtering and draw options respectively, but decidedly unimpressive to me compared to some other options in this sphere.
Some other cards are strong but feel out of place in this deck.
Braids is a sweet value engine, but this deck doesn’t run many tokens or ETB permanents that would be desirable sacrifice options.
Kaervek could be nice if he sticks around, but seven mana is a bit too much to pay for what he does in this deck.
Finally, Barbflare Gremlin is a card with potential, but this deck isn’t well set up to break parody on the mana boost it gives; pro tip, doubling your opponent’s mana is generally not recommended.
Treasonous Ogre is a reasonably popular card, and our Gremlin friend here gives a three-times-better rate for our opponents to translate life into additional mana.

What goes in in place of these cards?
Well, most of my additions follow one core principle: this deck will make opponents square the hell up.
One form of this will be that the commander easily forms many disgusting value engines and, as a result, will be a high-priority target for removal.
One way I choose to combat this is protection equipments, specifically Swiftfoot Boots and Champion’s Helm.
The other way I choose to combat this is Glimpse the Impossible, which works as a potential boost of mana to recast the commander as well as serving extra purposes of draw and blockers.
I actually dislike a couple of cards that I kept in this deck, namely Rakdos, Lord of Riots and Séance Board, but these were kept in because I expect this deck to do a lot of recasting its commander, and these cards are undeniably helpful for that.

Moving on, I mentioned squaring up earlier, and aside from removal, the way I expect this to manifest is this deck getting attacked heavily.
Opponents do not like feeling like they’re on a clock, and cards that do consistent player damage over time will trigger exactly that feeling.
To mitigate this, I’m choosing to add some defensive cards to the deck, namely goad and life gain.
Goad also has the side benefit of enabling the deck’s Blood Artist effects, since opponents being in combat with each other is a thing that often results in creatures dying at no cost to the goading player.
Vengeful Ancestor is a classic goad granter that also triggers Valgavoth, and Frenzied Gors Spawn is another goad ETB creature with some added utility.
For non-creature options, Disrupt Decorum is that lovely effect of goading everything, and Spectacular Showdown is a similar card that can also be used to suddenly kill an opponent with an enlarged Valgavoth.
For life gain, Raphael, Fiendish Savior is a lovely package: lifelink for the commander and blockers in the form of Devils, and these Devils can even enable Valgavoth after they block.
I’m also choosing to add Tainted Bonds, which serves double duty as life total stabilizer and Valgavoth enabler.

With defense and protection out of the way, let’s talk about other Valgavoth enablers.
I mentioned earlier that a common choice for naive players building Valgavoth will be cramming in as many cards as possible that deal damage to opponents on each turn.
While I do think that going all in on these would amplify the deck’s weaknesses to removal and opposing aggression, adding in a handful of these to improve consistency still seems useful now that we’ve contained the deck’s weaknesses.

#68
Deck TechJul 20252,737 words in transcript

Commander Deck: Sibsig Ceremony, Aether Vial, Birthing Ritual

I Was Given Three Perplexing Cards to Build a Deck With
We explain how to build an EDH deck around Sibsig Ceremony, Aether Vial, and Birthing Ritual.It uses Iname as One to cheat spirits into play and sacrifice them for value before Sibsig Ceremony destroys them.The deck leverages sacrifice outlets, spirit synergies, and a lands package to win.
Use this in your deckInclude at least 10 sacrifice outlets to turn Sibsig Ceremony's downside into value.
Iname as OneBlackGreenAristocratsReanimatorMidrangeDeckbuilding RestrictionsSynergyResilienceCurveDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • Cards that put creatures into play without casting them, like Aether Vial and Birthing Ritual, can circumvent negative triggered abilities from cards like Sibsig Ceremony.
Evidence
  • Birthing Ritual provides card selection by looking at the top seven cards and replacing a sacrificed creature without casting.
  • The deck uses 10 sacrifice outlets to convert Sibsig Ceremony's destruction into card draw, mana, or discard pressure.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Run a high creature count and a low average mana cost to maximize Aether Vial's impact.
  • Include indestructible commanders or regeneration effects to play around Sibsig Ceremony's destruction.
Limitation
  • Aether Vial does nothing the turn it is played and needs multiple turns to accumulate counters, making it slow in a format where early impact is crucial.
  • Sibsig Ceremony makes it difficult to recast your commander if it dies, so backup plans like graveyard recursion are necessary.
Example
  • With Sibsig Ceremony out, casting Iname as One tutors Iname, Death Aspect, which fills the graveyard, and the commander's death trigger reanimates a spirit like Iname, Life Aspect to repeat the loop.
More quotes
  1. The most challenging thing here is definitely the Sibsig Ceremony, and that's because it comes with a fat downside.
  2. Your creatures literally get destroyed as soon as they enter.
  3. What if your commander wants to die? This brings me to my choice of champion, Ename.
  4. In some sense, this game plan may seem like a combo, but in truth, having as many of its spirits in hand as it wants does not win the game.
  5. No, instead, this deck is a patchwork of different payoffs and win cons that align with the central pillars of this deck.
  6. There's a perfectly adequate spirits mid-range plan.
  7. The final option is Aether Vial. Though this deck doesn't fit the conventional mana curve of an Aether Vial deck, it is at least somewhat predictable in what spirits it will have available and can plan ahead.
  8. The other major option for a win con here is the lands package. And this actually came about quite organically.
  9. Field of the Dead can get us even more zombies. Dark Depths can do its dark and depthy things.
  10. My amount of interaction is already lower than I'd like. So, the only way to make more space would be to cut spirits, which would weaken my spirit synergy cards.
Full transcript
The Challenge: Three Perplexing Cards
Initial Thoughts on the Three Cards

The most challenging thing here is definitely the S6 Ceremony, and that’s because it comes with a fat downside.
Your creatures literally get destroyed as soon as they enter.
Among the five commanders this currently sees significant play in, none include green.
So, we’re going to have to get creative here.

Choosing a Commander
How Iname Breaks the S6 Ceremony
Win Conditions: Spirits and Lands

The other major option for a wincon here is the lands package.
And this actually came about quite organically.
First, a number of specific lands really help out with our game plan.
Urborg for casting the S6 Ceremony, Reliquary Tower for holding a fat hand of spirits, a Cursed Ronin for getting around the S6 Ceremony, Command Beacon for getting Iname into hand, and a few second-tier lands as well.
In addition, a number of cards that naturally fit into the deck just inherently work well with a lands package.
Kura is a classic lands card that’s also a spirit with a death trigger; Wight of the Reliquary is a lovely sackable zombie; and Spirit of the Aldergard is a friendly bear who can find us a Dark Depths.
So, herein lies the final wincon: Field of the Dead can get us even more zombies; Dark Depths can do its dark and depthy things; and a couple manlands can swing real big as a result of this deck’s strange quirks.

Setting Up the Engine: Ramp, Draw, and Space Constraints
Community Builds with the Same Prompt
#69
Deck TechJul 2024253K views

$100 Malcolm Francisco Time Sieve Combo Deck

How I Built My Most Powerful Deck (for $100)
We explain how to build a high-power Commander deck on a $100 budget using Malcolm, Keen-Eyed Navigator and Francisco, Foul Marauder as partners.It focuses on assembling a consistent turn-five combo with Time Sieve by generating treasures and extra artifacts through cards like Academy Manufactor and Worldwalker Helm.
Use this in your deckBridge the gap between Malcolm's three treasures and Time Sieve's five-artifact cost with Academy Manufactor or Worldwalker Helm to begin taking extra turns.
Malcolm, Keen-Eyed Navigator // Francisco, Foul MarauderBlueBlackRedComboBudgetDeck ConsistencyWin ConditionsSynergyBudget Building
Principle
  • Choose a commander pairing that generates both mana and card advantage to support a compact combo.
Evidence
  • The Malcolm and Francisco duo provides a turn-three engine of treasure generation and card filtering, enabling a turn-five combo with Time Sieve.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Include ten one-cost pirates to maximize early treasure production from Malcolm.
  • Use artifact-specific tutors like Fabricate, Reshape, and Whir of Invention to reliably find Time Sieve.
  • Add combat damage-based draw effects such as Bident of Thassa and Curiosity effects to supplement Francisco's filtering.
Limitation
  • The combo speed is limited by Malcolm's maximum of three treasures per turn, requiring setup turns or extra artifact generators.
Example
  • Storm Fleet Negotiator attacks to create map tokens, which Academy Manufactor converts into additional artifacts to help pay for Time Sieve.
More quotes
  1. Malcolm hopefully creates three Treasures per turn and Time Sieve spends five artifacts to grant an extra turn. Something you may notice is that these two numbers, three and five, aren't very far apart.
  2. With two additional artifacts per turn we can just keep on taking turns indefinitely.
  3. [The deck's game plan is a] five turn map. Turns one to three in a conventional game consist of a one cost pirate turn one, Francisco turn two, and Malcolm turn three.
  4. Turn four is the setup turn where I play any other cards that might assist in my combo. Turn five is the combo turn.
  5. Francisco's filtering is lovely but I found that if I don't already have what I need to combo in hand I'm generally using that filtering to aggressively dig for tutors and combo pieces, passing up potentially useful cards.
  6. I added an alternative wincon: Haunted One. This card pumps my team's power by four if both commanders swing, which can turn a handful of evasive Pirates into a veritable player killing machine.
  7. My deck is running 13 counter spells, a number that may strike a lot of casual EDH players as mildly obscene.
  8. A great thing about Treasures is that they're never wasted mana due to their one-time use nature. The fact that they serve this dual purpose of being both stored mana and also artifact tokens for Time Sieve is quite good.
  9. I want untapped colored mana as often as possible. Following from this goal every single land in the deck can theoretically play a pirate turn one.
  10. The deck can often sacrifice a couple treasures and still combo off on time, and even when it does need to spend enough Treasures to delay its combo by a turn, that's still not too bad for me as it forces my opponents to spend mana and lets me avoid needing to cop up the mana to recast Malcolm.
Full transcript

Recently I've been mostly indulging my casual EDH instincts in my videos, as I think there are lots of often neglected things to look for in a deck aside from its ability to win.
Unfortunately, those things are going to have to go on the back burner for this piece, because I'm going to be talking about how I built my sweatiest deck, a deck whose construction process focused on making it as powerful as possible on the budget I gave it.
My hope is that this piece can serve as a bit of a guide on how to build something powerful without breaking the bank, a useful thing to do in a game where different players have different monetary resources to build decks with.
With that said, my construction of this deck began in the place where a lot of decks start: figuring out an intriguing Commander to look into.
In this case, I opted for the partner Commander pairing of Malcolm, Keen-Eyed Navigator and Francisco, Foul Marauder, a pair I picked out because I enjoy Dimir and I also enjoy Pirates.
Malcolm is going to be doing the heaviest lifting of these two, generating Treasures whenever I hit opponents, but Francisco is no slouch either, offering great filtering and rapidly building up in power and toughness for purposes of smacking my opponents.
Ultimately, the sheer versatility that this pairing offers is what makes them so potent.

The Resource Core
Finding a Combo

With this revelation, I went looking for things to help me bridge this gap.
A card that immediately jumped to mind was Academy Manufactor, and this ended up being accompanied by Storm Fleet Negotiator, an evasive pirate that typically produces two to three Map tokens per attack.
The third dedicated option I have for this is Worldwalker Helm, a card printed in The Big Score that operates very similarly to Manufactor; this ended up replacing Ruthless Knave as my third supporting piece.
There are also a few cards that produce one artifact per turn, as well as some cards that produce a single artifact, and I call these "play extenders", cards that serve to buy the deck time when it's beginning to take extra turns but doesn't yet have a way to guarantee these turns being indefinite.

Tutoring for the Combo
The Game Plan and Consistency
The Setup Turn and Supporting Pieces

Another type of card I tend to develop on my setup turn is mana rocks.
Since all of these tap for mana immediately, the two-mana ones effectively cost a single colorless, and weaving them into a turn four can offer a nice burst of mana for my early combo turns and also an artifact to be sacrificed to Time Sieve if needed.

The last main type of card I find myself playing on my setup turn is additional Pirates.
Theoretically, a one-drop pirate, a Francisco, and a Malcolm max out my commander triggers per turn, but realistically creatures die sometimes, and there are also games where I have a one-drop on the ground and need a flyer for an opponent with blockers, or alternatively an unblockable creature to get past flyers an opponent has.
Some of my Pirates have that extra little bit of evasion, and others have other upsides, such as having some sweet removal stapled to them or offering a bit of extra filtering.
A final supportive pirate is Oaken Siren, a bulk common that feels like it was designed just for this deck: it flies, it can tap post-combat to cast my combo pieces, and it itself is an artifact that can be sacrificed to Time Sieve.
Beautiful card; love it.

Since this deck is still a Pirates deck in addition to being a combo deck, I also ended up adding an alternative win condition: Haunted One.
This card pumps my team's power by four if both commanders swing, which can turn a handful of evasive Pirates into a veritable player-killing machine.

The Interaction Suite
The Mana Base
Weaknesses and How to Solve Them
#70
Deck TechJul 2024229K views

Build a Greedy High Curve EDH Deck

How to be a Greedy Bastard in EDH (and get away with it)
We argue that using a low-cost commander as a consistent early mana dork allows a deck to skip cheap ramp spells, filling the 99 with high-cost ramp and large creatures.This structure guarantees powerful cascade hits by avoiding low-mana cards and keeps topdecks relevant.By ramping on turn three into six- and seven-drops, the deck plays as a resilient battlecruiser that can withstand board wipes.Budget constraints and high-curve synergies lead to unique card choices like Screamer-Killer and Emergent Woodworm.
Use this in your deckInclude 19 or more ramp spells that can be cast on turn three when using a two-mana commander to reliably cast a six-drop on turn four.
Radha, Heir to KeldRedGreenRampMidrangeBudgetDeck ConsistencyCurveDesign IntentDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • A commander that provides early mana lets you eschew low-cost ramp in the 99, enabling a higher curve and better topdecks.
  • Eliminating spells costing less than four mana guarantees that cascade and discover triggers always find ramp or impactful plays.
Evidence
  • In a test deck with 19 turn-three ramp spells, the probability of drawing one in the opening hand is 79 percent, enough to consistently cast a six-drop on turn four.
  • When cascading for four or less, there is an 85 percent chance to hit a ramp spell due to the high density of four-mana ramp, making cascade outcomes predictable.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Run at least 19 cards that can ramp on turn three and zero cards that cost less than four mana, excluding the commander, to maximize cascade reliability.
  • Fill the top end with six- and seven-mana creatures that stay relevant as late-game topdecks and pair well with massive land ramp.
  • On a budget, opt for underplayed synergy cards like Screamer-Killer and Emergent Woodworm instead of expensive staples.
Limitation
  • This approach is vulnerable to board wipes, so the ramp suite should prioritize land-based ramp over mana dorks to preserve resources after a wipe.
Example
  • The commander R ada acts as a guaranteed two-drop mana dork, enabling a turn-three four-mana ramp spell like Explosive Vegetation into a turn-four six-drop like Kogla or Etali, Primal Storm.
More quotes
  1. I like making decks that make people question their preconceptions about deck building and about the EDH format as a whole.
  2. This deck plays a shitload of removal to disrupt opposing game plans.
  3. People get annoyed if I spend too much time as the final boss.
  4. What sucks to draw in the late game? Low-cost ramp spells.
  5. Running a lot of low-cost ramp leads to beautiful curve outs and buttery smooth early to mid games, but it also leads to running out of cards faster and worsens the quality of later game top decks.
  6. What if there was a way to do the inverse of what was done in the Goreclaw deck? To have the commander cover the low-cost ramp and then run a massive pile of four-cost ramp?
  7. I run all ten of these [Explosive Vegetation] effects that exist, as well as Blighted Woodland.
  8. I eventually decided on literally none at all [for low-cost cards], and this is for one key reason: cascade.
  9. Even though the deck gets massive land counts, since roughly a third of the deck is creatures that cost five or more mana, it often stocks up a handful of large threats earlier in the game.
  10. For one, it lacks basically any instant speed interaction. If I'm being generous, it has a grand total of four instant speed removal cards and one instant speed protection card.
Full transcript
A Greedy Battlecruiser Problem

A little while back, somebody mentioned to me the idea of running Radha, Heir to Keld as a commander, and this got my noggin joggin’.
You know what sucks to draw in the late game?
Low-cost ramp spells.
Any ramp deck worth its salt needs to coordinate the ratio of ramp to other stuff, and also the ratio of low-cost ramp to high-cost ramp.
Running a lot of low-cost ramp leads to beautiful curve-outs and buttery smooth early to mid games, but it also leads to running out of cards faster and worsens the quality of later game top decks.
I built a Goreclaw deck a while back that ran 16 low-cost ramp spells with the goal to land Goreclaw on turn two or three and then vomit out a massive amount of power and toughness on turns four and five; the deck did a great job of accomplishing exactly that task, but it pretty much either won quickly or ran out of steam after eating a couple removal spells or a board wipe.
Running a lot of draw is the best way to avoid this in a deck that runs a lot of low-cost dorks, but despite my best efforts I couldn’t manage to cram the necessary amount of draw into the deck without incurring a substantial cost to the beefiness of the deck.

On the opposite end of the ramp spectrum, there’s the option of running a lot of high-cost ramp instead; this preserves top-deck quality and lessens the number of cards needed to achieve the same degree of ramp, however it will lead to slower games and fewer keepable hands, worsening the mulligan phase and its ability to get off the ground.
But what if there was a way to do the inverse of what was done in the Goreclaw deck , to have the commander cover the low-cost ramp and then run a massive pile of four-cost ramp?
This is where Radha comes back in as a two-drop mana dork that’s available at all times.

Rethinking Ramp with Radha

So this was my start in building Radha: four-cost ramp that I can play on turn three.
There are some different options here, including some really potent mana dorks that might allow for an extra explosive turn four to five, but I made my decision of what to put here largely with the intent of hedging around a potential weakness for the deck.
See, green stompy decks tend to struggle with one type of card above all others: board wipes.
While mana dorks often allow for faster acceleration than other types of ramp, they have the downside that they generally get eaten by board wipes alongside the big threats that prompted the wipe.
A big goal for this deck was to be able to ramp relatively quickly into six- and seven-drops but also be able to play through hate and keep scaling into the late game.

Four-Cost Ramp and the Cascade Guarantee

With my core idea of the deck consisting of Radha and a pile of four-cost ramp, I had a question on my hands: how much low-cost stuff should I run?
I knew that it wouldn’t be a big amount, and I eventually decided on literally none at all.
This is for one key reason: cascade.
A big downside of cascade is how luck-based it is , when you play a five-cost cascade card, it might hit a four-cost card, but it also might hit a one-cost card; however, if you simply don’t run any cards that cost less than four, you’re guaranteed a pretty decent minimum level of value from your cascade trigger.
This fact, paired with my density of ramp on four, means that cascades and discovers are actually reasonably predictable.
Cascading for a four-or-less cost card yields an 85% chance of finding a card with some sort of ramp attached, and cascading for a five-or-less card yields a two-thirds chance of finding specifically a ramp spell but an additional 14% chance of finding a card that cascades for four , which, I must remind you, has an 85% chance of finding a ramp spell.
Cascading for a six-or-less cost card has a more modest 40% chance of finding a ramp spell, but if we account for the cascade and discover cards, this ends up getting boosted up to a 55% chance.
What this silly jumble of numbers means in practice is that the deck is effectively running a high-twenties number of ramp spells, and all of them ramp for at least two mana or have other upside.
Translation: we’re in lands town, baby.

Spending the Mana: Threats and Removal

A big theme among the rest of the Radha deck’s high-cost creatures is something that it shares with my Glissa deck: a large portion of the deck’s creatures have some sort of removal attached.
This is great, as it allows the deck to pack a lot of ways to deal with opposing threats while also allowing the deck to put on pressure by having a large density of big creatures that it starts playing on turn four.
The deck also packs some amount of draw, but far less than you’d expect; there are seven engines and two one-time draw spells.
While playing the first dozen or so games with the deck, I’ve kept expecting to need to add more draw, but it just hasn’t been necessary.
The deck does a perfectly fine job maintaining resources in its hand.

Draw and Deck Thinning
Flexibility and Resilience
The Downsides of Consistency
Chasing the Vibe
#71
Deck TechJul 2024145K views

How a Budget Boros Deck Uses Board Wipes as Sac Outlets

The Most Complicated Deck I've Ever Played
A budget Boros artifacts deck in Commander uses Gerrard, Weatherlight Hero for Second Sunrise effects.Lacking expensive cards like Krark-Clan Ironworks, it substitutes board wipes as single-use sacrifice outlets.This allows a midrange gameplan that controls aggressive opponents before comboing off with Reckless Fireweaver.
Use this in your deckSupplement your five sacrifice outlets with eight artifact-and-creature-destroying board wipes to enable budget Eggs loops.
Gerrard, Weatherlight HeroRedWhiteComboMidrangeAristocratsBudgetDeckbuilding RestrictionsSynergyInteractionDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • Budget constraints often force deckbuilders to find creative alternatives to expensive staple cards.
  • Board wipes can serve multiple roles: as sacrifice outlets, control tools, and enablers for combo decks.
Evidence
  • The deck supplemented its five sacrifice outlets with eight board wipes to ensure consistent artifact recursion.
  • In play, board wipes were used to clear aggressive boards while simultaneously triggering Gerrard's ability to recur artifacts.
Deckbuilding Application
  • If you cannot afford KCI or Altars, run at least eight board wipes that destroy artifacts and creatures.
  • Adopt a midrange gameplan: use early board wipes to generate incremental value before committing to the combo turn.
Limitation
  • Without fast mana from KCI, the deck needs more permanents in play to become mana-positive during loops.
  • The Boros color identity lacks tutors, making it harder to find critical pieces like sacrifice outlets.
Example
  • Gerrard, Weatherlight Hero provides a built-in Second Sunrise effect when he dies, key to the Eggs loop.
  • Reckless Fireweaver turns every artifact entry into damage, serving as the deck's primary win condition.
More quotes
  1. It's an immensely complex and layered deck that defies a lot of conventional wisdom about deck building.
  2. The deck's convoluted nature is an emergent factor from a number of pressures that existed during the deck building process.
  3. Board wipes function as a single-use sack outlet for all nonland permanence, allowing a full refresh of the artifacts and creatures that the deck has on board.
  4. Without [Krark-Clan Iron Works], the deck needs to develop a lot more permanence that can generate mana before its loops with Gerrard can become mana positive.
  5. The budget constraint results in a slower deck that inherently needs to play more reactively.
  6. A common play pattern for the deck is to perform one or more isolated loops prior to its big turn, wiping the board with Gerrard in play in order to slow down opponents and generate a handful of cards off of a developing board of eggs.
  7. The deck blend of mid-range control and storm elements means that a player must simultaneously balance a whole host of strategic factors.
  8. A pilot of the deck needs to consider the merits of a number of different play paths, but each of those play paths involves dozens of different decisions along with a number of probabilistic factors.
  9. Finding its way to a win can feel like threading the needle where there are countless factors to balance and where a single tiny mistake can doom the deck to failure.
  10. [Hans,] the resident combo player and the person who built the deck, had a 28% win rate with it, while all other players had a collective 19% win rate.
Full transcript
A Complicated Boros Combo Deck

In my last video I discussed a group of $25 decks I played with a few years back.
The details aren't important, but the general idea was that my playgroup developed a pool of budget decks that evolved over the course of two years and developed its own internal metagame.
Over the course of that video, I referred to the Boros deck of the pool as a combo deck, a graveyard‑centric artifacts deck, a flexible combo deck, and finally as an “eggs” deck.
Later on, I intentionally didn't talk about how that deck actually functioned, and this is for the reason that it's an immensely complex and layered deck that defies a lot of conventional wisdom about deck building.
On the surface, it's not that hard to describe what the deck is trying to do.
When I posted the deck list to the deck a while back, I wrote in the description that it was an artifact‑based deck that is looking to stay alive through Aristocrat‑style mid‑range in the early and mid game and eventually launch into an “egg” style storm turn to close out the game.
The most common way it won was by playing a lot of artifacts with a Reckless Fireweaver in play in order to burn out its opponents, a decidedly comprehensible game plan to most players.
So where's the complexity?
Well, the deck's convoluted nature is an emergent factor from a number of pressures that existed during the deck building process, and to get to those I need to go back to that phrase “eggs.”

What Are Eggs?

If you're unfamiliar, an “egg” is a low‑cost artifact that you can crack for a card, often played alongside egg variants that give a card on entry or that can be sacrificed to give some other benefit.
An “eggs” deck is generally one that looks to win in a single explosive turn looping a bunch of these eggs to generate cards, mana, and other resources.
This is done through cards like Second Sunrise, which can recur all of the artifacts a player sacrificed earlier in a turn, thereby netting value off of cards with effects when they enter, leave, or are sacrificed.
Though the archetype saw enough Modern play to get one of its key cards banned along with several supporting cards, it hasn't seen nearly as much exploration in Commander.
However, it's decidedly a possible deck in Commander, with one card above all others bolstering the deck to being a terrifying machine: Krark‑Clan Iron Works.
This card serves a few purposes.
For one, it's an artifact sacrifice outlet, which offers easy sacrificing of artifacts before a Second Sunrise and can also allow the deck to mitigate the danger of certain types of removal by sending cards to the graveyard instead of a different zone.
It's also an incredible mana engine; eggs is a storm deck at its core, one that is hungry for ways to keep piling on cards and mana to prolong its storm turn, and Krark‑Clan Iron Works basically single‑handedly guarantees that the deck will have all the colorless mana it could ever need on its big turn.
Finally, as with Phyrexian Altar and Ashnod's Altar, the card is involved in a truly upsetting number of different infinite combos, so it's a pretty phenomenal card, able to take an eggs deck from a modest board position to a win in pretty short order.
However, the catch here is that the card is decidedly out of the price range for a $25 deck, and it is joined in this fact by the Altars, cards that could offer a similar benefit for creature sacrificing.
So how did the deck actually end up getting built without these pivotal tools?
To get into that, let's take a step back and talk about the commander.

Gerrard, Weatherlight Hero as Commander

My friend Hans picked up for the deck Gerrard, Weatherlight Hero, a somewhat niche commander from 2019, and basically what he does is trigger a Second Sunrise effect when he dies.
This is nice for adapting eggs to the Commander format, as there aren't that many Second Sunrise‑type cards, a fact that might otherwise pose difficulties in a 100‑card Singleton format.
However, the Boros color identity does pose some problems on its own.
A lack of black and blue excludes some of the more explosive win conditions, such as Marionette Master, and more importantly it mostly excludes the deck from having access to tutors in order to get easier access to critical cards.
So despite having a potential solution for the Second Sunrise problem, the deck still had to solve the problem of needing to draw into a sacrifice outlet in order to extract full value out of its commander, and the nature of Gerrard means that the deck wants both an artifact sacrifice outlet and an option for sacrificing Gerrard.
Even if we set aside the premium sacrifice outlets I mentioned earlier, there is still an unfortunate pattern: most of the stronger sacrifice outlets were out of the budget range for a $25 deck as of 2020, and most of the cheaper sacrifice outlets are pretty mediocre, either not doing much else or requiring additional costs to activate.
The deck ended up running three artifact sacrifice outlets: Ravenous Intruder and Atog offer a potential beatdown win condition in addition to being sacrifice outlets, and Slobad, Goblin Tinkerer can also provide protection for key artifacts.
Likewise, Martyr's Cause offers creature protection, and Thermopod is a sort of budget Phyrexian Altar, meaning that all five of these have additional utility outside their role as sacrifice outlets.
But these numbers are decidedly not enough for a deck where having a sacrifice outlet is so fundamental to the operation.

Board Wipes as an Emergent Strategy

Hans opted to supplement these with eight board wipes that could destroy both artifacts and creatures.
This is the first type of special sauce that made the deck work: board wipes function as a single‑use sacrifice outlet for all nonland permanents, allowing a full refresh of the artifacts and creatures that the deck has on board.
Not only this, they help address a key problem caused by the deck lacking access to Krark‑Clan Iron Works and the Altars: it's speed.
Without the explosive mana boost provided by these sorts of cards, the deck needs to develop a lot more permanents that can generate mana before its loops with Gerrard can become mana positive and allow it to fully pop off, and while this will usually happen eventually, it can take a while.
Running a bunch of board wipes helps with that, giving the deck tools to clear out a concerning board that might otherwise spell its demise.
This is part of why I refer to the deck as “mid‑range eggs.” A common play pattern for the deck is to perform one or more isolated loops prior to its big turn, wiping the board with Gerrard in play in order to slow down opponents and generate a handful of cards off of a developing board of eggs.
This would be an unusual choice in a lot of more conventional eggs decks, a flagrant waste of a precious Second Sunrise effect, but at a budget point without access to powerful cards that would speed up the deck, it's an entirely sensible choice.
In this way, we can see how the budget constraint results in a slower deck that inherently needs to play more reactively, and play more reactively it does.

Adapting to an Aggressive Metagame

The deck's slowness gets compounded by another factor that the thumbnail of my last video alludes to: the fact that the pool got increasingly aggressive over time.
In theory, a slow combo deck is the worst possible thing to be against a pod of aggro decks.
The explosive nature of the deck will draw attacks from nervous opponents, and the focus on putting together the combo rather than building a sizable board will often leave combo decks without the blockers needed to stave off pressure from opponents.
Indeed, this was the grizzly fate of several attempted combo decks in the pool, most notably the Lazav infect deck and the Dralnu artifact combo deck.
So how did this deck avoid that fate?
Well, for starters, a lot of the stuff that's in the deck anyway does a decent job at holding off aggro.
The deck runs a decent number of utility creatures it can block with in a pinch, and as previously discussed, the deck's high density of board wipes discourages aggressive opponents from fully committing to the board in order to go for a quick kill.
However, as the pool got more aggressive, it nonetheless became clear that the deck was struggling to keep its life total from getting chipped down before it was able to storm off.
This problem found two solutions.
For the first, it simply added additional creatures.
Cards like Pilgrim's Eye and Wall of Omens are worse than equivalent non‑creature artifacts in a game without opponents, but when you consider what an asset blockers can be for staying alive, they become more justifiable.
Beyond these, the addition of cards like Reveillark, Vesperlark, and Sun Titan offered both additional blockers and a more robust grind factor.
Not only did these cards allow the deck to exist comfortably in the pre‑storm phase for longer, the deck also ends up with a non‑storm game plan that's powerful enough to compete and win on its own as long as an opponent hasn't pulled too far ahead.
Opponents might even feel the need to pull the trigger on using removal and graveyard hate during the deck's mid‑range phase, which then opens the door to rebuild and storm off without the obstacle of that interaction.

The Complexity of Piloting the Deck

With all this context in mind, how exactly did this eggs deck end up being such a complex deck and such a challenging deck to play?
Well, on the surface there are some decision chains that exist for toolbox combo decks in general, such as how early to play a developing card like a tutor and what to search for with it.
There are also some difficulties in knowing exactly when to attempt a storm turn and when not to.
However, I'd wager that the most difficult thing about this deck is its sheer flexibility and the way it blends mechanical complexity and strategic complexity.
On a purely mechanical level, performing various tasks with this deck requires a fine attention to detail, and optimizing a turn that has potentially dozens of different possible play paths can require a lot of concentration, even for an experienced pilot of the deck.
At the same time, the deck's blend of mid‑range, control, and storm elements means that a player must simultaneously balance a whole host of strategic factors: are opponents doing something that demands the deck come up with an answer, or something that implies that opponent has hate the deck should play around?
If an opponent is playing aggressively, should the deck dig in its heels and play defensively, or would doing so slow it down to where a different opponent is able to get too far ahead?
Is an opponent's game plan scaling to where the deck needs to storm off in an approaching turn in order to avoid losing, and would setting up for that storm turn expose the deck to attacks or hate cards?
Is the deck even capable of storming off in that time frame?
Even a decision as simple as whether to sacrifice an egg can have big implications: sacrificing it might allow the deck to dig for useful cards and keep its card velocity up, but each egg sacrificed weakens Gerrard's loops, and sacrificing too many may leave the deck with too few eggs to begin storming off in a reasonable time frame.
Ultimately, it's this combination of macro and micro that makes the deck so difficult to play.
A pilot of the deck needs to consider the merits of a number of different play paths, but each of those play paths involves dozens of different decisions along with a number of probabilistic factors, so figuring out the correct decision can involve thinking multiple turns in advance down play paths which each individually have a tremendous amount of complexity and elements to consider.
Finding its way to a win can feel like threading the needle, where there are countless factors to balance and where a single tiny mistake can doom the deck to failure.

Win Rates and the Challenge of Mastery

For this reason, the deck was by far the hardest to play of any of the decks in the deck pool I talked about last video.
As the resident combo player and the person who built the deck, Hans had a 28% win rate with it over the course of all the games with it, while all other players had a collective 19% win rate with the deck.
Even once I knew the deck well enough to win with it, I still slow‑played like crazy, because the task of finding the way to a win is so inherently packed with difficulty, even for an experienced pilot of the deck.
In that way, the deck was probably not the most ideal as a part of a public deck pool, but it was an insanely cool deck at the correct power level, so it wasn't like we were going to dismantle it.

What Makes a Deck Complex?

There are plenty of concepts that are gestured to by a deck like this, but perhaps the most pronounced is what it says about what makes decks complex in the first place.
A lot of EDH players seem to have the mindset that higher‑powered decks are going to be inherently more interesting, more layered, more complicated, and as a matter of averages they're not necessarily wrong.
Your average cEDH deck is going to involve a lot more decision‑making and is going to leverage player skill much more seriously than your average mid‑power battlecruiser deck, but this factor isn't inherent to power level or budget or deck speed.
Primarily, a deck's complexity relates to two factors: how difficult it is for the deck to complete various tasks and how hard a deck needs to try to navigate around its opponents.
Your average cEDH game is certainly going to involve a whole lot of that second one due to the nature of competitive EDH, but on the other hand, Thoracle Consultation is pretty piss‑easy as far as ways to win the game go; there's more complexity involved in finishing off opponents with Slivers or Ur‑Dragon than a two‑card instant win combo.
Likewise, if we consider the hypothetical non‑budget eggs deck that runs Krark‑Clan Iron Works and ways to tutor for it, it's naturally going to be easier than the budget version on both of these axes.
Winning is easier because KCI effortlessly bypasses most issues that might slow the deck's storm plan, and navigating around opponents is also easier because there's no need to engage with the board.
The deck could add in a healthy suite of protection, removal, and potential blockers, but as with most storm decks, it's almost certainly better to focus on speed and streamlining the deck's proactive plan rather than fussing over whatever opponents are doing.

#72
Deck TechJul 20243,460 words in transcript

Grand Larceny Precon Deck Analysis and Upgrades

Is Gonti Confused or am I Confused?
We analyze the Grand Larceny Commander precon, highlighting its combination of theft and evasive creatures.We argue the deck lacks sufficient low-cost evasive creatures to reliably trigger Gonti’s ability.The mana base and removal are praised, but the overall cohesion is criticized.Suggestions include adding cheaper evaders and cutting non-synergistic theft cards.
Use this in your deckIncrease the number of evasive creatures that cost less than five mana to consistently trigger Gonti’s theft ability each turn.
Gonti, Canny InquisitorBlueBlackRedAggroMana BaseSynergyCurveUpgradesDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • Theft decks need a dedicated win condition beyond stealing opponents’ bombs.
  • Linking theft to combat damage forces deckbuilders to include evasive creatures that can win games.
Evidence
  • The precon has only 12 evasive creatures below five mana, which is too few to reliably trigger Gonti each turn.
  • The mana base is solid, with few colorless utility lands and a variety of dual lands.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Add more cheap evasive creatures to start triggering Gonti’s theft ability early.
  • Cut theft spells that don’t reduce casting cost or don’t benefit from Gonti’s discount.
Limitation
  • Gonti’s one-mana discount is modest, so failing to trigger him consistently causes you to fall behind.
  • Many evasive creatures in the precon are overcosted, delaying the commander synergy without additional ramp.
Example
  • Ramirez DePietro Pillager is a similar commander that combines theft and cost reduction, illustrating the design pattern.
More quotes
  1. I classify Gonti as a soft payoff: a payoff that is pretty easy to get some value off of, but which scales only modestly with additional inputs.
  2. Wizards, please just put things to tap for colored mana in your precons. Just do it, I know you can.
  3. Wizards of the Coast seems to have made the choice to give this precon a $30 mana base rather than give one or two powerful cards to justify the precon price, and I honestly fully support this. New players need good lands a lot more than they need a Trouble in Pairs.
  4. This deck reminds me a lot of older precons in terms of the cohesiveness of its vision, or lack thereof.
  5. The current suite of evasive creatures just doesn't work for me. This deck wants three Gonti triggers a turn and that's just not going to be consistently fulfilled by this selection.
  6. Cunning Rhetoric is a mixture of a defense card and a theft card, but it doesn't actually accomplish either task.
  7. The average non-land card in an EDH deck is like 3.5 mana, so you're getting less value than the amount of mana you paid [with Stolen Goods].
  8. Running either of these cards when the deck doesn't even have enough evasive creatures to consistently get its triggers each turn feels wrong on a pretty fundamental level.
  9. High cost counter spells with added bonuses do work, but mostly only in draw-go style control decks.
  10. Plasm Capture is also very symbol dense, so I'd cut it for a lower cost counter spell like Negate.
Full transcript
Introduction
Gonti, Canny Inquisitor and the Theft Game Plan

With that out of the way, let’s talk about Gonti in particular.
I classify Gonti as a “soft payoff,” a payoff that is pretty easy to get some value off of but which scales only modestly with additional inputs.
The amount of cards stolen per turn by Gonti caps at three, and a one-mana discount on stolen spells is also pretty modest for a five-mana commander, meaning that we’ll likely be falling behind if we aren’t maxing out the advantage we can from Gonti starting reasonably early in the game.
For this reason I expect some amount of a Gonti deck to be made up of evasive creatures that can get down to thieving not long after Gonti has hit the board; it doesn’t need to be a whole ton, one trigger for each opponent means we’re not playing Edric or anything, but if we’re not getting three triggers per turn a decent amount of the time, we’re leaving advantage on the table.
Another possible choice with Gonti would be running additional cards that steal, but running too many of these effects might be a bit of a trap; we only have so much mana to spend on opposing cards.
