So these are going to be the immediate two things I look at: a decent amount of evasive creatures for getting Gonti triggers, and maybe a modest amount of other cards that steal.

Looking at the creature base of the deck, we do see 18 creatures with flying or another form of hard evasion; there are also a number of cards that encourage dealing combat damage to a player, a category which obviously includes the commander.
As a result, this deck has a decently potent evasive creatures game plan which it can use to generate value off the commander and other cards.
As expected, we can also see a small suite of cards in the 99 that can steal cards and reduce the cost of stolen cards, and these will naturally lead the deck into a late game plan of playing lots of opposing cards, using theft in tandem with its evasive creatures to bring the deck to victory.

The Mana Base and Early Interaction

Another thing I like is that this deck has a decent amount of removal.
The deck has two creatures that bonk stuff when they hit players, a couple board wipes, and a handful of pieces of targeted removal; it’s also running a couple counterspells, though both are playing into the theft vibe here and end up being a bit subpar as a result for reasons I’ll talk about a bit later.
In addition to this, the deck’s ramp suite and value engines are decent enough, which should allow the deck to keep on chugging into the late game and not run out of steam.
The deck’s ramp, draw, and interaction will also be supplemented by cards stolen from opponents, though I support the choice to have a decent amount in to start with in these categories so that we’re not relying too much on opponents in order to play the game.

Where the Deck Falls Short: Cohesion and Curve

Another issue is that there are lots of cards that do some sort of stealing-oriented thing but don’t require paying mana to cast the cards.
This might seem normal at first blush, but there’s actually basically no mechanical connection between these cards and the theft theme as a whole; they’re identical to a random mid-range beater as far as cohesion and synergy are concerned.
This is not to say that these cards are all bad or not worth running, Thieving Skydiver is a lovely card, Dazzling Sphinx is a solid evasive creature, and the ninjas are fun payoffs for evasive creatures, what it is to say, though, is that this deck is effectively spending some number of card slots on flavor.
That isn’t an objectively wrong choice, but for a mass-market playable out-of-the-box product, it’s certainly a choice.

Playtest Results
Problem Cards

Next up, redundant cards.
I strongly dislike Predator’s Hour; it does the same thing as the command zone but without any real upside other than a possible tokens plan.
I like Dream-Thief’s Bandana a bit better, but it still compares very unfavorably to a similar equipment such as Mask of Memory or Mask of Riddles.
Running either of these cards when the deck doesn’t even have enough evasive creatures to consistently get its triggers each turn feels wrong on a pretty fundamental level.
It’s at this point I should jump back to the two counterspells I mentioned earlier.
Both cost a lot of mana, and high-cost counterspells with added bonuses do work, but mostly only in draw-go style control decks; this is because these decks will often run spells that further their game plan at instant speed, most often card draw, and this allows them to counter a thing if they need to but still have a way to spend that mana on the end step before their turn if they don’t end up countering anything.
By contrast, this deck runs very little at instant speed, meaning that if it holds up four or five mana for a counterspell, that’s just wasted mana if the counterspell doesn’t get used, and this will often incentivize players to counter something largely irrelevant in order to not waste the mana.
Plasm Capture is also very symbol-dense, so I’d cut it for a lower-cost counterspell like Negate.
Smirking Spelljacker will stay in for now, despite my misgivings about its mana cost, and this is because it’s also a 3/3 creature with flying, which means that countering something largely irrelevant is still a mostly acceptable outcome.

Improving the Deck: Two Paths
Cuts to Refocus
#73
Deck TechJul 20242,415 words in transcript

Gruul Control Deck Analysis with Erinis and Street Urchin

Can Gruul Control? | Patron deck breakdown
We analyze a Gruul control deck pairing Erinis, Gloom Stalker with Street Urchin.It uses land recursion and sacrifice-based removal to control the board, then wins with commander damage or big creatures.The deck is highly consistent but can frustrate opponents at mid-power, and upgrading it for higher-power tables is challenging.
Use this in your deckWhen building a deck around land recursion and sacrifice, limit generic ramp to one piece and prioritize cards that put lands in your graveyard or create sacrifice fodder.
Erinis, Gloom StalkerGreenRedControlVoltronLandfallDeck ConsistencyPower LevelUpgradesMana BaseDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • Control decks can win games with less powerful finishers than mid-range decks because they generally have more time and less onboard resistance.
Evidence
  • The deck has 19 token generators and 11 other sacrificial artifacts or creatures to fuel the removal engine.
  • The only non-synergistic ramp is Sol Ring, enabling a consistent curve that plays Erinis on turn 3 and Street Urchin on turn 2 or 4.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Prioritize ramp like Harrow effects and sacrifice lands that directly support your commander.
  • Include protection spells for a commander-reliant deck, but also leverage the commander’s low cost and built-in ramp to make recasting less punishing.
  • Use artifact lands and treasure generators as dual-purpose cards for mana and sacrifice fodder.
Limitation
  • The deck’s creature removal efficiency can create social friction at mid-power tables where opponents expect to develop a board.
  • Adding faster generic ramp like mana dorks reduces the density of synergy enablers, making the deck harder to upgrade to higher power levels.
Example
  • Canan of Calamity creates Myriad tokens that can be sacrificed for removal after dealing damage.
  • Humble Defector can be sacrificed in response to its ability for free removal.
More quotes
  1. The removal engine in the command zone is pretty fearsome: at any point in time you can pay one and sacrifice an artifact or creature to kill an opposing creature.
  2. This is a decidedly mid-power deck in terms of its speed and how it wins the game, but for a mid-power deck it's brutally effective at killing creatures.
  3. There will naturally be players at that power who aren't thrilled to have their board kept empty of meaningful threats the whole game.
  4. The deck's choice not to run ramp that doesn't support the command zone is part of what lets it jump into its core game plan with such a high degree of consistency.
  5. If you start replacing Harrow effects and treasure generators with ramp that lets you get out Erinis on turn two, you'll get Erinis triggers earlier but you'll have on average fewer lands in the graveyard and fewer tokens to sacrifice, negating some of that increase in speed.
  6. I don't think it's worth it to make major changes to this deck, and even the handful of additions and cuts I would make are subjective choices and still maintain the middling power level of this deck.
  7. The deck's conundrum where upgrading is hard but playing at mid power can feel weird is one I find familiar.
  8. A Child of Alara and Maze's End deck will likely perform decently at a midrangey mid power table, but it's a deck that will fall on its face in a handful of matchups while creating a miserable table experience in all the other ones.
  9. As long as people know what the deck is capable of and to expect their creatures to get yeeted, it can play into a flexible archenemy dynamic.
  10. This deck's game plan should be to outgrind, not outrun. Generating resources and stabilizing the board state should be your focus, not rushing to find combo pieces.
Full transcript
The Commander Pairing: Two Tools in One
The Game Plan: Land Recursion and Removal
Assessing the Removal Engine
Supporting the Engine: Lands, Draw, and Consistency
The Ramp Suite and Curve
Winning the Game: Control Finishers
Mana Base and Protection
The Social Challenge and Power Level
Route One: Increasing Speed
Route Two: Adding Protection and Dealing with Color Limits
Route Three: Upgrading Finishers
The Upgrade Conundrum and Mid-Power Dynamics

As long as people know what the deck is capable of and expect their creatures to get yeeted, it can play into a flexible archenemy dynamic where the table tries to figure out how many resources to spend suppressing it while also keeping in mind that fully locking it out removes a roadblock for their opponents to win with a creature-based win condition.
This can lead to some really cool gameplay; my Ghisa deck generally tends to play into a similar dynamic, but it’s important to make sure that the table is into that sort of gameplay.
It’s also a type of deck that should be played sparingly, in my opinion.
It can be fun to occasionally pull out a deck that demands the table’s full attention, especially if the table is full of solitaire mid-range, but pulling out that type of deck too often can lead to other people feeling like a side character in the four-player game they’re playing.

Final Thoughts
#74
Deck TechJul 20241,944 words in transcript

Fixing the Blame Game Precon's Mana and Defense Issues

What is going on with Blame Game?
The Blame Game precon has a fun goad-focused commander and decent mid-game plan, but its mana base relies too heavily on colorless utility lands and it includes too many narrow defensive cards.We recommend cutting about a quarter of those defensive cards, improving colored mana sources, and adding haste enablers to keep the goad engine active.These changes smooth out the curve and increase consistency.
Use this in your deckCut at least eight defensive cards from the Blame Game precon and replace them with haste enablers and board protection to improve consistency.
Nelly BorcaWhiteRedMidrangeControlGroup HugMana BaseCurveTable PoliticsUpgradesDeckbuilding Principle
Principle
  • A goad deck should balance defense with proactive threats and reliable colored mana, avoiding overreliance on reactive enchantments.
Evidence
  • The precon's manabase contains 11 colorless utility lands, leaving only 17 red sources far below the 26 needed for double-red cards like Vengeful Ancestor.
  • Playtesting revealed a lack of early drops and only a handful of good three-drops, forcing aggressive mulligans to find a decent curve.
  • The deck's 33 defensive cards often became dead draws when opponents developed non-combat win conditions.
Deckbuilding Application
  • Cut approximately eight of the most narrow or expensive defensive cards.
  • Add haste enablers such as Anger to re-enable Nelly's goad ability after removal.
  • Replace several colorless utility lands with colored sources to improve mana consistency.
Limitation
  • The deck's late-game finishing power relies on Nelly having drawn extra cards through her attack trigger.
Example
  • Vengeful Ancestor is a double-red card that requires 26 red sources, but the stock precon provides only 17.
  • Cards like Ghostly Prison and Disrupt Decorum are defensive, but the deck runs too many similar effects, cluttering hands.
More quotes
  1. I've never owned a precon. Wizards of the Coast has been releasing preconstructed Commander decks ever since I started playing in 2011 and with increasing frequency, and I've just kept on ignoring them.
  2. I didn't start playing Commander until 2014 at which point I felt like a skilled enough player to build my own mono removal pile to piss off my friends.
  3. 33 slots is way too many for a task that won't win or prevent our opponents from winning on its own.
  4. Wizards, please just put things that tap for colored mana in your precons. Just do it, I know you can. Thank you.
  5. You need 26 red sources to consistently cast a Vengeful Ancestor on curve and yet here we are with 17.
  6. This 37 land and six rock two-color manabase has symbol numbers that are similar to those of the budget 30 land 4-color manabase from my lands video.
  7. The deck has only a handful of good three-drops and basically no two-drops other than rocks.
  8. The ideal scenario is that you'll have key opposing creatures being tossed around wildly with opposing game plans and life totals crumbling before our very eyes, but how often will this actually happen?
  9. Temple of the False God is weird and very bad if you're running less than like 43 other lands.
  10. I'd also cut Relic Tower and Thought Vessel; this deck doesn't draw insane numbers of cards and it also doesn't need to hold up a bunch of interaction and niche tech pieces.
Full transcript
Introduction

I've never owned a precon.
Wizards of the Coast has been releasing preconstructed Commander decks ever since I started playing in 2011, and with increasing frequency, and I've just kept on ignoring them.
The reason for this is simple enough: I didn't start playing Commander until 2014, at which point I felt like a skilled enough player to build my own mono-removal pile to piss off my friends.
However, as I've continued to make videos and exist more in the Magic: The Gathering space, it's become increasingly clear to me that precons play a major role in the way people learn and conceptualize the EDH format, so I figure I should take a moment to engage with the world of precons.
I polled my patrons and they thought I should look at the “Blame Game” precon, so I'm going to do just that.
I'm going to break down what I think the deck is trying to do, how well it's accomplishing that plan, and some potential improvement routes for making it function better.

What the Commander Offers

Let's first break down what the commander is offering.
Nelly's first ability contains the “suspect” component, which offers immediate evasion by disabling a blocker, as well as the “goad” component, which offers a way to keep threats away from our life total as long as Nelly keeps attacking and guarantees that our opponents will be attacking each other.
The second ability is a source of draw that can offer up to three cards per turn cycle while also feeding opponents that resource as a reward for hitting each other, whether due to being compelled or otherwise.
This is a pretty fun package; the first ability will scale to great effect over time, and the second ability offers some fun politics gameplay and discourages our opponents from removing Nelly for fear of losing out on those sweet, sweet cards.
Less to the point here, the flavor here is great: a commander that is constantly accusing people of murder, which is causing the accused to lash out in order to curry favor as long as Nelly is around.

The Game Plan

Good stuff to go along with the commander: the deck is running lots of cards with the intent of getting our opponents to attack each other rather than attacking us.
There are a bunch of good cards; there are cards that give people advantages for attacking our opponents, there are cards that punish people for attacking us, and so on.
Basically, the deck is trying to control the mid game by getting our opponents to fight amongst themselves, and Nelly draws us additional cards when they do this.
This card draw should hopefully fuel a powerful late game where a suite of bigger creatures means that, once our opponents are dropping in life total and dwindling in number, we can take control of the game with our board state.

How Well Does the Deck Accomplish Its Plan?

So that's the game plan, but how effectively can the deck accomplish it?
Well, there are a handful of things that the deck is doing decently well when it comes to fundamentals.
It has eight cards that I can broadly label as draw, which isn't a bad number when paired with a commander that will likely be drawing one to two cards per turn cycle.
There are also 11 cards that one could label as ramp, which is a solid number, and this also works well with a creature base that includes a number of higher-costed beaters.
Finishing off the final opponent is something I'd naturally worry about for a goad deck, but I think the creature base is just beefy enough that the deck should be able to finish off a game a lot of the time as long as the commander has been getting in the desired number of draw triggers.
The deck contains nine cards that I'd call removal, and although I'm a little concerned at how high cost most of it is, I'm mostly willing to overlook that due to how many other options the deck has for interacting with opposing creatures.
The deck also includes 10 extra goad cards, which bolster the commander's card draw and will also help keep the heat off us even if our commander-based goad engine goes offline.

What I don't like is the amount of other player protective cards in the deck.
I tallied up all the cards that discourage or disallow attacking us in some way, and the total for these was 33 cards.
One third of the deck is trying to get our opponents to attack each other instead of us, and I get that's a big thing the deck is trying to do, but 33 slots is way too many for a task that won't win or prevent our opponents from winning on its own.
Like, okay, the ideal scenario is that you'll have key opposing creatures being tossed around wildly with opposing game plans and life totals crumbling before our very eyes, but how often will this actually happen?
There will be games where our opponents are developing horrifying wincons that don't use combat, and we just sit there twiddling our thumbs as we goad their utility 2/3s into Johnny Noard over there and draw them into the gas they need to murder us.
My guess is that different defensive cards are meant to serve somewhat different purposes throughout the game, but this is quite inefficient slots-wise.
Additionally, and this is kind of a precon classic, but the mana base is bad.
37 lands might be an okay count in the abstract, but it includes 11 solely colorless-producing utility lands.
This results in the deck having 20 white sources and 17 red sources between its artifacts and lands; that's right, this 37-land and six-rock two-color manabase has symbol numbers that are similar to those of the budget 30-land four-color manabase from my lands video.
And this deck has like 10 double red cards in it; you need 26 red sources to consistently cast a Vengeful Ancestor on curve, and yet here we are with 17.
Wizards, please just put things that tap for colored mana in your precons; just do it.
I know you can.
Thank you.

Playtesting Results

I ran 15 playtests with the deck, and my previous hunches were pretty well backed up.
The deck can have a decent mid-game curve out with solid ramping and drawing capabilities, but it frequently has mana issues and also frequently gets stuck on a handful of nothing but niche defensive cards.
Another issue that was on my radar but which became much more clear in playtesting was that the deck has only a handful of good three-drops and basically no two-drops other than rocks.
When I playtested it, I was aggressively mulliganing for early plays, which led to the deck having what I'd consider to be a decent curve out in eight of the 15 playtests; but I imagine a new player piloting the deck would probably happily keep a more average hand for the deck, one that plays nothing on turn one and two, a weird enchantment on turn three, and the commander on turn four.
Which I guess might work out in a game with other precons, but leaving your two-drop slot on the table in a two-color deck is not good and will hurt your chances of winning.
So, yeah, there are definitely issues here, but overall this deck has a very fun, useful commander and a pretty solid mid-game plan of influencing the attacks of the game and leveraging politics to its favor.
It's pretty well-rounded in most areas, with the three standout issues being mana base, mana curve, and an excess of defensive cards.

Tuning Up the Deck

So how do we go about tuning it up?
For improving this deck, I want to maintain the affordability and spirit of the deck but smooth out some of the rough edges.
First of all, I would want to cut a bunch of defensive cards because they are taking up too much goddamn space in this deck.
Specifically, I dislike the ones whose use cases are too narrow, whose effect is too limited, or which cost too much mana, and there are about a quarter of these that I would cut right off the bat.
From there, I think it's up to the player to some extent; I opted to cut an additional six for my final deck list.
In their place, there are a number of things that could be added, and I think a good option to somewhat fill the same role is cards that grant haste.
If Nelly dies on the declare combat step, well, that sucks for us, but if she dies any other time we want to be able to play her again and keep her wall of goad up.
I'd also add a bit of board protection in; if Nelly gets destroyed we're vulnerable to attacks, and if the board gets wiped we lose basically all momentum.

Next up, let's talk mana base.
The goal here is simple: we want several more symbols of each color, and to get there we cut colorless lands and rocks for ones that produce colored mana.
I like a lot of the utility lands in principle, evasion is good, graveyard hate is good, draw is good, so I'm going to start with the ones I see as adding basically nothing to the deck.
Temple of the False God is weird and very bad if you're running less than like 43 other lands.
Reliquary Tower and Thought Vessel are next up on my list; this deck doesn't draw insane numbers of cards, and it also doesn't need to hold up a bunch of interaction and niche tech pieces.
I'd also cut Kher Keep, and I'd consider cutting some number of Scavenger Grounds, Throne of the High City, and Sunhome if the deck was going to stay at the same land count, though these cards at least have some identifiable purpose here.
Speaking of land count, I think there are two options for this deck with regard to mana curve.
Option A would be to keep the higher curve at a couple lands and add four to five two-cost rocks in order to make sure that we're able to swing with the commander on turn four as often as possible; I'd also fill out the deck with a few more beaters in order to guarantee that we'll have the raw power to finish off the final player.
Option B would be to drop the curve and add a bunch of two- and three-drop creatures that could lead to us having a more aggressive game plan.
I think either of these two game plans would be valid, but in my list I went for Option A, as it seemed more in line with the original plan of the deck.
Though you could certainly make a much better deck with this commander, my goal here was to only swap out 20 cards to scrape out a bit of the precon jank factor and make the deck play more smoothly.

Closing Thoughts

I posted the original deck as well as the improved version I made in the description, so you can check out the changes I made and let me know in the comments if you think I utterly bastardized the entire point of the deck.
This is also a bit different than most of my other videos, so you can let me know if this sort of content is something you want to see more of in the future.
All right, bye now.
